E_6: Correct. Exposures can include url: pointing to the dashboard or report, making it easy for team members to find and view the downstream artifact. This improves discoverability.
Explanation: OVERALL: Exposures are dbt's way of documenting the 'last mile' of your data pipeline—how models are used in dashboards, reports, ML models, or applications. They create complete lineage from source data through transformations to business consumption. Exposures include metadata like owner, URL, description, and model dependencies. This documentation aids in impact analysis (which dashboards break if I change this model?), stakeholder communication, and understanding the business value of data assets. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/exposures

Question: Which selector syntax would you use to run a specific model and all its downstream dependencies?
Q_1: dbt run --select my_model
Q_2: dbt run --select my_model+
Q_3: dbt run --select +my_model
Q_4: dbt run --select my_model --with-children
Q_5: dbt run --downstream my_model
E_1: Incorrect. This runs only my_model itself, not its downstream dependencies. You need the + operator for dependencies.
E_2: Correct. The + operator after a model name selects that model and all downstream dependencies (children). my_model+ means 'my_model and everything downstream'. The + before would select upstream (my_model and parents). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/graph-operators
E_3: Incorrect. +my_model selects my_model and all upstream dependencies (parents/ancestors), not downstream. For downstream, put + after the name.
E_4: Incorrect. There's no --with-children flag. Use my_model+ to select a model and its children.
E_5: Incorrect. There's no --downstream flag. Graph traversal is done through + operators in --select: my_model+ for downstream.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt uses graph operators in selection syntax to traverse the DAG. The + operator selects dependencies: +my_model selects my_model and all ancestors (upstream), my_model+ selects my_model and all descendants (downstream), and +my_model+ selects the model and both upstream and downstream. You can also use n+ or +n for n degrees of separation. This powerful selection syntax enables targeted builds, testing impact of changes, and efficient CI/CD workflows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/graph-operators

Question: You want to control which other models can reference a specific dbt model, limiting its access to only certain downstream models. Which model configuration should you use?
Q_1: Set enabled: false to prevent the model from being referenced by other models
Q_2: Use the grants configuration to specify database-level permissions for who can query the model
Q_3: Set contract: true to enforce that only models with matching schemas can reference it
Q_4: Use the docs configuration to document which models should reference this model
Q_5: Set the access configuration to 'private' or 'protected' to limit which models can reference it using ref()
E_1: Incorrect. The enabled configuration controls whether a model is built at all, not whether it can be referenced. Setting enabled: false would completely disable the model, preventing it from running rather than controlling access.
E_2: Incorrect. The grants configuration sets database-level permissions (SELECT, INSERT, etc.) for database users or roles, not dbt model access. This controls warehouse-level access, not which dbt models can use ref() to reference it.
E_3: Incorrect. Model contracts enforce the model's output schema (columns, types, constraints), not which other models can reference it. Contracts ensure structural integrity, not access control between models.
E_4: Incorrect. The docs configuration is for documentation purposes only—it doesn't enforce any access control. Documentation can guide users but won't prevent models from being referenced inappropriately.
E_5: Correct. The access configuration controls model access levels in dbt. Setting access: private restricts the model so only models in the same directory/group can reference it. Setting access: protected allows access within the same project. The default is access: public. This provides governance over your DAG structure. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
Explanation: OVERALL: Model access configuration is a governance feature that controls which other dbt models can reference a given model using ref(). The access property has three levels: 'public' (default—can be referenced by any model), 'protected' (can only be referenced within the same dbt project), and 'private' (can only be referenced by models in the same directory or group). This allows you to create clear boundaries in your DAG, preventing inappropriate dependencies and maintaining clean separation between layers (staging, intermediate, marts). Access control helps enforce architectural patterns and prevents teams from creating unwanted cross-dependencies. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access

Question: You want a model to use a specific database separate from the default target database. How should you configure this?
Q_1: Use the database config: {{ config(database='analytics_db') }}
Q_2: Modify profiles.yml to change the target database for all models
Q_3: Use a different schema: {{ config(schema='other_db') }}
Q_4: Databases cannot be configured per-model; all models use the target database
Q_5: Create a custom materialization that specifies the database
E_1: Correct. The database configuration overrides the default target database for a specific model. Use in model file: {{ config(database='custom_db') }}, in schema.yml, or in dbt_project.yml for path-based config. The model creates in custom_db instead of the default database from profiles.yml. Common for cross-database models or multi-tenant scenarios. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/database
E_2: Incorrect. Changing profiles.yml affects ALL models, not just one. The question asks about a specific model. Use the database config for model-level control.
E_3: Incorrect. Schema and database are different concepts. Schema is within a database. To use a different database, use the database config, not schema.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt supports per-model database configuration via the database config. This enables cross-database models and flexible deployment patterns.
E_5: Incorrect. While custom materializations can control where models build, this is overly complex. Use the database config for straightforward database selection.
Explanation: OVERALL: The database config overrides target database for specific models. Syntax: {{ config(database='db_name') }}. Use cases: (1) Cross-database analytics, (2) Multi-tenant architectures, (3) Separating staging/prod data, (4) Legacy system integration. Combine with schema config for full location control. The model builds in specified_database.configured_schema.model_name. Note: your warehouse user needs permissions on the specified database. Database config works with generate_database_name macro for dynamic naming. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/database

Question: In a multi-team dbt project, Team A owns models in the 'marketing' group and Team B owns models in the 'finance' group. A Team B model tries to reference a Team A model with access: private. What happens?
Q_1: The ref() compiles successfully but fails at runtime when querying the table
Q_2: dbt throws a compilation error because the model is private to the marketing group
Q_3: The model builds but dbt logs a warning about the cross-group reference
Q_4: The ref() works if both teams are in the same dbt project
Q_5: dbt automatically changes the access to protected to allow the reference
E_1: Incorrect. Access violations are caught during compilation, not runtime. dbt checks access permissions when building the DAG—it won't even compile if a model tries to ref() a private model from another group.
E_2: Correct. Models with access: private can only be referenced by models in the same group. When Team B (finance group) tries to ref() a Team A model (marketing group) with private access, dbt fails during compilation with an access violation error. To allow cross-group references, the model must be access: protected or access: public. This enforces governance boundaries. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_3: Incorrect. Access controls are enforced strictly, not as warnings. private access prevents ref() from other groups entirely—it's a hard boundary. If you want to allow references but track them, use protected or public access with monitoring.
E_4: Incorrect. Being in the same project doesn't override access controls. Groups and access settings work within a single project to establish boundaries between teams. private means private to the group, regardless of project structure.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt never automatically changes configurations. Access levels must be explicitly set. If a private model needs to be referenced across groups, the model owner must explicitly change access to protected or public.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model access controls enforce governance boundaries in multi-team projects. Access levels: (1) private (default): Only models in the same group can ref() this model. Use for internal implementation details. (2) protected: Models in the same project (any group) can ref() it. Use for shared utilities within a project. (3) public: Any project can ref() it (including installed packages). Use for stable, documented APIs. Workflow: Team A marks internal models as private, Team A marks shared models as protected/public with contracts, Team B can only ref() protected/public models, violations fail at compile time (not runtime). This prevents unintended dependencies and clarifies ownership. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access

Question: Which file format is used to define dbt package dependencies in your project?
Q_1: requirements.txt, similar to Python package dependencies
Q_2: packages.yml, which lists package sources and versions
Q_3: dbt_project.yml, under the packages: section
Q_4: dependencies.json, which dbt parses during compilation
Q_5: packages.json, following the npm package manager convention
E_1: Incorrect. requirements.txt is for Python pip packages, not dbt packages. dbt packages are dbt projects (models, macros, tests), not Python libraries. dbt uses its own package management system with packages.yml.
E_2: Correct. dbt package dependencies are defined in a packages.yml file in your project root. This YAML file specifies packages from dbt Hub (package: org/name), git repositories (git: URL, revision: branch/tag), or local paths. After editing packages.yml, run dbt deps to install. This is the standard dbt package definition format. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages
E_3: Incorrect. dbt_project.yml contains project configuration (name, version, model configs), not package dependencies. Package dependencies are declared in a separate packages.yml file. This separation keeps concerns distinct: dbt_project.yml for project settings, packages.yml for dependencies.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt doesn't use a dependencies.json file. Package dependencies are defined in packages.yml (YAML format). JSON is used for dbt's internal artifacts (manifest.json, run_results.json), not for user-defined package specifications.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt uses packages.yml (YAML), not packages.json (JSON). While inspired by package managers like npm, dbt chose YAML for consistency with other dbt configuration files (dbt_project.yml, schema.yml, profiles.yml).
Explanation: OVERALL: packages.yml defines external dbt packages your project depends on. Three package source types: (1) dbt Hub packages: 'package: dbt-labs/dbt_utils version: 1.0.0', (2) Git repositories: 'git: https://github.com/org/repo revision: main', (3) Local packages: 'local: ../path/to/package'. After defining packages, run dbt deps to install them into dbt_packages/. Packages provide reusable macros (dbt_utils), tests (dbt_expectations), models, and more. Always pin versions for reproducibility. The packages.yml file is YAML format and sits in your project root alongside dbt_project.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following statements about singular tests in dbt are TRUE? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Singular tests are SQL files in the tests/ directory that return rows representing test failures
Q_2: Singular tests can reference models using the ref() function
Q_3: Singular tests are automatically applied to all models in your project
Q_4: Singular tests are faster than generic tests because they're optimized by dbt
Q_5: Singular tests can use Jinja macros and variables for dynamic logic
Q_6: Singular tests can be configured with severity, tags, and other configs using config blocks
E_1: Correct. Singular tests are .sql files containing SELECT statements. Any rows returned indicate test failures—an empty result means the test passed. Place them in tests/ directory. Use for custom business logic or complex validations not covered by generic tests. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#singular-data-tests
E_2: Correct. Singular tests support all Jinja functions available in models, including ref() and source(). This allows testing relationships between models, validating business rules across multiple tables, and leveraging dbt's dependency resolution. Example: SELECT * FROM {{ ref('orders') }} WHERE amount < 0. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#singular-data-tests
E_3: Incorrect. Singular tests are explicit SQL files that run independently—they're not automatically applied to anything. Each singular test targets specific models/logic defined in its SQL. Generic tests (not singular) can be applied across multiple models via schema.yml.
E_4: Incorrect. Test performance depends on the SQL logic and data volume, not the test type. Both singular and generic tests compile to SQL executed in your warehouse. Neither has inherent performance advantages—efficiency depends on your query logic.
E_5: Correct. Singular tests support full Jinja templating, allowing use of macros, variables ({{ var('...') }}), target information ({{ target.name }}), and custom logic. This enables flexible, environment-aware testing. Example: {% if target.name == 'prod' %} WHERE critical_field IS NULL {% endif %}. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#singular-data-tests
E_6: Correct. Singular tests support configuration using {{ config(...) }} blocks at the top of the file, just like models. Configure severity, tags, enabled, store_failures, etc. Example: {{ config(severity='warn', tags=['critical']) }}. This allows flexible test management. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/test-configs
Explanation: OVERALL: Singular tests provide flexibility for custom validation logic that doesn't fit generic test patterns. They're SQL SELECT statements that return failing rows (empty = pass). Best practices: (1) Use for complex business rules (e.g., 'total sales must match sum of line items'), (2) Test cross-model relationships not covered by the relationships test, (3) Validate domain-specific logic unique to your business, (4) Name files descriptively (e.g., assert_revenue_matches_line_items.sql), (5) Use config blocks for severity and metadata, (6) Leverage Jinja for dynamic, environment-aware tests. For reusable validation patterns, create custom generic tests instead. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#singular-data-tests

Question: In a CI/CD pipeline, you want to run only the models that changed in a pull request plus their downstream dependencies. Which command should you use?
Q_1: dbt run --select @changed to run changed models and their parents
Q_2: dbt run --modified to automatically detect and run changed models
Q_3: dbt run --select state:modified+ --state path/to/prod/artifacts
Q_4: dbt run --select result:fail to rerun failed models from previous runs
Q_5: dbt run --select tag:pr to run models tagged for pull request testing
E_1: Incorrect. The @ syntax selects both parents and children, but 'changed' isn't a valid selector. You need state:modified for change detection, and @ selects both directions (parents+children), not just downstream.
E_2: Incorrect. There's no --modified flag. Change detection requires state comparison using --state flag with state:modified selector.
E_3: Correct. This is the Slim CI pattern: (1) --state points to production's manifest.json (previous state), (2) state:modified selects models with code changes, (3) + selects downstream dependencies. dbt compares current code against prod artifacts, identifies changes, and runs changed models plus everything downstream. This dramatically speeds CI by running only impacted models instead of the full DAG. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods
E_4: Incorrect. result:fail reruns models that failed in the last run—useful for dbt retry. The question asks about running models that changed in a PR, which requires state:modified, not result:fail.
E_5: Incorrect. While you could manually tag models, this doesn't automatically detect changes. You'd need to maintain tags every time models change. State comparison (state:modified) automatically detects changes without manual tagging.
Explanation: OVERALL: Slim CI uses state comparison to run only impacted models in PRs, dramatically reducing CI time. Setup: (1) Store production manifest.json as artifact, (2) In CI, fetch prod manifest, (3) Run: dbt run --select state:modified+ --state path/to/prod/manifest, (4) Optionally add state:new+ for newly added models. The + selects downstream dependencies—models that depend on changed models. Common patterns: state:modified+ (changed models and children), +state:modified+ (changed models, parents, and children), state:modified state:new (only changed/new, no deps). This enables fast feedback in PRs while ensuring downstream impacts are tested. Reference: https://discourse.getdbt.com/t/how-we-sped-up-our-ci-runs-by-10x-using-slim-ci/2603

Question: What is the primary purpose of using the --state flag with dbt commands?
Q_1: To compare the current project against a previous run and select only modified models
Q_2: To save the current project state to a file for version control
Q_3: To resume a failed dbt run from where it stopped
Q_4: To persist model state across different dbt projects for cross-project dependencies
Q_5: To maintain incremental model state and track which records have been processed
E_1: Correct. The --state flag points to a previous dbt run's artifacts (manifest.json) to enable state-based selection. Combined with selectors like state:modified or state:new, you can run only models that have changed since the previous run. This enables efficient CI/CD—run only what changed in a PR instead of the entire project. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods
E_2: Incorrect. The --state flag reads existing state artifacts; it doesn't save state. dbt automatically creates manifest.json and other artifacts in target/ after each run. The --state flag compares your current code against these previous artifacts to determine what changed.
E_3: Incorrect. Resuming failed runs uses the dbt retry command, not --state. While --state enables state comparison for selection, it doesn't resume incomplete runs. dbt retry re-executes failed models from the most recent run.
E_4: Incorrect. While state artifacts can be used across projects, the primary purpose of --state is comparing current code against previous runs to select changed models. Cross-project dependencies are handled through dbt packages and model references, not state comparison.
E_5: Incorrect. Incremental models use database tables themselves to track state (via unique keys and filters), not the --state flag. The --state flag is for comparing project code changes, not data processing state in incremental models.
Explanation: OVERALL: State comparison enables selective runs based on code changes. Workflow: (1) A previous dbt run creates artifacts (manifest.json, run_results.json) in target/, (2) Make code changes to your project, (3) Run with --state pointing to previous artifacts: dbt run --select state:modified --state path/to/artifacts, (4) dbt compares current code against previous manifest, identifies changes, (5) Only changed models (and optionally their children with +) run. Common selectors: state:modified (changed models), state:new (newly added), state:modified+ (changed models and downstream). This dramatically speeds up CI/CD in large projects—instead of running 1000s of models, run only the 5 that changed. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are differences between generic tests and singular tests in dbt? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Generic tests are defined in schema.yml; singular tests are .sql files in the tests/ directory
Q_2: Generic tests run faster than singular tests because they're optimized by dbt
Q_3: Generic tests are reusable across multiple models and columns; singular tests are model-specific
Q_4: Generic tests can accept parameters like values for accepted_values; singular tests cannot accept parameters
Q_5: Generic tests are defined as macros; singular tests are SQL SELECT statements
Q_6: Generic tests only check for NULL values; singular tests can check any condition
E_1: Correct. Generic tests (formerly schema tests) are applied to models and columns in schema.yml configuration files. Singular tests are standalone SQL files in the tests/ directory that return rows that fail the test. This is a key structural difference in how they're authored. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests
E_2: Incorrect. Test performance depends on the SQL logic and data volume, not the test type. Both generic and singular tests compile to SQL queries executed in your warehouse. Neither has inherent performance advantages—efficiency depends on how you write the test logic.
E_3: Correct. Generic tests are parameterized templates that can be applied to any model/column (e.g., not_null, unique, accepted_values). Singular tests are custom SQL files for specific validation logic. Generic tests promote reusability and consistency; singular tests handle one-off validations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests
E_4: Correct. Generic tests accept parameters configured in schema.yml (e.g., accepted_values: {values: [...]}, relationships: {to: ref('table'), field: 'column'}). Singular tests are plain SQL files without parameterization (though you can use Jinja variables). This makes generic tests flexible and configurable. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests
E_5: Correct. Generic tests are implemented as Jinja macros in tests/generic/ (or macros/) that accept parameters like {{ model }}, {{ column_name }}. Singular tests are .sql files containing SELECT statements that return failing rows. This reflects their different purposes: reusable templates vs specific validations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#generic-data-tests
E_6: Incorrect. Generic tests can check any condition—not_null checks for NULLs, but unique checks uniqueness, accepted_values checks allowed values, relationships checks referential integrity. You can also write custom generic tests for any validation pattern. Both test types support arbitrary SQL logic.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt provides two complementary test types: (1) Generic tests: Reusable, parameterized test templates defined as macros and applied in schema.yml. Built-in examples: not_null, unique, accepted_values, relationships. Create custom generic tests for reusable validation patterns. (2) Singular tests: Standalone SQL files in tests/ for specific, one-off validations. Write SELECT statements that return rows failing the condition. Use generic tests for common validations across your project, singular tests for complex business logic or model-specific rules that aren't reusable. Both types fail when they return rows (empty result = passing test). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: You have a staging model stg_orders that cleans raw order data. Multiple downstream models depend on it. What materialization should you typically use for staging models?
Q_1: Use incremental materialization to process only new orders and optimize for large datasets
Q_2: Use ephemeral materialization to avoid creating database objects and save storage
Q_3: Use view materialization for lightweight, simple transformations that are queried by downstream models
Q_4: Use table materialization to pre-compute results and optimize downstream model performance
Q_5: The materialization doesn't matter for staging models; use whatever is set as the project default
E_1: Incorrect. While incremental can optimize large datasets, staging models typically perform simple cleaning and renaming—not complex aggregations. The overhead of incremental logic (unique keys, merge statements) often isn't worth it for staging. Reserve incremental for large fact tables in the mart layer. Keep staging simple with views or tables.
E_2: Incorrect. While ephemeral avoids database objects, it inlines CTEs into downstream models, which can create performance problems when multiple models reference the same staging model (the CTE is repeated). Staging models with multiple dependents should be views or tables, not ephemeral.
E_3: Correct. Staging models typically perform simple transformations (renaming, casting, basic filtering) and follow the principle of being lightweight pass-throughs of source data. Views are ideal because: (1) they're computationally cheap for simple transforms, (2) they always reflect current source data, (3) they don't duplicate storage, (4) downstream models query them efficiently. This is a dbt best practice for staging layers. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging
E_4: Incorrect for typical staging models. Tables pre-compute results, which is unnecessary for simple cleaning/renaming operations. Staging transformations are usually fast enough that materializing as tables adds overhead without benefit. Reserve table materialization for complex transformations in intermediate or mart layers.
E_5: Incorrect. Materialization strategy should be intentional based on the model's purpose and complexity. dbt best practices specifically recommend views for staging models because they perform simple transformations. Following these conventions makes your project structure predictable and maintainable.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt's recommended project structure follows a layered approach: Staging → Intermediate → Mart. Each layer has typical materialization patterns: (1) Staging models use views—they perform simple, 1:1 transformations of source tables (renaming, casting, basic cleaning). Views ensure freshness and avoid unnecessary storage. (2) Intermediate models use views or ephemeral—they contain reusable business logic but aren't end-user-facing. (3) Mart models use tables—they perform complex aggregations and are queried by BI tools, so pre-computation improves query performance. Following these patterns creates efficient, maintainable projects. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging

Question: What is the primary purpose of the meta configuration in dbt models?
Q_1: To configure model metadata like owner, maturity level, or custom business context
Q_2: To automatically generate model documentation from metadata
Q_3: To control model execution order in the DAG
Q_4: To store custom structured metadata that can be accessed in docs, tests, and macros
Q_5: To define database-level metadata tags and labels
E_1: Incorrect. While meta CAN store this information, the primary purpose is broader: meta stores arbitrary custom metadata that doesn't fit other config fields. It's a flexible key-value store for any structured data you need.
E_2: Incorrect. meta doesn't automatically generate docs—descriptions in schema.yml do that. meta provides structured metadata accessible in docs and programmatically, but doesn't auto-generate narrative documentation.
E_3: Incorrect. DAG order is determined by ref() and source() dependencies, not meta configuration. meta stores metadata; it doesn't affect execution.
E_4: Correct. meta is a flexible configuration for storing arbitrary key-value metadata: meta: {owner: 'team@co.com', tier: '1', contains_pii: true, sla_hours: 4}. This metadata: (1) appears in generated documentation, (2) is accessible in macros via model.meta, (3) can drive custom logic (e.g., PII handling), (4) enables metadata-driven workflows. Use for business context that doesn't fit standard configs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta
E_5: Incorrect. meta is dbt project metadata, not warehouse-level tags. To create database tags/labels, use post-hooks with warehouse-specific SQL. meta is for dbt's internal metadata management.
Explanation: OVERALL: The meta configuration provides flexible, structured metadata storage for custom project needs. Common use cases: (1) Ownership: meta: {owner: 'team@company.com', slack: '#data-eng'}, (2) Data governance: meta: {contains_pii: true, retention_days: 90, tier: '1'}, (3) Business context: meta: {department: 'finance', stakeholder: 'CFO', update_frequency: 'daily'}, (4) SLA/monitoring: meta: {sla_hours: 4, criticality: 'high'}. Access in macros: {{ model.meta.owner }}, appears in docs, enables custom logic. Meta doesn't affect dbt's core behavior—it's purely for storing your custom metadata. Combine with groups, tags, and contracts for comprehensive model governance. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta

Question: Your organization requires that all data transformations be auditable with clear ownership. Which dbt feature BEST supports this requirement?
Q_1: Use model groups with defined owners to establish clear ownership in documentation
Q_2: Add comments in SQL files explaining who owns each model
Q_3: Create a separate ownership tracking spreadsheet outside of dbt
Q_4: Use Git commit messages to track who made changes
Q_5: Set file permissions to restrict who can edit each model
E_1: Correct. Groups with owner metadata (name/email) provide clear ownership attribution. In schema.yml, define groups with owners, assign models to groups. This appears in dbt docs, making ownership discoverable. Combined with Git history (code changes) and dbt Cloud run history (execution audit trail), this provides comprehensive auditability. Groups also enable access controls for governance. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access#groups
E_2: Incorrect. SQL comments aren't structured metadata and don't appear in generated documentation. They're not discoverable by consumers and aren't machine-readable for reporting. Use formal metadata (groups, meta fields) instead.
E_3: Incorrect. External documentation becomes stale and isn't co-located with code. dbt provides built-in ownership features (groups, meta fields) that stay in sync with the project and appear in documentation.
E_4: Incorrect for ownership attribution. Git tracks who changed code, not who owns/maintains it. Multiple people might edit a model, but ownership (who's responsible, who to ask questions) is different from authorship.
E_5: Incorrect. File permissions control edit access, not ownership attribution. Also, restricting edits in a collaborative environment reduces agility. Ownership should be documented and discoverable, not enforced through permissions.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt supports auditability and ownership through multiple features: (1) Groups: Define teams/domains with owner: {name: 'Team Name', email: 'team@company.com'}, assign models to groups, ownership shows in docs. (2) Meta fields: Store custom metadata like owner, SLA, criticality: meta: {owner: 'team@company.com', sla: '1 hour'}. (3) Git integration: Code changes tracked with commit history. (4) dbt Cloud: Run history shows who ran what, when. (5) Tags: Organize by team/domain for selection and filtering. Best practice: use groups for ownership boundaries and access control, add meta for additional context, maintain consistent patterns across your project. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access#groups

Question: What command generates column-level lineage in dbt documentation?
Q_1: Run dbt compile --with-columns
Q_2: Add column_lineage: true to dbt_project.yml
Q_3: Run dbt docs generate which queries warehouse metadata for column details
Q_4: Column lineage is automatically tracked through ref() functions
Q_5: Use dbt run --catalog to build models and generate column metadata simultaneously
E_1: Incorrect. There's no --with-columns flag for dbt compile. Column metadata comes from dbt docs generate querying the warehouse, not compilation.
E_2: Incorrect. There's no column_lineage configuration. Column information in docs comes from querying warehouse metadata via dbt docs generate.
E_3: Correct. dbt docs generate creates catalog.json by querying your warehouse's information schema for table/column metadata (names, types, stats). This powers column-level information and lineage in docs. Run after building models to get accurate catalog data. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs
E_4: Incorrect. ref() creates model-level lineage, not column-level. Column details come from querying built tables' metadata via dbt docs generate.
E_5: Incorrect. There's no --catalog flag for dbt run. Catalog generation is separate: dbt run (build models), then dbt docs generate (query metadata).
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt documentation has two components: manifest.json (from project files) and catalog.json (from warehouse metadata). Run dbt docs generate to create both. For column details: (1) dbt queries information_schema, (2) Retrieves column names, types, comments, (3) Calculates statistics (row counts, etc.), (4) Creates catalog.json. Workflow: dbt run → dbt docs generate → dbt docs serve. Without catalog, docs show only schema.yml descriptions. With catalog, you see actual warehouse metadata. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs

Question: You want to use a different alias for a model in production versus development. How can you accomplish this?
Q_1: Create separate model files for each environment with different names
Q_2: Use the enabled configuration to disable the model in one environment
Q_3: Use Jinja conditionals in the alias config: {{ config(alias='name_' ~ target.name) }}
Q_4: Set different schemas for each environment using custom_schema
Q_5: Aliases cannot be environment-specific; they must be the same across all environments
E_1: Incorrect. Duplicating model files violates DRY principles and creates maintenance burden. dbt provides configuration mechanisms for environment-specific behavior without code duplication.
E_2: Incorrect. Enabled controls whether a model builds at all, not what it's named. You need the model in both environments, just with different names.
E_3: Correct. The alias configuration accepts Jinja expressions, allowing environment-specific names. Example: {{ config(alias='customers_' ~ target.name) }} creates 'customers_dev' in dev, 'customers_prod' in prod. Or use conditionals: {{ config(alias='customers' if target.name == 'prod' else 'customers_staging') }}. This enables flexible naming without code duplication. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/alias
E_4: Incorrect. Schema determines database location, not model name. While you can use different schemas per environment, the question asks about aliases (the table/view name itself), not schemas.
E_5: Incorrect. Aliases support Jinja templating, enabling environment-specific values using target.name, variables, or other conditional logic. This is a supported and common pattern.
Explanation: OVERALL: The alias configuration determines the table/view name in your warehouse (as opposed to the model name in your project). It supports Jinja templating for dynamic values. Common patterns: (1) Environment-specific: alias='table_' ~ target.name, (2) Conditional: alias='table' if target.name == 'prod' else 'table_dev', (3) Variable-based: alias=var('table_prefix') ~ '_customers'. By default, models use their filename as the alias. Custom aliases let you: match legacy naming conventions, add prefixes/suffixes per environment, create cleaner names than filenames allow. Combine with custom schemas for complete control over table location and naming. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/alias

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are valid ways to configure a relationships test in dbt? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Define the test in SQL as: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM customers)
Q_2: Use a relationships test between two columns in the same model
Q_3: Configure in schema.yml with the column parameter for the local foreign key
Q_4: Use the to parameter to specify the parent model: to: ref('parent_model')
Q_5: Use the field parameter to specify the column in the parent model to check against
Q_6: Apply the test in schema.yml under the column's tests array with both to and field parameters
E_1: Incorrect. While this SQL pattern checks referential integrity, it's a singular test (custom SQL file), not a relationships test (generic test). The question asks about configuring the relationships generic test, not writing custom SQL.
E_2: Incorrect. The relationships test validates foreign key relationships between two different models/tables (e.g., orders.customer_id → customers.id). For validating relationships within the same model, you'd use different logic or a custom singular test.
E_3: Incorrect. The relationships test is applied to a column in schema.yml, so you don't specify a 'column' parameter—you're already defining it on a column. The configuration should be under that column's tests array.
E_4: Correct. The relationships test requires a 'to' parameter specifying the parent model using ref(). Example: tests: [relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'id'}]. This validates that all values in the child column exist in the parent model's specified field. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/relationships
E_5: Correct. The 'field' parameter specifies which column in the parent model to validate against. Example: on orders.customer_id, configure tests: [relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'customer_id'}]. This checks that all customer_ids in orders exist in customers.customer_id. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/relationships
E_6: Correct. The standard syntax is to apply relationships tests in schema.yml under a column's tests array: columns: - name: customer_id tests: - relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'id'}. This is the recommended, documented approach. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/relationships
Explanation: OVERALL: The relationships test validates foreign key constraints, ensuring referential integrity between models. Configuration requires: (1) Apply to a column in the child model (e.g., orders.customer_id), (2) Specify 'to' parameter with parent model: to: ref('parent_model'), (3) Specify 'field' parameter with parent column: field: 'column_name', (4) Define in schema.yml under the column's tests array. Example: columns: - name: customer_id tests: - relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'id'}. The test fails if any values in the child column don't exist in the parent column. This catches data quality issues like orphaned records or invalid references. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/relationships

Question: When using incremental models, what does the is_incremental() macro return?
Q_1: True if new data exists in the source table since the last run
Q_2: True if the target table exists and this is not a full-refresh run
Q_3: True if the model's SQL has changed since the last run
Q_4: True if the unique_key configuration is set on the model
Q_5: True only during the first run of an incremental model
E_1: Incorrect. is_incremental() doesn't check source data—it checks if the target model already exists as a table in the warehouse. It doesn't know about source data freshness or changes.
E_2: Correct. is_incremental() returns True when: (1) The model is configured as incremental, (2) The target table already exists in the warehouse, (3) The run is NOT using --full-refresh flag. This determines whether to run incremental logic (filter for new data) or full logic (select all data). Use in {% if is_incremental() %} blocks to add incremental filters. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models#understanding-the-is_incremental-macro
E_3: Incorrect. is_incremental() doesn't compare code changes. It simply checks if the target table exists. For change detection, use state comparison (state:modified), not is_incremental().
E_4: Incorrect. is_incremental() checks table existence, not configuration. unique_key affects how incremental merges work but doesn't determine if is_incremental() returns True.
E_5: Incorrect. This is backwards—is_incremental() returns False on the first run (table doesn't exist yet), True on subsequent runs (table exists). First run builds the full table; later runs incrementally update it.
Explanation: OVERALL: The is_incremental() macro controls incremental model logic flow. On first run: table doesn't exist → is_incremental() = False → run full SELECT (no filter) → create initial table. On subsequent runs: table exists → is_incremental() = True → run SELECT with filter (new records only) → merge/append to existing table. With --full-refresh: is_incremental() = False (forced) → rebuild entire table. Common pattern: SELECT * FROM source {% if is_incremental() %} WHERE updated_at > (SELECT MAX(updated_at) FROM {{ this }}) {% endif %}. This builds full table initially, then adds only new records incrementally. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models

Question: After making changes to your model descriptions in schema.yml, what command should you run to update the documentation website?
Q_1: Run dbt docs update to refresh the documentation with new descriptions
Q_2: Run dbt compile to update the documentation artifacts
Q_3: Run dbt run to rebuild models, which automatically updates documentation
Q_4: Run dbt docs generate to create updated documentation artifacts, then dbt docs serve to view them
Q_5: Documentation updates automatically when you save schema.yml files
E_1: Incorrect. There is no dbt docs update command. The two documentation commands are: dbt docs generate (creates documentation artifacts) and dbt docs serve (starts a local web server to view them).
E_2: Incorrect. While dbt compile generates compiled SQL, it doesn't create or update documentation artifacts. You need dbt docs generate to create the documentation json files that power the documentation website.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt run builds models in the database but doesn't update documentation artifacts. Building models and generating documentation are separate processes. You must explicitly run dbt docs generate to update documentation.
E_4: Correct. dbt docs generate reads your project files (schema.yml, model SQL, etc.) and creates documentation artifacts (manifest.json, catalog.json). Then dbt docs serve starts a local web server to view the documentation. Always run generate after changing descriptions to see updates reflected in the docs site. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs
E_5: Incorrect. dbt doesn't watch files or automatically regenerate documentation. You must explicitly run dbt docs generate to update documentation artifacts after making changes. In dbt Cloud IDE, changes appear after regenerating docs.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt's documentation workflow involves two commands: (1) dbt docs generate creates documentation artifacts by parsing your project (models, schema.yml, sources, tests, etc.) and generates manifest.json and catalog.json files. Optionally, include catalog information by running 'dbt docs generate' which queries your warehouse for column types, freshness, and statistics. (2) dbt docs serve starts a local web server (typically on port 8080) to view the generated documentation in your browser. The workflow is: make changes → run dbt docs generate → (optionally) run dbt docs serve to view locally. In production, dbt Cloud hosts documentation automatically after successful runs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs

Question: A dbt model fails with the error 'Compilation Error: dbt found two relations with the database representation "analytics.customers"'. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: Two different models are trying to write to the same table simultaneously
Q_2: Two models in your project have the same name or compile to the same database object
Q_3: The customers table exists in both your dev and prod schemas
Q_4: A circular dependency exists between the customers model and another model
Q_5: The model name conflicts with a source table name defined in sources.yml
E_1: Incorrect. dbt runs models sequentially based on the DAG (unless using dbt Cloud with parallelism on independent models). This error is about ambiguous references during compilation, not runtime conflicts. The issue is dbt can't determine which model to reference when multiple models compile to the same database object name.
E_2: Correct. This error occurs when dbt detects multiple models that would create the same relation in the database. Common causes: (1) duplicate model filenames in different directories, (2) different models with custom aliases that resolve to the same name, (3) models in different schemas configured to write to the same schema with the same name. Fix by renaming one model or adjusting alias/schema configurations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/faqs/Models/unique-model-names
E_3: Incorrect. Dev and prod schemas are separate database schemas—having tables with the same name in different schemas is normal and expected. This error is about duplicate model definitions within a single dbt project configuration, not about tables in different environments.
E_4: Incorrect. Circular dependencies produce a different error: 'Found a cycle' or 'Cycle detected'. The error message about 'two relations with the same database representation' specifically indicates duplicate model names or aliases, not dependency issues.
E_5: Incorrect. While naming conflicts between models and sources are poor practice, they don't cause this specific error. dbt distinguishes models (referenced with ref()) from sources (referenced with source()). This error is about duplicate model definitions, not model-source naming overlaps.
Explanation: OVERALL: The 'two relations with the same database representation' error indicates dbt found multiple models that compile to the same database object. This typically happens when: (1) two .sql files have the same name in different subdirectories, (2) models use custom aliases via {{ config(alias='...') }} that conflict, (3) schema configurations cause different models to write to the same location. To fix: ensure all model names are unique within your project, review alias configurations for conflicts, check schema configurations don't inadvertently overlap. Model names must be unique because dbt uses them for DAG resolution and ref() function lookups. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/faqs/Models/unique-model-names

Question: Your dbt pipeline is experiencing slow performance. Which optimization strategy is MOST effective?
Q_1: Convert all models to ephemeral materialization to avoid database writes
Q_2: Run dbt with --threads 1 to avoid warehouse overload
Q_3: Disable all tests to reduce execution time
Q_4: Use incremental materializations for large tables to process only new data
Q_5: Analyze the DAG to identify bottlenecks, then optimize specific slow models and increase parallelism where appropriate
E_1: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are CTEs inlined into dependents—for models with multiple downstream dependencies, this repeats computation and can hurt performance. Use ephemeral sparingly for simple single-dependent transformations.
E_2: Incorrect. Reducing threads to 1 serializes execution, dramatically increasing runtime. Warehouse overload is better addressed by: right-sizing warehouse, managing concurrency limits, staggering jobs, not by serializing dbt execution.
E_3: Incorrect. While tests add time, disabling them eliminates data quality gates. Instead, optimize tests (where clauses, limit), run critical tests only in production, or use --exclude for specific test categories in dev.
E_4: Incorrect for this specific scenario. While incremental helps with large tables, the question asks for the MOST effective general strategy. Incremental is one optimization among many, not universally most effective.
E_5: Correct. Effective performance optimization requires measurement and targeted fixes: (1) Run with --debug to see execution times, (2) Identify slowest models, (3) Optimize expensive SQL (indexes, partitioning, query rewrites), (4) Use appropriate materializations (incremental for large tables), (5) Increase --threads if warehouse can handle it, (6) Use state comparison in CI to run fewer models. Blanket strategies like 'convert everything to X' are less effective than targeted optimization. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deploy-environments
Explanation: OVERALL: Performance optimization requires data-driven analysis: (1) Measure: Run with --debug, analyze logs, identify bottlenecks. (2) Optimize SQL: Fix expensive joins, add filters, improve aggregations. (3) Right-size materializations: incremental for large append-only tables, tables for complex aggregations, views for simple transforms. (4) Leverage parallelism: Increase --threads based on warehouse capacity, use DAG structure for natural parallelism. (5) Reduce scope: Use model selection for dev (--select), Slim CI for PR validation, scheduled jobs by frequency. (6) Warehouse optimization: Appropriate warehouse size, clustering/partitioning, query optimization. Avoid premature optimization—measure first, optimize bottlenecks, validate improvements. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deploy-environments

Question: [SELECT 2] Which of the following are valid ways to specify dbt package sources in packages.yml? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Specify a PyPI package name: pypi: dbt-utils
Q_2: Specify a git repository URL with revision: git: https://github.com/org/repo, revision: main
Q_3: Download and install via npm: npm install dbt-package-name
Q_4: Specify a package from dbt Hub: package: dbt-labs/dbt_utils, version: 1.0.0
Q_5: Copy package code into your models/ directory to install it
Q_6: Use environment variables: env_var: DBT_PACKAGE_URL
E_1: Incorrect. dbt packages are not Python/PyPI packages. They're dbt projects hosted on dbt Hub, git repositories, or local paths. Use package:, git:, or local: for dbt package sources, not pypi:.
E_2: Correct. Git repositories are a valid package source. Specify the repository URL and a revision (branch, tag, or commit SHA). Example: - git: 'https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-utils' revision: 1.0.0. This allows using packages not on dbt Hub or in-development packages. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages#git-packages
E_3: Incorrect. dbt packages aren't npm packages. dbt has its own package management system. Define packages in packages.yml and install with dbt deps, not npm.
E_4: Correct. dbt Hub packages use the package: format with organization/package-name and version. Example: - package: dbt-labs/dbt_utils version: 1.0.0. This is the simplest method for public packages on hub.getdbt.com. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages#hub-packages
E_5: Incorrect. While you could copy code, this defeats package management: no version control, no updates, no dependency resolution. Always use packages.yml and dbt deps for external dependencies.
E_6: Incorrect. packages.yml doesn't support env_var() for package specifications. Package sources must be literal strings. Environment variables are for profiles.yml (credentials), not package definitions.
Explanation: OVERALL: packages.yml supports three package source types: (1) dbt Hub: 'package: org/name version: X.X.X' for packages on hub.getdbt.com, (2) Git: 'git: URL revision: branch/tag/sha' for any git repository, (3) Local: 'local: ../path' for local packages in development. After editing packages.yml, run dbt deps to install packages into dbt_packages/. Best practices: pin specific versions for reproducibility, use Hub packages when available (easier maintenance), use git for unreleased or forked packages, use local only during development. Keep packages.yml in version control. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following state-based selection methods are available in dbt? (Select THREE)
Q_1: state:modified selects models whose code or configuration has changed
Q_2: state:failed selects models that failed in the previous run for re-execution
Q_3: state:new selects models that didn't exist in the previous project state
Q_4: state:stale selects models whose source data has become stale
Q_5: state:unchanged selects models that have not been modified since the previous run
Q_6: state:modified.body selects models whose SQL has changed but config hasn't
E_1: Correct. The state:modified selector compares your current project against a previous manifest (via --state) and selects models with changes to SQL, config, or dependencies. Combined with +, you can also select downstream models: state:modified+. This is the primary selector for efficient CI/CD—run only what changed. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods#the-modified-state
E_2: Incorrect. While you can rerun failed models, this uses dbt retry, not state:failed. State comparison methods (state:modified, state:new) compare project code/structure, not execution results. For reruns, use dbt retry or dbt run --select result:fail (result status, not state comparison).
E_3: Correct. The state:new selector identifies models that are present in your current project but weren't in the previous manifest. This catches newly added models in a PR. Combine with state:modified for comprehensive change detection: --select state:modified+ state:new+. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods#the-new-state
E_4: Incorrect. There is no state:stale selector. Source freshness is monitored with dbt source freshness, not state comparison. State selectors compare project code/structure, not data freshness or recency.
E_5: Incorrect. There's no state:unchanged selector. To select unchanged models, you'd exclude modified ones: --exclude state:modified+. State selectors focus on changes (modified, new), not on explicitly selecting unchanged resources.
E_6: Correct (though less commonly used). dbt provides granular state comparison: state:modified (any change), state:modified.body (SQL changed), state:modified.configs (config changed), state:modified.relation (materialization changed), state:modified.macros (macro dependencies changed). This enables fine-grained selection based on change type. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods
Explanation: OVERALL: State-based selection compares current code against previous runs for efficient builds. Key selectors: (1) state:modified—models with any changes (SQL, config, dependencies), (2) state:new—newly added models, (3) state:modified+—changed models and their children, (4) state:modified.body—SQL changes only, (5) state:modified.configs—config changes only. Workflow: save previous manifest, make changes, run with --select state:modified+ --state path/to/artifacts. This runs only changed models and downstream dependencies, dramatically speeding CI/CD in large projects. Requires --state flag pointing to previous manifest.json. Commonly used in Slim CI: test PRs by running only impacted models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods

Question: Your dbt run fails with 'Compilation Error: dbt found a cycle'. What does this error indicate?
Q_1: Two or more models reference each other creating a circular dependency
Q_2: A model is referencing itself using ref() in its own SQL
Q_3: An incremental model is running in a loop trying to merge data repeatedly
Q_4: dbt detected an infinite loop in a Jinja macro
Q_5: A model ran too many times and exceeded the retry limit
E_1: Correct. A cycle error means dbt detected circular dependencies in your DAG: Model A refs Model B, Model B refs Model C, Model C refs Model A (or A→B→A). This is impossible to execute—dbt can't determine build order. Fix by: examining the models in the error message, tracing their ref() calls, identifying the circular path, refactoring to break the cycle (often by creating an intermediate model or restructuring logic). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/faqs/Models/dbt-found-a-cycle
E_2: Incorrect. While self-reference would create a cycle, it's a specific case. The cycle error generally indicates circular dependencies between multiple models, not just self-reference. A model referencing itself would be caught, but cycles typically involve 2+ models.
E_3: Incorrect. Incremental models don't cause cycle errors. They execute once per run, merging new data into existing tables. The cycle error is about the DAG structure (which models depend on which), not runtime execution loops.
E_4: Incorrect. While macros can have infinite loops (causing compilation hangs or errors), the specific 'dbt found a cycle' error refers to circular dependencies in the model DAG, not Jinja execution logic. Macro loops would cause different errors.
E_5: Incorrect. Cycle errors occur during compilation when dbt builds the DAG, not during execution. They indicate a structural problem with model dependencies that prevents dbt from determining build order. Retry limits and execution count aren't relevant here.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) from ref() calls to determine execution order. A cycle error means the graph contains a cycle, violating the 'acyclic' requirement. Common causes: (1) A refs B, B refs A (simple cycle), (2) A refs B, B refs C, C refs A (longer cycle), (3) Refactoring that accidentally introduces circular dependencies. To debug: read the error message (dbt lists models in the cycle), visualize model dependencies in dbt docs or using 'dbt list --select model_name+', trace ref() calls to find the circular path, refactor by creating intermediate models or changing logic structure. The solution often involves extracting common logic into a new upstream model both original models can reference. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/faqs/Models/dbt-found-a-cycle

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are components of dbt's model governance framework? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Model contracts that enforce schema definitions and prevent breaking changes
Q_2: Access controls (private, protected, public) that determine which models can ref() each other
Q_3: Performance monitoring that tracks query execution times and warehouse costs
Q_4: Automated data quality tests that run on scheduled intervals
Q_5: Row-level security policies that restrict data access based on user roles
Q_6: Model versions that allow multiple versions of a model to coexist during transitions
E_1: Correct. Contracts allow you to define an explicit schema (columns, types, constraints) that a model must produce. If the model's output doesn't match, the build fails. This prevents breaking changes to downstream consumers and ensures API stability. Enable with contract: {enforced: true}. Contracts are essential for public models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-contracts
E_2: Correct. The access configuration controls model visibility for references: private (same group only), protected (same project), public (any project). This prevents unauthorized cross-team dependencies and establishes clear API boundaries. Combine with groups and contracts for robust governance. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_3: Incorrect. While important for operations, performance monitoring and cost tracking aren't part of dbt's governance framework. Governance focuses on model contracts, access control, ownership, and versioning. Performance monitoring is handled by warehouse tools and observability platforms.
E_4: Incorrect. While tests are important for data quality, they're not specifically part of the governance framework. Governance components focus on model interfaces, access, and ownership. Tests are a separate dbt feature, though they complement governance.
E_5: Incorrect. Row-level security is implemented at the warehouse level (Snowflake row access policies, BigQuery row-level security, etc.), not in dbt's governance framework. dbt governance controls model referencing and interfaces, not data-level security.
E_6: Correct. Model versioning enables multiple versions (v1, v2) to coexist, allowing gradual migration of downstream consumers. Each version can have its own contract and deprecation_date. Consumers explicitly choose which version to reference: ref('model', v=2). This enables non-breaking changes and controlled deprecation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-versions
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt's governance framework provides tools for managing models in large, multi-team projects: (1) Contracts: Enforce schema stability with explicit column definitions, preventing breaking changes, (2) Access: Control model visibility (private/protected/public) to manage dependencies, (3) Groups: Define ownership boundaries and organize models by team/domain, (4) Versions: Allow multiple model versions to coexist during transitions, enabling gradual migration, (5) Deprecation dates: Communicate sunset timelines for old versions. These features work together: create a public model with a contract, version it when making breaking changes, control access to prevent unauthorized dependencies, assign group ownership. This enables stable APIs, clear ownership, and safe evolution of data products. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/about-model-governance

Question: After adding a new package to packages.yml, dbt run fails with an error that a macro from the package is not found. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: The package was installed in the wrong directory location
Q_2: The package is not compatible with your dbt version
Q_3: The package needs to be enabled in dbt_project.yml before macros are available
Q_4: You need to restart the dbt Cloud IDE or reload your terminal for packages to be recognized
Q_5: You forgot to run dbt deps after adding the package to packages.yml
E_1: Incorrect. dbt deps automatically installs packages to the correct location (dbt_packages/ directory by default). You don't manually control where packages are installed. If the installation succeeded, packages are in the right place.
E_2: Incorrect. While version incompatibility can cause errors, they typically manifest as installation failures or explicit version mismatch errors from dbt deps, not 'macro not found' errors at runtime. If dbt deps succeeded, the package likely installed.
E_3: Incorrect. There is no enable/disable setting for packages in dbt_project.yml. Once a package is installed via dbt deps, its macros are automatically available for use. No additional configuration is needed to activate packages.
E_4: Incorrect. Restarting the IDE or terminal isn't necessary. dbt reads the dbt_packages/ directory on each invocation. The issue is that dbt deps hasn't been run yet or needs to be re-run to install the newly added package.
E_5: Correct. Adding a package to packages.yml only declares the dependency—it doesn't install it. You must run dbt deps to download and install packages into dbt_packages/. This is a very common mistake: editing packages.yml, then immediately trying to use the package without running dbt deps first. Always run dbt deps after modifying packages.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/deps
Explanation: OVERALL: The workflow for adding dbt packages is: (1) Add package specification to packages.yml with source (git URL, dbt Hub package name, or local path) and version/revision, (2) Run dbt deps to download and install the package into dbt_packages/ directory, (3) Use the package's macros in your models with {{ package_name.macro_name() }} syntax. If you skip step 2 (dbt deps), dbt won't find the package's macros because they aren't installed yet. Always run dbt deps after: adding new packages, changing package versions, or switching branches that modify packages.yml. In CI/CD pipelines, include dbt deps before dbt run/test. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/deps

Question: Your production dbt pipeline needs to send alerts when specific models fail. What is the BEST approach to implement this?
Q_1: Add try-catch blocks in your model SQL to catch errors and send alerts
Q_2: Use dbt Cloud's notification settings or your orchestration tool's alerting capabilities
Q_3: Create a post-hook that queries run_results.json and sends an email if errors are found
Q_4: Write a custom Python script that parses dbt logs and sends alerts based on error patterns
Q_5: Use the on-run-end hook to check for errors and trigger alerts
E_1: Incorrect. SQL doesn't have try-catch blocks (that's Python/Java), and you can't send alerts from within model SQL. Error handling and alerting should be implemented at the orchestration level, not within model logic.
E_2: Correct. Production alerting should be configured in your orchestration layer. dbt Cloud provides email/Slack notifications for job failures. External orchestrators (Airflow, Prefect, Dagster) have built-in alerting for task failures. Configure alerts at the job/task level, specify recipients, customize conditions (all failures vs specific models). This is the standard, maintainable approach. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/job-notifications
E_3: Incorrect. Post-hooks run after individual model builds, not after the entire job. They don't have access to final run_results.json or error summaries. More importantly, hooks shouldn't handle infrastructure concerns like alerting—use orchestration tools for this.
E_4: Incorrect. While possible, this is unnecessarily complex and hard to maintain. Modern orchestration tools and dbt Cloud provide built-in alerting that's more reliable, easier to configure, and better integrated with notification systems. Don't build custom solutions for standard infrastructure needs.
E_5: Incorrect. While on-run-end hooks execute after a run completes, they run within dbt's context and are meant for dbt operations (like uploading metadata), not external alerting. Hooks can't easily send emails or Slack messages. Use orchestration-level alerting instead.
Explanation: OVERALL: Production alerting is an orchestration concern, not a dbt concern. Best practices: (1) dbt Cloud: Configure job notifications in job settings—email, Slack, or webhooks on run failure/success. Filter by specific models if needed. (2) Airflow: Use on_failure_callback in your DbtTaskGroup or BashOperator. (3) Prefect/Dagster: Use their native notification systems. (4) Custom orchestration: Parse run_results.json after dbt completes and trigger alerts based on status. Separate concerns: dbt builds data, orchestration manages execution and notifications. This keeps dbt portable and orchestration flexible. For advanced scenarios, use webhooks to integrate with PagerDuty, Datadog, or custom alerting systems. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/job-notifications

Question: You need to create a custom test that verifies revenue values are always positive across multiple models. What is the BEST way to implement this reusable test?
Q_1: Create a generic test in the tests/generic directory that accepts a model and column as parameters
Q_2: Write a singular test for each model in the tests directory that checks that specific model's revenue
Q_3: Add a WHERE clause filter to each model: WHERE revenue > 0
Q_4: Use the built-in not_null test, since non-null implies positive for revenue
Q_5: Create a macro that logs a warning if revenue is negative, called from each model
E_1: Correct. Generic (schema) tests are reusable test templates that accept parameters like model and column_name. Create a .sql file in tests/generic/ (or macros/ for older dbt versions) that defines a test macro accepting {{ model }} and {{ column_name }}. Then apply it in schema.yml to any model/column. This enables reuse across all models with revenue fields. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#generic-data-tests
E_2: Incorrect. While you could write individual singular tests for each model, this violates the DRY principle and creates maintenance burden. If the logic changes, you'd need to update multiple files. Generic tests are specifically designed for reusable validation logic like 'column should be positive'.
E_3: Incorrect. Filtering out invalid data in models hides data quality issues rather than surfacing them. Tests should fail loudly when bad data appears, alerting you to investigate the root cause. Silently filtering creates blind spots and prevents you from fixing upstream data problems.
E_4: Incorrect. The not_null test only verifies a column doesn't contain NULL values. It doesn't validate that values are positive—revenue could be zero or negative and still pass not_null. You need a custom test for domain-specific validation like 'must be positive'.
E_5: Incorrect. Macros that log warnings don't enforce data quality or fail the build when violations occur. Tests should fail when conditions aren't met, stopping the pipeline and preventing bad data from propagating. Logging warnings is passive and easy to miss in production.
Explanation: OVERALL: Generic tests (formerly called schema tests) are dbt's mechanism for reusable data validation. To create one: (1) Create a .sql file in tests/generic/ (e.g., positive_values.sql), (2) Define a test macro that accepts {{ model }} and {{ column_name }} parameters, (3) Write SQL that returns rows failing the test (e.g., WHERE {{ column_name }} <= 0), (4) Apply in schema.yml to any model/column. Generic tests promote code reuse, reduce maintenance, ensure consistent validation logic, and are discoverable in documentation. For one-off validations specific to a single model, use singular tests. For reusable validation patterns, always create generic tests. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are valid use cases for ephemeral materialization? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Models with multiple downstream dependencies that save redundant computation
Q_2: Intermediate transformation logic that shouldn't create a database object
Q_3: Models queried directly by BI tools or end users
Q_4: Complex calculations that are expensive to compute
Q_5: Breaking down complex models into readable, modular pieces
Q_6: Simple transformations with a single downstream dependent
E_1: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are inlined as CTEs into each downstream model—if multiple models reference an ephemeral, the CTE is repeated in each, NOT shared. This can hurt performance. For models with many dependents, use view or table materialization.
E_2: Correct. Ephemeral models are perfect for intermediate steps you don't want cluttering your schema: cleaning steps, complex calculations that feed into one model, transformation logic that's purely organizational. They don't create tables/views, keeping your database clean. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral
E_3: Incorrect. Ephemeral models can't be queried directly—they don't exist as database objects. They're compiled as CTEs within other models. For BI tools, use views or tables.
E_4: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are CTEs, so expensive calculations get re-executed in each downstream model that references them. For complex/expensive logic referenced multiple times, use table materialization to compute once.
E_5: Correct. Ephemeral models improve code organization by extracting complex logic into named, testable pieces without creating database clutter. Example: break a complex 500-line model into 3 ephemeral intermediates plus a final table model. This improves readability and maintainability. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral
E_6: Correct. When a transformation is used by only one model, ephemeral avoids creating an unnecessary database object. The CTE is inlined into the single dependent model, keeping schemas clean without performance penalty. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral
Explanation: OVERALL: Ephemeral materialization creates CTEs (common table expressions) instead of database objects. Ephemeral models are inlined into dependent models at compile time. Use ephemeral for: (1) Intermediate logic that doesn't need to be queryable, (2) Code organization—breaking complex models into readable pieces, (3) Single-dependent transformations to avoid schema clutter. Avoid ephemeral for: (1) Multiple dependents (CTE is repeated, not shared), (2) Expensive calculations (computed in each dependent), (3) BI tool queries (no database object exists). Trade-offs: cleaner schemas (no intermediate tables) vs performance (logic re-executed per dependent). For reusable, expensive logic with multiple dependents, use view or table materialization. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following test configurations can help reduce test execution time in large dbt projects? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Disable all tests in development environments to speed up iteration
Q_2: Use the limit configuration to test only a sample of rows instead of the full dataset
Q_3: Use the where configuration to test only recent data or specific subsets
Q_4: Convert all tests to severity: warn so they don't slow down the build
Q_5: Use the store_failures configuration to save test results for faster debugging
Q_6: Remove unique and relationships tests as they're the slowest to execute
E_1: Incorrect. Disabling all tests in development eliminates valuable feedback during development. You want fast feedback on data quality issues while developing. Instead, use selective test execution, store_failures for faster reruns, or limit/where clauses to test subsets of data.
E_2: Correct. The limit configuration restricts how many failing rows a test returns, reducing query time. Example: tests: - unique: {config: {limit: 100}}. The test still validates all data but stops after finding the configured number of failures. This speeds up tests on large tables while still catching issues. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/limit
E_3: Correct. The where clause filters which rows are tested. Example: tests: - not_null: {config: {where: "created_at > current_date - 7"}}. This tests only recent data, dramatically reducing query time on large historical tables. Useful when recent data quality is most critical or for development environments. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/where
E_4: Incorrect. Test severity (warn vs error) affects build behavior when tests fail, not test execution speed. Tests with severity: warn still execute fully—they just don't stop the build on failure. Severity doesn't reduce test execution time.
E_5: Correct. store_failures saves failing rows to tables for inspection instead of just logging them. While this adds a small write operation, it enables faster debugging by preserving failure details. More importantly, you can configure store_failures_as='view' for ephemeral storage, reducing overhead. This helps iterate faster when fixing test failures. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_6: Incorrect. While unique and relationships tests can be expensive on large tables, removing them eliminates critical data quality checks. Instead, optimize them using limit, where clauses, or strategic application to high-value columns only. Don't sacrifice data quality for speed.
Explanation: OVERALL: Test performance optimization strategies: (1) limit: Caps failing rows returned, reducing result set size, (2) where: Filters test scope to recent/relevant data (especially useful in dev), (3) store_failures: Preserves failure details for faster debugging iterations, (4) Selective execution: Use --select to run subset of tests during development, (5) Strategic test application: Apply expensive tests (unique, relationships) only to critical columns, (6) Environment-specific configs: Run full tests in prod, limited tests in dev. Balance data quality with execution speed—faster tests enable quicker feedback loops and more frequent validation. For very large tables, consider testing strategies like statistical sampling or incremental validation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/test-configs

Question: Your dbt model performs complex aggregations on millions of rows and is queried frequently by dashboards. The underlying source data changes daily. Which materialization strategy is MOST appropriate?
Q_1: Use view materialization to ensure data is always fresh and avoid storing duplicates
Q_2: Use table materialization to pre-compute results and optimize dashboard query performance
Q_3: Use ephemeral materialization to avoid creating database objects and save storage costs
Q_4: Use incremental materialization to process only new rows since the last run
Q_5: Use a hybrid approach with both view and table materializations in different environments
E_1: Incorrect. Views execute the query every time they're queried. For complex aggregations on millions of rows queried frequently, this would create severe performance problems. Views are best for simple transformations or infrequently-queried models, not compute-intensive aggregations.
E_2: Correct. Table materialization pre-computes and stores results, making queries fast. Since dashboards query frequently and the source data changes only daily (not continuously), rebuilding the table once per day via scheduled runs provides optimal performance. The trade-off of slightly stale data (max 24 hours) is acceptable for daily-changing sources. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#table
E_3: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are CTEs inlined into downstream models—they don't create queryable objects. Dashboards cannot query ephemeral models. Ephemeral is for intermediate transformations, not end-user-facing analytics.
E_4: Incorrect for this scenario.
