While incremental can be efficient, the question states the model performs 'complex aggregations'—aggregations typically require full dataset access to compute correctly (think SUM, AVG across all data). Incremental is best for append-only event data, not aggregated metrics that need full refreshes.
E_5: Incorrect. While you can configure different materializations per environment, this doesn't address the core requirement. The question asks for the MOST appropriate strategy given the specific constraints (complex aggregations, frequent queries, daily source changes). Table materialization is clearly optimal here.
Explanation: OVERALL: Materialization strategy depends on query complexity, query frequency, and data freshness requirements. Tables pre-compute results for fast queries (best for complex transformations queried often). Views provide real-time data but query the underlying logic each time (best for simple transformations or infrequent queries). Incremental processes only new data (best for large append-only datasets). Ephemeral models are CTEs for intermediate logic (not queryable endpoints). For complex aggregations queried frequently with daily-changing sources, table materialization offers the best performance-freshness trade-off. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations

Question: When should you use the store_failures_as: view configuration instead of store_failures_as: table?
Q_1: When failures need to be stored permanently for compliance auditing
Q_2: When you need faster test execution by caching failure results
Q_3: When test failures must be encrypted at rest
Q_4: When testing in development and failures only need temporary storage
Q_5: When you want to inspect failures without storing data and save warehouse storage costs
E_1: Incorrect. Views don't store data—they query the underlying tables each time. For permanent storage and audit trails, use store_failures_as: table which persists failure data.
E_2: Incorrect. Views don't cache results—they execute the query each time they're queried. Tables would provide faster access to historical failures. Views are for temporary inspection, not performance.
E_3: Incorrect. Encryption depends on warehouse settings, not whether failures are tables or views. Both respect warehouse-level encryption. The table vs view choice is about persistence and storage, not security.
E_4: Incorrect. Even in development, using views would still create view objects in the database. If you want truly temporary storage without database objects, you might skip store_failures entirely in dev or use ephemeral (though ephemeral isn't supported for test failures).
E_5: Correct. Views don't store data—they're queries that execute when accessed. Use store_failures_as: view when: (1) you want to inspect failures during development without permanent storage, (2) failure data changes frequently and you want current data each query, (3) saving storage costs is important. Views are lightweight alternatives to tables for temporary failure inspection. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures_as
Explanation: OVERALL: store_failures_as controls how test failures are materialized: (1) table (default): Creates permanent tables with failure data. Use for: persistent failure history, compliance auditing, long-term trend analysis, production environments. (2) view: Creates views that query failures dynamically. Use for: development environments, temporary inspection, reducing storage costs, when failure data changes frequently. (3) ephemeral: Not supported for test failures (tests need queryable results). Choose based on needs: permanent audit trail → table, temporary debugging → view. In practice, many projects use tables in production (audit trail) and views in development (cost savings). Combine with retention policies to manage table storage over time. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures_as

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are recommended practices for scheduling and orchestrating dbt runs in production? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Run dbt run without any selectors to ensure all models are always rebuilt on every schedule
Q_2: Use dbt Cloud's scheduler or an orchestration tool like Airflow to run dbt on a regular schedule
Q_3: Configure separate jobs for different cadences (e.g., critical models hourly, others daily)
Q_4: Run dbt test after dbt run to validate data quality before downstream consumers use it
Q_5: Schedule all dbt jobs to run simultaneously to minimize total pipeline duration
Q_6: Use dbt run --full-refresh on every scheduled run to ensure complete data accuracy
E_1: Incorrect. Running all models on every schedule is inefficient and wasteful. Use model selection (--select) to run only models that need updates, or leverage incremental models and state-based selection (--select state:modified+) to run only changed models and their downstream dependencies. Full rebuilds should be reserved for specific situations like weekly full-refreshes.
E_2: Correct. Production dbt pipelines should run on automated schedules using orchestration tools. dbt Cloud provides built-in scheduling, or you can use external orchestrators like Airflow, Prefect, or Dagster. Scheduled runs ensure data freshness and eliminate manual intervention. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deployments
E_3: Correct. Different models have different freshness requirements. Create multiple jobs/schedules: run critical dashboard-backing models frequently (hourly), run standard reports daily, run historical aggregations weekly. Use model selection in each job to target appropriate models. This optimizes compute costs while meeting SLAs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deploy-jobs
E_4: Correct. Always run tests after building models in production pipelines. Configure your jobs to run 'dbt build' (which runs and tests in one command) or sequence 'dbt run && dbt test'. If tests fail, the pipeline should fail, preventing bad data from reaching consumers. Configure test severity appropriately (error vs warn). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_5: Incorrect. Running all jobs simultaneously can overwhelm your warehouse, cause resource contention, and lead to failures. Stagger jobs appropriately, respect warehouse capacity, and use dbt Cloud's concurrency controls or your orchestrator's resource management. Some jobs may need to run sequentially if they have dependencies.
E_6: Incorrect. Full refresh truncates and rebuilds tables completely—it's expensive and time-consuming. Reserve --full-refresh for specific situations: initial loads, schema changes, or periodic full rebuilds (e.g., weekly). Incremental models and regular builds handle most production schedules efficiently without full refreshes.
Explanation: OVERALL: Production dbt orchestration follows these best practices: (1) Use automated scheduling tools (dbt Cloud, Airflow, etc.) for reliable, repeatable runs, (2) Create multiple jobs with appropriate cadences based on business requirements and data freshness needs, (3) Always test after building to catch data quality issues before they reach consumers, (4) Use model selection and state comparison to run only necessary models, (5) Respect warehouse resources by staggering jobs and managing concurrency, (6) Reserve full refreshes for specific scenarios, not regular schedules. The 'dbt build' command is particularly useful as it runs, tests, and snapshots in the correct order. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deployments

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following statements about dbt test severity configurations are TRUE? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Setting severity: warn allows tests to fail without stopping the dbt run
Q_2: Test severity can only be configured at the project level in dbt_project.yml
Q_3: Tests with severity: warn don't execute if previous tests have failed
Q_4: You can set different severities for different tests on the same column
Q_5: Test severity can be set conditionally using Jinja based on the target environment
Q_6: Tests with severity: warn don't appear in the test results or documentation
E_1: Correct. Test severity controls build behavior when tests fail. severity: warn causes test failures to generate warnings but not stop the build—dbt continues executing and exits with code 0. severity: error (default) stops the build on test failure with a non-zero exit code. Use warn for tests you want to monitor but not enforce strictly. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_2: Incorrect. Test severity can be configured at multiple levels: project-wide in dbt_project.yml, per-model or per-test in schema.yml, or inline in the model using config blocks. More specific configurations override general ones, following dbt's configuration hierarchy.
E_3: Incorrect. All tests run regardless of severity configuration or other test results. Severity only affects what happens after a test fails—it doesn't control test execution. Both error and warn tests execute; they just behave differently when failures occur.
E_4: Correct. Each test can have its own severity configuration. For example, on a customer_id column, you might set not_null as severity: error (critical requirement) and unique as severity: warn (important but not blocking). Configure in schema.yml under each test's configuration. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_5: Correct. You can use Jinja to set severity dynamically based on environment: severity: "{{ 'error' if target.name == 'prod' else 'warn' }}". This enables strict enforcement in production while allowing flexibility in development. Common pattern: enforce data quality in prod, warn in dev for faster iteration. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_6: Incorrect. Tests with severity: warn execute, produce results, and appear in test output and documentation exactly like error-severity tests. The only difference is build behavior: warn tests don't stop the build when they fail. They're fully visible and tracked.
Explanation: OVERALL: Test severity provides flexible data quality enforcement. severity: error (default) fails the build when tests fail—use for critical data quality requirements that must pass. severity: warn logs failures but continues the build—use for important-but-not-blocking validations, gradual rollout of new tests, or development environments. Configure severity in schema.yml, dbt_project.yml, or using config() blocks. Common patterns: set stricter severity in production vs development using Jinja conditionals, apply different severities to different tests on the same resource based on business criticality, use warn during test development before promoting to error. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity

Question: What happens when you configure a model with {{ config(schema='reporting') }}?
Q_1: The model will fail because schema cannot be configured at the model level
Q_2: The model will be created in a schema named exactly 'reporting' in your warehouse
Q_3: The model will reference source tables from the 'reporting' schema
Q_4: The model documentation will be grouped under a 'reporting' section
Q_5: The model will be created in a schema with 'reporting' appended to your target schema name
E_1: Incorrect. Schema can be configured at the model level using config(). This is a valid and common configuration to organize models into different database schemas based on their purpose or audience.
E_2: Incorrect. By default, dbt uses the generate_schema_name macro which concatenates target schema + custom schema. So if your target schema is 'analytics_dev', the model creates in 'analytics_dev_reporting', not just 'reporting'. You can customize this behavior by overriding the generate_schema_name macro.
E_3: Incorrect. The schema config controls where dbt creates the model's table/view, not where it reads source data from. Source table locations are defined in sources.yml or determined by the tables you reference in your SQL.
E_4: Incorrect. Schema configuration controls database schema location, not documentation organization. Documentation grouping uses tags, groups, or folder structure, not schema config. The schema config is purely about where the table exists in your warehouse.
E_5: Correct. By default, dbt's generate_schema_name macro creates schemas by combining your target schema (from profiles.yml) with the custom schema from config(). For example, if target schema is 'dbt_prod', config(schema='reporting') creates the model in 'dbt_prod_reporting'. In dev environments (target.name != 'prod'), this prefix behavior ensures dev schemas don't collide with production. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/custom-schemas
Explanation: OVERALL: Custom schemas organize models into different database schemas. When you set config(schema='reporting'), dbt uses the generate_schema_name macro to determine the final schema name. Default behavior: _ (e.g., 'analytics_dev_reporting'). You can override generate_schema_name in macros/ to customize this logic—common pattern: use custom schema name only in prod (e.g., just 'reporting'), but prefix in dev to avoid conflicts. Benefits: organize models by purpose (staging, reporting, ml), control permissions (grant different access to different schemas), separate data domains. Set at model level with config() or for groups of models in dbt_project.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/custom-schemas

Question: What is the purpose of model contracts in dbt?
Q_1: To enforce rate limits on how many times a model can be queried per day
Q_2: To define legal agreements about who owns the data in each model
Q_3: To specify which users have permission to read or write to the model's table
Q_4: To set up billing and cost tracking for models that consume significant warehouse resources
Q_5: To enforce that a model's schema matches a predefined specification, preventing breaking changes
E_1: Incorrect. dbt doesn't control query frequency or impose rate limits. Contracts are about schema enforcement and API stability, not query throttling. Rate limiting would be handled by your data warehouse or BI tool.
E_2: Incorrect. While 'contract' suggests legal agreements, dbt contracts are technical schema enforcement mechanisms. They don't define data ownership, licensing, or legal terms—they enforce column names, types, and constraints at build time.
E_3: Incorrect. User permissions and access control are managed through your warehouse's security system (GRANT statements, roles, etc.), not through dbt contracts. Contracts enforce schema definitions, not user permissions.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt doesn't handle billing or cost tracking. Contracts enforce that models adhere to specified schemas (column names, data types, constraints). Cost management is handled by warehouse resource monitors and billing systems.
E_5: Correct. Model contracts allow you to define an explicit schema (column names, data types, constraints) that a model must produce. If the model's actual output doesn't match the contract, the build fails. This prevents breaking changes to downstream models and ensures API stability, especially important for public models that other teams depend on. Combine with access and versions for robust governance. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-contracts
Explanation: OVERALL: Model contracts are part of dbt's governance features for ensuring schema stability. When you define a contract on a model, you explicitly specify the expected columns, data types, and constraints (not null, unique, etc.). dbt validates the model's output against this contract at build time—if the schema doesn't match, the build fails. This is crucial for: (1) public models that serve as APIs for other teams, preventing breaking changes, (2) models with access: public or protected, ensuring interface stability, (3) version management, where each version has a guaranteed schema, (4) collaboration in large teams where model consumers need reliability. Enable with contract: {enforced: true} in model config. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-contracts

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are benefits of using the ref() function instead of hardcoded table names in dbt models? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: dbt can automatically build models in the correct order based on dependencies
Q_2: Models will run faster because ref() caches query results
Q_3: You can reference models that haven't been built yet without errors
Q_4: Model references automatically adapt to different schemas across dev and prod environments
Q_5: You can use model selection syntax to run a model and its dependencies with --select
Q_6: The dbt documentation automatically shows lineage and dependencies between models
E_1: Correct. When you use ref(), dbt parses these references to build a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of dependencies. This allows dbt to determine the correct execution order—upstream models build before downstream models. Without ref(), dbt can't infer dependencies and may try to build models in the wrong order, causing failures. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_2: Incorrect. The ref() function doesn't cache results or affect query performance. It compiles to the appropriate table name at build time. Performance depends on your materialization strategy (view, table, incremental) and SQL logic, not on using ref() vs hardcoded names.
E_3: Incorrect. If you reference a model that doesn't exist or hasn't been built, you'll get a runtime error when the query executes against the warehouse. The ref() function doesn't create forward references or allow queries against non-existent tables—it just resolves to the correct table name.
E_4: Correct. The ref() function resolves to the appropriate schema based on your target configuration in profiles.yml. In dev, it might resolve to analytics_dev.customers; in prod, to analytics_prod.customers. This environment-awareness is automatic—you don't need different code for different environments. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_5: Correct. Model selection (--select, --exclude) relies on the DAG built from ref() references. You can run commands like 'dbt run --select my_model+' to run my_model and all its upstream dependencies, or 'dbt run --select +my_model' for downstream dependents. This is only possible because ref() establishes the dependency graph. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/syntax
E_6: Correct. dbt docs use the DAG constructed from ref() calls to visualize lineage. The documentation's interactive DAG graph shows how models connect, which upstream sources and models feed into each model, and which downstream models depend on it. This documentation is automatically generated from ref() relationships. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation
Explanation: OVERALL: The ref() function is fundamental to dbt's architecture, enabling dependency management, environment portability, and developer experience features. By using ref() consistently: (1) dbt builds the dependency DAG and executes models in the correct order, (2) model references automatically resolve to appropriate schemas in different environments (dev/prod), (3) you can use powerful model selection to run subsets of your DAG, (4) documentation automatically shows model lineage and relationships, (5) dbt can detect circular dependencies and invalid references at compile time. Never hardcode table names when referencing dbt models—always use ref(). Reserve hardcoded names for external tables outside dbt's control (or better yet, define them as sources). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref

Question: A model compiles successfully with dbt compile but fails when running dbt run with a SQL syntax error. What is the MOST likely explanation?
Q_1: The dbt compile command doesn't validate SQL syntax, only Jinja templating
Q_2: Different dbt versions are being used for compile vs run commands
Q_3: The SQL uses warehouse-specific syntax that's valid in compilation but unsupported by your warehouse
Q_4: The compile command uses a different profiles.yml target than the run command
Q_5: The model references another model that hasn't been built yet
E_1: Incorrect. While dbt compile does focus on resolving Jinja to SQL, it doesn't execute SQL against the warehouse. Both compile and run generate the same SQL. The question implies the SQL itself has warehouse-specific issues that only appear during execution.
E_2: Incorrect. In a normal workflow, you'd use the same dbt version for both commands. Even if versions differed, both commands compile models identically—compilation logic doesn't differ significantly between minor versions for basic syntax.
E_3: Correct. dbt compile generates SQL without executing it. The SQL might use syntax/functions specific to a different warehouse than your target. For example: BigQuery's STRING_AGG in a Snowflake project, Snowflake's ARRAY_AGG in BigQuery, warehouse-specific date functions. The SQL compiles fine (it's valid SQL text) but fails when executed against your specific warehouse. Fix by using dbt adapters or cross-database macros. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile
E_4: Incorrect. Both commands use the same target from profiles.yml unless you specify --target differently. Even if targets differed, this would cause connection issues or wrong database/schema, not SQL syntax errors during execution.
E_5: Incorrect. Missing referenced tables would cause a 'table does not exist' error, not a SQL syntax error. Syntax errors indicate invalid SQL for your warehouse, not missing dependencies.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt compile resolves Jinja and generates SQL but doesn't execute it against the warehouse. SQL syntax errors only surface during execution (dbt run) when the warehouse parser encounters unsupported syntax. Common causes: (1) Warehouse-specific functions (STRING_AGG, LISTAGG, etc. vary by warehouse), (2) Syntax differences (CAST vs ::, LIMIT vs TOP, etc.), (3) Feature support differences (some warehouses lack certain window functions or CTEs). Solutions: (1) Use dbt's cross-database macros from dbt-utils, (2) Check adapter documentation for syntax differences, (3) Write conditional SQL using target.type in Jinja, (4) Test against your actual warehouse type. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are best practices for implementing dbt tests in production? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Run all tests with severity: warn to avoid blocking deployments
Q_2: Run tests as part of the dbt build command to test models in dependency order
Q_3: Monitor test results over time to identify patterns and improve data quality
Q_4: Configure critical tests with severity: error and less critical tests with severity: warn
Q_5: Store test failures in tables for debugging and audit trails using store_failures: true
Q_6: Disable all tests in production to maximize performance
E_1: Incorrect. Setting all tests to warn eliminates data quality gates. Production should have critical tests as severity: error to prevent bad data from reaching consumers. Use warn selectively for non-critical validations, error for critical ones.
E_2: Correct. dbt build runs models and tests in DAG order, ensuring: (1) models build before their tests run, (2) downstream models don't build if upstream tests fail (with severity: error), (3) efficient single-command workflow. Use 'dbt build' in production instead of separate 'dbt run' and 'dbt test' for integrated quality gates. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_3: Incorrect. While monitoring helps, the question asks about implementation best practices, not monitoring. Monitoring is valuable but separate from how you structure and run tests.
E_4: Correct. Differentiate test criticality: critical data quality requirements (uniqueness of IDs, required fields) → severity: error (block on failure), important but not blocking validations → severity: warn (log but continue). This balances data quality enforcement with deployment velocity. Configure per-test in schema.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_5: Correct. In production, store_failures preserves failure details for: (1) root cause analysis after incidents, (2) compliance and audit requirements, (3) trend analysis of data quality issues. Configure store_failures: true and store_failures_as: table in dbt_project.yml for production. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_6: Incorrect. Tests are critical data quality gates in production. Disabling them risks bad data reaching consumers, dashboards, and decision-makers. Instead, optimize test performance (limit, where clauses) while maintaining quality checks.
Explanation: OVERALL: Production testing best practices: (1) Use 'dbt build' for integrated model building and testing in DAG order, (2) Set appropriate severity—critical tests as error (block bad data), others as warn (monitor without blocking), (3) Store failures for audit and debugging—use store_failures: true with store_failures_as: table, (4) Optimize performance—use where clauses, limit for large tables, strategic test application, (5) Monitor and alert—track test failures, set up notifications for critical test failures, (6) Document expectations—clear descriptions in schema.yml explaining what each test validates. Balance data quality enforcement with deployment velocity. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build

Question: dbt run fails with 'Database Error: permission denied for schema'. What should you check FIRST?
Q_1: Verify that the dbt_project.yml file has the correct schema configurations
Q_2: Check if the model's SQL syntax is correct and compatible with your warehouse
Q_3: Run dbt clean to clear cached artifacts that might contain old schema references
Q_4: Verify the database user in profiles.yml has CREATE privileges on the target schema
Q_5: Change the materialization strategy to ephemeral to avoid schema creation
E_1: Incorrect. dbt_project.yml schema configs control where dbt tries to create objects, not whether it has permission. The error indicates a database permission issue, not a configuration issue. You need to check warehouse-level permissions.
E_2: Incorrect. SQL syntax errors produce different error messages ('syntax error near...', not 'permission denied'). Permission errors are about database access rights, not SQL correctness.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt clean removes compiled files and targets/, but this doesn't affect database permissions. Permission denied errors come from the warehouse when dbt tries to CREATE/DROP objects—cleaning local files won't fix this.
E_4: Correct. 'Permission denied for schema' means the database user dbt is using (defined in profiles.yml) lacks necessary permissions. dbt needs CREATE, DROP, and SELECT privileges on schemas it manages. Check: (1) warehouse user permissions (GRANT CREATE ON SCHEMA), (2) role assignments, (3) profiles.yml credentials are correct. Contact your DBA if you don't have admin access. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/core/connect-data-platform/connection-profiles
E_5: Incorrect. While ephemeral models don't create database objects, this is a workaround that doesn't solve the underlying problem. You need proper database permissions to run dbt effectively. Changing all models to ephemeral isn't practical or appropriate.
Explanation: OVERALL: Database permission errors indicate your warehouse user lacks required privileges. dbt needs: (1) CREATE privileges to build models (tables/views), (2) DROP privileges to rebuild or replace models, (3) SELECT privileges to read from sources and models, (4) USAGE on schemas and databases. Setup: work with your DBA to grant appropriate permissions, use role-based access control (RBAC) for organized permission management, consider separate service accounts for dev vs prod with different permission levels. In development, you might need full access to your dev schema. In production, use a dedicated dbt service account with minimal necessary privileges. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/core/connect-data-platform/connection-profiles

Question: [SELECT 2] A model builds successfully but produces incorrect results. Which debugging approaches are MOST effective? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Run dbt clean to clear cached artifacts that might contain stale logic
Q_2: Examine the compiled SQL in target/compiled/ to verify the logic is correct
Q_3: Query the model's output table and compare results against expected values
Q_4: Increase the model's materialization to table instead of view
Q_5: Run dbt run --full-refresh to rebuild the model from scratch
Q_6: Disable tests to see if they're interfering with the model build
E_1: Incorrect. dbt clean removes compiled files, but incorrect results stem from SQL logic errors, not cached artifacts. The compiled SQL is regenerated fresh each run. Cleaning doesn't fix logic bugs.
E_2: Correct. Compiled SQL shows exactly what executes in the warehouse after Jinja resolution. Check target/compiled/<project>/<path>/<model>.sql to verify: ref() resolved correctly, macros expanded properly, conditionals evaluated as expected, filters applied correctly. Copy and run the compiled SQL directly in your warehouse to debug. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile
E_3: Correct. Direct inspection of output data helps identify logic errors. Compare: row counts against sources, aggregations against manual calculations, specific records against business rules, edge cases for unexpected behavior. Use dbt tests to codify these checks for ongoing validation. Combining compiled SQL review (what should run) with output inspection (what actually happened) efficiently isolates bugs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests
E_4: Incorrect. Materialization affects where/how results are stored, not the correctness of results. A view and table execute the same SQL and produce the same results. Changing materialization doesn't fix logic errors.
E_5: Incorrect for general logic errors. Full refresh helps with incremental model state issues, not SQL logic bugs. If the SQL logic is wrong, full refresh runs the same wrong logic on all data. Fix the SQL first.
E_6: Incorrect. Tests run after models build—they don't affect model logic or results. If a model produces incorrect results, tests might catch it (test failure), but disabling tests doesn't fix the underlying logic error.
Explanation: OVERALL: Debugging incorrect model results requires understanding what SQL executed and what data resulted: (1) Review compiled SQL (target/compiled/) to verify logic correctness—check Jinja resolution, macro expansion, conditional logic. (2) Inspect output data in the warehouse—compare against expectations, check edge cases, validate aggregations. (3) Add logging/debugging: intermediate CTEs, breakpoints in complex queries, temporary columns with diagnostic info. (4) Simplify: reduce data scope (LIMIT, WHERE), remove complexity incrementally, isolate the problematic logic. (5) Compare: dbt run in dev vs prod, before/after code changes, similar working models. Effective debugging combines code review (compiled SQL) and data validation (query results). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile

Question: You receive an error 'Compilation Error: Model X depends on a node named Y which was not found'. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: The model Y exists but is disabled with enabled: false configuration
Q_2: Model Y is in a different schema and cannot be referenced
Q_3: Model Y hasn't been run yet and doesn't exist in the database
Q_4: The ref() function has a typo or references a model name that doesn't match any .sql filename
Q_5: Model Y is defined in a package and requires package_name.model_name syntax
E_1: Incorrect. While this could cause the error, it's less likely than a typo. If Y is disabled, dbt correctly reports it's not found. However, the most common cause is simpler: the ref() name doesn't match the actual model filename.
E_2: Incorrect. Models can be referenced across schemas using ref()—that's one of ref()'s key features. Schema location doesn't affect ref() ability, only database permissions might.
E_3: Incorrect. The error is a compilation error, not a runtime error. Compilation happens before execution—dbt is looking for the model definition in your project, not the table in the database. If the table didn't exist, you'd get a runtime 'relation does not exist' error.
E_4: Correct. This is the most common cause: {{ ref('customer') }} but the file is named customers.sql, or {{ ref('stg_orders') }} but it's actually staging_orders.sql. dbt looks for model definitions by matching ref() names to filenames. Check: (1) model filename spelling, (2) ref() spelling, (3) the model exists in your models/ directory. The model name is the filename without .sql. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_5: Incorrect. You can ref() models from packages using just the model name: {{ ref('model_name') }}. The package_name.model_name syntax is for macros, not models. If there's a name conflict, you might need to qualify it, but that's rare.
Explanation: OVERALL: The 'node not found' error means dbt can't find a model definition matching your ref() call during compilation. Common causes: (1) Typo in ref() name or filename, (2) Model file doesn't exist in models/ directory, (3) Model is in a subdirectory you haven't searched, (4) Case sensitivity issues (ref('Model') vs model.sql), (5) Model is disabled with enabled: false. Debug by: checking ref() spelling against actual filenames (case-sensitive), verifying the .sql file exists, checking for enabled: false configs, running dbt list to see all available models. The model name in ref() must exactly match the filename (without .sql extension). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref

Question: Your team wants to implement a deployment strategy where models build on different schedules based on their update frequency. Which approach is BEST?
Q_1: Use a single dbt job with complex --select logic to choose models based on time
Q_2: Create separate dbt packages for different update frequencies
Q_3: Create multiple dbt Cloud jobs or orchestration tasks, each with appropriate schedules and model selection
Q_4: Use the enabled configuration with time-based Jinja to control which models run
Q_5: Run all models on the highest frequency schedule to ensure data is always fresh
E_1: Incorrect. While possible, this creates a complex, hard-to-maintain job. Multiple jobs with clear scopes are more maintainable and flexible than one job with complex selection logic.
E_2: Incorrect. Packages are for code reuse and dependencies, not scheduling. Organizing by packages adds unnecessary complexity and doesn't provide scheduling benefits. Use jobs/deployment configuration instead.
E_3: Correct. This is the recommended pattern: (1) Hourly job: critical real-time models (--select tag:hourly), (2) Daily job: standard reports (--select tag:daily), (3) Weekly job: heavy aggregations (--select tag:weekly). Each job has its own schedule, selection criteria, and resource configuration. This provides clear separation, easier monitoring, and independent scaling. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deploy-jobs
E_4: Incorrect. enabled controls whether models exist in the DAG, not when they run. Using time-based Jinja in enabled would require running dbt constantly to check conditions. Scheduling should be handled by orchestration tools, not model configuration.
E_5: Incorrect. This wastes compute resources and warehouse costs. Models that don't need hourly updates shouldn't run hourly. Right-size schedules to business needs: run frequently only what's time-sensitive, run less frequently for stable historical data.
Explanation: OVERALL: Production deployment best practices: (1) Multiple jobs with clear purposes—critical hourly, standard daily, heavy weekly, (2) Use tags for selection—tag models appropriately, select by tag in jobs, (3) Separate concerns—different SLAs, different schedules, different monitoring, (4) Resource optimization—allocate warehouse resources per job needs, (5) Clear naming—'Hourly Critical Models', 'Daily Standard Reports', (6) Independent monitoring—each job has its own alerting. Implementation: in schema.yml/model config, add tags like tags: ['hourly', 'critical'], in dbt Cloud or orchestrator, create jobs with --select tag:hourly. This enables precise control, clear ownership, and efficient resource usage. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deploy-jobs

Question: You need to create a model that should only exist in your development environment for testing, but never be deployed to production. How should you configure this?
Q_1: Use the enabled configuration with a Jinja conditional: {{ config(enabled=target.name == 'dev') }}
Q_2: Create the model in a separate dev/ directory and exclude that directory in production runs
Q_3: Set materialized: ephemeral so the model doesn't create a database object
Q_4: Use dbt Cloud's environment configuration to exclude the model from production environments
Q_5: Add the model name to a .dbtignore file in production deployments
E_1: Correct. The enabled config controls whether a model is built or skipped. Using Jinja to set enabled based on target.name allows environment-specific behavior. With {{ config(enabled=target.name == 'dev') }}, the model builds only in dev environments and is skipped in prod. This is perfect for experimental models, debug helpers, or dev-only analysis. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/enabled
E_2: Incorrect. While you could use directory organization and --exclude flags, this is fragile and error-prone—someone might forget the exclusion in production jobs. The enabled config is the explicit, maintainable approach for environment-specific models that's built into dbt's configuration system.
E_3: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are CTEs inlined into downstream models—they're not environment-specific. An ephemeral model would still run in production (just not create a separate table), which isn't the goal. You need to prevent the model from building at all in prod.
E_4: Incorrect. While dbt Cloud environments can target different schemas/databases, there's no native dbt Cloud feature to exclude specific models per environment (beyond what you configure in your dbt code). The enabled config in your model code is the proper approach.
E_5: Incorrect. .dbtignore excludes files from the entire dbt project, affecting all environments. It's used for permanently excluding files (like tests or examples), not for environment-specific exclusions. The enabled config provides dynamic, environment-specific control.
Explanation: OVERALL: The enabled configuration controls whether dbt builds a resource (model, test, snapshot). It accepts boolean values or Jinja expressions that evaluate to boolean. Common patterns: {{ config(enabled=target.name == 'dev') }} for dev-only models, {{ config(enabled=var('include_experimental', false)) }} for opt-in experimental models, {{ config(enabled=target.name in ['dev', 'staging']) }} for multiple environments. This is more maintainable than manual exclusions in run commands and makes intent explicit in the model's configuration. Disabled models don't appear in the DAG or compiled artifacts. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/enabled

Question: What dbt command should you use to check if source data has been updated recently?
Q_1: dbt test --source-freshness to run freshness checks on all sources
Q_2: dbt run --check-freshness to validate sources before building models
Q_3: dbt compile --sources to analyze source table update timestamps
Q_4: dbt source freshness to check when source tables were last updated
Q_5: dbt deps --validate-sources to verify source availability and freshness
E_1: Incorrect. Source freshness is checked with a dedicated command, not dbt test. While dbt test runs data quality tests on models and sources, freshness checks require querying source table metadata to determine last update time.
E_2: Incorrect. There is no --check-freshness flag for dbt run. Source freshness is checked using a separate dbt source freshness command, not as part of the model build process.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt compile generates SQL from Jinja but doesn't query the warehouse or check data freshness. Freshness checks require actually querying your source tables' metadata to find last update times.
E_4: Correct. The dbt source freshness command queries your warehouse to determine when source tables were last updated, comparing against the loaded_at_field you specify in sources.yml. It checks against warn_after and error_after thresholds to alert on stale data. This ensures your source data is fresh before transformation, catching upstream pipeline issues early. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/source#dbt-source-freshness
E_5: Incorrect. dbt deps installs package dependencies from packages.yml. It doesn't interact with your warehouse or check source data freshness. Source freshness is checked with dbt source freshness.
Explanation: OVERALL: Source freshness monitoring ensures upstream data pipelines are working. Configure in sources.yml: (1) Add loaded_at_field (timestamp column indicating last load), (2) Set freshness thresholds (warn_after: {count: 12, period: hour}, error_after: {count: 24, period: hour}). Run dbt source freshness to query source tables and check recency. dbt compares current time against MAX(loaded_at_field) for each source. Results show: pass (fresh), warn (stale but within error threshold), error (critically stale). Use in CI/CD before dbt run to catch upstream failures early. In dbt Cloud, freshness checks can run on schedules. This is crucial for detecting ETL failures, pipeline delays, or data ingestion problems before they affect your analytics. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources#source-freshness

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are TRUE about the store_failures configuration for tests? (Select THREE)
Q_1: It stores failing rows in a table for analysis and debugging
Q_2: It can be configured globally in dbt_project.yml or per-test in schema.yml
Q_3: Stored failures can be materialized as tables or views depending on configuration
Q_4: It automatically fixes data quality issues by removing failing rows
Q_5: Enabling it improves test execution performance by caching results
Q_6: It requires data warehouse admin privileges to create failure tables
E_1: Correct. When store_failures is enabled (true or 'view'/'ephemeral'), dbt creates database objects containing rows that failed the test. This allows you to query and analyze failures directly: SELECT * FROM schema.failing_test_name. Much more useful than just seeing counts in logs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_2: Incorrect. While most configs support multiple levels, store_failures configuration can be set in dbt_project.yml (globally or per-path), but it's more commonly configured at the project level rather than per individual test in schema.yml. It CAN be configured in both places, though.
E_3: Correct. store_failures_as determines materialization: 'table' (default, persistent storage of failures), 'view' (query-time view of failures), or 'ephemeral' (CTE, not queryable). Choose based on needs: tables for persistent failure history, views for temporary inspection. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures_as
E_4: Incorrect. store_failures doesn't modify your data—it's purely diagnostic. It stores copies of failing rows for analysis but doesn't alter source tables or fix issues. Data quality fixes must be implemented in your models' SQL logic.
E_5: Correct. While not the primary purpose, storing failures can speed up iterative debugging. Instead of re-running expensive tests to see failures, query the stored failure table. However, there's a small overhead to create the failure tables initially. The main benefit is debugging convenience, not performance. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_6: Incorrect. store_failures uses the same database permissions as regular model builds. If you can create tables/views in your schema (which is required for dbt), you can use store_failures. No special admin privileges needed.
Explanation: OVERALL: store_failures preserves test failure details for analysis. Configuration: (1) store_failures: true/false - enables feature, (2) store_failures_as: table/view/ephemeral - controls materialization. Usage: enable globally in dbt_project.yml for all tests or specific paths, failures create objects named _dbt_test__, query failures with SELECT * FROM _dbt_test__audit_not_null_column. Benefits: detailed failure analysis, historical failure tracking (with tables), faster debugging iterations, root cause investigation. Trade-off: additional database objects and storage. Recommended for dev environments and critical prod tests. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures

Question: You are building a dbt project and want to reference another model called 'stg_customers' in your current model. What is the correct way to reference this model?
Q_1: Use a direct table reference: SELECT * FROM analytics.stg_customers
Q_2: Import the model at the top: {% import 'stg_customers' %} then query it normally
Q_3: Use the ref() function: SELECT * FROM {{ ref('stg_customers') }}
Q_4: Use the source() function: SELECT * FROM {{ source('stg_customers') }}
Q_5: Use a Jinja variable: {% set table = 'stg_customers' %} SELECT * FROM {{ table }}
E_1: Incorrect. Direct table references bypass dbt's dependency management. If you reference tables directly, dbt cannot track dependencies, determine build order, or update references when schemas change. Always use the ref() function to reference dbt models.
E_2: Incorrect. There is no import mechanism for dbt models. The ref() function is the only way to reference other dbt models. Jinja's {% import %} is used for macros, not models.
E_3: Correct. The ref() function is the proper way to reference dbt models. It creates dependencies in the DAG, allows dbt to determine correct build order, automatically resolves to the correct schema/database in different environments, and updates references when you change target configurations. This is fundamental to dbt's dependency management. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_4: Incorrect. The source() function is used to reference raw source tables defined in sources.yml, not dbt models. Since 'stg_customers' is a dbt model (indicated by the 'stg_' prefix for staging models), you must use ref(), not source().
E_5: Incorrect. While this would compile to valid SQL, it doesn't establish a dependency in dbt's DAG. dbt wouldn't know this model depends on stg_customers, leading to incorrect build order and potential runtime failures. Always use ref() for model dependencies.
Explanation: OVERALL: The ref() function is dbt's core mechanism for referencing models and establishing dependencies. When you use {{ ref('model_name') }}, dbt: (1) creates a dependency edge in the DAG to ensure correct build order, (2) resolves to the appropriate schema/database for your environment (dev vs prod), (3) allows you to run specific models and their dependencies with model selection syntax, and (4) enables impact analysis when models change. Never use direct table references or workarounds—always use ref() for dbt models and source() for raw source tables. This is fundamental to dbt's architecture and workflow. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref

Question: What is the purpose of the access configuration in dbt model governance?
Q_1: It controls which users can query the model in the data warehouse
Q_2: It determines which environments (dev, prod) the model can be deployed to
Q_3: It controls which other models and projects can reference this model using ref()
Q_4: It restricts who can modify the model's code in version control
Q_5: It configures row-level security policies on the model's data
E_1: Incorrect. The access configuration doesn't set database-level permissions or control who can query tables. Database permissions are managed through your warehouse's security system (GRANT statements, roles, etc.). Access in dbt controls ref() visibility between models within your dbt project.
E_2: Incorrect. Environment deployment is controlled by the enabled configuration or deployment jobs, not the access configuration. Access specifically controls model reference visibility in the dbt DAG, not deployment targets.
E_3: Correct. The access configuration sets model visibility for dbt references. Options are: 'private' (default for most models—only models in the same group/project can ref() it), 'protected' (models in the same project and installed packages), 'public' (any model in any project can ref() it). This enables API-like contracts for shared models and prevents unintended dependencies. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_4: Incorrect. Version control permissions (who can edit files) are managed by your Git repository settings (branch protection, CODEOWNERS, etc.), not by dbt configurations. The access configuration is about dbt's dependency graph, not Git permissions.
E_5: Incorrect. Row-level security is implemented at the warehouse level using database-specific features (Snowflake row access policies, BigQuery row-level security, etc.). dbt's access configuration controls ref() visibility in the dbt project, not data-level security.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model access is part of dbt's governance features for managing dependencies in large projects and multi-project environments. By setting access: private, protected, or public, you control which models can be referenced: (1) private (default): only models in the same group can reference it—use for internal implementation details, (2) protected: same project and dependencies can reference it—use for shared utilities within a project, (3) public: any project can reference it—use for stable, documented models that serve as APIs. Combine access with model contracts and versions to create stable interfaces between teams. This prevents breaking changes and clarifies model ownership. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access

Question: What happens when a model with an enforced contract produces a different schema than specified?
Q_1: The build fails with a contract violation error
Q_2: dbt logs a warning but creates the table with the actual schema
Q_3: dbt automatically updates the contract definition to match the output
Q_4: The model builds successfully but the contract is marked as invalid in documentation
Q_5: dbt creates the table but drops any columns not in the contract
E_1: Correct. When contract: {enforced: true}, dbt validates that the model's output matches the defined contract (column names, data types, constraints). If the actual output differs—missing columns, wrong types, or constraint violations—dbt fails the build with a clear contract violation error. This prevents breaking changes from reaching downstream consumers and ensures API stability. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-contracts
E_2: Incorrect. Contract enforcement is strict, not advisory. Contracts with enforced: true cause build failures on violations—they don't just warn. If you want non-blocking validation, don't enforce contracts or use tests instead.
E_3: Incorrect. Contracts are explicit specifications that must be manually updated. dbt never automatically changes your code or configurations. If the model output changes, you must intentionally update the contract definition.
E_4: Incorrect. Contracts are enforced at build time, not just documented. With enforced: true, violations prevent the model from building at all. The build fails before the table is created or documentation is updated.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt doesn't modify your model's output to match contracts. Contracts validate output; they don't transform it. If the output doesn't match, the build fails—dbt doesn't silently drop columns or change the schema.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model contracts enforce schema stability for governed models. Define contracts in schema.yml with explicit columns, data types, and constraints. Enable enforcement with contract: {enforced: true}. When enforced: (1) dbt validates output matches contract before finalizing the build, (2) mismatches cause build failures with clear error messages, (3) downstream consumers are protected from breaking changes. Contracts are essential for public models that serve as APIs. Workflow: define contract in schema.yml, enable enforcement, test the model, update the contract when making intentional breaking changes (preferably through versioning). Unenforced contracts (enforced: false) serve as documentation without validation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-contracts

Question: You want to pass custom configuration values to your models at runtime, such as a date range for analysis. What is the BEST way to accomplish this?
Q_1: Define the values as macros and call the macros in your model
Q_2: Store the values in dbt_project.yml and reference them with {{ config() }}
Q_3: Use the --vars flag at runtime and access values in your model with {{ var('variable_name') }}
Q_4: Use environment variables and reference them with {{ env_var('VARIABLE_NAME') }}
Q_5: Hardcode the values in your model and manually change them when needed
E_1: Incorrect. Macros are functions that generate SQL, not runtime parameters. While you could hardcode values in macros, this doesn't allow runtime customization. You'd need to edit the macro file each time you want different values. Use variables (vars) for runtime parameters.
E_2: Incorrect. The config() function sets model configurations like materialization and schema, not runtime parameters. While you can store vars in dbt_project.yml, you'd reference them with {{ var() }}, not config(). Also, values in dbt_project.yml are static, not runtime-customizable.
E_3: Correct. dbt variables (vars) allow runtime parameterization. Define defaults in dbt_project.yml under 'vars:', then override at runtime using --vars '{"start_date": "2024-01-01"}'. Access in models with {{ var('start_date') }}. This enables dynamic behavior without code changes—perfect for date ranges, feature flags, or environment-specific values. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/var
E_4: Incorrect for this use case. While env_var() accesses environment variables, it's designed for sensitive values like credentials, not runtime parameters. Environment variables require setting in your shell/deployment environment, which is less convenient than --vars for user-facing parameters like date ranges. Use env_var() for secrets, var() for parameters.
E_5: Incorrect. Hardcoding defeats the purpose of parameterization. Every time you need different values, you'd modify model code, recompile, and redeploy. This is error-prone, violates DRY principles, and makes models inflexible. Variables enable dynamic behavior without code changes.
Explanation: OVERALL: Variables (vars) provide runtime parameterization for dbt projects. Define default values in dbt_project.yml under the 'vars:' key. Override at runtime with --vars in JSON format: dbt run --vars '{"key": "value"}'. Access in models, tests, and macros using {{ var('key') }} or {{ var('key', 'default') }} with fallback. Common use cases: date ranges for analysis, environment-specific behavior (e.g., sampling rates), feature flags for gradual rollouts, test data selectors. Variables make models flexible without requiring code changes for each run. For sensitive values (credentials, API keys), use env_var() instead. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/var

Question: A model runs successfully in development but fails in production with 'column does not exist'. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: The production database doesn't have enough storage space for the new column
Q_2: Source data schemas differ between dev and prod environments
Q_3: The model materialization differs between dev and prod configurations
Q_4: Production runs use a different dbt version that doesn't support the column syntax
Q_5: The model wasn't included in the production deployment due to selector configuration
E_1: Incorrect. Storage space issues cause different errors (out of disk space, quota exceeded). A 'column does not exist' error means SQL is trying to SELECT or reference a column that isn't in the source table—this is a schema mismatch, not a storage issue.
E_2: Correct. This is the classic cause of environment-specific failures. Your dev source tables have a different schema than prod—perhaps a column was added to dev but not prod, or columns are named differently. Verify source table schemas match between environments, check ETL/ingestion processes aren't loading different schemas, and consider using schema tests on sources to catch mismatches early. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources
E_3: Incorrect. Materialization (view, table, incremental) doesn't affect which columns exist in source data. Whether a model builds as a view or table, it executes the same SQL SELECT statement. Column existence errors point to source schema issues, not materialization configuration.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt versions don't affect which columns exist in your warehouse tables. Column names and availability are determined by your source data and database schema. While dbt version differences can cause compilation errors, 'column does not exist' is a database error about table schemas.
E_5: Incorrect. If a model isn't selected, it wouldn't run at all—you wouldn't see a 'column does not exist' error from it. This error indicates the model is running and executing SQL that references a non-existent column, pointing to schema mismatches between environments.
Explanation: OVERALL: Environment-specific failures often stem from differences between dev and prod: (1) Source schema mismatches—dev tables have columns that prod tables lack (or vice versa), (2) Timing issues—ETL loads data to dev before prod, creating schema drift, (3) Different data sources—dev points to sample/test data with different schemas than prod, (4) Manual schema changes in dev not replicated to prod. To prevent: (1) Use dbt source freshness checks to monitor source availability, (2) Add schema tests on source columns you depend on, (3) Ensure ETL processes deploy schema changes to all environments, (4) Use dbt Cloud's schema checks and contracts for critical models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources

Question: You're debugging a dbt model error and want to see the actual SQL that dbt generated. Where can you find the compiled SQL code?
Q_1: In the logs directory at logs/compiled/<model_name>.sql
Q_2: In the target/compiled directory at target/compiled/<project_name>/<path>/<model_name>.sql
Q_3: In the model file itself after running dbt compile, which overwrites the original
Q_4: In the database warehouse as a stored procedure or view definition
Q_5: In the manifest.json file in the target directory under the 'compiled_sql' key
E_1: Incorrect. The logs/ directory contains execution logs (dbt.log, run results), not compiled SQL. Compiled SQL files are stored in the target/ directory, specifically in target/compiled/.
E_2: Correct. When you run dbt compile, dbt run, or dbt test, dbt compiles your model files (resolving Jinja, ref() functions, etc.) and writes the plain SQL to target/compiled/<project_name>/<path>/<model_name>.sql. This compiled SQL shows exactly what will be executed against your warehouse, making it invaluable for debugging. You can copy this SQL and run it directly in your warehouse for troubleshooting. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile
E_3: Incorrect. dbt never modifies your source model files. The original .sql files in your models/ directory remain unchanged. Compiled SQL is written to separate files in the target/compiled/ directory, preserving your source code.
E_4: Incorrect. While some warehouses store view definitions, dbt's compiled SQL (with Jinja, macros, etc. resolved) isn't stored in the warehouse metadata. You need to check dbt's compilation artifacts in the target/ directory to see the full compiled SQL.
E_5: Incorrect. While manifest.json contains metadata about your project (models, dependencies, configurations), it stores raw_sql (original code) and compiled_sql, but it's in JSON format and harder to read. The dedicated compiled SQL files in target/compiled/ are more accessible for debugging.
Explanation: OVERALL: The target/ directory contains dbt's compilation and execution artifacts: (1) target/compiled/ has the compiled SQL (Jinja resolved, macros expanded, ref() replaced with table names), (2) target/run/ has the SQL that was actually executed (same as compiled for most materializations, different for incremental), (3) target/manifest.json has project metadata and lineage. To debug models: run dbt compile (or any command that compiles), navigate to target/compiled//

Question: Your dbt documentation shows model lineage, but column-level lineage is missing. What command provides column-level information in the documentation?
Q_1: Run dbt compile --include-columns to add column metadata to documentation
Q_2: Run dbt test to validate columns, which automatically populates column documentation
Q_3: Column lineage requires manual entry in schema.yml; it cannot be automatically generated
Q_4: Run dbt run --populate-catalog to build models and capture column information
Q_5: Run dbt docs generate which queries the warehouse information schema for column metadata
E_1: Incorrect. There is no --include-columns flag for dbt compile. Column-level lineage comes from querying your warehouse for table metadata (column names, types, etc.), which is done by dbt docs generate, not dbt compile.
E_2: Incorrect. dbt test validates data quality but doesn't populate documentation metadata. Tests can appear in docs if they're defined on columns in schema.yml, but running tests doesn't query table schemas or add column-level information to docs.
E_3: Incorrect. While you manually add column descriptions in schema.yml, column-level metadata (names, types, lineage) can be automatically generated. dbt docs generate queries your warehouse's information schema to retrieve actual column details from built tables.
E_4: Incorrect. There is no --populate-catalog flag for dbt run. Building models creates tables in the warehouse, but capturing their metadata for documentation requires dbt docs generate, which queries the information schema.
E_5: Correct. dbt docs generate does two things: (1) creates manifest.json from your project files (models, schema.yml, etc.), (2) optionally queries your warehouse's information schema to create catalog.json with actual table/column metadata (names, types, statistics). This catalog data powers column-level lineage and the detailed column information in dbt docs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt documentation consists of two artifacts: (1) manifest.json: Project structure, DAG, configurations, descriptions from schema.yml—generated from your dbt project files, (2) catalog.json: Actual table and column metadata from your warehouse—generated by querying information schema when you run dbt docs generate. For rich documentation with column types, statistics, and accurate metadata, run dbt docs generate after building your models. This queries your warehouse and captures the real schema. Without catalog information, docs show only what you've manually documented in schema.yml. For complete docs, the workflow is: dbt run (build models) → dbt docs generate (catalog metadata) → dbt docs serve (view docs). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are TRUE about model versioning in dbt? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Multiple versions of a model can coexist, allowing gradual migration of downstream dependencies
Q_2: Each version can have its own contract, ensuring schema stability for each variant
Q_3: Versions are automatically created when you change a model's SQL code
Q_4: You can set deprecation_date on old versions to communicate sunset timelines
Q_5: Versioning is only available for incremental models
Q_6: Versions must be sequential integers starting at 1
E_1: Correct. Model versioning enables v1 and v2 to exist simultaneously. Downstream models explicitly choose which version to reference: {{ ref('model_name', v=1) }} or {{ ref('model_name', v=2) }}. This allows gradual migration: release v2, let consumers migrate at their own pace, deprecate v1 after all have migrated. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-versions
E_2: Correct. Versions work with contracts to guarantee API stability. v1 might have contract A, v2 has contract B with new columns. Each contract is enforced independently, ensuring v1 consumers see consistent schema while v2 evolves. This is crucial for breaking changes—add new version with new contract instead of modifying existing one. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-versions
E_3: Incorrect. Versions must be explicitly defined in schema.yml. dbt doesn't automatically version models when code changes. You intentionally create versions when making breaking changes to governed models.
E_4: Correct. Add deprecation_date: 'YYYY-MM-DD' to a version's configuration to formally communicate when it will be removed. This appears in documentation, giving consumers clear notice to migrate. Workflow: create v2, set deprecation_date on v1, wait for migration period, remove v1 after date passes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-versions#deprecating-a-model-version
E_5: Incorrect. Versioning works with any materialization (view, table, incremental, ephemeral). It's about API management, not materialization strategy. Use versioning for any public models where downstream dependencies need stability.
E_6: Incorrect. While common practice uses v1, v2, v3, versions can be any identifier. You could use v1, v2_beta, v2_stable if meaningful for your workflow. However, integers are recommended for clarity.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model versioning manages breaking changes to governed models. Define versions in schema.yml under models: - name: model_name, versions: - v: 1, - v: 2. Each version: (1) Can have its own contract, (2) Is referenced explicitly: {{ ref('name', v=2) }}, (3) Can be deprecated with deprecation_date, (4) Exists as separate database objects (model_name_v1, model_name_v2). Use versioning when: making breaking changes to public models, ensuring API stability, providing migration periods for downstream consumers. Workflow: v1 exists → need breaking change → create v2 with new contract → deprecate v1 → consumers migrate → remove v1. This prevents breaking downstream models while allowing evolution. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-versions

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are valid ways to configure a dbt model's materialization? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Use a config block at the top of the model file: {{ config(materialized='table') }}
Q_2: Define it in dbt_project.yml under the models section for the model's path
Q_3: Specify it in the model's schema.yml file under the model's config property
Q_4: Pass it as a command-line argument when running dbt: dbt run --materialized table
Q_5: Set it as an environment variable: export DBT_MATERIALIZATION=table
Q_6: Define it in the profiles.yml file under the target configuration
E_1: Correct. You can configure materialization directly in the model's .sql file using a Jinja config block. This is the most common approach and takes precedence over project-level configurations. Place it at the top of your SELECT statement. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/model-configs
E_2: Correct. You can set default materializations for groups of models in dbt_project.yml using path-based configuration. For example, models: my_project: staging: +materialized: view applies view materialization to all models in the staging folder. Model-level configs override project-level configs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/model-configs
E_3: Correct. You can configure materialization in schema.yml (or any properties file) under a model's config block. This approach is useful for centralizing configurations with documentation and tests. Example: models: - name: my_model config: materialized: table. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/model-configs
E_4: Incorrect. There is no --materialized command-line flag. Materialization must be configured in your project files (model file, schema.yml, or dbt_project.yml). Command-line arguments like --vars and --target control runtime variables and environments, not materializations.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt doesn't read materialization from environment variables. Materialization is configured in project files using the three correct methods above. Environment variables are used for things like credentials and database connections in profiles.yml.
E_6: Incorrect. profiles.yml contains connection and credential information for database targets (dev, prod), not model configurations. Materialization is a model property, not a connection property, so it belongs in dbt_project.yml, schema.yml, or the model file itself.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt provides three locations to configure model properties like materialization, following a precedence hierarchy: (1) In-model config blocks (highest precedence), (2) schema.yml/properties files (medium precedence), (3) dbt_project.yml (lowest precedence). This hierarchy allows you to set sensible defaults at the project level while overriding for specific models. Configurations cascade: project defaults apply unless overridden by schema.yml, which is overridden by in-model configs. Choose based on scope—project-wide defaults go in dbt_project.yml, model-specific settings go in the model file or schema.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/configs-and-properties

Question: What is the default behavior when a dbt data test fails?
Q_1: The test logs a warning but dbt continues executing other models and tests
Q_2: dbt exits with a non-zero exit code and stops executing subsequent commands
Q_3: The failing model is automatically excluded from subsequent runs
Q_4: dbt sends an email notification but continues the build process
Q_5: The test result is stored but has no effect on the build's success status
E_1: Incorrect. This describes severity: warn behavior. The default severity is error, which stops the build when tests fail.
E_2: Correct. By default, test failures have severity: error, causing dbt to exit with code 1 and stop execution. This prevents bad data from propagating and ensures data quality issues are addressed. In CI/CD, this fails the pipeline. To allow builds to continue despite test failures, configure severity: warn on specific tests. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_3: Incorrect. Test failures don't change model selection or exclude models from future runs. They simply fail the current execution. Model selection is controlled explicitly via --select/--exclude flags.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt doesn't send notifications—that's handled by orchestration tools (dbt Cloud, Airflow, etc.). Test failures stop the build by default; they don't trigger notifications or allow continuation.
E_5: Incorrect. Test failures directly affect build status. With default severity: error, failures cause non-zero exit codes and stop execution. Tests aren't just informational—they enforce data quality gates.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt test failures have two possible severities: (1) error (default): Test failures cause dbt to exit with code 1, stopping the build. In CI/CD, this fails the pipeline. Use for critical data quality requirements that must pass. (2) warn: Test failures log warnings but don't stop the build. Exit code is 0. Use for important-but-not-blocking validations or during test development. Configure severity in schema.yml, dbt_project.yml, or model config blocks. Common pattern: error in production (enforce quality), warn in development (faster iteration). The 'dbt build' command respects severity settings and tests models in DAG order. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity

Question: What is the purpose of the pre-hook and post-hook configurations in dbt models?
Q_1: To validate data quality before and after model execution
Q_2: To automatically generate documentation before and after model builds
Q_3: To send notifications when models start and finish building
Q_4: To execute SQL statements before or after a model is built in the database
Q_5: To define dependencies between models that ref() cannot express
E_1: Incorrect. Data quality validation is handled by dbt tests, not hooks. While you could technically write validation logic in hooks, this isn't their intended purpose. Use dbt test for data quality, hooks for SQL operations.
E_2: Incorrect. Documentation generation is done via dbt docs generate, not hooks. Hooks execute SQL in your warehouse during model builds—they don't generate documentation artifacts.
E_3: Incorrect. Hooks execute SQL statements in your warehouse—they can't send notifications. Notifications should be configured in your orchestration tool (dbt Cloud, Airflow, etc.), not in hooks.
E_4: Correct. Pre-hooks run SQL before dbt builds the model; post-hooks run after. Common uses: granting permissions (post-hook: GRANT SELECT), creating indexes (post-hook: CREATE INDEX), inserting audit records, running ANALYZE/VACUUM. Hooks accept SQL or macro calls. Configure in models, dbt_project.yml, or schema.yml. Hooks are powerful but should be used sparingly—prefer dbt-native features when possible. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/hooks-operations
E_5: Incorrect. Hooks don't establish dependencies. Dependencies are created through ref() and source() functions. While hooks execute at specific times during a model's build, they don't affect the DAG or model dependencies.
Explanation: OVERALL: Hooks allow executing custom SQL before/after model builds. Pre-hooks run before the model builds (before CREATE TABLE/VIEW). Post-hooks run after the model builds successfully. Common patterns: (1) Post-hooks for grants: 'post-hook: GRANT SELECT ON {{ this }} TO role_name', (2) Post-hooks for indexes: 'post-hook: CREATE INDEX idx ON {{ this }} (column)', (3) Pre-hooks for setup: 'pre-hook: CREATE TEMP TABLE ...', (4) Post-hooks for auditing/metadata. Configure at model level (in-file config), in schema.yml, or globally in dbt_project.yml. Use {{ this }} to reference the current model. Hooks are warehouse-specific SQL, so they may reduce portability. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/hooks-operations

Question: Your incremental model is running but not adding new records. When you check the target table, it only contains data from the initial full refresh. What should you check FIRST?
Q_1: Verify that the materialization is set to 'incremental' in the model configuration
Q_2: Check if the --full-refresh flag is being used in the dbt run command
Q_3: Review the is_incremental() conditional to ensure new records are being selected correctly
Q_4: Ensure the model has a unique_key configured to prevent duplicates
Q_5: Run dbt clean to clear cached artifacts that might be interfering
E_1: Incorrect. If the model is running as incremental (not failing or rebuilding completely), the materialization is correctly configured. The question states the model is running, so config isn't the issue—the incremental logic itself is likely wrong.
E_2: Incorrect. Using --full-refresh would cause the model to rebuild completely each time, showing new data (all data) in the table. The question states only the initial data exists, suggesting incremental logic isn't capturing new records, not that full refreshes are happening.
E_3: Correct. The most common cause of incremental models not adding new records is incorrect logic in the {% if is_incremental() %} block. Check: (1) the WHERE clause correctly identifies new records using the incremental filter, (2) the unique_key is configured properly, (3) the filter references the correct timestamp/ID column, (4) the comparison logic (e.g., > vs >=) is correct. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models#how-do-i-rebuild-an-incremental-model
E_4: Incorrect for this specific issue. While unique_key is important for preventing duplicates and ensuring proper updates, its absence wouldn't prevent new records from being added—it would just cause duplicates. The issue is that new records aren't being selected at all, pointing to filter logic problems.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt clean removes compiled files and targets, but it doesn't affect how incremental models filter data. Cached artifacts don't cause incremental models to skip new records—the issue is in the model's SQL logic, specifically the is_incremental() conditional.
Explanation: OVERALL: When incremental models fail to add new records, the issue is almost always in the {% if is_incremental() %} conditional logic. This block determines which new records to add on incremental runs. Common mistakes: (1) incorrect timestamp column name in the filter, (2) wrong comparison operator (using < instead of >, or vice versa), (3) comparing to the wrong value (e.g., hardcoded date instead of this.max_column), (4) filter logic that excludes all new records. Debug by: examining the compiled SQL (in target/compiled/), manually running the incremental query against your data, verifying the max value in your table matches expectations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models

Question: What is the purpose of the source() function in dbt models?
Q_1: To reference other dbt models and establish dependencies between them
Q_2: To import external data files (CSV, JSON) into your dbt project
Q_3: To execute SQL queries against external databases not defined in profiles.yml
Q_4: To load macros from external dbt packages
Q_5: To reference raw source tables defined in sources.yml and enable source freshness checks
E_1: Incorrect. The ref() function references other dbt models. The source() function specifically references raw source tables that exist in your warehouse but aren't managed by dbt—these are defined in sources.yml configuration files.
E_2: Incorrect. dbt doesn't import data files directly. The source() function references tables that already exist in your warehouse (loaded by ETL tools like Fivetran, Stitch, or Airbyte). For seed data (small CSV files), use the dbt seed command, not source().
