E_3: Incorrect. dbt models execute against the database defined in your profiles.yml target. The source() function doesn't connect to external databases—it references tables in your configured warehouse that are defined in sources.yml.
E_4: Incorrect. Macros from packages are referenced using the package_name.macro_name() syntax after installing via dbt deps. The source() function is specifically for referencing raw source tables defined in sources.yml files.
E_5: Correct. The source() function references raw tables in your warehouse that are loaded by external processes (ETL tools) and defined in sources.yml. Using {{ source('source_name', 'table_name') }} instead of direct table references enables: (1) source freshness monitoring with dbt source freshness, (2) lineage tracking from raw sources through dbt models, (3) centralized source configuration, (4) automatic schema/database resolution. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources
Explanation: OVERALL: Sources represent raw tables in your warehouse loaded by external systems (Fivetran, Stitch, custom ETL). Define sources in sources.yml with source name, tables, and metadata. Reference them in models using {{ source('source_name', 'table_name') }} instead of direct table names. Benefits: (1) dbt source freshness checks validate data recency using loaded_at_field, (2) documentation shows complete lineage from sources to final models, (3) source configurations (database, schema) are centralized and environment-aware, (4) you can version and test source schemas. Use source() for raw tables you don't control, ref() for dbt models you build. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources

Question: [SELECT 2] Which of the following are correct ways to install and use a dbt package in your project? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Add the package to packages.yml with its repository and version, then run dbt deps to install it
Q_2: Install packages using pip install <package-name> like Python packages
Q_3: Reference package macros using the package name: {{ package_name.macro_name() }}
Q_4: Copy the package's code directly into your macros/ directory to avoid dependencies
Q_5: Enable packages in dbt_project.yml by setting packages: enabled: true
Q_6: Import packages at the top of your model files using {% import 'package_name' %}
E_1: Correct. This is the standard workflow for installing dbt packages. Create or edit packages.yml in your project root, specify the package (either from dbt Hub, git repository, or local path) with version/revision, then run dbt deps to download and install packages into the dbt_packages/ directory. After installation, the package's macros and models are available in your project. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages
E_2: Incorrect. dbt packages are not Python packages installed via pip. They're dbt projects with models, macros, and tests installed via dbt deps based on packages.yml configuration. Don't confuse dbt packages (dbt code/models) with Python packages (Python libraries).
E_3: Correct. After installing a package via dbt deps, reference its macros using the qualified syntax {{ package_name.macro_name() }}. For example, to use the surrogate_key macro from dbt_utils, call {{ dbt_utils.surrogate_key(['col1', 'col2']) }}. This namespacing prevents conflicts between packages and your project's macros. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages#using-a-package
E_4: Incorrect. While you could copy code, this defeats the purpose of packages: version management, updates, and dependency resolution. Copying code creates maintenance burden—you won't receive updates, bug fixes, or security patches. Always use the package system for external dependencies.
E_5: Incorrect. There's no packages: enabled setting in dbt_project.yml. Packages are enabled by simply listing them in packages.yml and running dbt deps. Once installed, they're automatically available in your project.
E_6: Incorrect. Jinja's {% import %} is used for importing macros from files, not for installing or enabling dbt packages. Packages installed via dbt deps are globally available in your project without imports. Just reference their macros using the qualified syntax.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt packages are reusable dbt projects that provide macros, models, tests, and other resources. To use packages: (1) Create packages.yml in your project root, (2) List packages with sources (dbt Hub, git URLs, or local paths) and versions, (3) Run dbt deps to download and install into dbt_packages/, (4) Reference package macros using {{ package_name.macro_name() }} syntax, (5) Run dbt deps again when you update package versions. Popular packages include dbt_utils (utility macros), dbt_expectations (data quality tests), and source-specific packages (fivetran, stitch, etc.). Always pin package versions for reproducibility. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages

Question: [SELECT 2] Which of the following are benefits of using dbt's state comparison in CI/CD pipelines? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Dramatically reduces CI runtime by running only changed models and their dependencies
Q_2: Automatically fixes failed models by reverting to previous state
Q_3: Eliminates the need for testing in development environments
Q_4: Provides automatic rollback when tests fail in production
Q_5: Caches test results to avoid re-running unchanged tests
Q_6: Enables running tests only on models that changed, not the entire project
E_1: Correct. State comparison (Slim CI) compares PR code against production state, identifying only changed models. Combined with + selectors (state:modified+), you run only impacted models. For large projects with 1000s of models, this reduces CI from hours to minutes—running 10 changed models instead of 1000 total models. Reference: https://discourse.getdbt.com/t/how-we-sped-up-our-ci-runs-by-10x-using-slim-ci/2603
E_2: Incorrect. State comparison identifies changes; it doesn't fix failures or revert code. Fixing failures requires code changes. State is for selection and comparison, not automatic remediation.
E_3: Incorrect. State comparison in CI doesn't replace dev testing—it optimizes CI by running only changed models. Developers should still test locally. Slim CI makes CI faster, not dev testing unnecessary.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt doesn't perform automatic rollbacks. Rollback strategies depend on your deployment process (blue/green deployments, warehouse features, etc.). State comparison is for selection, not deployment orchestration.
E_5: Incorrect. While state comparison can help select which tests to run (tests on modified models), it doesn't cache test results. Tests execute fresh each time—state comparison just determines which to run.
E_6: Correct. With state:modified+ selection, you run and test only changed models and their children. This means tests run only on impacted portions of the DAG, not everything. Combined with dbt build, this provides fast, focused validation in PRs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/state-comparison-methods
Explanation: OVERALL: State comparison transforms CI/CD efficiency through selective execution. Setup: (1) Save production manifest.json after successful runs, (2) In CI, download prod manifest, (3) Run: dbt build --select state:modified+ --state prod/, (4) Only changed models + dependencies build and test. Benefits: (1) 10-100x faster CI in large projects, (2) Faster feedback in PRs, (3) Lower warehouse costs (less compute), (4) Focused testing on impacted areas. The key insight: most PRs change <1% of models, but traditional CI rebuilds 100%. Slim CI runs only what's impacted. Combine with dbt Cloud's CI features or build custom CI with manifest artifacts. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/cloud-ci-job

Question: You have a complex business logic that's reused across multiple models. What is the BEST dbt approach to avoid code duplication?
Q_1: Copy and paste the SQL logic into each model that needs it
Q_2: Create a view in your database with the logic and query it from your models
Q_3: Use Jinja variables to store the logic and reference the variable in each model
Q_4: Create a macro that contains the logic and call it from models using {{ macro_name() }}
Q_5: Create an ephemeral model with the logic and reference it using ref() in dependent models
E_1: Incorrect. Copying code violates the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle. If the logic changes, you'd need to update multiple models, creating maintenance burden and risk of inconsistency. dbt provides better mechanisms for code reuse.
E_2: Incorrect. Creating database views outside dbt bypasses dbt's dependency management, documentation, and version control. dbt won't track dependencies on external views or document them properly. Use dbt models (ephemeral or views) to keep logic in your dbt project.
E_3: Incorrect. Jinja variables are for simple values, not complex SQL logic. For reusable SQL patterns, use macros. Variables don't support parameterization or complex logic—macros are specifically designed for reusable SQL code.
E_4: Correct. Macros are dbt's mechanism for reusable SQL logic. Define a macro in macros/ directory that accepts parameters and returns SQL. Call it from models using {{ macro_name(params) }}. This centralizes logic, makes updates easy, supports parameterization, and follows DRY principles. Use macros for reusable SQL patterns that don't need to be separate models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_5: Incorrect for this use case. While ephemeral models can contain reusable logic, they're best for intermediate transformations that produce a dataset, not for generic business logic snippets. If the logic is a SQL pattern (not a full transformation), macros are more appropriate. Ephemeral models are CTEs; macros are SQL generators.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt provides multiple mechanisms for code reuse, each suited for different scenarios: (1) Macros: For reusable SQL patterns, calculations, or logic snippets. Return SQL that's injected into models. Perfect for date calculations, standardized transformations, or business logic formulas. (2) Ephemeral models: For intermediate transformations that produce datasets. Used as CTEs in downstream models via ref(). (3) Regular models (view/table): For transformations that should be queryable and documented as data products. Choose based on what you're reusing: SQL patterns → macros, intermediate datasets → ephemeral, data products → models. Macros support parameters, can be packaged and shared, and promote consistency. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros

Question: Your team wants to add rich documentation to a model that includes a multi-paragraph explanation with formatting, code examples, and links. What is the BEST approach?
Q_1: Write the documentation directly in the schema.yml file using multi-line YAML strings
Q_2: Create a README.md file in the models directory and reference it using {{ doc('readme') }}
Q_3: Add the documentation as SQL comments in the model file using -- or /* */ syntax
Q_4: Use the dbt docs generate command with a custom template that includes the documentation
Q_5: Create a markdown file with a docs block, then reference it in schema.yml using {{ doc('block_name') }}
E_1: Incorrect. While you can write multi-line descriptions in schema.yml using YAML multi-line syntax, this becomes unwieldy for long documentation with formatting and code examples. YAML files are meant for structured configuration, not rich content. For extensive documentation, use separate markdown files.
E_2: Incorrect. While the doc() function references markdown documentation blocks, it requires specific markdown block syntax ({% docs block_name %}) in .md files, not standard README.md files. Also, README files typically describe directories/projects, not individual models.
E_3: Incorrect. SQL comments in model files are not parsed or displayed in dbt's generated documentation. They're useful for developers reading the raw SQL but don't appear in dbt docs serve. Documentation must be in schema.yml or separate .md files referenced via the doc() function.
E_4: Incorrect. The dbt docs generate command creates documentation artifacts from existing descriptions in schema.yml and doc blocks—it doesn't define the documentation itself. You can customize the documentation site's appearance, but the content must come from schema.yml or doc blocks.
E_5: Correct. For rich, multi-paragraph documentation, create a .md file containing {% docs block_name %}...{% enddocs %} blocks with markdown-formatted content. Then reference these blocks in schema.yml using description: '{{ doc("block_name") }}'. This separates lengthy documentation from configuration, supports rich formatting, and keeps schema.yml clean and readable. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation#using-docs-blocks
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt supports two documentation approaches: (1) Simple descriptions directly in schema.yml for short documentation, (2) Markdown doc blocks for rich, formatted content. To use doc blocks, create a .md file (often docs.md or overview.md in your models directory), define named blocks using {% docs block_name %}, write your content with markdown formatting, close with {% enddocs %}, then reference in schema.yml with {{ doc('block_name') }}. This pattern enables maintainable, well-formatted documentation with code examples, tables, and links while keeping schema.yml focused on configuration. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation

Question: You want to test that the 'order_status' column in your orders model only contains valid values: 'pending', 'shipped', 'delivered', or 'cancelled'. Which dbt test should you use?
Q_1: Use a not_null test to ensure the column always has a value
Q_2: Use an accepted_values test with the list of valid statuses
Q_3: Use a unique test to ensure each status appears only once
Q_4: Use a relationships test to verify the status exists in a reference table
Q_5: Write a custom singular test with a WHERE clause checking for invalid values
E_1: Incorrect. The not_null test only verifies that a column doesn't contain NULL values. It doesn't validate that values are from an allowed set. You need accepted_values to enforce a specific list of valid values.
E_2: Correct. The accepted_values test ensures a column only contains values from a specified list. Configure it in your schema.yml: tests: [accepted_values: {values: ['pending', 'shipped', 'delivered', 'cancelled']}]. This test will fail if any rows contain values outside this list. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/accepted_values
E_3: Incorrect. The unique test verifies that a column contains no duplicate values (e.g., for IDs). It doesn't validate that values match an allowed list. Multiple orders can have the same status, so unique wouldn't make sense here.
E_4: Incorrect for this scenario. While you could create a reference table with valid statuses and use a relationships test, this is overly complex for a simple enumeration. The accepted_values test is specifically designed for this use case and is more direct.
E_5: Incorrect. While you could write a custom singular test (a .sql file that returns invalid rows), the accepted_values generic test already provides this functionality. Don't reinvent built-in tests—use dbt's generic tests for common validation patterns.
Explanation: OVERALL: The accepted_values test validates that a column contains only values from a predefined list. It's perfect for enum-like columns such as status fields, categories, or types. Define it in schema.yml under the column's tests array. dbt generates SQL that counts rows where the column value is NOT IN the specified list—if any are found, the test fails. For complex validation logic not covered by generic tests, write singular tests. But for standard validations like allowed values, unique keys, not null, and relationships, always use dbt's built-in generic tests. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: You want to add a link to an external dashboard in your model's documentation. How should you do this?
Q_1: Add a URL field in schema.yml: url: https://dashboard.example.com
Q_2: Include the link in the model's description using Markdown link syntax
Q_3: Create a custom meta field: meta: {dashboard_url: 'https://...'}
Q_4: Add the link as a comment in the model's SQL file
Q_5: Use a post-hook to inject the link into the documentation
E_1: Incorrect. There's no built-in url: field in schema.yml. URLs and links must be embedded in description text using Markdown formatting.
E_2: Correct. Model descriptions in schema.yml support Markdown, including links. Use standard Markdown syntax: description: 'This model powers the [Sales Dashboard](https://dashboard.example.com/sales)'. The link will be clickable in the generated documentation. For longer content with multiple links, use doc blocks in .md files. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation
E_3: Incorrect for making links clickable in docs. While you can store URLs in meta fields for programmatic access, they won't appear as clickable links in the documentation UI. Meta is for structured metadata, not user-facing documentation. Use description with Markdown for visible links.
E_4: Incorrect. SQL comments don't appear in generated documentation. dbt docs are created from schema.yml descriptions and doc blocks, not SQL file comments. Links in SQL comments won't be visible to documentation users.
E_5: Incorrect. Post-hooks execute SQL in the warehouse—they can't modify documentation. Documentation content comes from schema.yml and .md files, not runtime hooks.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt documentation supports Markdown formatting in descriptions, enabling rich content including links, formatting, code blocks, and lists. Common patterns: (1) External dashboards: [Dashboard Name](URL), (2) Related documentation: [See docs](URL), (3) Jira tickets: [TICKET-123](URL), (4) Multiple links: Use doc blocks in .md files for cleaner formatting. Markdown features: **bold**, *italic*, `code`, [links](URL), bullet lists, numbered lists, code blocks, headers. For extensive documentation with many links, create a docs block in a .md file and reference it: description: '{{ doc("block_name") }}'. This keeps schema.yml clean while supporting rich content. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation

Question: What is the purpose of the {{ this }} variable in dbt models?
Q_1: It references the current user running the dbt command
Q_2: It references configuration settings for the current model
Q_3: It references the current dbt project name
Q_4: It provides metadata about the dbt version being used
Q_5: It references the current model's database relation (schema.table_name)
E_1: Incorrect. User information isn't available via {{ this }}. For environment/user context, use {{ target }} which provides target name, schema, user, etc.
E_2: Incorrect. Model configuration is accessed via {{ config.get('key') }}, not {{ this }}. {{ this }} specifically refers to the model's database relation.
E_3: Incorrect. Project information is available via {{ project_name }} variable, not {{ this }}. {{ this }} is model-specific, not project-wide.
E_4: Incorrect. Version information would be in {{ dbt_version }} or similar, not {{ this }}. {{ this }} specifically represents the current model's database object.
E_5: Correct. {{ this }} resolves to the current model's full database relation in the format database.schema.table_name (or schema.table_name depending on warehouse). It's commonly used in: (1) Incremental models to reference the existing table, (2) Pre/post-hooks to operate on the current model: GRANT SELECT ON {{ this }}, (3) Custom materialization logic. {{ this }} is only available during execution, not compilation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/this
Explanation: OVERALL: {{ this }} is a dbt-provided variable representing the current model's database relation. Common use cases: (1) Incremental models: reference the existing table with DELETE FROM {{ this }} WHERE ..., (2) Post-hooks: GRANT SELECT ON {{ this }} TO role_name, (3) Custom materializations: accessing the target relation. Important: {{ this }} is only available at execution time (dbt run, dbt build), not during compilation (dbt compile, dbt parse). In incremental models, use {% if is_incremental() %} to check if {{ this }} exists. The exact format depends on your warehouse (some include database, some don't). Related: {{ this }}.database, {{ this }}.schema, {{ this }}.identifier for components. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/this

Question: What is the purpose of the tags configuration in dbt models?
Q_1: To create database tags or labels on tables in the warehouse
Q_2: To automatically generate documentation categories for models
Q_3: To control which warehouse or database the model builds in
Q_4: To group and select models for execution using --select tag:name syntax
Q_5: To version models and track changes over time
E_1: Incorrect. dbt tags are project metadata for organization and selection, not warehouse-level tags. To create database tags, you'd use post-hooks with warehouse-specific SQL (ALTER TABLE ... SET TAG).
E_2: Incorrect. Tags don't create documentation categories automatically. While tags appear in docs as metadata, documentation organization follows folder structure and model hierarchy, not tags.
E_3: Incorrect. Warehouse/database location is controlled by profiles.yml (target configuration) and schema/database configs, not tags. Tags are for organization and selection within dbt.
E_4: Correct. Tags enable flexible model selection. Add tags: ['hourly', 'critical'] to models, then use dbt run --select tag:hourly to run all hourly models, dbt test --exclude tag:slow to skip slow tests. Common uses: execution frequency (hourly/daily), criticality (critical/standard), team ownership (team_finance), environment (prod_only). Tags are metadata for organization and selection. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags
E_5: Incorrect. Model versioning uses the versions configuration in schema.yml, not tags. Tags are for grouping and selection, not version management.
Explanation: OVERALL: Tags provide flexible model organization and selection. Configure in model files: {{ config(tags=['tag1', 'tag2']) }}, in schema.yml: tags: ['tag1'], or in dbt_project.yml for path-based tagging. Selection syntax: --select tag:name (models with tag), tag:tag1,tag:tag2 (union), tag:tag1 tag:tag2 (intersection), --exclude tag:name (exclude tagged models). Common tagging strategies: (1) Schedule: hourly, daily, weekly, (2) Criticality: critical, standard, (3) Team: team_analytics, team_finance, (4) Environment: prod_only, dev_only, (5) Type: report, metric, snapshot. Tags complement other selection methods (model names, paths, dependencies). Use consistently across your project for effective model management. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags

Question: What is the primary purpose of using model groups in dbt?
Q_1: To organize models into folders in the file system for better project structure
Q_2: To execute multiple models together in a single batch for better performance
Q_3: To define ownership boundaries and control which models can reference each other
Q_4: To automatically generate separate documentation sites for each group
Q_5: To run models on different warehouse compute clusters based on their group assignment
E_1: Incorrect. While folder organization helps project structure, groups are a governance feature separate from folder structure. Groups are defined in schema.yml and control access and ownership, not file organization. You can have groups that don't match folder structure.
E_2: Incorrect. Groups don't affect execution or batching. dbt determines execution order based on the DAG (dependencies from ref()), not groups. You can run models by group using selection syntax, but groups themselves don't batch execution.
E_3: Correct. Groups are a governance feature that establishes ownership and access boundaries. When you assign models to groups and set group ownership (owner: name/email), you create clear boundaries. Combined with access: private (default), models can only be referenced by other models in the same group, preventing unwanted cross-team dependencies. This is crucial for large multi-team projects. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access#groups
E_4: Incorrect. Groups don't generate separate documentation sites. All models appear in the unified dbt docs regardless of groups. Groups affect ownership metadata shown in docs and ref() access control, but don't split documentation into separate sites.
E_5: Incorrect. Groups don't control compute resources or warehouse clusters. Warehouse resource allocation is managed through connection configurations in profiles.yml, dbt Cloud environment settings, or warehouse-specific configurations, not through model groups.
Explanation: OVERALL: Groups are part of dbt's governance features for multi-team collaboration. Define groups in schema.yml with an owner (name/email/both). Assign models to groups in their configuration. Benefits: (1) Establishes clear ownership—documentation shows who owns each model, (2) Controls access—models with access: private (default) can only be ref'd within their group, preventing unauthorized dependencies, (3) Enables organization at scale—teams can own their domain models without interference, (4) Works with contracts and versions for robust APIs. Use groups when multiple teams share a dbt project, when you need to prevent cross-team ref() calls, or when ownership clarity is important. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access#groups

Question: A dbt test passes in development but fails in production on the same data. What should you investigate FIRST?
Q_1: The test configuration is different between environments
Q_2: The where clause references environment-specific values like target.name that differ between dev and prod
Q_3: Development uses a newer dbt version with bug fixes
Q_4: The test severity is set differently in each environment
Q_5: Tests are cached in development but not in production
E_1: Incorrect. Test configurations are in your project code (schema.yml), which should be identical across environments if properly version controlled. Environmental differences typically stem from data or warehouse settings, not test configs.
E_2: Correct. Tests with conditional logic using {{ target.name }}, {{ var() }}, or environment-specific filters can behave differently across environments. Example: where: "created_at > '{{ var('start_date') }}'" might have different variable values in dev vs prod, causing different test results on the same data. Always check Jinja conditionals in test configurations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/where
E_3: Incorrect. While dbt version differences can cause issues, they're unlikely to make a test pass in one environment and fail in another on identical data. Version differences usually cause compilation errors or feature availability issues, not test result changes.
E_4: Incorrect. Test severity (warn vs error) affects build behavior when tests fail, not whether they pass or fail. Severity doesn't change test logic or results—just what happens after failure.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt doesn't cache test results across runs. Each execution runs tests fresh. Caching doesn't affect test pass/fail outcomes.
Explanation: OVERALL: Environment-specific test failures on identical data typically indicate conditional logic in test configurations that evaluates differently per environment. Common causes: (1) where clauses with {{ target.name }} conditionals, (2) {{ var() }} references with different values in each environment, (3) Environment-specific date/time filters, (4) Jinja logic based on target.schema or other environment variables. Debug by: (1) Check test where clauses for Jinja, (2) Compare var values between environments, (3) Review target information (target.name, target.schema), (4) Examine compiled SQL in target/compiled/ for both environments. Best practice: minimize environment-specific test logic or document it clearly. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/where

Question: What is the primary use of the run_results.json artifact in dbt?
Q_1: To store compiled SQL for all models in the project
Q_2: To track the execution results and timing of each node from the most recent dbt invocation
Q_3: To define the dependency graph (DAG) for the project
Q_4: To configure database connection settings for different environments
Q_5: To store source freshness check results
E_1: Incorrect. Compiled SQL is stored in target/compiled/ directory, not run_results.json. run_results.json contains execution metadata (timing, status, errors), not SQL code. For compiled SQL, reference target/compiled/[project]/models/[model].sql. run_results.json is about run history, not query code.
E_2: Correct. run_results.json (in target/) captures detailed execution metadata from the last dbt run/test/build/seed: For each node (model, test, seed, snapshot): Status (success, error, skipped), execution time (how long it took), rows affected (rows created/updated), error messages (if failed), thread ID. Overall run metadata: dbt version, invocation ID, start/end times, command executed. Generated after: dbt run, dbt test, dbt build, dbt seed, dbt snapshot. Use cases: CI/CD monitoring (parse for failures, track timing), performance analysis (identify slow models), alerting (send notifications on failures), and reporting (aggregate execution metrics). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/run-results-json
E_3: Incorrect. The DAG is stored in manifest.json (nodes, parent_map, child_map), not run_results.json. manifest.json describes project structure and dependencies. run_results.json describes execution outcomes. Different artifacts, different purposes. For DAG information, reference manifest.json.
E_4: Incorrect. Database connections are configured in profiles.yml (in ~/.dbt/), not run_results.json. run_results.json is a read-only artifact generated after runs—it doesn't configure anything. Profiles.yml contains credentials and connection parameters. run_results.json is output, not input.
E_5: Incorrect. Source freshness results are in sources.json, not run_results.json. sources.json contains: max_loaded_at times, freshness status (pass/warn/error), snapshot times. run_results.json is for run/test/build execution. dbt source freshness generates sources.json. Different commands, different artifacts.
Explanation:
OVERALL: run_results.json provides detailed execution telemetry for observability and monitoring. Structure: {"metadata": {"dbt_version": "1.5.0", "generated_at": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z", "invocation_id": "abc123", "env": {...}}, "results": [{"unique_id": "model.project.fct_orders", "status": "success", "execution_time": 12.5, "rows_affected": 15000, "thread_id": "Thread-1", "timing": [{"name": "compile", "started_at": "...", "completed_at": "..."}, {"name": "execute", "started_at": "...", "completed_at": "..."}], "message": null}], "elapsed_time": 125.3}. Key fields: status: success, error, skipped, fail (for tests). execution_time: Seconds to execute node. rows_affected: Rows created/updated (for models). message: Error message if failed. Use cases: 1) CI/CD failure detection: Parse for status != "success", fail CI pipeline on errors, identify which models failed. 2) Performance monitoring: Track execution_time over runs, identify regressions (models getting slower), optimize slow models. 3) Alerting: Send Slack/email on failures, include error messages from run_results.json, alert on execution time exceeding thresholds. 4) Reporting/Dashboarding: Aggregate timing data for trends, visualize model execution times, track test pass rates. 5) Debugging: Identify failed nodes and error messages, see execution order (thread_id, timing), understand what ran vs skipped. Example parsing (Python): import json
with open('target/run_results.json') as f:
 results = json.load(f)
failed = [r for r in results['results'] if r['status'] != 'success']
if failed:
 print(f"Failed models: {[r['unique_id'] for r in failed]}"). Integration patterns: CI/CD: dbt run → parse run_results.json → fail if errors. Monitoring: Store run_results.json in data warehouse, query for trends. Alerting: dbt Cloud webhooks or custom scripts parse results. Analytics: Build dashboards from historical run_results.json. Comparison with other artifacts: manifest.json: Project structure/DAG (what exists). run_results.json: Execution outcomes (what happened). catalog.json: Database metadata (column types, stats from introspection). sources.json: Source freshness results. Lifecycle: Generated/updated after each dbt run/test/build. Overwrites previous run_results.json. For historical tracking, archive after each run (copy to timestamped file or database). Best practices: Parse in CI/CD for automated quality gates. Archive for trend analysis (S3, database, dbt Cloud stores automatically). Monitor timing for performance regressions. Use with manifest.json for complete picture (what ran + how it went). Limitations: Only reflects most recent run (previous runs overwritten). Doesn't include future runs or scheduled runs (those generate their own run_results.json). Not suitable for long-term storage without archiving. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/run-results-json

Question: [SELECT 4] Which statements about ephemeral models in dbt are correct? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Ephemeral models are not materialized as database objects—they're compiled as CTEs in dependent models
Q_2: Ephemeral models can be queried directly in the database using SELECT statements
Q_3: Ephemeral models are automatically deleted from the database after each dbt run
Q_4: Ephemeral models are useful for intermediate transformations that don't need to be persisted
Q_5: Ephemeral models improve performance by caching results across multiple downstream models
Q_6: Ephemeral models can be referenced by other models using the ref() function
E_1: Correct. Ephemeral models don't create tables or views. Configuration: {{ config(materialized='ephemeral') }}. When a downstream model references an ephemeral model via ref(), dbt inlines the ephemeral model's SQL as a CTE (WITH clause) in the downstream model's compiled SQL. Example: model_a (ephemeral): SELECT * FROM raw.orders; model_b: SELECT * FROM {{ ref('model_a') }}; Compiles to: WITH model_a AS (SELECT * FROM raw.orders) SELECT * FROM model_a. Benefits: No database objects created (reduces clutter), useful for simple transformations. Drawbacks: Can't query directly, no performance benefits (recompiled in each downstream model). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral
E_2: Incorrect. Ephemeral models don't exist in the database—they're not materialized. You can't SELECT from them directly. They only exist as CTEs within compiled downstream models. To query a model directly, use view or table materialization. Ephemeral is purely a compilation strategy.
E_3: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are never created in the database, so they can't be deleted. They're not temporary tables that get cleaned up—they simply don't exist as database objects. Each time a downstream model runs, the ephemeral model's SQL is re-compiled inline as a CTE.
E_4: Correct. Use ephemeral for lightweight intermediate logic that's only used by one or two downstream models. Example: simple column renaming, basic filtering, or type casting that doesn't warrant a separate table/view. Benefits: Reduces database objects (less clutter in catalog), simplifies small transformations. Cautions: If multiple models reference an ephemeral model, the SQL is duplicated in each (inefficient). If logic is complex or used widely, use view instead for better performance and queryability. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral
E_5: Incorrect. Ephemeral models don't cache results—they recompile the SQL in every downstream model that references them. If 3 models ref() an ephemeral model, the ephemeral SQL runs 3 times (once per dependent model). This can hurt performance with complex ephemeral models or many downstream dependencies. For performance, use view or table materialization.
E_6: Correct. Ephemeral models are referenced exactly like other models: {{ ref('ephemeral_model_name') }}. dbt handles the compilation difference automatically—instead of generating a table reference, it inlines the ephemeral model's SQL as a CTE. From a developer perspective, ref() works the same way regardless of materialization. The DAG respects dependencies: ephemeral models must be compiled before downstream models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
Explanation: OVERALL: Ephemeral materialization is a unique dbt strategy where models don't create database objects. Instead, their SQL is inlined as CTEs in downstream models. Configuration: {{ config(materialized='ephemeral') }} in model or dbt_project.yml: models: staging: +materialized: ephemeral. Compilation behavior: Upstream ephemeral model (model_a.sql): SELECT id, name FROM {{ source('raw', 'users') }}. Downstream model (model_b.sql): SELECT * FROM {{ ref('model_a') }} WHERE id > 100. Compiles to: WITH model_a AS (SELECT id, name FROM raw.users) SELECT * FROM model_a WHERE id > 100. Multiple references: If model_b and model_c both ref('model_a'), model_a's SQL is duplicated in both—no sharing or caching. When to use: Simple, lightweight transformations (renaming, casting, basic filtering), logic used by only 1-2 downstream models, reducing database object clutter (dev environments). When NOT to use: Complex SQL (will be duplicated across dependents), logic referenced by many models (creates bloated compiled SQL), need to query model directly (for debugging or ad-hoc analysis), performance-critical transformations (no caching/materialization benefits). Best practices: Keep ephemeral models simple, limit downstream dependencies (prefer view if >2 models reference), document why ephemeral was chosen, and use views for debugging (switch to view temporarily to query directly). Comparison: Ephemeral (no database object, CTE inline, can't query directly), View (database view, can query, shared across models), Table (database table, best performance, storage cost). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral

Question: When you get an error 'Incremental model has not been built yet', what should you do?
Q_1: Change the materialization from incremental to table
Q_2: Remove the is_incremental() macro from the model
Q_3: Add a unique_key configuration to the model
Q_4: Run the model with the --full-refresh flag to create the initial table
Q_5: Delete the target table manually and run dbt again
E_1: Incorrect. Changing materialization is not necessary—incremental models are designed to work. This error occurs on first run because the table doesn't exist yet. dbt expects incremental models to be built with a full refresh initially. Don't change strategy; just run with --full-refresh to create the initial table.
E_2: Incorrect. The is_incremental() macro is essential for incremental logic—it prevents the filter from running on first build. Removing it would break incremental functionality. The error indicates the table needs initial creation, not that the macro is wrong. Run with --full-refresh to build the table initially.
E_3: Incorrect. While unique_key is important for merge/delete+insert strategies, missing unique_key doesn't cause this specific error. This error means the table doesn't exist yet. unique_key affects how incremental updates work (upsert vs append), not initial table creation. Run --full-refresh first.
E_4: Correct. Incremental models require an initial full build to create the base table. Error occurs when: Table doesn't exist (first run, dropped manually, wrong schema). Solution: dbt run --select my_model --full-refresh. This creates the table with all historical data, ignoring is_incremental() logic (runs the full SELECT without filters). Subsequent runs execute incrementally, processing only new data. Workflow: First run: --full-refresh (loads all data). Ongoing runs: dbt run (incremental updates). Manual refresh: --full-refresh when needing to rebuild from scratch. The --full-refresh flag makes is_incremental() return false, executing the full query. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_5: Incorrect. Deleting the table doesn't solve the problem—it creates the same scenario (table doesn't exist). You'd still need --full-refresh to build it. The proper solution is --full-refresh which handles table creation correctly. Manual deletion adds unnecessary steps and potential errors.
Explanation: OVERALL: Incremental models need initialization with full data before incremental updates can work. Error scenarios: First time building model (table never created). Table was manually dropped or deleted. Schema was recreated (dev environment refresh). Wrong target schema (profiles.yml misconfigured). Solution workflow: 1) dbt run --select my_incremental_model --full-refresh (creates table with all data). 2) Verify: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM schema.model_name in warehouse. 3) Subsequent runs: dbt run --select my_incremental_model (incremental updates). How --full-refresh works: Sets is_incremental() to false, ignores incremental filters (WHERE clauses), runs full SELECT loading all data, and creates table from scratch (drops if exists). When to use --full-refresh: Initial model build, incremental logic has bugs (rebuild with corrected logic), source data changed historically (need to reprocess), testing new incremental strategy. Best practices: Design incremental models to work both ways (full and incremental), test full-refresh regularly (ensure it works), document full-refresh requirements in model, and automate initial builds in CI/CD (detect new models, run with --full-refresh). Common patterns: on_schema_change config for handling schema changes in incremental models, incremental_strategy selection (append, merge, delete+insert), and proper unique_key definition for merge strategies. Debugging: Check if table exists: SHOW TABLES LIKE 'model_name'. Review compiled SQL in target/run/ for full vs incremental logic. Verify is_incremental() conditions. Prevention: Include initial --full-refresh in deployment scripts. Document incremental models clearly. Test in dev with --full-refresh to ensure correctness. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models

Question: What is the benefit of using asset properties in dbt documentation?
Q_1: Asset properties automatically generate test cases for all models
Q_2: Asset properties control database-level permissions and access
Q_3: Asset properties provide additional context and metadata that appears in the generated documentation site
Q_4: Asset properties determine which models are included in documentation generation
Q_5: Asset properties optimize query performance by adding database hints
E_1: Incorrect. Asset properties (meta fields, descriptions, owner info) provide metadata for documentation—they don't generate tests. Tests must be explicitly defined in schema.yml using tests: configuration. Asset properties enhance documentation with context, ownership, and custom metadata but don't create automated testing. Testing and documentation are separate concerns.
E_2: Incorrect. Asset properties are metadata for documentation, not access control. Database permissions are managed via GRANT statements (often in post-hooks) or database-native tools. While you could use meta fields to inform permission scripts (e.g., if meta.sensitivity = 'high', apply strict grants), properties themselves don't control access.
E_3: Correct. Asset properties enrich dbt documentation with custom metadata beyond basic descriptions. Key properties: 1) meta: Custom key-value metadata: models: - name: fct_orders, meta: {owner: 'analytics-team', priority: 'critical', refresh_frequency: 'daily'}. Appears in docs site under 'Meta' section. Use for: ownership, SLAs, contact info, business context. 2) description: Model/column descriptions (basic property): description: 'Daily orders fact table with revenue calculations'. Supports Markdown. 3) columns: Column-level metadata: columns: - name: order_id, description: 'Unique order identifier', meta: {pii: false}. Benefits: Searchable documentation, business context for users, operational metadata (ownership, refresh schedules), and discovery (find models by custom attributes). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation
E_4: Incorrect. All models are automatically included in dbt docs generate—you can't selectively exclude models using properties. Asset properties add metadata to documentation but don't control inclusion. Every model, test, source, and snapshot appears in docs. Properties enhance entries, not filter them.
E_5: Incorrect. Asset properties are documentation metadata with no effect on query execution or performance. They don't generate SQL hints or influence database optimization. Performance comes from materialization strategies, indexes, partitioning, and SQL quality. Documentation properties are for human readers, not query engines.
Explanation: OVERALL: Asset properties provide rich, structured metadata for comprehensive documentation. Property types: 1) description: Basic text descriptions: models: - name: fct_orders, description: | Core orders fact table containing all order transactions with calculated metrics including revenue, quantity, and discount amounts. Updated daily at 6 AM UTC. Supports Markdown (headers, lists, links, code blocks). Use {{ doc('doc_block') }} for long descriptions. 2) meta: Custom key-value metadata: models: - name: fct_orders, meta: {owner: 'data-team@company.com', refresh_schedule: 'daily at 06:00 UTC', data_classification: 'internal', last_review_date: '2024-01-15', sla_hours: 24, contains_pii: false}. Arbitrary structure—define standards for your org. Appears in 'Meta' section of model page in docs. 3) Column properties: columns: - name: customer_id, description: 'Foreign key to dim_customers', meta: {is_pii: false, data_type: 'integer'}, - name: email, description: 'Customer email address', meta: {is_pii: true, masked_in_dev: true}. 4) tags: Categorical metadata: tags: ['daily', 'critical', 'finance']. Appears in docs, also used for selection (dbt run --select tag:daily). Use cases: Ownership tracking: meta: {owner: 'team@company.com', slack_channel: '#data-eng'}. Contact info in docs for questions. SLA documentation: meta: {refresh_frequency: 'hourly', max_lag_minutes: 60}. Users know data freshness expectations. Data classification: meta: {sensitivity: 'high', contains_pii: true}. Security context. Business context: meta: {business_owner: 'Jane Doe', approved_uses: ['reporting', 'analysis']}. Data governance. Custom attributes: meta: {cost_center: '1234', project: 'customer360'}. Organizational metadata. Best practices: Define standard meta schema: Document required/optional meta fields (create meta_standards.md). Establish naming conventions (snake_case keys). Use consistent value formats (dates as YYYY-MM-DD). Validate in CI (custom script checking meta completeness). Use for searchable attributes: Meta fields are searchable in docs site. Add discoverability metadata (domain, subject area). Combine with dbt Explorer (dbt Cloud): Meta fields appear in dbt Explorer UI, enabling filtering and discovery. Document in README: Explain your org's meta field standards. Example with ownership and SLA: models: - name: fct_revenue, description: 'Revenue fact table aggregated from orders', meta: {owner_team: 'finance-analytics', owner_contact: 'finance-data@company.com', refresh_schedule: 'daily at 02:00 UTC', sla_hours: 6, data_classification: 'confidential', business_critical: true, last_schema_change: '2024-01-10', documentation_last_reviewed: '2024-01-15'}, columns: - name: revenue_usd, description: 'Revenue in US dollars', meta: {calculation: 'quantity * unit_price', currency: 'USD', can_be_null: false}. Accessing in docs: Navigate to model page, view Meta section for custom metadata, search docs site for specific meta values. Programmatic access: Parse manifest.json or catalog.json for meta fields. Build custom reports from metadata. dbt Cloud API provides access to meta fields. Advanced: Use meta for automation: Post-hooks based on meta: {% if meta.contains_pii %}GRANT SELECT ON {{ this }} TO pii_role{% endif %}. Tagging based on meta (in macros). Documentation generation from meta (custom scripts). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta

Question: How can you configure a test to run only in specific environments (e.g., production but not development)?
Q_1: Create separate schema.yml files for each environment
Q_2: Use different dbt projects for development and production
Q_3: Use the enabled config with conditional logic based on target.name
Q_4: Tests automatically run only in production by default
Q_5: Use the --target flag to specify which tests to run
E_1: Incorrect. Maintaining separate schema.yml files for environments is impractical and error-prone. Tests should be defined once with conditional logic based on target/environment. dbt provides better solutions: enabled config with Jinja expressions referencing target. Don't duplicate YAML across environments.
E_2: Incorrect. Separate projects for environments defeats dbt's purpose of consistent code across dev/prod. Environments are managed via profiles.yml targets, not separate projects. Use one project with environment-aware configurations. Separate projects create maintenance nightmares and prevent environment parity testing.
E_3: Correct. Configure tests to run conditionally using enabled and Jinja: columns: - name: customer_id, tests: - unique: {enabled: "{{ target.name == 'prod' }}"}. This runs the test only when target is 'prod'. More complex conditions: enabled: "{{ target.name in ['prod', 'staging'] }}", enabled: "{{ env_var('RUN_EXPENSIVE_TESTS', 'false') == 'true' }}", or enabled: "{{ not flags.FULL_REFRESH }}". Use cases: Expensive tests only in production, data quality checks only in non-dev, environment-specific validation. Can also use config() in test files for singular tests. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/enabled
E_4: Incorrect. Tests run in all environments by default when you execute dbt test. There's no automatic production-only behavior. You must explicitly configure enabled conditions to limit tests to specific environments. Default behavior is inclusive—tests run everywhere unless configured otherwise.
E_5: Incorrect. --target flag switches between profiles.yml target configurations (dev, prod) but doesn't control which tests run. It changes database connection settings, not test selection. To conditionally run tests by environment, use enabled config with target.name logic, not --target flag itself.
Explanation: OVERALL: Environment-specific test configuration enables flexible testing strategies across dev/staging/prod. Configuration approaches: 1) enabled config in schema.yml: tests: - unique: {enabled: "{{ target.name == 'prod' }}"}, - relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'id', enabled: "{{ target.name != 'dev' }}"}. 2) Project-wide in dbt_project.yml: tests: +enabled: "{{ target.name != 'dev' }}". 3) Per-directory: models: marts: +tests: +enabled: "{{ target.name == 'prod' }}". Common patterns: Expensive tests only in prod: enabled: "{{ target.name == 'prod' }}". Skip certain tests in dev: enabled: "{{ target.name != 'dev' }}". Use environment variable: enabled: "{{ env_var('RUN_TESTS', 'true') | as_bool }}". Conditional on flags: enabled: "{{ not flags.FULL_REFRESH }}". Use cases: Performance: Skip slow tests in development. Cost: Avoid expensive warehouse queries in dev. Data availability: Some reference data only exists in prod. Validation strictness: Relax rules in dev, enforce in prod. Advanced techniques: Combine with severity: tests: - unique: {severity: "{{ 'error' if target.name == 'prod' else 'warn' }}"}. Use tags + selection: Run specific test suites per environment. Variables for thresholds: tests: - relationships: {enabled: "{{ var('strict_mode', false) }}"}. Testing strategy: Development: Fast, essential tests only (primary keys, critical relationships). Staging: Comprehensive test suite (full validation before prod). Production: All tests plus production-specific checks (SLA validation, cross-system reconciliation). Best practices: Default to running tests (enabled: true), selectively disable for good reasons (document why), test your disabled conditions (ensure they work as expected), and maintain environment parity where possible (minimize differences). Debugging: Run dbt list --resource-type test to see which tests are enabled. Check compiled YAML to verify conditions. Use --debug to see configuration resolution. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/enabled

Question: What is the recommended approach for handling slowly changing dimensions (SCD Type 2) in dbt?
Q_1: Use dbt snapshots to track historical changes with automatic timestamping
Q_2: Create an incremental model with merge strategy to update existing records
Q_3: Use a view materialization with window functions to track changes
Q_4: Write custom SQL in a table model that manually manages valid_from and valid_to dates
Q_5: Create separate models for each day's data and union them together
E_1: Correct. Snapshots are dbt's built-in solution for SCD Type 2, tracking how records change over time. Create in snapshots/ directory with configuration: {% snapshot customers_snapshot %} {{ config(target_schema='snapshots', target_database='analytics', unique_key='id', strategy='timestamp', updated_at='updated_at') }} SELECT * FROM {{ source('raw', 'customers') }} {% endsnapshot %}. Strategies: timestamp (uses updated_at column to detect changes) or check (compares specified columns). Snapshots add metadata columns: dbt_valid_from (when record became active), dbt_valid_to (when record was superseded, NULL for current), and dbt_updated_at (snapshot run timestamp). Run with dbt snapshot. Best for: tracking customer dimension changes, maintaining product history, and auditing data changes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/snapshots
E_2: Incorrect. Incremental models with merge update existing records in place—they don't maintain history. If a customer's address changes, merge overwrites the old value. You lose historical state. Incremental models are for fact tables where you want upserts, not SCD Type 2 which requires preserving all historical versions. Use snapshots for history tracking.
E_3: Incorrect. Views don't store data—they query source tables on demand. If source data changes, the view reflects current state only, losing history. Window functions can identify changes but don't persist historical records. SCD Type 2 requires storing multiple versions of the same record over time, which needs physical materialization. Use snapshots, not views.
E_4: Incorrect. While you could manually implement SCD Type 2 logic in SQL (MERGE statements with complex valid date management), snapshots provide this functionality out-of-box with less code and fewer bugs. Custom SCD logic is error-prone (missing edge cases, incorrect date handling) and harder to maintain. Always prefer dbt's snapshots feature for SCD Type 2. Don't reinvent built-in functionality.
E_5: Incorrect. Daily models with union create massive, unmanageable SQL and don't handle mid-day changes or provide proper SCD Type 2 structure (valid_from/valid_to). This approach doesn't scale—you'd need to modify SQL every day. Snapshots automatically handle new snapshots, change detection, and metadata columns without manual intervention.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt snapshots implement SCD Type 2 by capturing table states over time. Key concepts: Snapshot tracks source table changes on each dbt snapshot run, new records are added with dbt_valid_from set to snapshot time, changed records: old version gets dbt_valid_to set to snapshot time, new version gets inserted with current dbt_valid_from, unchanged records remain as-is. Configuration: unique_key (identifies records across snapshots, usually primary key), strategy (timestamp or check), updated_at (for timestamp strategy), check_cols (for check strategy). Snapshot strategies: 1) timestamp: Uses updated_at column—efficient, requires source has update timestamp. 2) check: Compares specified columns or all columns—works without timestamp, more compute intensive. Best practices: Run snapshots on regular schedule (daily/hourly), snapshot source tables before any transformations, use timestamp strategy when possible (faster), store snapshots in dedicated schema, avoid changing snapshot config (breaks history tracking), and test snapshots (unique key + not null on unique_key). Query current state: SELECT * FROM snapshot WHERE dbt_valid_to IS NULL. Query historical state: SELECT * FROM snapshot WHERE '2024-01-01' BETWEEN dbt_valid_from AND COALESCE(dbt_valid_to, '9999-12-31'). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/snapshots

Question: You receive an error 'Syntax error: Expected end of input but got keyword SELECT'. What is the most likely cause?
Q_1: The model is using an unsupported materialization strategy
Q_2: There's a missing semicolon at the end of the SQL statement
Q_3: There's a duplicate SELECT statement or a Jinja templating error causing SQL to be generated incorrectly
Q_4: The model is trying to create a table that already exists
Q_5: The model references a source that doesn't exist
E_1: Incorrect. Materialization strategies don't cause SQL syntax errors—they control how dbt creates database objects. Syntax errors come from malformed SQL in the model's SELECT statement. Invalid materializations cause different errors (unknown materialization type), not SQL syntax errors in parsing.
E_2: Incorrect. dbt models shouldn't have semicolons at the end—they're SELECT statements, not complete SQL scripts. Adding a semicolon might actually cause errors in some adapters. The error 'Expected end of input but got SELECT' suggests extra SQL after where the query should end, not a missing semicolon.
E_3: Correct. This error typically occurs when: 1) Jinja templating generates duplicate SELECT statements: {% for table in tables %}SELECT * FROM {{ table }}{% endfor %} without UNION. 2) Unclosed Jinja blocks causing model SQL to appear twice. 3) Accidentally including a SELECT statement in a macro call that generates another SELECT. 4) Using {{ ref() }} outside of FROM clause, causing SQL fragmentation. Debug: Run dbt compile and review target/compiled/[project]/models/[model].sql. Look for duplicate SELECT keywords, check Jinja syntax ({% if/for/set %} must have matching {% endif/endfor %}), verify macro outputs. Common issue: {{ ref('model') }} instead of FROM {{ ref('model') }} creates floating table references. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile
E_4: Incorrect. Duplicate table errors produce 'Relation already exists' messages, not SQL syntax errors. Syntax errors are about malformed SQL (duplicate keywords, incorrect structure), not database state. If a table exists, dbt typically uses CREATE OR REPLACE or handles the conflict, not fail with syntax errors.
E_5: Incorrect. Missing sources cause 'Source not found' or 'Relation does not exist' errors, not SQL syntax errors about unexpected SELECT keywords. Syntax errors are about SQL structure being malformed, not about missing database objects. Reference resolution errors are separate from syntax errors.
Explanation: OVERALL: SQL syntax errors in dbt models usually stem from Jinja templating issues or SQL structure problems. Common causes: 1) Duplicate SELECT from Jinja loops: {% for source in sources %}SELECT * FROM {{ source('raw', source) }}{% endfor %}. Without UNION/UNION ALL between iterations, generates: SELECT * FROM table1 SELECT * FROM table2 (syntax error). Fix: {% for source in sources %}SELECT * FROM {{ source('raw', source) }}{% if not loop.last %}UNION ALL{% endif %}{% endfor %}. 2) Unclosed Jinja blocks: {% if condition %}SELECT ...—missing {% endif %} causes Jinja to include subsequent model code incorrectly. 3) Misplaced ref/source: {{ ref('orders') }} instead of FROM {{ ref('orders') }}. Generates bare table name without FROM. 4) Accidentally nested SELECTs: Macro returning SELECT used inside another SELECT without proper subquery syntax. 5) Commented-out SQL with Jinja: {# SELECT * FROM table #} doesn't hide the SELECT from SQL parser if Jinja is malformed. Debugging workflow: 1) Run dbt compile --select failing_model. 2) Open target/compiled/[project]/models/failing_model.sql. 3) Look for: Duplicate keywords (SELECT SELECT, FROM FROM), missing UNION between query blocks, bare table names without FROM, malformed subqueries. 4) Review Jinja in original model: Check for unclosed {% %}. Verify loop syntax. Test macros independently. 5) Simplify: Remove Jinja temporarily to isolate issue, test SQL directly in warehouse, add Jinja back incrementally. Common fixes: Add UNION/UNION ALL in loops: {% if not loop.last %}UNION ALL{% endif %}. Close all Jinja blocks: Every {% if %} needs {% endif %}, every {% for %} needs {% endfor %}. Wrap ref() in FROM: FROM {{ ref('model') }}, not just {{ ref('model') }}. Use proper subquery syntax: (SELECT ... FROM {{ ref('model') }}) AS subquery. Prevention: Test Jinja logic separately (use dbt compile frequently). Follow dbt style guide for Jinja usage. Use CTEs to structure complex queries clearly. Comment Jinja for clarity: {# Loop over sources to union #}. Warehouse-specific errors: Some warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake) have different SQL syntax rules. Error messages vary. Always test in target warehouse. Use warehouse-appropriate SQL syntax. Jinja best practices: Keep Jinja simple and readable, test loops with {{ log() }} to see output, use macros for complex logic (easier to test), and validate Jinja output with dbt compile before running. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros

Question: What is the primary purpose of model access configurations in dbt (private, protected, public)?
Q_1: To control which models can be referenced by other models in the same or different projects
Q_2: To define database-level permissions for who can query the models
Q_3: To specify which users in dbt Cloud can edit the model files
Q_4: To optimize query performance by caching frequently accessed models
Q_5: To automatically generate documentation for public-facing models only
E_1: Correct. Model access levels define governance boundaries for model references. Configuration in schema.yml or model file config: access: private | protected | public. Private: Only models in same directory/subdirectories can ref(). Protected: Only models in same project can ref(). Public (default): Any project can ref(), including external projects via packages. This enables governance by preventing unintended dependencies. Example: mark staging models as protected to prevent external projects from bypassing your marts and referencing raw staging data. Enforcement happens at compile time—invalid refs cause errors. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_2: Incorrect. Model access configurations don't control database permissions or SQL grants. They govern dbt-level references (ref() function calls) within dbt projects. Database permissions are managed separately via post-hooks with GRANT statements or database-native access controls. Access levels are about project structure governance, not database security.
E_3: Incorrect. Model access doesn't control dbt Cloud user permissions or file editing rights. That's managed through dbt Cloud's role-based access control (RBAC) and environment permissions. Access levels govern how models reference each other in code, not who can edit them.
E_4: Incorrect. Access configurations don't affect query performance or caching. They're purely for governance—controlling which models can be referenced. Performance is controlled by materialization strategies (table, view, incremental), not access levels. Access and performance are separate concerns.
E_5: Incorrect. All models appear in generated documentation regardless of access level. dbt docs generate includes private, protected, and public models equally. Access levels affect ref() usage, not documentation visibility. If you want to exclude models from docs, you'd need to manually filter the manifest.json, which isn't a standard practice.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model access levels (introduced in dbt v1.5) provide governance for multi-project and multi-team environments. Three levels: 1) private: Only models in the same directory and subdirectories can ref() this model. Use for internal implementation details. 2) protected (default for most models): Only models in the same project can ref(). External projects (packages) cannot. Use for models you don't want external teams depending on. 3) public: Any model in any project can ref(). Required for models in dbt packages that external projects will use. Configuration: models: - name: stg_orders, access: protected or {{ config(access='protected') }} in model file. Benefits: Prevent external dependencies on internal models (e.g., staging shouldn't be referenced by other projects—only marts), enforce architectural boundaries (keep implementation details private), enable safe refactoring (private models can change without breaking downstream projects), and document intended interfaces (public models are your API). Access is enforced at compile time—violations cause clear error messages. Best practices: Mark staging and intermediate models as protected, mark marts intended for reuse as public, use private for complex multi-step transformations within one directory. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access

Question: What is the purpose of the --defer flag in dbt?
Q_1: To delay model execution until a specific time or trigger event
Q_2: To skip models that have already been successfully built
Q_3: To automatically retry failed models after a specified interval
Q_4: To use production data for upstream dependencies instead of rebuilding them in development environments
Q_5: To defer database writes to a separate transaction for better performance
E_1: Incorrect. --defer doesn't schedule or delay execution. It's about environment resolution—using production data for upstream dependencies instead of rebuilding them in development. Scheduling is handled by orchestration tools (Airflow, dbt Cloud scheduler), not the --defer flag.
E_2: Incorrect. --defer doesn't skip models—it changes where upstream dependencies are read from (production instead of dev). All selected models still run; they just query production relations for dependencies not being rebuilt. To skip already-built models, you'd need state-based selection or custom logic.
E_3: Incorrect. --defer has no retry functionality. It's for environment resolution. Retry logic for failed models must be implemented in orchestration tools (Airflow retry policies, dbt Cloud job retries). dbt itself doesn't automatically retry failures with --defer or any flag.
E_4: Correct. The --defer flag allows development/CI runs to reference production tables for upstream models not being rebuilt. Usage: dbt run --select my_model --defer --state ./prod-artifacts. Behavior: If my_model depends on upstream_model and upstream_model isn't in the selection, dbt reads from production's upstream_model instead of requiring it in dev. Benefits: Faster dev builds (don't rebuild entire DAG), smaller dev environments (only build what you're changing), cost savings (less compute), and realistic testing (use production data for context). Setup requires --state pointing to production manifest.json. Example: Editing model D in a chain A→B→C→D: Without defer: Must build A, B, C, D in dev. With defer: dbt run --select D --defer reads A, B, C from production, only builds D in dev. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/defer
E_5: Incorrect. --defer doesn't affect database transactions or write patterns. It's about reference resolution (which database objects to read from), not how writes are executed. Database transaction handling is controlled by the warehouse and dbt's adapter, not the --defer flag.
Explanation: OVERALL: The --defer flag enables efficient development and CI workflows by allowing dbt to use production data for upstream dependencies. Configuration: dbt run --select my_model+ --defer --state ./prod-artifacts. Requirements: --state flag with path to production manifest.json, production database accessible (same credentials or read access). How it works: 1) dbt identifies models to run (via --select). 2) For dependencies NOT in selection: Check if they exist in state manifest. If yes, use production relation instead of dev. 3) Selected models read from production upstream, write to dev downstream. Use cases: Development (dev builds without rebuilding entire project), CI/CD (test only changed models: dbt build --select state:modified+ --defer), and cost optimization (reduce warehouse usage in dev/CI). Example scenario: Project DAG: stg_orders → fct_orders → dashboard. Editing dashboard: Without defer: dbt run --select dashboard requires building stg_orders and fct_orders first in dev. With defer: dbt run --select dashboard --defer --state ./prod reads stg_orders and fct_orders from production, only builds dashboard in dev. Benefits: 10x+ faster dev/CI builds, significantly reduced dev warehouse costs, realistic data (production context for testing), and isolation (change only what you're working on). Configuration in profiles.yml (optional default state): outputs: dev: threads: 4, defer_to: prod. Best practices: Always use with --state pointing to recent production manifest, combine with state:modified+ for CI pipelines, ensure dev environment can read production (permissions), and validate defer behavior: Check compiled SQL (should reference production schemas for deferred models). Limitations: Requires production data accessibility, state manifest must be recent (stale manifest can cause issues), and mixed data sources (dev + prod) may cause inconsistencies in testing. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/defer

Question: [SELECT 2] Which of the following are valid reasons to use incremental materialization? (Select TWO)
Q_1: To ensure data is always real-time and reflects the most current source state
Q_2: To avoid creating any database objects and save storage space
Q_3: To reduce build time for large tables by processing only new or changed records
Q_4: To handle large datasets where full table rebuilds are too slow or expensive
Q_5: To automatically partition tables for better query performance
Q_6: To enable concurrent writes from multiple dbt processes
E_1: Incorrect. Incremental models don't provide real-time data—they process data periodically when dbt runs. For real-time data, you'd query views directly or use streaming solutions. Incremental models trade some data freshness for performance on large datasets.
E_2: Incorrect. Incremental models create tables just like table materialization—they don't save storage. They optimize build time by processing only new data, not storage space. For no database objects, use ephemeral materialization.
E_3: Correct. This is the primary use case for incremental models. Instead of rebuilding millions of historical rows each run, process only new records since last run. For append-only event data (logs, transactions, events) this dramatically reduces build time from hours to minutes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_4: Correct. When tables grow to millions/billions of rows, full rebuilds become impractical—too much warehouse compute time and cost. Incremental models make large-scale analytics feasible by updating only changed data. Common for fact tables, event logs, and historical data warehouses. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_5: Incorrect. Incremental materialization doesn't automatically create partitions—that's a warehouse-specific feature configured separately. While incremental models can benefit from partitioning, the materialization itself doesn't create partitions.
E_6: Incorrect. Incremental models don't enable concurrent writes—dbt runs should not overlap. Concurrent dbt runs could cause conflicts. Incremental is for processing new data efficiently in sequential runs, not parallel execution.
Explanation: OVERALL: Incremental materialization optimizes performance for large, growing datasets. Use when: (1) Tables are too large for efficient full rebuilds (millions+ rows), (2) Data is primarily append-only (events, logs, transactions), (3) Build time or compute cost is prohibitive for full refreshes, (4) Data freshness requirements allow periodic updates rather than real-time. Trade-offs: increased complexity (managing unique_key, is_incremental() logic, handling late-arriving data), potential for state issues (run --full-refresh periodically), more difficult debugging. Not suitable for: small tables, frequently updated historical data, complex aggregations requiring full dataset access. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models

Question: What is the purpose of model versioning in dbt?
Q_1: To automatically track all changes to model files in version control
Q_2: To store different versions of query results for historical comparison
Q_3: To support multiple versions of a model simultaneously when making breaking schema changes
Q_4: To enable rollback to previous model states when builds fail
Q_5: To assign version numbers to dbt packages for dependency management
E_1: Incorrect. Model versioning is a dbt feature for managing breaking changes to model schemas, not Git version control. Git tracks all file changes automatically. dbt model versioning (v1.5+) allows multiple versions of a model to coexist, enabling graceful migration for downstream consumers when making breaking changes (schema modifications). It's about backward compatibility, not file history.
E_2: Incorrect. Model versioning doesn't store query results or data—it manages model definitions. For historical data tracking, use snapshots (SCD Type 2) or incremental models. Versioning is about code/schema versions (v1, v2 of a model), allowing different downstream models to reference different versions during migration.
E_3: Correct. Model versioning (dbt v1.5+) enables backward-compatible schema changes by allowing multiple model versions to coexist. Use case: Your dim_customers model needs a breaking change (remove column, change data type). Without versioning: All downstream models break immediately. With versioning: Create dim_customers v2 with new schema, keep dim_customers v1 active, migrate downstream models incrementally (some ref v1, others ref v2), deprecate v1 after all consumers migrated. Configuration: models: - name: dim_customers, latest_version: 2, versions: - v: 1, - v: 2. File structure: dim_customers_v1.sql, dim_customers_v2.sql. Reference: {{ ref('dim_customers', v='1') }} or {{ ref('dim_customers') }} (uses latest). Benefits: Graceful migrations, no breaking changes for consumers, and time to update dependencies. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-versions
E_4: Incorrect. Model versioning isn't a rollback mechanism for failed builds. Database rollback (if needed) is handled by transactions or backup/restore processes. Versioning is for managing concurrent versions during schema migrations. For build failure recovery, use orchestration retries or manual database restoration.
E_5: Incorrect. Package versioning (in packages.yml: version: 1.1.1) is separate from model versioning. Package versions manage external dependencies. Model versioning manages multiple active versions of models within a single project for schema migration. Different concepts with similar names but distinct purposes.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model versioning enables schema evolution without breaking downstream dependencies. Workflow: 1) Identify breaking change needed (remove customer_email column from dim_customers). 2) Create new version: dim_customers_v2.sql with updated schema. 3) Configure versions in schema.yml: models: - name: dim_customers, latest_version: 2, versions: - v: 1, columns: [...v1 schema...], - v: 2, columns: [...v2 schema...]. 4) Keep v1 active: dim_customers_v1.sql unchanged. 5) Migrate consumers: Update downstream models to ref('dim_customers', v='2') or ref('dim_customers') (latest). 6) Deprecate v1: After all consumers migrated, remove v1 files and configuration. Referencing versions: {{ ref('dim_customers') }}: Uses latest_version (v2). {{ ref('dim_customers', v='1') }}: Explicitly uses v1. {{ ref('dim_customers', v='2') }}: Explicitly uses v2. Database objects: v1 creates dim_customers_v1 table/view, v2 creates dim_customers_v2. Both coexist during migration. Use cases: Breaking schema changes (column removal, rename, type change), API evolution for shared models (multi-team projects), gradual migration (time for downstream teams to adapt), and deprecation path (communicate version sunset timelines). Best practices: Use for public models (access: public) with external consumers, document breaking changes in version descriptions, communicate deprecation timelines, combine with model contracts for schema enforcement, and monitor version usage before deprecating old versions. Configuration options: deprecation_date (in versions:): Set sunset date for old versions. include/exclude: Control which versions are built. Comparison with snapshots: Snapshots track data changes over time (SCD Type 2), versioning tracks schema changes over time (multiple code versions). Different purposes, both valuable. Limitations: Requires dbt v1.5+, adds complexity (multiple files to maintain), and requires consumer coordination (teams must migrate). Only use for true breaking changes—additive changes (new columns) don't need versioning. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-versions

Question: What is the primary advantage of using generic tests over singular tests in dbt?
Q_1: Generic tests can be reused across multiple columns and models with simple configuration
Q_2: Generic tests automatically fix data quality issues when they fail
Q_3: Generic tests run faster than singular tests due to query optimization
Q_4: Generic tests can only be applied to models, while singular tests work on sources
Q_5: Generic tests are automatically generated by dbt without manual configuration
E_1: Correct. Generic tests are parameterized, reusable tests defined once and applied many times via YAML configuration. Built-in generic tests (unique, not_null, relationships, accepted_values) can be applied to any column: columns: - name: order_id, tests: [unique, not_null], - name: status, tests: [{accepted_values: {values: ['pending', 'shipped']}}].
