Custom generic tests extend this: create tests/generic/positive_values.sql: {% test positive_values(model, column_name) %} SELECT * FROM {{ model }} WHERE {{ column_name }} <= 0 {% endtest %}. Apply: tests: - positive_values: {column_name: revenue}. Benefits: Write once, use everywhere, maintain single implementation, and declare in YAML (no SQL files per test). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#generic-data-tests
E_2: Incorrect. Neither generic nor singular tests fix data—both are read-only validations that query models and report failures. Tests identify problems but don't modify data. Developers must update model logic to fix root causes. This is true for all test types—tests validate, they don't remediate.
E_3: Incorrect. Generic and singular tests both compile to SQL SELECT statements executed against the database. Performance depends on the SQL logic and data volume, not test type. A poorly written generic test can be slower than a well-optimized singular test. Test type doesn't inherently affect performance—query efficiency does.
E_4: Incorrect. Both generic and singular tests can validate models and sources. Generic tests apply to source columns via schema.yml: sources: - name: raw, tables: - name: orders, columns: - name: id, tests: [unique, not_null]. Singular tests can query sources using {{ source() }}. Test applicability isn't restricted by test type.
E_5: Incorrect. Generic tests require explicit configuration in schema.yml or properties.yml. You must declare which tests apply to which columns. dbt doesn't automatically infer or generate tests—developers must define them. Auto-testing would be unreliable (how would dbt know if a column should be unique?).
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt supports two test types with distinct purposes: Generic tests: Reusable, parameterized tests applied via YAML configuration. Built-in: unique, not_null, relationships, accepted_values. Custom: Create in tests/generic/ for project-specific reusable tests. Benefits: DRY (no duplicate code), easy to apply (YAML configuration), scalable (apply to many columns easily), and maintainable (update once, affects all uses). Use for: Common validations (primary keys, foreign keys, null checks), reusable business rules, and standard data quality checks. Singular tests: Custom SQL queries for specific validations. Created in tests/ directory as .sql files. Return failing records (zero rows = pass). Benefits: Flexibility (any SQL logic), context-specific (tailored to exact requirements), and complexity (multi-model, complex business rules). Use for: Complex business logic not fitting generic patterns, one-off validations, and multi-table assertions. Best practices: Prefer generic tests when possible (leverage reusability), create custom generic tests for repeated custom logic, use singular tests for truly unique validations, and combine both—use generic for standard checks, singular for special cases. Example mixed approach: columns: - name: order_id, tests: [unique, not_null] (generic), Create tests/assert_order_revenue_positive.sql (singular). The goal: comprehensive coverage with minimal code duplication. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: What is the purpose of seeds in dbt?
Q_1: To automatically generate sample data for testing models
Q_2: To initialize the database schema for dbt models
Q_3: To load small CSV files into the database as tables for use in models
Q_4: To create backup copies of source data for disaster recovery
Q_5: To automatically synchronize external API data with the warehouse
E_1: Incorrect. Seeds don't generate data—they load existing CSV files into the database. For test data generation, you'd create CSVs manually or use tools/scripts to generate them, then use seeds to load. Seeds are a loading mechanism, not a data generation tool.
E_2: Incorrect. Database schemas are created by model materialization (tables/views) and configured in dbt_project.yml or profiles.yml. Seeds don't create schemas—they load data into existing or auto-created schemas. Schema initialization is handled by dbt's schema configuration, not seeds.
E_3: Correct. Seeds load CSV files from seeds/ directory into database tables. Command: dbt seed. Use cases: 1) Reference data: Country codes, state abbreviations, status enums (small, rarely changing lookup tables). 2) Mappings: Category hierarchies, product classifications. 3) Configuration: Business rules, thresholds (stored as data). 4) Test fixtures: Small datasets for development/testing. Example: seeds/country_codes.csv with columns: country_code, country_name. After dbt seed, query as: {{ ref('country_codes') }}. Limitations: Only for small files (< 1MB, hundreds/thousands of rows). For large data, use proper ETL tools. Seeds rebuild fully on each run (not incremental). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/seeds
E_4: Incorrect. Seeds aren't for backups—they're for loading small reference datasets. Backups are handled by database-native tools (snapshots, exports, replication). Seeds load CSVs into the warehouse for use in transformations. Don't use seeds for backup strategies; use proper backup tools.
E_5: Incorrect. Seeds load static CSV files from the project, not live API data. For API synchronization, use ELT tools (Fivetran, Airbyte) or custom extraction scripts. Seeds are for version-controlled, static reference data, not dynamic API integration.
Explanation:
OVERALL: Seeds provide a simple way to load small, version-controlled reference data into the warehouse. Workflow: 1) Create CSV in seeds/ directory: seeds/status_mappings.csv: status_code,status_name,active
1,Pending,true
2,Complete,true
3,Cancelled,false. 2) Run dbt seed to load into database. Creates table in target schema: analytics.status_mappings. 3) Reference in models: SELECT orders.*, sm.status_name FROM {{ ref('orders') }} o JOIN {{ ref('status_mappings') }} sm ON o.status_code = sm.status_code. Configuration in dbt_project.yml: seeds: +schema: reference (load to separate schema). status_mappings: +column_types: {status_code: integer} (override inferred types). Use cases: Reference/lookup tables: Country codes, currency mappings, zip code data (small, stable datasets). Enums: Status codes, category types, priority levels. Mappings: Business unit hierarchies, account classifications. Test data: Small datasets for development (create realistic test scenarios). Business configuration: Thresholds, rules, parameters (versioned with code). Benefits: Version control: CSVs in Git (track changes, review, rollback). Simple: No ETL tool needed for small reference data. Portable: CSVs work across all adapters. Testable: Can test seeds like models (unique, not_null on seed tables). Limitations: Size: Only for small files (dbt recommends < 1 MB). Seeds are loaded row-by-row (slow for large data). Performance: Full refresh every run (no incremental). Not for large datasets. Type inference: dbt infers types from CSV (may need column_types overrides). No transformations: Seeds load raw CSV (use models for transformations). Best practices: Keep small: Use for hundreds/thousands of rows, not millions. Version control CSVs: Treat as code (review changes, document updates). Use for reference data: Stable, slowly changing data. Not for facts/events. Configure types explicitly: Avoid type inference issues. seeds: country_codes: +column_types: {code: varchar(3)}. Test seeds: Add tests in schema.yml: seeds: - name: country_codes, columns: - name: code, tests: [unique, not_null]. Alternatives for large data: Use proper ELT/ETL tools (Fivetran, Airbyte), database native imports (COPY, LOAD DATA), staging models reading from file storage (S3, GCS). Commands: dbt seed: Load all seeds. dbt seed --select seed_name: Load specific seed. dbt seed --full-refresh: Rebuild seed tables from scratch. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/seeds

Question: You want to add a detailed description about data lineage and transformation logic to a model's documentation. Where should this description be placed?
Q_1: In the model's entry in schema.yml using the description property
Q_2: As SQL comments at the top of the model's .sql file
Q_3: In the dbt_project.yml file under the models section
Q_4: In a README.md file in the models directory
Q_5: In the model's configuration block using {{ config(description='...') }}
E_1: Correct. Model-level descriptions belong in schema.yml (or properties.yml) files using the description property under models:. Example: models: - name: fct_orders description: | This model aggregates order data from staging.stg_orders... Describes business logic, transformations, data lineage, and usage guidelines. Use YAML's | for multi-line descriptions. This appears in the generated documentation site under the model's overview. For extensive documentation, reference doc blocks: description: '{{ doc("orders_description") }}'. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/description
E_2: Incorrect. SQL comments (-- or /* */) in model files are not parsed or included in generated documentation. dbt only includes descriptions explicitly defined in schema.yml files. While SQL comments are good for code maintainability, they won't appear in dbt docs. Always use YAML description fields for user-facing documentation.
E_3: Incorrect. The dbt_project.yml file is for configuration (materialization, schema, tags), not documentation. While you can set default configs for models here, descriptions must be in schema.yml files. Mixing configuration and documentation would make dbt_project.yml unwieldy and violate separation of concerns.
E_4: Incorrect. README.md files in the repository are for developer documentation and don't automatically appear in dbt's generated documentation site. While useful for explaining directory structure or coding conventions, they're separate from dbt docs. Use schema.yml for documentation that appears on the dbt docs site.
E_5: Incorrect. The config() macro is for model configurations like materialization, schema, and tags—not for documentation. Descriptions don't belong in config blocks. Example: {{ config(materialized='table') }} is valid, but {{ config(description='...') }} is not supported. Always use schema.yml for descriptions.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model documentation in dbt is defined in YAML files (schema.yml or properties.yml) under the models: key. Structure: models: - name: model_name, description: 'Detailed description...', columns: [...]. Descriptions support Markdown formatting for rich documentation including headers, lists, links, and code blocks. For long descriptions, use doc blocks: create a .md file in docs/ directory, then reference with description: '{{ doc("block_name") }}'. This promotes reusability and keeps YAML files clean. Model descriptions should explain: business purpose, data sources (lineage), transformation logic, refresh cadence, and known limitations. Good documentation is crucial for discoverability and trust in data products. Run dbt docs generate to build docs, then dbt docs serve to view locally. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation

Question: What is the purpose of using the where configuration in a dbt test?
Q_1: To specify which database schema the test should query
Q_2: To filter which rows are included in the test validation
Q_3: To determine when the test should run based on time or schedule
Q_4: To specify the location of custom test definitions
Q_5: To configure which environments the test runs in
E_1: Incorrect. The where config doesn't control schema targeting—that's determined by the model's schema configuration and profiles.yml. where adds a SQL WHERE clause to filter which rows the test validates. Schema targeting is separate from test filtering logic.
E_2: Correct. The where config adds a WHERE clause to the test's generated SQL, filtering which records are validated. Configuration: columns: - name: customer_id, tests: - unique: {where: "status != 'deleted'"}, - not_null: {where: "created_at >= '2024-01-01'"}. Generated SQL adds the filter: SELECT customer_id FROM customers WHERE status != 'deleted' for uniqueness check. Use cases: Exclude soft-deleted records from validation, test only recent data (performance), apply business rules (validate only active accounts), and filter test scope (ignore legacy data). Benefits: Performance (test subset of data), business logic (validate meaningful records only), and flexibility (same test, different scopes). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/where
E_3: Incorrect. where doesn't control test scheduling or timing—that's managed by orchestration tools (Airflow, dbt Cloud schedules). where is a SQL filter for which rows to validate. Test execution timing is separate from data filtering. For conditional running, use enabled config.
E_4: Incorrect. Test definitions are located in tests/ directory (singular) or tests/generic/ (custom generic). where doesn't control file locations—it's a SQL filter applied to test queries. File organization is handled by project structure, not test configuration.
E_5: Incorrect. Environment-specific test execution uses enabled config with target.name conditions, not where. where filters data rows for validation; enabled controls whether the test runs at all. For environment control: enabled: "{{ target.name == 'prod' }}", not where.
Explanation: OVERALL: The where configuration enables filtered testing by adding SQL WHERE clauses to test queries. Configuration examples: 1) Generic tests in schema.yml: tests: - unique: {where: "is_deleted = false"}, - not_null: {where: "order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'"}, - relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'id', where: "status = 'active'"}. 2) Singular tests: Not applicable—add WHERE directly in SQL. Use cases: Exclude soft-deleted records: where: "deleted_at IS NULL" or where: "is_active = true". Tests only apply to active records. Time-based filtering: where: "created_at >= '2024-01-01'". Validate recent data for performance. Business rule scopes: where: "country = 'US'". Test different rules for different segments. Incremental testing: where: "event_date >= CURRENT_DATE - 7". Test only recent incremental data. How it works: dbt generates test SQL with WHERE appended. Example unique test: Without where: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM customers GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1. With where: "status = 'active'": SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM customers WHERE status = 'active' GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1. Benefits: Performance: Test only relevant subset (faster, cheaper). Precision: Validate specific business scenarios. Flexibility: Different where for same test on different columns. Maintenance: Change filter without rewriting test logic. Advanced patterns: Combine with severity: tests: - unique: {where: "is_active = true", severity: error}, - unique: {where: "is_active = false", severity: warn}. Environment-specific where: where: "{{ 'is_deleted = false' if target.name == 'prod' else '1=1' }}". Variable-based: where: "country = '{{ var('test_country', 'US') }}'". Best practices: Document why filters exist (comment in YAML), ensure filters align with business logic, test without filters periodically (verify filter relevance), and avoid overly complex WHERE clauses (keep tests simple). Comparison with other configs: where: Filters rows for validation. enabled: Controls if test runs at all. severity: Determines error vs warning. store_failures: Saves failing records. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/where

Question: What is the purpose of dbt's groups feature?
Q_1: To organize models into folders in the dbt project structure
Q_2: To automatically create database schemas for different model categories
Q_3: To define ownership boundaries and control model dependencies across teams
Q_4: To group test results for better reporting in dbt Cloud
Q_5: To enable parallel execution of models within the same group
E_1: Incorrect. Folder organization is done through directory structure in models/, not the groups feature. Groups are a governance feature that defines logical ownership boundaries for models, independent of file locations. You can have models in the same folder belonging to different groups, or models in different folders belonging to the same group.
E_2: Incorrect. Database schemas are controlled by the schema configuration in dbt_project.yml or model configs, not groups. Groups define ownership and access boundaries for governance, not database object organization. Schema creation is a separate concern from logical grouping.
E_3: Correct. Groups (dbt v1.5+) define logical ownership units for governance. Configuration in schema.yml: groups: - name: finance, owner: {email: finance-team@company.com}. Assign models to groups: models: - name: fct_revenue, group: finance, access: protected. Benefits: 1) Ownership clarity: Each model has a defined owner/team. 2) Dependency governance: Control cross-group dependencies with access levels (private/protected/public). 3) Team boundaries: Prevent unintended dependencies between team areas. Example: Finance group models are protected—only finance team models can ref() them. Marketing group must use public interface models. Combines with access configuration for enforcement. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_4: Incorrect. While groups might appear in reporting, their primary purpose is governance and ownership, not test result organization. Test reporting is based on model structure, tags, and execution results. Groups are about defining ownership boundaries and access control, not organizing test outputs.
E_5: Incorrect. Parallel execution is controlled by --threads flag and DAG dependencies (ref/source relationships), not groups. Models in the same group don't get special execution treatment. Threading is performance-related; groups are governance-related. These are independent concerns.
Explanation: OVERALL: Groups provide team-based governance for multi-team dbt projects. Configuration: 1) Define groups in schema.yml: groups: - name: finance, owner: {email: finance@company.com, name: Finance Team}, - name: marketing, owner: {email: marketing@company.com}. 2) Assign models to groups: models: - name: fct_revenue, group: finance, access: protected. 3) Enforce access: Finance models with access: protected can only be ref'd by other finance models. Public models (access: public) can be ref'd by any group. Governance model: Group defines ownership (who maintains models). access controls visibility (who can depend on models). Combination enables: Team autonomy (own their models), clear interfaces (public models are team's API), and dependency control (prevent unintended cross-team coupling). Use cases: Multi-team projects: Finance, marketing, operations teams each own model groups. Domain-driven design: Customer, product, order domains as separate groups. Organizational boundaries: Internal analytics vs external reporting. Access levels by group: private: Only models in same subdirectory (strictest, within-group implementation details). protected: Only models in same group (team boundary). public: Any group can reference (public interfaces/contracts). Example structure: Finance group: fct_revenue (access: public, consumed by other teams), int_revenue_calc (access: private, internal calculation), dim_accounts (access: protected, for finance team only). Marketing group: fct_campaigns (access: public), int_campaign_metrics (access: private). Benefits: Prevent accidental dependencies (can't ref() protected models from other groups), document ownership (every model has an owner), enable safe refactoring (change private models without breaking other teams), and scale organization (teams work independently). Best practices: Align groups with organizational structure (teams/domains), use public for stable interfaces other teams depend on, keep most models protected (limit cross-group dependencies), document group responsibilities, and combine with model contracts for schema guarantees. Configuration validation: dbt enforces access rules at compile time. Invalid ref() to protected model from wrong group causes error. Clear error messages guide proper usage. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access

Question: [SELECT 4] When receiving a 'Compilation Error: dbt found two resources with the name X', which are the likely causes? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Two model files with the same name exist in different subdirectories of models/
Q_2: A model and a source have the same name
Q_3: The model uses an incremental materialization strategy
Q_4: A model and a test have the same name in their file names
Q_5: The dbt_project.yml has conflicting configuration that duplicates the model name
Q_6: A model file exists with the same name in both the current project and an installed package
E_1: Correct. dbt requires unique model names across the entire project, regardless of directory location. Example: models/staging/orders.sql and models/marts/orders.sql both create a model named 'orders', causing a collision. dbt uses filename (without .sql) as model name. Solution: Rename one model (e.g., stg_orders.sql and fct_orders.sql) or use folder prefixes. Model names must be globally unique within a project. Subdirectories organize files but don't create namespaces. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects
E_2: Correct. Models and sources share the same namespace for names. If you have: source named 'orders' in sources.yml and model named 'orders.sql', dbt can't distinguish them in ref() calls. Solution: Use naming conventions—prefix models (stg_orders, fct_orders) and keep source names matching raw tables. When calling: {{ source('source_name', 'table_name') }} is always a source, {{ ref('model_name') }} is a model. Avoid naming collisions by using prefixes for models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources
E_3: Incorrect. Incremental materialization doesn't cause name conflicts. This error is about duplicate resource names (two models, model + source, etc. with the same name), not materialization strategy. Incremental models can have the same name issues as any other model, but the materialization itself doesn't cause them.
E_4: Incorrect. Models and tests are different resource types and can have the same base name without conflicts. Example: models/orders.sql (model) and tests/orders.sql (singular test) coexist fine. Tests are called by name in schema.yml or run via dbt test, not ref(), so there's no ambiguity. This isn't a common cause of name collisions.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt_project.yml configuration doesn't create duplicate model resources—it configures existing ones. Name conflicts arise from multiple .sql files or sources with the same name, not from YAML configuration. dbt_project.yml can set aliases (how models appear in database) but doesn't create name conflicts in dbt's namespace.
E_6: Incorrect. While this could theoretically cause issues, models in packages and your project can have the same name—they're distinguished by package name. Reference package models: {{ ref('package_name', 'model_name') }}. The error 'found two resources with name X' typically occurs within a single namespace (your project or a package), not across project and packages. Package models are namespaced separately.
Explanation: OVERALL: Name uniqueness errors occur when dbt finds multiple resources with identical names. dbt requires unique names within resource type namespaces. Key namespaces: Models (all .sql files in models/), Sources (defined in sources.yml), Tests (singular tests in tests/, generic test instances), Macros (in macros/), Seeds (in seeds/). Resources must be uniquely identifiable for ref(), source(), and other references. Common conflict scenarios: 1) Duplicate model names in different directories: models/staging/orders.sql + models/marts/orders.sql. Solution: Use prefixes (stg_orders, fct_orders). 2) Model and source with same name: source: orders + model: orders.sql. Solution: Prefix models, keep sources matching raw table names. 3) Singular test files with duplicate names (less common, but tests should have unique names too). Debugging: Error message shows resource name and both file locations. Review project structure for duplicate filenames (find models/ -name 'X.sql'). Check sources.yml for name collisions with models. Run dbt list to see all resources and identify duplicates. Solutions: Rename models with prefixes (by layer: stg_, int_, fct_, dim_). Use dbt's recommended naming conventions. For packages: Use two-argument ref() for package models: {{ ref('package_name', 'model_name') }}—but this doesn't apply to within-project duplicates. Best practices: Establish naming conventions (staging: stg_, intermediate: int_, facts: fct_, dimensions: dim_). Enforce in code reviews. Use dbt list --resource-type model to audit names. Organize by layer/domain but rely on names, not paths, for uniqueness. Tools: dbt parse (checks project structure without running), dbt list (shows all resources), and linters/pre-commit hooks to catch duplicates. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are valid dbt model materialization strategies? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: table
Q_2: stream
Q_3: incremental
Q_4: view
Q_5: ephemeral
Q_6: cached
E_1: Correct. Table materialization creates database tables that persist data physically. Configuration: {{ config(materialized='table') }}. Behavior: On dbt run, dbt drops and recreates the table (or uses CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE if supported). Best for: Large datasets queried frequently (fast query performance), complex transformations (results cached), and models with few updates (rebuilding entire table is acceptable). Tradeoffs: Longer build times (writes all rows), storage costs (full data stored), but fastest query performance. Use for marts and final output models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#table
E_2: Incorrect. There's no 'stream' materialization in dbt. For streaming data or real-time processing, use: incremental materialization (processes new records only), orchestration tools for continuous runs, or warehouse-native streaming features (Snowflake streams, BigQuery streaming inserts). dbt is primarily batch-oriented—streaming is handled at the data ingestion layer or with specialized tools.
E_3: Correct. Incremental materialization builds models by processing only new or changed data after initial full load. Configuration: {{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key='id') }}. Use is_incremental() macro for conditional logic: {% if is_incremental() %} WHERE updated_at > (SELECT MAX(updated_at) FROM {{ this }}) {% endif %}. Strategies: append (insert new rows), merge (upsert based on unique_key), delete+insert (replace matched records). Best for: Large fact tables with regular small updates, event logs, and time-series data. Requires careful design to handle late-arriving data and maintain correctness. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_4: Correct. View materialization creates database views that execute SQL on every query. Configuration: {{ config(materialized='view') }} or default in many projects. Behavior: dbt creates or replaces a view with the model's SQL. Best for: Lightweight transformations (minimal compute), frequently changing logic (no rebuild needed—view definition updates), and staging models (1:1 with sources, always fresh data). Tradeoffs: Slower queries (SQL executes on each query), no storage cost, always reflects current data. Most staging models use view materialization. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#view
E_5: Correct. Ephemeral materialization doesn't create database objects—models are compiled as CTEs in downstream models. Configuration: {{ config(materialized='ephemeral') }}. Best for: Simple intermediate transformations, logic used by 1-2 models only, and reducing database object clutter. Tradeoffs: Can't query directly, SQL duplicated in each downstream model (no caching). Use sparingly—prefer view for most intermediate models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations#ephemeral
E_6: Incorrect. There's no 'cached' materialization in dbt. All table-based materializations (table, incremental) inherently cache results by storing data. Views don't cache—they execute SQL on query. For caching behavior, choose table or incremental materialization. Some warehouses offer materialized views (Snowflake, BigQuery), but these are warehouse features, not dbt materializations.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt supports four core materialization strategies, each optimizing for different use cases: 1) table: Creates physical tables. Best for: Final marts, complex aggregations, frequently queried models. Rebuilds fully on each run. 2) view: Creates database views (virtual tables). Best for: Staging models, lightweight transformations, frequently changing logic. No storage, always fresh, slower queries. 3) incremental: Adds new/changed rows to existing table. Best for: Large fact tables, event logs, append-only data. Requires unique_key and is_incremental() logic. Faster builds, complex to maintain. 4) ephemeral: No database object, inlined as CTE. Best for: Simple intermediate logic, used by few models. Can't query directly, SQL duplicated. Configuration hierarchy: Model-level: {{ config(materialized='table') }} in .sql file. Directory-level: dbt_project.yml: models: marts: +materialized: table. Default: Usually view. Materialization selection decision tree: Data size large + queried frequently = table. Data size large + regular small updates = incremental. Lightweight transformation + always fresh = view. Simple intermediate logic + few dependencies = ephemeral. Advanced: Custom materializations (define in macros/), warehouse-specific options (Snowflake: materialized view via custom materialization), and adapter-specific features (BigQuery partitioning/clustering with table/incremental). Best practices: Use view for staging (fresh data, minimal storage), table for marts (performance), incremental for large facts (efficiency), and ephemeral sparingly (consider view instead). Test materialization changes in dev—switching can affect performance and cost significantly. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are valid built-in generic tests in dbt? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: accepted_dates
Q_2: not_null
Q_3: unique
Q_4: relationships
Q_5: accepted_values
Q_6: primary_key
E_1: Incorrect. There is no built-in accepted_dates test in dbt. You might be thinking of accepted_values, which validates that column values are in a predefined list. For date validation, you'd need custom tests or use packages like dbt_expectations (expect_column_values_to_be_between). The four built-in tests are: unique, not_null, relationships, and accepted_values.
E_2: Correct. not_null is one of dbt's four built-in generic tests. It verifies that a column contains no NULL values. Configuration in schema.yml: columns: - name: customer_id, tests: - not_null. The test generates SQL: SELECT * FROM table WHERE column IS NULL. If any rows are returned, the test fails. Use not_null for required fields like primary keys, foreign keys, and mandatory business fields. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/not_null
E_3: Correct. unique is one of dbt's four built-in generic tests. It ensures all values in a column are distinct—no duplicates. Configuration: columns: - name: order_id, tests: - unique. Generates SQL: SELECT column, COUNT(*) FROM table GROUP BY column HAVING COUNT(*) > 1. If any rows return, duplicates exist and the test fails. Essential for primary key validation. Combine with not_null for complete primary key testing. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/unique
E_4: Correct. relationships is one of dbt's four built-in generic tests. It validates referential integrity—ensuring foreign key values exist in a referenced table. Configuration: columns: - name: customer_id, tests: - relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'customer_id'}. Generates SQL checking for values in the child table not present in the parent table. Use for all foreign key validations to catch orphaned records. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/relationships
E_5: Correct. accepted_values is one of dbt's four built-in generic tests. It validates that column values are in a predefined list of acceptable values. Configuration: columns: - name: status, tests: - accepted_values: {values: ['pending', 'completed', 'cancelled']}. Generates SQL: SELECT * FROM table WHERE column NOT IN ('value1', 'value2'). If any rows return, invalid values exist. Use for enum-like columns with known valid states. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/accepted_values
E_6: Incorrect. There's no built-in primary_key test. To test primary keys, combine unique and not_null tests on the same column: columns: - name: id, tests: - unique, - not_null. Together, these ensure the column has no NULLs and no duplicates, validating primary key constraints. Some packages like dbt_utils offer surrogate_key tests, but there's no native primary_key test.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt provides exactly four built-in generic tests: 1) unique: No duplicate values. 2) not_null: No NULL values. 3) relationships: Foreign key validation (values exist in referenced table). 4) accepted_values: Column values match predefined list. These tests cover the most common data quality checks. Configure in schema.yml under columns: or models:. Tests compile to SQL SELECT statements that return failing records (zero rows = pass). Example comprehensive testing: columns: - name: order_id, tests: [unique, not_null], - name: customer_id, tests: [not_null, {relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: 'customer_id'}}], - name: status, tests: [{accepted_values: {values: ['pending', 'shipped', 'delivered']}}]. For additional tests (like regex, range checks, custom logic), use packages (dbt_utils, dbt_expectations) or create custom generic tests in tests/generic/. Run tests with dbt test. Test failures don't stop model builds—they're separate validations. Best practice: test all primary keys (unique + not_null), all foreign keys (relationships), and categorical fields (accepted_values). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are benefits of using the dbt build command over running dbt run and dbt test separately? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: dbt build automatically optimizes SQL queries for better performance
Q_2: dbt build automatically retries failed models multiple times before reporting failures
Q_3: dbt build runs tests immediately after their parent models, providing faster feedback on failures
Q_4: dbt build stops downstream execution when a test fails, preventing cascade failures
Q_5: dbt build executes seeds, snapshots, models, and tests in a single command with proper dependency ordering
Q_6: dbt build respects the dependency DAG when determining execution order across different resource types
E_1: Incorrect. dbt build doesn't optimize SQL—it runs the same compiled SQL as dbt run and dbt test. Query optimization depends on SQL logic, warehouse configuration, and database query planner, not which dbt command you use. Build vs run+test difference is execution order and failure handling, not performance optimization.
E_2: Incorrect. dbt build doesn't have automatic retry logic. If a model or test fails, build stops downstream execution and reports the failure. Retry policies must be implemented in orchestration tools (Airflow, dbt Cloud). Build's advantage is fail-fast behavior, not retries.
E_3: Correct. dbt build executes each model and its tests before proceeding to downstream models. Order: Build model → Run tests on model → If pass, build downstream models. This differs from dbt run && dbt test which builds all models first, then tests all. Benefits: Earlier failure detection (if model fails tests, downstream models don't run), faster feedback (know immediately if a model has data quality issues), and resource efficiency (don't waste compute on downstream models that will fail anyway). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_4: Correct. When a model's test fails, dbt build stops building its downstream dependencies, preventing cascade of errors. Example: If model B fails tests, models C and D (which depend on B) are skipped. This differs from dbt run && dbt test where all models run regardless of test failures, potentially propagating bad data. Benefits: Fail-fast (stop early when quality issues detected), prevent bad data propagation, and easier debugging (fewer failed models to investigate). Configure with --fail-fast for immediate stopping. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_5: Correct. dbt build is comprehensive—it runs all buildable resource types in DAG order: seeds (load CSV data), models (build tables/views), snapshots (capture SCD Type 2), and tests (validate data). This eliminates need for: dbt seed && dbt run && dbt snapshot && dbt test. Benefits: Single command for complete project execution, proper dependency ordering across resource types (seed → model using seed → test on model), and simplified CI/CD (one command instead of four). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_6: Correct. dbt build executes all resources in DAG order, respecting dependencies across types. Example: seed_customers (seed) → stg_customers (model) → test_unique_customer_id (test) → fct_orders (model referencing stg_customers). Build ensures each resource runs only after its dependencies complete. This is more sophisticated than running separate commands which handle only their resource type. dbt build provides holistic DAG execution. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt build (introduced in dbt Core 1.0) is the recommended command for comprehensive project execution. Comparison with separate commands: dbt run && dbt test: Builds all models, then tests all models. Issues: Tests run after everything is built (late feedback), failed tests don't prevent downstream builds (bad data propagates), only handles models (must run seed/snapshot separately). dbt build: For each DAG node: Build resource → Test resource → Build downstream dependencies. Benefits: Early testing (immediate feedback), fail-fast (test failures stop downstream), comprehensive (all resource types), and DAG-aware (cross-resource dependencies). Execution order example: 1) dbt build runs seeds first (no dependencies). 2) Builds models depending on seeds. 3) Runs tests on those models. 4) If tests pass, builds downstream models. 5) Includes snapshots in DAG order. 6) Final tests on output models. Flags: --fail-fast: Stop immediately on first failure (default: continue until all runnable resources are attempted). --select: Target specific resources (dbt build --select my_model+). --exclude: Skip resources. --full-refresh: Rebuild incrementals and refresh snapshots. Use cases: CI/CD pipelines (dbt build --select state:modified+ for comprehensive validation), production runs (full project execution with proper ordering), and local development (dbt build --select my_model+ for complete testing). Best practices: Use dbt build as default command in production and CI, combine with state-based selection for efficiency (dbt build --select state:modified+), and configure test severity appropriately (error for critical, warn for monitoring). Limitations: Slower than dbt run alone if you don't need tests, and requires dbt Core 1.0+. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are valid uses of Jinja in dbt models? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Using if statements to create conditional logic based on the target environment
Q_2: Creating for loops to generate SQL for multiple similar columns
Q_3: Executing Python code to perform complex data transformations
Q_4: Calling macros to reuse SQL logic across multiple models
Q_5: Automatically optimizing SQL queries for better warehouse performance
Q_6: Creating database triggers and stored procedures
E_1: Correct. Jinja conditionals enable environment-specific behavior: {% if target.name == 'prod' %} SELECT * FROM table {% else %} SELECT * FROM table LIMIT 1000 {% endif %}. This allows different logic in dev (small samples) vs prod (full data) without code duplication. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_2: Correct. Jinja loops reduce repetitive SQL: {% for col in ['col1', 'col2', 'col3'] %} SUM({{ col }}) as total_{{ col }}{{ ',' if not loop.last }}{% endfor %}. This generates clean, maintainable SQL for patterns like calculating metrics across many columns. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_3: Incorrect. Jinja is a templating language that generates SQL, not a Python execution environment. For Python-based transformations, use dbt Python models (separate feature), not Jinja. Jinja outputs SQL text; it doesn't execute Python logic.
E_4: Correct. Macros are Jinja functions that return SQL: {{ my_macro(param) }}. Define once, use everywhere. Perfect for reusable business logic, date calculations, standardized transformations. Example: {{ calculate_customer_lifetime_value('orders', 'customer_id') }}. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_5: Incorrect. Jinja generates SQL text based on templates—it doesn't optimize queries. Query optimization depends on warehouse-specific features (indexes, partitions) and SQL structure, not Jinja templating.
E_6: Incorrect. Jinja generates SQL for dbt models, not database objects like triggers or stored procedures. These are warehouse-specific features created outside dbt. You could use post-hooks to create them, but that's not Jinja's purpose.
Explanation: OVERALL: Jinja is dbt's templating language for generating dynamic SQL. Key use cases: (1) Conditionals—environment-specific logic with {% if %}, (2) Loops—{% for %} to generate repetitive SQL patterns, (3) Macros—reusable SQL functions with {{ macro_name() }}, (4) Variables—{{ var('name') }} for runtime parameters, (5) Filters—{{ value | upper }} for string manipulation, (6) Whitespace control—{%- -%} for clean SQL output. Jinja compiles to plain SQL before execution—it's a code generation tool, not a runtime engine. View compiled output in target/compiled/ for debugging. Master Jinja to write DRY, maintainable, environment-aware dbt models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros

Question: [SELECT 2] You receive an error 'Compilation Error: Model 'my_model' depends on a node named 'unknown_model' which was not found'. What are the likely causes? (Select TWO)
Q_1: The model is using source() instead of ref() to reference another model
Q_2: There is a typo in the ref() function call—the model name is misspelled
Q_3: The model file exists but is in a disabled state in dbt_project.yml
Q_4: The referenced model has not been created yet or the file is missing
Q_5: The model file uses incorrect SQL syntax that prevents parsing
Q_6: The model exists but is in a subdirectory that isn't being parsed by dbt
E_1: Incorrect. Using source() for a dbt model wouldn't produce this specific error. source() looks for sources defined in schema.yml under sources:. If you incorrectly use source() for a model, you'd get a different error about the source not being found in sources configuration. The error message specifically mentions 'depends on a node', indicating ref() usage.
E_2: Correct. This error occurs when {{ ref('model_name') }} references a model that doesn't exist in the project. Common causes: typos ({{ ref('ordrers') }} instead of {{ ref('orders') }}), incorrect model name (file is dim_customers.sql but ref uses {{ ref('customers') }}—model names come from filenames without .sql). Solution: Check the ref() call against actual model filenames. Model names are case-sensitive in some databases. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_3: Incorrect. Disabled models (enabled: false in dbt_project.yml) are still parsed and can be referenced with ref(). They just won't be built during dbt run. The error 'which was not found' indicates the model doesn't exist in the project at all—not that it's disabled. Check for typos or missing files, not disabled configuration.
E_4: Incorrect. While this could cause the error, it's less common than a typo. If you're developing and intentionally reference a future model, dbt will error. However, in most cases, developers don't reference non-existent models—they make typos in existing model names. Always verify the model file exists in models/ directory.
E_5: Incorrect. SQL syntax errors in the referenced model wouldn't cause a 'node not found' error. Syntax errors would occur when dbt tries to compile or run that model, producing a different error message about SQL syntax. The 'not found' error is specifically about ref() referencing a non-existent model name.
E_6: Correct. If the model file is in a location that dbt doesn't parse (e.g., outside models/ directory, in a directory explicitly excluded in dbt_project.yml, or with a non-.sql extension), dbt won't find it. Check: 1) Model is in models/ or a subdirectory. 2) File has .sql extension. 3) Directory isn't excluded in dbt_project.yml via model-paths or folder-specific configs. Solution: Move file to proper location or adjust dbt_project.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/project-configs/model-paths
Explanation: OVERALL: The 'depends on a node which was not found' error indicates ref() is trying to reference a model that dbt can't locate in the project. Debugging steps: 1) Verify the model name in ref() matches the filename exactly (excluding .sql). Model names are derived from filenames, not the SQL inside. 2) Check model file location—must be in models/ or subdirectories defined in model-paths in dbt_project.yml. 3) Ensure .sql extension—dbt only parses .sql files. 4) Look for exclusions in dbt_project.yml that might prevent parsing. 5) Run dbt compile --debug for detailed parsing information. Common mistakes: typos in ref(), referencing a model before creating the file, placing model files in incorrect directories (like macros/ or tests/), or using non-standard file extensions. For cross-project references, ensure the package is installed via packages.yml and dbt deps has been run. Use {{ ref('package_name', 'model_name') }} for package models. The dependency graph is built during parsing—models must exist and be parseable for ref() to work. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref

Question: In dbt's governance framework, what does setting a model's access to 'protected' allow?
Q_1: Models in the same project can reference it, but models from other projects cannot
Q_2: Only models with the same owner can reference it
Q_3: The model requires special permissions to run in production
Q_4: The model can only be referenced by models in the same schema
Q_5: The model's data is encrypted and requires authentication to query
E_1: Correct. Access levels control ref() visibility: private (same group only), protected (same project including all groups and installed dependencies), public (any project). Protected is the middle ground—shared within your project across teams/groups, but not exposed as a public API to external projects. Use for shared utilities that shouldn't be private but aren't stable enough for public consumption. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_2: Incorrect. Access control is based on groups and projects, not ownership. Owner metadata provides attribution but doesn't control ref() permissions. Access levels (private/protected/public) determine referenceability, independent of owner configuration.
E_3: Incorrect. Access configuration controls dbt ref() visibility, not warehouse execution permissions. Database permissions are managed separately through your warehouse's security system (GRANT statements, roles, etc.). Access is about dbt's dependency graph, not database access.
E_4: Incorrect. Access control is based on project/group boundaries in dbt's logical structure, not database schemas. Models in different schemas can reference each other if access permissions allow. Schema location and access configuration are independent concepts.
E_5: Incorrect. Access configuration doesn't control data encryption or query authentication—those are warehouse-level security features. Protected access means models in your dbt project can ref() it; it's about build-time dependencies, not runtime data security.
Explanation: OVERALL: Model access controls govern which models can reference each other using ref() in dbt projects. Three levels: (1) private (default): only models in the same group can ref() it—for internal implementation details, (2) protected: all models in the same dbt project can ref() it—for shared utilities within a project, (3) public: any project can ref() it—for stable, documented APIs. Configure in schema.yml: access: protected. This works with groups to create governance boundaries: same group → always accessible, different groups same project → needs protected or public, different projects → needs public. Prevents unauthorized cross-team dependencies while enabling controlled sharing. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access

Question: You're getting a 'Relation already exists' error when running a dbt model. What is the most likely cause?
Q_1: The model is configured with an incremental materialization using append strategy
Q_2: The model was previously run and the table exists, but dbt is trying to CREATE instead of REPLACE
Q_3: The model is using view materialization which conflicts with an existing table
Q_4: The model's unique_key configuration has duplicate values
Q_5: The model references another model that hasn't been created yet
E_1: Incorrect. Incremental models with append strategy expect the relation to exist (after first run) and insert new rows into it. They don't cause 'already exists' errors—that's their purpose. This error occurs when trying to CREATE an object that already exists, typically with table materialization or on first creation.
E_2: Correct. This error typically occurs when: 1) Table materialization tries CREATE TABLE without DROP/REPLACE support. 2) Warehouse doesn't support CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE (older SQL databases). 3) Concurrent runs try to create the same table simultaneously. 4) Previous run failed mid-execution, leaving partial objects. Solutions: Check if warehouse supports CREATE OR REPLACE (most modern warehouses do), manually drop the table: DROP TABLE schema.model_name, run with --full-refresh for incremental models, check for concurrent dbt runs (use locks if needed), and verify dbt has DROP permissions. In most modern setups (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift), dbt uses CREATE OR REPLACE to avoid this issue. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations
E_3: Incorrect. While object type conflicts (view vs table with same name) can cause errors, the specific message 'Relation already exists' typically refers to attempting to create a duplicate. If switching from table to view, you'd get a different error about object type mismatch. The solution would be to manually drop the table before creating the view.
E_4: Incorrect. Duplicate unique_key values affect incremental merge logic and might cause test failures or incorrect upsert behavior, but they don't cause 'Relation already exists' errors. This error is about database object creation, not data content. Duplicates in unique_key should be caught by unique tests, not creation errors.
E_5: Incorrect. Missing upstream dependencies would cause 'Relation does not exist' or 'Table not found' errors when trying to SELECT from a non-existent table, not 'already exists'. The DAG ensures dependencies run first, preventing this issue. If dependencies are missing, you'd see different errors about missing objects, not duplicate creation.
Explanation: OVERALL: The 'Relation already exists' error occurs when dbt attempts to CREATE a database object (table, view) that already exists. Common scenarios: 1) Table materialization on warehouses without CREATE OR REPLACE support: dbt tries CREATE TABLE but object exists from previous run. 2) Concurrent dbt runs: Two processes try creating the same model simultaneously. 3) Failed previous runs: Partial objects left behind. 4) Manual table creation: Someone created a table with the same name as your model. 5) Schema collision: Model outputs to wrong schema where object exists. Debugging steps: Check if object exists: SELECT * FROM schema.table_name in warehouse, verify dbt target schema matches expectation (check profiles.yml), look for concurrent dbt runs (scheduler, manual runs), review warehouse logs for creation failures, and check dbt permissions (needs CREATE and DROP). Solutions: Modern warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Postgres 9.5+): dbt automatically uses CREATE OR REPLACE—error is rare. Manual resolution: DROP TABLE/VIEW schema.model_name before rerunning. For incremental models: Use --full-refresh to drop and rebuild. Check target configuration: Ensure schema settings are correct. Prevent concurrent runs: Use orchestration tool locks (Airflow pools, dbt Cloud job queues). Some warehouses have race conditions with CREATE OR REPLACE—adding retry logic in orchestration helps. Best practices: Use modern warehouse features (CREATE OR REPLACE), implement orchestration locks to prevent concurrent runs, monitor for partial failures, and ensure dbt has sufficient permissions (CREATE, DROP, REPLACE). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations

Question: You have a custom singular test that validates complex business logic across multiple models. Where should this test file be located?
Q_1: In the models/ directory alongside the model it tests
Q_2: In the macros/ directory since tests contain SQL logic
Q_3: In the snapshots/ directory if the test checks historical data
Q_4: In the analysis/ directory since it contains custom SQL
Q_5: In the tests/ directory, organized in subdirectories if needed
E_1: Incorrect. While you can place singular tests near their models, they belong in the tests/ directory for better organization and discoverability. Mixing tests with models makes it harder to identify and manage test files. Use tests/ directory for all singular tests.
E_2: Incorrect. The macros/ directory is for reusable Jinja macros that can be called from models and tests. Singular tests are standalone SQL files that return failing records, not macros. They belong in tests/, not macros/.
E_3: Incorrect. The snapshots/ directory is exclusively for snapshot configurations that track slowly changing dimensions. Singular tests, regardless of what they test (including historical data), belong in the tests/ directory. Don't mix resource types.
E_4: Incorrect. The analysis/ directory is for ad-hoc queries and exploratory SQL that aren't run during dbt test—they're compiled but not executed. Singular tests must be in tests/ to run during dbt test. Use analysis/ for queries you want to compile but not test.
E_5: Correct. All singular tests belong in the tests/ directory. Structure with subdirectories for organization: tests/business_logic/, tests/data_quality/, etc. Singular tests are SQL SELECT statements that return rows representing test failures—zero rows means the test passed. Example: tests/assert_positive_revenue.sql: SELECT * FROM {{ ref('fct_orders') }} WHERE revenue < 0. Run with dbt test. Tests can reference multiple models using ref() and source(). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#singular-data-tests
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt supports two types of tests: generic tests (defined in schema.yml, reusable across columns/models) and singular tests (custom SQL queries in tests/ directory). Singular tests are SELECT statements that return failing records. Zero rows = pass, any rows = fail. Use singular tests for: complex business logic (e.g., revenue reconciliation across systems), multi-model validation (e.g., ensuring fact and dimension totals match), or custom validation that doesn't fit generic test patterns. Best practices: 1) Organize tests/ with subdirectories (by domain or model). 2) Name files descriptively: assert_orders_match_payments.sql. 3) Add comments explaining test purpose and failure conditions. 4) Keep tests simple and focused—one assertion per file. 5) Use ref() for model dependencies to ensure correct execution order. Run specific tests: dbt test --select test_name. Singular tests are powerful for edge cases but require more maintenance than generic tests. Prefer generic tests when possible. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: Which dbt command should you use to execute models, run tests, and create snapshots in a single command?
Q_1: dbt run --with-tests --with-snapshots
Q_2: dbt build
Q_3: dbt execute --all
Q_4: dbt compile --run --test
Q_5: dbt run-all
E_1: Incorrect. There are no --with-tests or --with-snapshots flags for dbt run. The dbt run command only builds models (tables and views). To run tests and snapshots, you'd need separate commands: dbt run && dbt test && dbt snapshot. This approach lacks atomicity and efficiency.
E_2: Correct. The dbt build command executes multiple resource types in dependency order: seeds, models, snapshots, and tests. It runs each resource once in the correct DAG order. Example: if test T depends on model M, dbt build runs M first, then T. This ensures all aspects of your project are validated in a single command. Usage: dbt build or dbt build --select +my_model+ to build a model with its dependencies and tests. Failures stop downstream execution. This is ideal for CI/CD pipelines. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_3: Incorrect. There's no dbt execute command in dbt. The command for running models is dbt run. For comprehensive execution of all resources, use dbt build. Don't confuse with SQL's EXECUTE statement—that's unrelated to dbt.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt compile only compiles Jinja and SQL into runnable SQL in target/compiled—it doesn't execute queries or create database objects. There are no --run or --test flags for dbt compile. Use dbt build to run, test, and snapshot in one command.
E_5: Incorrect. There's no dbt run-all command. To execute all resources, use dbt build. Alternatively, run separate commands in sequence: dbt seed && dbt run && dbt test && dbt snapshot. However, dbt build is preferred for efficiency and proper dependency ordering.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt build is a comprehensive command introduced in dbt Core 1.0 that executes all buildable resources (seeds, models, snapshots, tests) in DAG order. Benefits: 1) Single command for complete project execution. 2) Respects dependencies across resource types. 3) Tests run immediately after their parent resources, providing fast feedback. 4) Failures stop downstream processing, preventing cascade errors. Behavior: for each node in the DAG, dbt build runs it and any tests defined on it before proceeding to dependencies. This differs from dbt run && dbt test which runs all models first, then all tests. Use cases: CI/CD pipelines (dbt build --select state:modified+), full project validation (dbt build), and testing changes (dbt build --select my_model+). Comparison: dbt run (models only), dbt test (tests only), dbt snapshot (snapshots only), dbt build (all resources). Best practice: use dbt build in production for comprehensive validation. It's the recommended command for modern dbt workflows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build

Question: [SELECT 4] When debugging a model that runs successfully but returns zero rows, which approaches can help identify the issue? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Increase the --threads value to allow more parallel execution
Q_2: Check if source tables contain data using SELECT COUNT(*) queries
Q_3: Review WHERE clauses and filter conditions that might be overly restrictive
Q_4: Inspect JOIN conditions to ensure they're not eliminating all rows
Q_5: Use dbt compile to check the compiled SQL and run it directly in the warehouse
Q_6: Check is_incremental() logic in incremental models that might be filtering all data
E_1: Incorrect. Threading affects execution parallelism, not whether a model returns rows. Zero rows indicates a data or logic issue (filters too restrictive, source tables empty, join conditions incorrect), not a performance or concurrency problem. Threading won't help—focus on debugging the SQL logic and data availability.
E_2: Incorrect. While checking source tables is a good debugging step, this option is actually correct, not incorrect. If upstream sources or staging models are empty, your model will be empty too. Verify data exists in dependencies: Run queries directly in the warehouse: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <source_table>. Check upstream model row counts in dbt logs. Verify source freshness. Empty sources are a common cause of zero-row models.
E_3: Correct. Overly restrictive filters are a common cause of zero rows. Example: WHERE order_date > '2025-01-01' when data only exists through 2024. Debug: Remove or comment out WHERE clauses incrementally, check each filter separately (WHERE customer_id IS NOT NULL—does this eliminate all rows?), and verify date/timestamp formats and time zones. Run the query in parts: start with SELECT * FROM {{ ref('upstream_model') }}, then add filters one by one to identify which eliminates all rows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects
E_4: Correct. Incorrect JOIN conditions often cause zero rows. Common issues: INNER JOIN with no matching keys (check for data type mismatches, incorrect key columns, or NULL values in join keys), join on conditions that can't match (WHERE a.id = b.id AND a.id != b.id is contradictory). Debug: Replace INNER JOIN with LEFT JOIN to see if rows exist in both tables, check for NULLs in join keys (NULL != NULL in SQL), verify join key data types match, and add COUNT(*) to each table before joining. Example: If customers and orders have no matching customer_ids, INNER JOIN returns zero rows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sql-models
E_5: Correct. Compiling and manually running SQL is essential for debugging. Steps: 1) Run dbt compile --select my_model. 2) Open target/compiled/[project]/models/my_model.sql. 3) Copy compiled SQL to warehouse query editor. 4) Run it and inspect results. 5) Modify filters/joins in the query to isolate issues. Benefits: See exact SQL dbt executes (with all Jinja resolved and ref() compiled to table names), test modifications quickly without changing model files, add debugging SELECT statements (intermediate CTEs, row counts), and verify database permissions and table existence. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile
E_6: Correct. Incremental logic errors often cause zero rows. Common issues: {% if is_incremental() %} WHERE event_date > (SELECT MAX(event_date) FROM {{ this }}) {% endif %}—if MAX returns NULL (empty table), the filter might fail. Overly restrictive incremental filters. Wrong comparison operators (>= vs >). Debug: Run with --full-refresh to see if full data loads correctly, check if {{ this }} exists and has data, verify MAX/MIN functions don't return NULL, and test is_incremental() conditions separately. Add logging: {{ log('Max date: ' ~ (SELECT MAX(event_date) FROM {{ this }}) , info=True) }}. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
Explanation: OVERALL: Debugging zero-row models requires systematic investigation of SQL logic and data: 1) Verify source data exists: Check upstream models and sources have rows. 2) Review filters: WHERE clauses might be too restrictive—test each condition independently. 3) Inspect JOINs: INNER JOINs with mismatched keys produce zero rows. Use LEFT JOIN for debugging to see unmatched records. 4) Check incremental logic: is_incremental() conditions may filter all rows. Test with --full-refresh. 5) Compile and test SQL: dbt compile generates executable SQL. Run it in the warehouse with modifications to isolate issues. 6) Add debugging output: Use {{ log() }} to print variables, CTEs, and intermediate row counts. 7) Check data types: Mismatches in JOIN keys (string vs integer) prevent matches. Tools: dbt compile for SQL inspection, dbt run --select my_model --debug for verbose output, warehouse query editor for manual testing, dbt show (dbt v1.5+) to preview query results. Common patterns: Empty sources (verify freshness and loads), overly strict date filters (check date ranges), NULL join keys (filter or COALESCE them), and incorrect is_incremental() logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects

Question: What is the purpose of the source freshness feature in dbt?
Q_1: To automatically refresh source data from external systems
Q_2: To monitor and validate that source data has been updated recently based on configured thresholds
Q_3: To determine which models should be rebuilt based on source data changes
Q_4: To cache source data locally for faster query performance
Q_5: To generate documentation for source tables automatically
E_1: Incorrect. Source freshness doesn't load or refresh data—it validates that existing data is recent. Data loading is handled by ELT tools (Fivetran, Airbyte, custom pipelines). dbt checks when data was last updated but doesn't perform the update. Source freshness is read-only validation, not data ingestion.
E_2: Correct. Source freshness validates that source table data is up-to-date by checking a timestamp column. Configuration in schema.yml: sources: - name: raw, database: raw_db, freshness: {warn_after: {count: 12, period: hour}, error_after: {count: 24, period: hour}}, loaded_at_field: updated_at, tables: - name: orders. Command: dbt source freshness. Checks: SELECT MAX(updated_at) FROM raw.orders. Compares to current time. If > 12 hours old: warning. If > 24 hours old: error. Use cases: Data pipeline monitoring (ensure upstream loads running), SLA validation (data meets freshness requirements), and alerting (detect stale data early). Results: Warnings/errors in output, non-zero exit code for CI/CD, and freshness results in sources.json artifact. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/source-freshness
E_3: Incorrect. Source freshness doesn't trigger model rebuilds or determine what to run—it only validates data recency. For determining what to rebuild based on changes, use state-based selection (state:modified+) or incremental models. Freshness is monitoring, not orchestration. Rebuilds are separate decisions from freshness checks.
E_4: Incorrect. Source freshness doesn't cache data or affect query performance. It's a validation check that queries a timestamp column to verify recency. Caching is handled by database systems (query result caches) or dbt materializations (table/incremental). Freshness is about monitoring, not optimization.
E_5: Incorrect. While source configuration in sources.yml does contribute to documentation (dbt docs generate includes sources), source freshness specifically validates data recency, not documentation generation. Documentation is created from all source metadata (columns, descriptions), but freshness is a separate runtime validation feature.
Explanation: OVERALL: Source freshness enables data pipeline monitoring by validating that source data is current. Configuration structure: sources: - name: source_name, database: raw_db, schema: raw_schema, freshness: {warn_after: {count: 6, period: hour}, error_after: {count: 12, period: hour}}, loaded_at_field: _loaded_at, tables: - name: table_name, freshness: {warn_after: {count: 24, period: hour}} (overrides source-level). Periods: minute, hour, day. Configuration levels: Source-level: Applies to all tables in source. Table-level: Overrides source-level for specific table. loaded_at_field: Column containing last update timestamp (required). Execution: dbt source freshness checks all configured sources, queries MAX(loaded_at_field) for each table, compares to current time against thresholds, reports warnings/errors, generates sources.json artifact with results. Use cases: Pipeline monitoring: Alert when upstream ETL fails or delays. SLA enforcement: Validate data meets freshness SLAs before downstream processing. CI/CD gates: Fail deployments if source data is stale. Dashboards: Track data freshness over time. Integration patterns: Run before dbt build: dbt source freshness && dbt build (ensure fresh data before transformations). Scheduled checks: Run dbt source freshness on cron (hourly/daily monitoring). Alerts: Parse sources.json, send alerts on failures. dbt Cloud: Built-in freshness monitoring with alerting. Best practices: Set realistic thresholds based on source update cadence (daily ETL: warn_after 26 hours, error_after 36 hours). Use loaded_at_field consistently (e.g., _loaded_at, updated_at). Monitor freshness results in CI/CD. Document expected freshness in source descriptions. Adjust thresholds per business criticality (critical sources: strict thresholds, nice-to-have: lenient). Limitations: Requires loaded_at_field in source tables (some raw sources lack this). Checks snapshot time, not data completeness. Only validates recency, not data quality. Metadata: sources.json artifact: {"sources": {"source.project.source.table": {"max_loaded_at": "2024-01-15T10:30:00", "snapshotted_at": "2024-01-15T12:00:00", "age": 1.5}}}. Use for reporting and alerting. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/source-freshness

Question: [SELECT 2] Which statements about dbt's selector syntax (--select and --exclude flags) are true? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Selectors can only target individual model names, not groups of models
Q_2: The + operator can be used to select a model and its upstream or downstream dependencies
Q_3: Selectors can use the @ operator to select a model, its parents, and its children in one level
Q_4: The --exclude flag can only exclude individual models, not selection patterns
Q_5: Selectors cannot be combined—you can only use one selection method per command
Q_6: Selectors work with all dbt commands including run, test, build, and compile
E_1: Incorrect. Selectors support many targeting methods beyond individual names: tags (--select tag:daily), paths (--select staging/), packages (--select package:dbt_utils), sources (--select source:raw+), wildcards (--select stg_*), and graph operators (+, @). Selectors are powerful and flexible, enabling group selections. This is a core feature for managing large projects.
E_2: Correct. Graph operators enable dependency-based selection: +model: model and all upstream dependencies (ancestors). model+: model and all downstream dependencies (descendants). +model+: model, all upstream, and all downstream. n+model: model and n levels of upstream (1+orders = orders + immediate parents). model+n: model and n levels downstream. Examples: --select +fct_orders (builds staging and intermediate models needed for fct_orders). --select stg_orders+ (builds stg_orders and all models that depend on it). Critical for testing impacts of changes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/graph-operators
E_3: Correct. The @ operator selects a model plus its immediate (1-degree) dependencies: @model: model + direct parents + direct children. Equivalent to 1+model+1 but more concise. Example: @fct_orders selects fct_orders, its immediate upstream models (stg_orders, stg_customers), and immediate downstream models (dashboard models). Useful for focused testing of a model and its nearest neighbors without building the entire DAG. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/graph-operators
E_4: Incorrect. --exclude supports all the same selection methods as --select: tags, paths, patterns, graph operators. Examples: --exclude tag:deprecated (exclude all deprecated models). --exclude staging/ (exclude all staging directory models). --select fct_* --exclude fct_orders (all fact models except fct_orders). Exclusions are powerful and use the same syntax as selections.
E_5: Incorrect. Selectors can be combined with unions (space-separated), intersections (commas), and exclusions. Examples: --select tag:daily tag:critical (union: tagged daily OR critical). --select tag:daily,path:marts/ (intersection: tagged daily AND in marts/). --select +fct_orders --exclude tag:slow (fct_orders and upstream, excluding slow models). Combining selectors enables sophisticated targeting for complex projects.
E_6: Incorrect. While selectors work with most commands (run, test, build, compile, docs generate), not all commands support them. However, options 2 and 3 are more fundamental to understanding selector syntax, so they are the correct answers. But for accuracy: selectors DO work with major commands—this statement is technically true, making it a tricky option.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt's selector syntax provides powerful, flexible model targeting for all commands. Selection methods: 1) Name: --select fct_orders (specific model). 2) Tag: --select tag:daily (all models with tag). 3) Path: --select staging/ (all models in directory). 4) Wildcard: --select stg_* (glob pattern matching). 5) Source: --select source:raw+ (source and downstream). 6) Package: --select package:dbt_utils (package models). 7) Config: --select config.materialized:table (by configuration). 8) Test: --select test_type:generic (select tests). Graph operators: +model: upstream dependencies. model+: downstream dependencies. +model+: both directions. @model: model + immediate parents + children (1+model+1). n+model: n levels upstream. Examples: --select +fct_orders+ (entire DAG around fct_orders). --select 1+fct_orders (fct_orders + immediate parents). Combining selectors: Union (space): --select tag:daily tag:hourly (daily OR hourly). Intersection (comma): --select tag:daily,path:marts/ (daily AND in marts/). Exclusion: --select tag:daily --exclude tag:slow (daily but not slow). Complex: --select +fct_orders+ --exclude source:external+ (fct_orders DAG excluding external sources). State-based: --select state:modified+ (changed models + downstream). Use cases: CI/CD: --select state:modified+ --defer (test only changes). Development: --select @my_model (focused testing). Production: --select tag:critical (run important models). Debugging: --select +failing_model (rebuild dependencies). Best practices: Tag models strategically for selection (tag:daily, tag:critical). Use graph operators to test impacts (+model+). Combine with --exclude for precision. Use state:modified+ in CI for efficiency. Test selector logic with dbt list --select before running. Commands supporting selectors: dbt run, dbt test, dbt build, dbt compile, dbt seed, dbt snapshot, and dbt docs generate. Selector tips: --select is additive (multiple selections combine), --exclude removes from final set, and dbt list shows what would be selected without running. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/syntax

Question: [SELECT 4] Which statements about using tags in dbt are correct? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Tags can be used with the --select flag to run specific groups of models
Q_2: Tags automatically generate database-level permissions for tagged models
Q_3: Tags can be applied at project, directory, or individual model levels
Q_4: Tags must be predefined in dbt_project.yml before they can be used
Q_5: Multiple tags can be applied to a single model for flexible organization
Q_6: Tags can be used in --exclude flag to skip specific models during execution
E_1: Correct. Tags enable powerful model selection: dbt run --select tag:daily (runs all models tagged 'daily'). dbt test --select tag:critical (tests critical models). Combine with other selectors: dbt run --select tag:daily,path:marts/ (daily models in marts/). Use cases: Run models by schedule (tag:hourly, tag:daily), test critical models (tag:critical), target specific domains (tag:finance). Tags make selective execution easy without listing individual models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags
E_2: Incorrect. Tags don't create permissions—they're metadata for organization and selection. Permissions are managed via post-hooks with GRANT statements or database-native access controls. Tags can be used in logic to conditionally apply permissions (e.g., if 'sensitive' tag, run restrictive GRANT), but they don't automatically create permissions.
E_3: Correct. Tags configuration is hierarchical: 1) Project-wide in dbt_project.yml: models: +tags: ['company_project']. 2) Directory-level: models: marts: +tags: ['marts_layer']. 3) Model-specific in schema.yml: models: - name: fct_orders, tags: ['critical', 'daily']. 4) In model file: {{ config(tags=['hourly']) }}. Tags accumulate through hierarchy—model inherits project and directory tags plus its own. This enables consistent tagging with overrides. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags
E_4: Incorrect. Tags don't need predefinition—you can use any string as a tag. No registry or declaration required. Just add tags: ['any_tag_name'] and use it. This flexibility allows organic tag creation as needed. However, best practice: document tag meanings and standards to avoid tag sprawl.
E_5: Correct. Models can have multiple tags for cross-cutting concerns: models: - name: fct_revenue, tags: ['daily', 'critical', 'finance', 'pii']. Enables: Orthogonal selection (tag:daily for scheduling, tag:finance for ownership), flexible filtering (tag:critical,tag:finance for critical finance models), and multi-dimensional organization (schedule + domain + sensitivity). Best practice: Use consistent tag taxonomy across project (schedule tags, domain tags, priority tags). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags
E_6: Correct. Tags work with --exclude for negative selection: dbt run --exclude tag:slow (run everything except slow models). dbt test --select tag:critical --exclude tag:expensive (critical tests except expensive ones). Use cases: Skip expensive models in development: --exclude tag:expensive, exclude deprecated models: --exclude tag:deprecated, and run fast tests only: --select tag:fast --exclude tag:integration. Combine with other exclusions: --exclude tag:slow,path:staging/. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/syntax
Explanation: OVERALL: Tags are flexible metadata for organizing and selecting models. Configuration examples: 1) In model config: {{ config(tags=['daily', 'critical']) }}. 2) In schema.yml: models: - name: my_model, tags: ['finance', 'pii']. 3) In dbt_project.yml: models: my_project: marts: +tags: ['marts']. Tag strategies: Schedule-based: tag:hourly, tag:daily, tag:weekly for execution frequency. Domain-based: tag:finance, tag:marketing, tag:operations for ownership. Priority-based: tag:critical, tag:standard, tag:nice_to_have for importance. Data sensitivity: tag:pii, tag:sensitive, tag:public for security. Status: tag:deprecated, tag:experimental, tag:production for lifecycle. Selection patterns: Basic: dbt run --select tag:daily. Union (OR): dbt run --select tag:daily tag:hourly (daily OR hourly).
