E_5: While this uses state comparison, it only compiles (doesn't run) models, uses @ instead of state:modified (@ selects parents, not using state method), and references ./target instead of production artifacts. The command also doesn't include downstream dependencies (+) which are crucial for comprehensive testing.
Explanation: OVERALL: Leveraging dbt state is essential for efficient CI/CD pipelines. The state:modified selector compares your current code against production artifacts (manifest.json from a previous run) to identify which models have changed. The + suffix includes all downstream dependencies, ensuring you test everything affected by your changes. The --defer flag tells dbt to reference production data for unselected upstream models instead of running them, and --state points to the directory containing production artifacts. This approach dramatically reduces CI run time while maintaining comprehensive test coverage. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/cloud-ci-job and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/defer

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following statements about the relationships test in dbt are TRUE? (Select THREE)
Q_1: The relationships test verifies that all values in a column exist in a column of another table (referential integrity/foreign key validation)
Q_2: The relationships test can reference both dbt models (using ref) and sources (using source) in the 'to' parameter
Q_3: Failed relationships tests can be configured to store the orphaned records using the store_failures configuration
Q_4: The relationships test automatically creates a foreign key constraint in the database
Q_5: The relationships test can only be used on primary key columns
Q_6: The relationships test requires both tables to be in the same schema
E_1: Correct. The relationships test implements foreign key validation by checking that every value in the specified column exists in the referenced column of another table. For example, verifying that all order.customer_id values exist in customer.id. This catches orphaned records and ensures referential integrity. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/data-tests#relationships
E_2: Correct. The to parameter accepts either ref('model_name') or source('source_name', 'table_name'), allowing you to validate relationships to both dbt-managed models and external source tables. This flexibility supports testing at different layers: sources to staging, staging to marts, etc. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/data-tests#relationships
E_3: Correct. Setting store_failures: true creates a table containing the rows that failed the test (orphaned records that don't have matches in the parent table). This is invaluable for debugging referential integrity issues - you can query the failure table to see exactly which records are problematic and investigate why. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_4: Incorrect. dbt tests run SELECT queries to validate data; they don't create database constraints. The relationships test checks referential integrity but doesn't enforce it at the database level. Most modern data warehouses don't enforce foreign key constraints anyway. dbt tests are for validation, not constraint creation.
E_5: Incorrect. The relationships test can reference any column in the parent table, not just primary keys. While it's commonly used to validate foreign keys against primary keys, it works with any column. You could validate against unique alternate keys or even non-unique columns (though that's less common).
E_6: Incorrect. The relationships test works across schemas and even databases (in dbt Cloud with cross-database refs enabled). The test uses ref() or source() which resolve to fully-qualified table names, allowing cross-schema validation.
Explanation: OVERALL: The relationships test is one of dbt's four built-in generic tests and is essential for data quality. Configuration example: tests: - relationships: {to: ref('customers'), field: customer_id}. The test generates SQL that selects records from the child table where the specified column value doesn't exist in the parent table's field. Best practices: (1) Test foreign keys at every layer (source to staging, staging to marts), (2) Combine with not_null to ensure no NULL foreign keys (unless allowed), (3) Use store_failures to capture orphaned records for investigation, (4) Consider severity: warn for relationships you expect to occasionally fail, (5) In the parent table, ensure the referenced field is unique (test it). The relationships test catches data quality issues like: deleted parent records, incorrect joins in upstream systems, data synchronization problems, or ID generation issues. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/data-tests#relationships and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/writing-custom-generic-tests

Question: You want to include information about data freshness and when models were last successfully updated in your dbt documentation. How can you achieve this?
Q_1: Manually update documentation files with last run timestamps
Q_2: Use a post-hook to write run timestamps to a metadata table and query it from documentation
Q_3: Add custom meta fields to models with static freshness information
Q_4: Configure source freshness checks which will display freshness information in dbt docs
Q_5: Information about when models last ran successfully is automatically included in dbt docs through the catalog.json and manifest.json artifacts
E_1: Incorrect. Manual updates don't scale and quickly become outdated. dbt provides automated mechanisms for capturing and displaying run metadata. Manual processes are error-prone and don't provide real-time information.
E_2: Incorrect. While you could create custom metadata tables, this is unnecessarily complex. dbt's artifacts and built-in features already capture this information. Documentation should leverage dbt's native metadata, not custom tables.
E_3: Incorrect. Static meta fields would need manual updates and wouldn't reflect actual run times. Meta fields are for static metadata (ownership, tags), not dynamic runtime information like last successful run.
E_4: Incorrect. Source freshness checks validate when source data was loaded and display that information, but the question asks about model freshness (when dbt models last ran successfully). While related, source freshness doesn't track model execution.
E_5: Correct. When you run dbt docs generate, it creates catalog.json (with database metadata and statistics) and uses manifest.json (with model metadata). The dbt docs site automatically displays: last run time for models, test results and their status, row counts and table sizes, source freshness if configured. In dbt Cloud, this information is enhanced with historical run data, showing execution trends over time. No additional configuration needed - just run dbt docs generate after your runs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt documentation automatically captures execution metadata when you run dbt docs generate. The documentation site displays: (1) Model-level: last run status, execution time, row counts from catalog.json, (2) Source-level: freshness check results if configured, loaded_at timestamps, (3) Test-level: test results and failure counts, (4) Overall project: DAG visualization, dependency relationships. In dbt Cloud, you get additional features: historical run data, execution trends over time, automatic doc generation after jobs, hosted documentation for the team. To keep docs fresh: (1) Run dbt docs generate after production runs, (2) Upload artifacts to cloud storage or use dbt Cloud hosting, (3) Configure source freshness checks for external data, (4) Set up automated doc regeneration in CI/CD. The docs site becomes a living data catalog that stays current with your pipelines. Users can see not just what models do, but when they last ran and their current state. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are best practices when working with dbt models in the 'staging' layer? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Include complex business logic and aggregations in staging models
Q_2: Join multiple sources together to create denormalized staging models
Q_3: Use view materialization for most staging models to ensure freshness and reduce build time
Q_4: Follow a consistent naming convention such as stg_<source>_<table>
Q_5: Perform all data quality validation and cleaning in staging models before passing to marts
Q_6: Select only the columns needed downstream and rename them to follow consistent conventions (e.g., snake_case)
E_1: Incorrect. Staging models should focus on light transformations: renaming, type casting, and basic cleaning. Complex business logic belongs in the intermediate or mart layers. Staging establishes a clean, standardized foundation - save transformations for later layers.
E_2: Incorrect. Staging models should maintain a 1:1 relationship with source tables - one staging model per source table. Joins belong in intermediate or mart layers. This keeps staging simple, understandable, and easy to maintain.
E_3: Correct. Views are the standard materialization for staging because: (1) They're always fresh (query the source each time), (2) They build instantly (no data movement), (3) They're appropriate for the simple transformations in staging. Only use table materialization if downstream queries are very slow or source queries are expensive. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging
E_4: Correct. Consistent naming makes projects navigable and predictable. The stg_ prefix clearly identifies staging models, the source name groups related tables, and the table name maps to the source. For example: stg_salesforce_accounts, stg_stripe_payments. This convention scales as projects grow and helps new team members understand structure. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging
E_5: Incorrect. While basic cleaning (standardizing formats, fixing obvious issues) happens in staging, comprehensive data quality testing should occur at multiple layers. Test sources for issues at ingestion, staging for standardization, and marts for business rule compliance. Don't try to handle all validation in one layer.
E_6: Correct. Staging models should: (1) Select only necessary columns (avoid SELECT *), (2) Rename columns to consistent naming conventions, (3) Cast to appropriate types, (4) Apply minor transformations (parsing JSON, extracting date parts). This creates a clean interface for downstream models and documents which source columns are actually used. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging
Explanation: OVERALL: The staging layer is the foundation of your dbt project, sitting directly on top of sources. Staging best practices: (1) One staging model per source table, (2) View materialization (with exceptions), (3) Light transformations only: renaming, type casting, basic cleaning, (4) Consistent naming: stg_ _, (5) Column selection and renaming, (6) No joins or aggregations, (7) Reference sources using source() not hardcoded names, (8) Document source peculiarities. Staging isolates source-specific quirks (weird column names, inconsistent types, system-specific formats) from your analytics logic. Downstream models work with clean, consistently named columns without worrying about source system details. Keep staging simple - complexity belongs in intermediate and mart layers. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging and https://docs.getdbt.com/guides/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging

Question: You want to use a popular dbt package from the dbt Hub to add utility macros to your project. After adding the package to packages.yml, what is the NEXT step?
Q_1: Run dbt compile to make the package available in your project
Q_2: Manually download the package code and add it to your macros/ directory
Q_3: Restart your dbt development environment to detect the new package
Q_4: Run dbt deps to download and install the package into your dbt_packages/ directory
Q_5: Add an import statement at the top of your dbt_project.yml file
E_1: Incorrect. While dbt compile will eventually use the package macros, it doesn't install them. You must first run dbt deps to download and install package dependencies. Without this step, dbt compile will fail because the package code won't be available.
E_2: Incorrect. This defeats the purpose of dbt's package management. Packages should be managed through packages.yml and dbt deps, not manually downloaded. Manual management makes updates difficult and can cause version conflicts.
E_3: Incorrect. Restarting your environment doesn't install packages. You must explicitly run dbt deps to download package code. The environment restart is irrelevant to package installation.
E_4: Correct. After adding a package to packages.yml, you must run dbt deps to install it. This command downloads the specified package version (and its dependencies) into the dbt_packages/ directory. Once installed, the package's macros, models, and other resources become available in your project. You can then reference package macros using their full name (e.g., {{ package_name.macro_name() }}) or via import. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages
E_5: Incorrect. dbt_project.yml doesn't have an import mechanism for packages. Package installation is handled by packages.yml and the dbt deps command. Once installed via dbt deps, packages are automatically available without additional imports.
Explanation: OVERALL: The dbt package workflow is: (1) Add package specification to packages.yml with version constraints, (2) Run dbt deps to install packages, (3) Use package resources in your models/macros. The dbt deps command reads packages.yml, resolves version dependencies, and downloads packages to dbt_packages/ (which should be in .gitignore). Run dbt deps when: adding new packages, updating package versions, or setting up a new development environment. In CI/CD, always run dbt deps before dbt run/test. Popular packages include dbt_utils (utility macros), dbt_expectations (additional tests), audit_helper (migration validation), and codegen (code generation). Packages can depend on other packages, and dbt deps resolves the full dependency graph. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages and https://hub.getdbt.com/

Question: When should you use the source() function instead of the ref() function in a dbt model?
Q_1: When referencing raw data tables that are loaded into your warehouse by external tools (not created by dbt)
Q_2: When referencing models in a different schema than the current model
Q_3: When referencing models from external dbt packages
Q_4: When you want better query performance through direct table references
Q_5: When referencing ephemeral models that don't create database objects
E_1: Correct. The source() function is specifically for referencing tables that exist in your warehouse but aren't managed by dbt - typically raw data loaded by ETL tools like Fivetran, Stitch, or custom pipelines. These tables are defined in sources.yml. Using source() instead of hardcoded table names provides documentation, lineage tracking, environment-aware resolution, and enables source freshness checks. The ref() function is exclusively for dbt models (tables/views created by dbt). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/source
E_2: Incorrect. ref() works across all schemas within your dbt project, regardless of where models are located. You use ref('model_name') whether the referenced model is in the same schema or a different one. Schema is not the determining factor - whether dbt manages the object is.
E_3: Incorrect. Package models are referenced using ref() with the package name: ref('package_name', 'model_name') or just ref('model_name') if unique. Source() is only for raw data tables, not dbt models from packages.
E_4: Incorrect. Both source() and ref() compile to direct table references in the final SQL - there's no performance difference. They're both Jinja functions that resolve to fully-qualified table names at compile time. The choice is about what type of object you're referencing.
E_5: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are still dbt models and are referenced using ref(). The source() function is only for tables not created by dbt. Ephemeral models are compiled as CTEs when referenced via ref().
Explanation: OVERALL: Understanding when to use source() vs ref() is fundamental to dbt: Use source() for: (1) Raw data tables loaded by external tools, (2) Tables not managed by dbt, (3) Any table defined in sources.yml. Use ref() for: (1) Models created by dbt (.sql files in models/), (2) Snapshots, (3) Seeds (CSV files), (4) Models from dbt packages. Benefits of source(): (1) Documents external data dependencies, (2) Enables source freshness monitoring, (3) Creates lineage from raw data through transformations, (4) Allows environment-specific source configuration, (5) Validates at compile time that sources are defined. Never hardcode raw table names - always define them as sources. The source() function takes two arguments: schema/source name and table name: {{ source('salesforce', 'accounts') }}. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/source and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources

Question: What is the purpose of the --defer flag when using state-based selection in dbt?
Q_1: To delay execution of selected models until a specific time
Q_2: To skip models that have already been built in a previous run
Q_3: To defer error handling to a later stage in the pipeline
Q_4: To reference upstream production data for models not being built in the current run, enabling efficient CI testing
Q_5: To delegate model execution to a different dbt project or warehouse
E_1: Incorrect. --defer has nothing to do with scheduling or delayed execution. It's about referencing production data for models not being built in the current run. The name 'defer' means deferring to production for upstream dependencies.
E_2: Incorrect. --defer doesn't skip models. It changes where unselected upstream models are referenced from. Models selected for the current run still execute; --defer affects only the upstream models you're not running.
E_3: Incorrect. --defer has no relationship to error handling. It's specifically for state-based operations, telling dbt where to find upstream models that aren't being run.
E_4: Correct. When using --defer with --state, dbt will reference production tables for upstream models that aren't being built in the current run, instead of trying to reference them in your development schema where they don't exist. This is essential for CI workflows: when testing a PR that only modifies fct_sales, you want stg_orders references to use production stg_orders data (via defer), not try to find stg_orders in CI schema. Combine with state:modified+ to build only changed models while deferring to production for everything else. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/defer
E_5: Incorrect. --defer doesn't delegate execution to other systems. It's a reference resolution mechanism within a single dbt run, telling dbt to use production database objects for models not being built.
Explanation: OVERALL: The --defer flag works with --state to enable slim CI. How it works: (1) --state points to production manifest.json, (2) state:modified identifies changed models, (3) --defer tells dbt: for upstream models NOT in the current run, use production tables instead of dev/CI tables. Example: dbt build --select state:modified+ --defer --state ./prod-artifacts/. Without --defer: CI would fail when trying to ref() upstream models that don't exist in CI schema. With --defer: CI uses production tables for upstream dependencies, only building modified models and their downstream dependencies. Benefits: faster CI (only build what changed), no need to build entire project in CI, test changes against production data. Requirements: production manifest.json must be available, production schema must be accessible. The --defer flag makes state-based selection practical for CI/CD. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/defer and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/cloud-ci-job

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are best practices when implementing data quality tests in dbt? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Test primary keys with both unique and not_null tests to ensure data integrity
Q_2: Run all tests with severity: error to ensure maximum data quality enforcement
Q_3: Avoid testing source data to reduce test execution time
Q_4: Use store_failures configuration to capture and analyze rows that fail tests
Q_5: Implement tests at multiple layers (sources, staging, marts) with different validation goals at each layer
Q_6: Use descriptive test names and add metadata (meta fields) to document test purpose and expected behavior
E_1: Correct. Primary keys should pass both tests: unique ensures no duplicates, not_null ensures no missing keys. Testing keys is critical because downstream joins and relationships depend on them. A key column that fails either test indicates serious data quality issues. Apply both tests to every primary key column. This is a fundamental data quality check. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests
E_2: Incorrect. While strict enforcement seems ideal, some tests should be warnings (severity: warn). Use warn for: known upstream issues being fixed, aspirational tests for future state, or checks that shouldn't block pipelines. A mix of error and warn severities provides flexibility while maintaining quality gates.
E_3: Incorrect. Testing sources is crucial for 'shift-left' quality management. Source tests catch data quality issues at ingestion before they propagate through transformations. Test sources for: not_null on critical fields, accepted_values for categories, relationships to other sources, and data type validity. Early detection is cheaper than downstream fixes.
E_4: Correct. store_failures: true creates a table with failing rows, enabling investigation: Which records failed? Why did they fail? Is it a data issue or test issue? How many failures over time? This transforms tests from binary pass/fail to investigative tools. Configure globally in dbt_project.yml or per-test. The failure tables become data quality dashboards. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_5: Correct. Layered testing strategy: (1) Sources: basic integrity (not_null, accepted_values), (2) Staging: standardization and keys (unique, relationships), (3) Marts: business rules and completeness (custom tests, aggregation validations). Each layer has different concerns. Don't wait until marts to test everything - test continuously through the pipeline. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/writing-custom-generic-tests
E_6: Correct. Tests are code and need documentation. For singular tests, use descriptive filenames: assert_revenue_never_negative.sql, not test1.sql. For generic tests in schema.yml, add context using meta fields: meta: {purpose: 'Ensure customer_id links to valid customers', severity_rationale: 'Orphaned orders break reporting'}. Good documentation helps future maintainers understand why tests exist and what failures mean. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta
Explanation: OVERALL: Comprehensive testing strategy includes: (1) Test coverage: all primary keys, all foreign keys (relationships), critical business rules, (2) Test organization: tag tests by purpose, layer, criticality, (3) Failure handling: store_failures for investigation, severity levels for appropriate responses, (4) Documentation: explain test purpose in meta fields, (5) Monitoring: track test failure rates over time, alert on critical failures, (6) Continuous improvement: when bugs escape to production, add tests that would have caught them. Balance test coverage with execution time - not every column needs every test. Focus on: keys (unique, not_null), relationships (foreign keys), business rules (custom tests), and data ranges (accepted_values, custom range tests). Tests are living documentation of data contracts and expectations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices

Question: You want to pass configuration values to your dbt models that can be different across development, staging, and production environments. What is the BEST way to manage these environment-specific values?
Q_1: Hardcode different values in each model and use Jinja if statements to check the target.name
Q_2: Create separate dbt_project.yml files for each environment and swap them during deployment
Q_3: Use environment variables directly in your models with the env_var() function
Q_4: Define a different macro for each environment and call the appropriate one based on target.name
Q_5: Define variables in dbt_project.yml with default values and override them using --vars flag or in target-specific configuration
E_1: Incorrect. Hardcoding environment-specific values in every model is not maintainable. This approach scatters configuration across many files, making it hard to manage and update. Centralized configuration through variables is much better.
E_2: Incorrect. Managing multiple dbt_project.yml files is error-prone and creates unnecessary complexity. dbt's variable system is designed specifically for environment-specific configuration without duplicating project files.
E_3: Incorrect. While env_var() works, it's not the best practice for environment-specific configuration. Environment variables are better suited for secrets (credentials) than for configuration values. For configuration, the var() function with different values per target is cleaner and more maintainable.
E_4: Incorrect. Creating multiple environment-specific macros adds unnecessary code duplication. The var() function with dbt_project.yml or CLI variable overrides is the standard, cleaner approach for environment-specific configuration.
E_5: Correct. This is the recommended pattern: (1) Define variables in dbt_project.yml with sensible defaults, (2) Override for specific targets in profiles.yml or using --vars flag in CI/CD, (3) Access in models with {{ var('variable_name') }}. For example, you might set a lookback_days variable that's 7 in dev but 30 in prod. This approach centralizes configuration, is easy to maintain, and follows dbt best practices. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/var
Explanation: OVERALL: The var() function is dbt's mechanism for project-level configuration that can vary by environment. Define variables in the vars: section of dbt_project.yml, then access them using {{ var('name') }} or {{ var('name', 'default') }} in your models. Override variables per environment by: (1) Using --vars CLI flag: dbt run --vars '{var_name: value}', (2) Setting in profiles.yml under target-specific config (less common), or (3) Using environment-based logic in dbt_project.yml: vars: {var_name: "{{ 'prod_value' if target.name == 'prod' else 'dev_value' }"}. Common use cases: date ranges, sample sizes for dev, feature flags, schema name suffixes. Variables are compiled at parse time, making them efficient. For secrets, use env_var() instead of var(). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/project-variables and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/var

Question: You need to apply the same configuration (like materialization or schema) to multiple models in a directory. What is the BEST way to do this?
Q_1: Add a config() block to each model file with the same settings
Q_2: Create a macro that generates the config and call it from each model
Q_3: Configure the directory in dbt_project.yml under the models: section using the directory path
Q_4: Use tags to identify models and configure based on tags
Q_5: Create a separate schema.yml file with model-level configuration for each model
E_1: Incorrect. While config blocks work, duplicating configuration across multiple files violates DRY principles and creates maintenance issues. When you need to change the configuration, you'd have to update every file. Centralized configuration is better.
E_2: Incorrect. Using a macro for configuration is unnecessarily complex. dbt provides built-in mechanisms for applying configuration to multiple models through dbt_project.yml's hierarchical structure. Save macros for SQL logic, not configuration.
E_3: Correct. dbt_project.yml allows hierarchical configuration where settings cascade to all models in a directory and subdirectories. For example: models: my_project: marts: materialized: table schema: marts. All models in the marts/ directory inherit these settings unless overridden. Use + prefix for configs that should be inherited: +materialized: table. This is the recommended way to apply consistent configuration across multiple models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/model-configs
E_4: Incorrect. Tags are for selection and metadata, not the primary configuration mechanism. While you can configure based on tags in dbt_project.yml, directory-based configuration is more maintainable for applying settings to all models in a location. Tags are better for cross-cutting concerns that don't follow directory structure.
E_5: Incorrect. schema.yml is for resource properties (tests, descriptions), not for model configurations like materialization or schema. Model configs belong in the model's config() block or in dbt_project.yml. Using schema.yml for this would be incorrect syntax.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt_project.yml provides hierarchical configuration following your directory structure. Configuration precedence (most specific wins): (1) In-file config() block, (2) schema.yml model-level config, (3) dbt_project.yml model-specific config, (4) dbt_project.yml directory config, (5) dbt_project.yml project config, (6) dbt defaults. Use + prefix for inheritable configs: +materialized: table vs materialized: table (non-inheritable). Common directory-based configurations: materialization strategy, schema names, tags, meta fields, database, pre/post-hooks. Example structure: models: project_name: staging: +materialized: view +schema: staging marts: +materialized: table +schema: analytics. This keeps configuration DRY, organized by directory structure, and easy to maintain. Override at the model level when exceptions are needed. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/model-configs and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt_project.yml

Question: You want to generate comprehensive dbt documentation that includes model lineage, column descriptions, and source freshness information. What command generates the documentation site?
Q_1: dbt compile --generate-docs
Q_2: dbt run --docs
Q_3: dbt docs generate
Q_4: dbt test --catalog
Q_5: dbt build --generate-catalog
E_1: Incorrect. dbt compile only compiles your models to SQL without running them or generating documentation. There's no --generate-docs flag. Documentation generation is a separate command.
E_2: Incorrect. dbt run executes your models but doesn't generate documentation. There's no --docs flag for dbt run. Documentation generation requires a specific command.
E_3: Correct. The dbt docs generate command creates the documentation artifacts (catalog.json and manifest.json) by introspecting your database and compiling your project. After generating, use dbt docs serve to view the documentation site locally in a browser. The documentation includes the DAG, model descriptions from schema.yml, column-level lineage, source freshness results, compiled SQL, and more. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs
E_4: Incorrect. dbt test runs data tests and doesn't generate documentation. There's no --catalog flag. The catalog.json artifact is created by dbt docs generate, not dbt test.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt build runs models and tests but doesn't generate documentation artifacts. There's no --generate-catalog flag. Use dbt docs generate specifically for documentation.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt's documentation workflow: (1) Add descriptions to models and columns in schema.yml files, (2) Create docs blocks for rich markdown content, (3) Run dbt docs generate to create manifest.json (project structure, dependencies) and catalog.json (warehouse metadata, statistics), (4) Run dbt docs serve to launch a local webserver and view docs in browser. In dbt Cloud, documentation is automatically generated and hosted. The docs site includes: interactive DAG visualization, model descriptions and column details, compiled SQL, source freshness results, test coverage, lineage graphs, and project metadata. Documentation artifacts can be uploaded to cloud storage for team access. Keep docs updated by running dbt docs generate after significant changes. Use --no-compile flag if you've already compiled: dbt docs generate --no-compile. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/cmd-docs

Question: You need to create a model that calculates a running total of sales by customer. Which dbt pattern is MOST appropriate for this use case?
Q_1: Use a macro that iterates through customers with a for loop and calculates running totals for each
Q_2: Create an incremental model that updates running totals with each new sale
Q_3: Use a SQL window function (SUM() OVER()) with appropriate PARTITION BY and ORDER BY clauses
Q_4: Create a Python model using pandas to calculate cumulative sums
Q_5: Use dbt's ref() function to create self-joins that calculate cumulative totals
E_1: Incorrect. Using Jinja for loops to iterate through data is an anti-pattern in dbt. Jinja compiles at build time, not runtime, so it can't iterate through actual data rows. Even if you could, this would be extremely inefficient. SQL window functions are the correct approach for running totals.
E_2: Incorrect. While incremental models are good for large datasets, maintaining running totals incrementally is complex and error-prone. When historical data changes or corrections occur, incremental running totals can become inaccurate. Window functions calculated on the full dataset are more reliable.
E_3: Correct. Window functions are the standard SQL approach for calculating running totals. For example: SUM(sales_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW). This calculates the running total per customer, ordered by date. Window functions are efficient, readable, and supported by all modern data warehouses. This is a standard dbt model pattern that leverages SQL capabilities. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/sql-reference/window-functions
E_4: Incorrect. While dbt supports Python models, using Python for a simple running total is overkill and less efficient than SQL window functions. Python models are best for complex statistical analysis, ML operations, or transformations that are difficult to express in SQL. Window functions handle running totals efficiently in SQL.
E_5: Incorrect. Self-joins for running totals are an old SQL pattern from before window functions existed. They create inefficient queries with poor performance, especially on large datasets. Modern SQL window functions are far more efficient and readable for this use case.
Explanation: OVERALL: Window functions (also called analytic functions) are essential for calculations that need to reference multiple rows relative to the current row. For running totals, the SUM() window function with ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW is the standard pattern. Key concepts: (1) PARTITION BY divides the data into groups (e.g., by customer), (2) ORDER BY determines the sequence for the running calculation, and (3) the frame clause (ROWS BETWEEN...) defines which rows to include in each calculation. Window functions are efficient because they're performed by the warehouse query engine, avoid self-joins, and produce clean, maintainable SQL. They're ideal for rankings, moving averages, lead/lag comparisons, and cumulative metrics. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/blog/how-to-write-window-functions and https://docs.getdbt.com/sql-reference/window-functions

Question: A dbt test is failing but the error message is unclear. You want to see the actual SQL query that dbt generated for the test. Where should you look?
Q_1: Check the compiled test SQL in the target/compiled/<project_name>/tests/ directory
Q_2: Run the test with --debug flag and search the console output for the SQL query
Q_3: Check the test definition in your schema.yml file
Q_4: Query your warehouse's query history to find the test SQL that was executed
Q_5: Tests don't generate SQL; they use dbt's built-in validation logic
E_1: Correct. All dbt tests compile to SQL queries that return rows representing test failures (0 rows = test passes). The compiled SQL is stored in target/compiled/<project_name>/tests/ for singular tests or in the appropriate path for generic tests. Examining this compiled SQL shows you exactly what dbt is running, including all Jinja rendering and parameter substitution. This is essential for debugging complex tests. You can also find the executed SQL in target/run/ after the test runs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/compiled-sql
E_2: Incorrect. While --debug provides verbose logging, it's not the most efficient way to see the compiled SQL. The logs can be overwhelming and the SQL may be truncated. Looking directly at the compiled SQL file in target/compiled/ is clearer and easier to copy for manual testing.
E_3: Incorrect. schema.yml contains the test configuration (which test to run, parameters), but not the compiled SQL. Generic tests are macros that generate SQL based on these parameters. You need to see the final compiled SQL with parameters substituted, which is in target/compiled/.
E_4: Incorrect. While the warehouse query history will show executed SQL, this requires warehouse access and searching through potentially many queries. The compiled SQL file is immediately available locally and is specifically organized by test name, making it much easier to find.
E_5: Incorrect. All dbt tests are ultimately SQL queries. Even built-in tests like unique and not_null are macros that generate SQL. The test SQL is compiled and executed against your warehouse, with failing rows being returned. Understanding that tests are SQL queries is fundamental to debugging them.
Explanation: OVERALL: Understanding that tests are SQL queries that return failing rows is key to debugging them. When a test fails, examine the compiled SQL to understand what's being checked. You can copy this SQL and run it manually in your warehouse to see which specific rows are failing and why. The test query pattern is: select rows that violate the test condition. For example, a unique test selects all duplicate values. When debugging: (1) Find the compiled SQL in target/compiled/, (2) Copy and run it in your warehouse, (3) Examine the failing rows, (4) Determine if it's a data issue or test configuration issue. For generic tests, you can also examine the macro definition in the package or dbt_packages/ directory to understand the underlying logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/run-results-json

Question: You need to create a model that unions multiple similarly structured tables together (e.g., sales_2021, sales_2022, sales_2023). What is the BEST dbt pattern for this use case?
Q_1: Write separate SELECT statements for each table and manually union them with UNION ALL
Q_2: Use the dbt_utils.union_relations() macro to dynamically union tables based on a pattern or list
Q_3: Create a Python dbt model that uses pandas concat to merge the dataframes
Q_4: Use a for loop in Jinja to iterate through table names and generate UNION statements
Q_5: Use dbt snapshots to capture historical versions of a single table instead of unioning multiple tables
E_1: Incorrect. While this works, it's not maintainable. Every time a new table is added, you must manually update the SQL. This approach doesn't scale and is error-prone. dbt provides better patterns for dynamic unions.
E_2: Correct. The dbt_utils.union_relations() macro is designed exactly for this use case. It accepts a list of relations (tables/views) and unions them together, handling column alignment, null filling for missing columns, and adding source metadata. Example: {{ dbt_utils.union_relations(relations=[ref('sales_2021'), ref('sales_2022'), ref('sales_2023')]) }}. For source tables, you can use source() instead of ref(). This macro is maintainable, reduces boilerplate, and handles edge cases. Reference: https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-utils#union_relations-source
E_3: Incorrect. Python models add unnecessary complexity for simple unions. SQL unions are more efficient and idiomatic in dbt. Python models are best for complex transformations that are difficult in SQL, not basic operations like unions.
E_4: Incorrect. While you could write custom Jinja loops, this is reinventing the wheel. The dbt_utils.union_relations() macro already provides this functionality with better error handling, column alignment, and maintainability. Don't build custom solutions when tested packages exist.
E_5: Incorrect. Snapshots are for tracking changes to records over time (SCD Type 2), not for combining multiple similar tables. The use case described needs a union, not snapshot functionality.
Explanation: OVERALL: The dbt_utils package provides several macros for common patterns. union_relations() specifically handles unioning similar tables/views. Key features: (1) Handles column alignment (matches columns by name), (2) Adds columns missing in some relations as NULL, (3) Can add source_relation column to track which table rows came from, (4) Supports include/exclude patterns for column filtering. Usage: First install dbt_utils in packages.yml, then: {{ dbt_utils.union_relations(relations=[ref('table1'), ref('table2')], exclude=['_metadata_column']) }}. You can also use graph.nodes or run_query to dynamically discover tables matching a pattern. For wildcard table unions (BigQuery) or external table patterns, you might combine this with custom macros or database-specific features. This pattern is common for consolidating data across time partitions, geographic regions, or business units. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/ and https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-utils

Question: Your dbt run completes successfully but the data in the materialized table looks incorrect. None of your tests are failing. What should you investigate FIRST?
Q_1: Check if the warehouse is experiencing performance issues
Q_2: Review recent git commits for changes to upstream models
Q_3: Increase the number of threads to improve data consistency
Q_4: Run dbt deps to ensure packages are up to date
Q_5: Examine the compiled SQL and test coverage to identify missing tests that would catch the data quality issue
E_1: Incorrect. Performance issues would cause slow queries or failures, not incorrect results. If the run completed successfully and tests passed, warehouse performance isn't the issue. The problem is likely in your logic or test coverage.
E_2: Incorrect. While upstream changes could affect results, if tests aren't catching the issue, checking git history without first understanding what's wrong isn't efficient. Start by understanding the specific data incorrectness, then trace backwards.
E_3: Incorrect. Thread count affects parallelization and performance, not data correctness. Threads don't cause incorrect results in properly written models. The issue is in the SQL logic or inadequate testing.
E_4: Incorrect. Package versions don't change spontaneously. If the model was working before and packages haven't been updated, this isn't the issue. Package problems would typically cause failures, not silent incorrect results.
E_5: Correct. If tests aren't failing but data is incorrect, you have a test coverage gap. First, examine the compiled SQL to understand what query is actually running and verify if the logic is correct. Then, identify what data quality checks are missing. For example: if a join is producing unexpected duplicates, you need a test for row counts or uniqueness. If aggregations are wrong, you need tests for expected totals. The workflow is: (1) Identify what's wrong with the data, (2) Check compiled SQL to see if logic matches intent, (3) Write tests that would have caught this issue, (4) Fix the model if needed. This prevents future regressions. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests
Explanation: OVERALL: When data is incorrect but tests pass, you have a test coverage problem. The debugging process: (1) Identify the specific incorrectness - which columns, which rows, what's wrong, (2) Examine compiled SQL to verify logic, (3) Determine what test would catch this issue (are there duplicates? wrong aggregations? missing records? incorrect calculations?), (4) Write the missing test, (5) Fix the model if needed. Common test gaps: row count validations (too many/few rows), relationship tests for joins, accepted_values for categorical data, range tests for numeric data, sum/total validations for aggregations, uniqueness for keys. Best practices: (1) Test at multiple layers, (2) Test both technical constraints (not_null, unique) and business rules (custom tests), (3) Use store_failures to examine failed rows, (4) Review test coverage regularly using dbt_project_evaluator. Remember: tests are as important as the models themselves - they're your quality gates and regression prevention. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are valid use cases for leveraging dbt state artifacts (manifest.json, run_results.json)? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Implementing Slim CI to run only modified models and their dependencies in pull request builds
Q_2: Storing historical data versions for time-travel queries
Q_3: Building custom observability dashboards by parsing run_results.json to track model execution times, test failures, and row counts
Q_4: Using manifest.json to understand project lineage, identify downstream impacts of changes, or generate documentation
Q_5: Replacing source tables with cached data to avoid querying the data warehouse
Q_6: Automating incident response by detecting failed models in run_results.json and triggering alerts
E_1: Correct. manifest.json from production enables state-based selection (state:modified+) which identifies code changes by comparing current code against production state. Combined with --defer, this enables Slim CI where only changed models and downstream dependencies are built/tested. This is the primary use case for state artifacts in CI/CD, reducing CI runtime by 90%+ in large projects. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/methods#the-state-method
E_2: Incorrect. State artifacts contain metadata about models, tests, and runs - not historical data. For historical data tracking, use snapshots (SCD Type 2). State artifacts are for comparing project structure and code changes, not data versions.
E_3: Correct. run_results.json contains detailed execution metadata: model timing, rows affected, test results, error messages. Teams parse this JSON to create custom dashboards showing: slowest models over time, test failure trends, data volume growth, execution patterns. This enables data-driven performance optimization and quality monitoring. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/run-results-json
E_4: Correct. manifest.json contains the complete project graph: all models, their dependencies, columns, tests, and metadata. Use cases: programmatically query lineage to find downstream impacts, generate custom documentation, validate governance policies, integrate with external tools (data catalogs, BI platforms), understand project complexity metrics. The manifest is a complete map of your dbt project. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json
E_5: Incorrect. State artifacts don't contain data, only metadata. They can't replace source tables or cache query results. For query result caching, use warehouse features or materialized views. State artifacts are metadata only.
E_6: Correct. Parsing run_results.json after dbt runs enables automated monitoring: detect model failures, identify which tests failed, track error patterns, trigger alerts (Slack, PagerDuty), and create incident tickets. Many orchestrators (Airflow, Prefect) parse run_results.json to determine downstream task execution. This creates observable, reliable data pipelines. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/run-results-json
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt generates several artifacts in target/ directory: manifest.json (project structure, lineage, metadata), run_results.json (execution results, timing, errors), catalog.json (database metadata from docs generate), sources.json (source freshness results). Use cases: (1) Slim CI with state comparison, (2) Custom observability/monitoring, (3) Lineage analysis and impact assessment, (4) Governance validation, (5) Integration with external tools, (6) Automated alerting and incident response. Best practices: (1) Upload production artifacts to cloud storage for state comparison, (2) Parse run_results.json in orchestrators for conditional logic, (3) Use manifest.json for custom documentation or validation, (4) Archive artifacts for historical analysis. Don't commit artifacts to git (they're generated files). In dbt Cloud, artifacts are automatically managed and accessible via API. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/dbt-artifacts and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/artifacts

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are valid use cases for dbt sources? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Documenting raw data tables from external systems that serve as inputs to your dbt models
Q_2: Implementing source freshness checks to monitor when upstream data was last updated
Q_3: Creating a central location to reference external tables using {{ source('schema', 'table') }} instead of hardcoded names
Q_4: Automatically creating staging models from source table definitions
Q_5: Replacing the need for staging models by directly using source tables in mart models
Q_6: Enabling source-level tests to validate data quality before transformation begins
E_1: Correct. This is the primary purpose of sources. They represent tables that exist in your warehouse but aren't created by dbt (loaded by Fivetran, Stitch, custom ETL, etc.). Defining sources in sources.yml provides documentation, enables source() references, and creates lineage from source systems to dbt models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources
E_2: Correct. Sources support freshness configuration with loaded_at_field and warn_after/error_after thresholds. dbt source freshness runs checks to validate that source data is being updated according to expected SLAs. This provides early warning of upstream pipeline failures. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources#snapshotting-source-data-freshness
E_3: Correct. The source() function is the recommended way to reference raw tables in dbt models. It provides: (1) Environment-aware resolution (can override source database/schema per target), (2) Automatic lineage tracking, (3) Compile-time validation that sources exist, (4) Protection from hardcoded table names. Always use source() for external tables, ref() for dbt models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/source
E_4: Incorrect. Sources are documentation and references, not model generators. While packages like dbt-codegen can generate staging model boilerplate based on sources, sources themselves don't automatically create models. You must explicitly create staging models that select from sources.
E_5: Incorrect. Sources are for referencing raw data, but best practice is to always create a staging layer between sources and marts. Staging models standardize naming, data types, and basic cleaning. Skipping staging and using sources directly in marts violates the layered modeling approach and creates maintenance issues.
E_6: Correct. You can apply tests (unique, not_null, accepted_values, custom tests) to source columns in sources.yml, just like model columns. Testing at the source level validates data quality at ingestion, helping you catch problems before they propagate through transformations. This 'shift-left' testing approach is a best practice. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/data-tests
Explanation: OVERALL: Sources are fundamental to dbt architecture, representing the boundary between data loading (EL) and transformation (T). Best practices: (1) Define all external tables as sources, (2) Never hardcode raw table names - always use source(), (3) Document sources with descriptions, (4) Configure freshness for time-sensitive data, (5) Test sources for data quality, (6) Create staging models that reference sources. Sources configuration includes: database, schema, tables, columns, loaded_at_field, freshness thresholds, tests, tags, and meta. The source() function resolves differently in different environments (dev database override in profiles.yml). Sources appear in dbt docs with lineage, freshness results, and test results. They're essential for understanding data provenance and managing the source-to-insight flow. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/source-properties

Question: [SELECT 2] When designing a data pipeline orchestration strategy with dbt, which approaches are considered BEST PRACTICES for handling failures? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Configure all models to use the --full-refresh flag by default to ensure data consistency after failures
Q_2: Set up the pipeline to automatically retry failed models with a --full-refresh flag
Q_3: Use a single large model to minimize the number of potential failure points in the pipeline
Q_4: Disable tests during production runs to prevent test failures from stopping the pipeline
Q_5: Implement alerts and monitoring to detect failures quickly, and use dbt retry or dbt run --select result:error+ to rerun failed models
Q_6: Design idempotent models that produce the same results when run multiple times, enabling safe reruns without data corruption
E_1: Incorrect. Running full-refresh by default is inefficient and unnecessary. Full refreshes should only be used when schema changes require it or when data corruption occurs. After a failure, you should use dbt retry or rerun only the failed models and their dependents, not refresh everything.
E_2: Incorrect. Automatically adding --full-refresh on retry is wasteful and can significantly extend recovery time. Most failures are transient (network issues, warehouse timeouts) or code errors that don't require full refreshes. Smart retry logic should preserve incremental behavior.
E_3: Incorrect. Large monolithic models are harder to debug, test, and maintain. They also mean that a single failure affects a larger portion of your pipeline. Following modular design principles with smaller, focused models makes failures easier to isolate and fix.
E_4: Incorrect. Tests are critical quality gates that should run in production. Disabling them means bad data can propagate downstream. Instead, you should configure which tests are blocking (fail the run) vs. warning (log but continue) using severity levels.
E_5: Correct. Quick detection through monitoring/alerting combined with intelligent retry logic is a best practice. The dbt retry command (or result:error+ selector) reruns only failed nodes from the previous run, avoiding unnecessary work while ensuring downstream dependencies are updated. This approach minimizes recovery time and compute costs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/retry
E_6: Correct. Idempotency is a fundamental principle for reliable data pipelines. Models should be designed so that running them multiple times produces the same result (e.g., using merge logic for incremental models, proper unique keys, deterministic transformations). This makes it safe to retry failed runs without worrying about duplicate data or inconsistent state. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models#how-do-i-rebuild-an-incremental-model
Explanation: OVERALL: Robust data pipeline management requires both operational practices (monitoring, alerting, smart retry logic) and design principles (idempotency, modularity). Monitoring helps you detect failures quickly, while dbt retry or result:error+ selectors allow efficient recovery. Idempotent model design ensures that reruns are safe and produce consistent results. Additional best practices include: using severity levels for tests (warn vs. error), implementing SLAs with dbt Cloud, modular model design for easier debugging, and proper error handling in custom macros. Avoid over-reliance on full-refresh, which should be reserved for schema changes or data corruption scenarios. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/job-triggers and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/test-selection-examples#run-models-that-failed-in-a-previous-run

Question: You want to include column-level lineage information in your dbt documentation to show how columns flow from sources through transformations to final models. What is the BEST way to achieve this?
Q_1: Manually document column lineage in the description field of each column in schema.yml
Q_2: Enable column-level lineage by documenting columns in schema.yml and ensuring dbt can parse column references in your SQL
Q_3: Install a third-party metadata management tool that integrates with dbt Cloud's API
Q_4: Use a post-hook macro that writes column relationships to a metadata table
Q_5: Add custom meta tags to each column definition that specify upstream column dependencies
E_1: Incorrect. Manual documentation of lineage is tedious, error-prone, and quickly becomes outdated as your models evolve. There's also no visual representation in dbt docs. dbt can automatically infer and display column lineage if properly configured.
E_2: Correct. dbt automatically generates column-level lineage when: (1) columns are documented in schema.yml files for sources and models, and (2) dbt can parse your SQL to understand column dependencies. The lineage appears in the dbt docs site, showing how each column flows through transformations. dbt parses SELECT statements to track which source columns contribute to each final column. This works best with explicit column references (not SELECT *). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/column-level-lineage
E_3: Incorrect. While third-party tools can provide enhanced lineage features, dbt has built-in column-level lineage in dbt Cloud (and partially in dbt Core). You don't need external tools for basic column lineage - it's available natively when you document your columns.
E_4: Incorrect. Post-hooks can't access column-level metadata about dependencies. They run SQL after model materialization but don't have access to the parsing information needed for lineage. dbt's built-in lineage feature is the correct approach.
E_5: Incorrect. While meta tags can store custom metadata, you don't need to manually specify upstream column dependencies. dbt automatically infers column lineage by parsing your SQL. Manual meta tags would be redundant and error-prone.
Explanation: OVERALL: Column-level lineage in dbt Cloud automatically traces how columns flow through your transformations. To enable it: (1) Document columns in schema.yml for sources and models, (2) Use explicit column references in SELECT statements (avoid SELECT *), (3) Ensure dbt can parse your SQL (some complex SQL patterns may not be fully parsed). In the dbt docs site, click on a column to see its lineage graph showing all upstream and downstream column dependencies. This helps with impact analysis (which columns are affected by changes?), debugging data quality issues, and understanding transformation logic. Column lineage is more detailed than model-level lineage and is particularly valuable for compliance, data governance, and troubleshooting. It's available in dbt Cloud and through metadata APIs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/column-level-lineage and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects#documenting-columns

Question: Your dbt project depends on multiple packages. You want to ensure consistent package versions across all team members and CI/CD environments. What is the BEST approach?
Q_1: Have each developer manually install the same package versions
Q_2: Use packages.yml with specific version pins and commit it to git, ensuring all environments use dbt deps
Q_3: Install packages globally on each machine's system to share across projects
Q_4: Commit the dbt_packages/ directory to git so everyone has identical package code
Q_5: Use environment variables to specify package versions dynamically
E_1: Incorrect. Manual coordination doesn't scale and inevitably leads to version drift. Different developers will have different versions, causing 'works on my machine' problems. Package management should be automated and version-controlled.
E_2: Correct. The packages.yml file should be committed to git with specific version constraints. Everyone running dbt deps will install the same versions. Best practices: (1) Pin exact versions for stability: version: 1.3.0, (2) Use version ranges for flexibility: version: ['>=1.3.0', '<2.0.0'], (3) Commit packages.yml to git (never commit dbt_packages/ directory), (4) Run dbt deps in all environments: local dev, CI/CD, production, (5) Update packages deliberately through PRs. This ensures consistency and prevents unexpected behavior from package updates. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages
E_3: Incorrect. dbt packages are project-specific, not system-level. Installing globally would create conflicts between projects that need different versions. Always install packages per-project using dbt deps.
E_4: Incorrect. Never commit dbt_packages/ to git. This directory should be in .gitignore. It's generated by dbt deps and can be large. Committing it causes merge conflicts, bloats your repository, and defeats the purpose of package management. Commit packages.yml instead.
E_5: Incorrect. While packages.yml supports Jinja, using environment variables for versions makes it hard to track which versions are actually being used and creates environment-specific behavior. Pin versions in packages.yml for consistency and clarity.
Explanation: OVERALL: Package management best practices: (1) Define dependencies in packages.yml with version constraints, (2) Commit packages.yml to git, (3) Add dbt_packages/ to .gitignore, (4) Run dbt deps after: cloning repo, pulling changes that modify packages.yml, or when packages.yml is updated, (5) Pin versions explicitly for production stability, (6) Use semantic versioning ranges for automatic minor/patch updates: '>=1.0.0,<2.0.0' gets bug fixes but avoids breaking changes. In CI/CD, always run dbt deps before dbt commands. For private packages or internal shared code, you can use git packages or local packages. Lock files (package-lock.yml, coming in future dbt versions) will provide even stronger consistency guarantees. Review package updates through PRs, test in dev before production, and document why specific versions are pinned. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/package-management

Question: A dbt snapshot is failing with an error about 'updated_at' timestamp values in the future. The error occurs during the snapshot comparison logic. What is the MOST likely root cause?
Q_1: Your source system uses a different time zone than your data warehouse
Q_2: The snapshot strategy is set to 'check' instead of 'timestamp'
Q_3: The snapshot's updated_at configuration is pointing to the wrong column
Q_4: The dbt_updated_at field in your snapshot table has become corrupted
Q_5: The source table contains data quality issues with future-dated or NULL timestamps in the updated_at column
E_1: Incorrect. While timezone differences can cause data inconsistencies, they typically don't cause snapshots to fail with 'future timestamp' errors. The snapshot logic itself doesn't validate whether timestamps are in the future - it just uses them for comparison.
E_2: Incorrect. The check strategy uses column value changes rather than timestamps, so it wouldn't produce a 'future timestamp' error. If this error is occurring, you're likely using timestamp strategy.
E_3: Incorrect. While pointing to the wrong column could cause issues, it wouldn't specifically cause a 'future timestamp' error. The error suggests the timestamp values themselves are problematic, not the column reference.
E_4: Incorrect. The dbt_updated_at field is managed by dbt and records when the snapshot captured changes. Source data corruption is more likely than dbt-managed field corruption. The error is about source timestamps, not dbt's metadata fields.
E_5: Correct. Snapshots using timestamp strategy rely on the updated_at column having valid, logical timestamp values. If source data has timestamps set to future dates (data quality issue), NULL values, or incorrect timestamps, snapshot comparison logic can fail or behave unexpectedly. This is a data quality problem in the source system. Solutions: (1) Fix data quality at the source, (2) Add data cleaning in a staging model before snapshotting, (3) Add a test to detect future timestamps, or (4) Use the check strategy instead of timestamp if the timestamp column is unreliable. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/snapshots#timestamp-strategy-recommended
Explanation: OVERALL: Snapshots capture slowly changing dimensions (SCD Type 2) by recording historical changes over time. The timestamp strategy tracks changes using an updated_at column, comparing source timestamps with previously captured values. Requirements for timestamp strategy: (1) A reliable updated_at column that increases monotonically, (2) No NULL values in updated_at, (3) No future-dated timestamps. When these assumptions are violated, snapshots can fail or produce incorrect results. Debugging snapshot issues: (1) Examine source data quality in the updated_at column, (2) Check for NULLs, future dates, or decreasing timestamps, (3) Review snapshot metadata columns (dbt_valid_from, dbt_valid_to, dbt_updated_at), (4) Consider check strategy if timestamp is unreliable. The check strategy compares specified columns for changes instead of relying on timestamps, making it more robust for poor quality timestamp data. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/snapshots and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/snapshot-configs

Question: Your organization wants to enforce a policy that all production models must have descriptions and at least two tests. Which dbt feature should you use to enforce this governance requirement?
Q_1: Configure dbt_project.yml with required_tests and required_docs settings at the project level
Q_2: Implement dbt project evaluator package or write custom checks using the dbt_project_evaluator or similar governance packages
Q_3: Add a pre-hook to every model that validates the presence of documentation and tests
Q_4: Manually review all pull requests to ensure models have proper documentation and tests
Q_5: Use dbt Cloud's API to query model metadata and reject deployments that don't meet standards
E_1: Incorrect. While dbt_project.yml supports many configurations, there are no built-in settings called 'required_tests' or 'required_docs' that enforce these requirements. You need a different approach to validate governance policies.
E_2: Correct. The dbt_project_evaluator package (and similar governance tools) provides pre-built checks for documentation coverage, test coverage, and other best practices. You can configure it to enforce rules like 'all models must have descriptions' and 'all models must have at least N tests'. These checks run as dbt tests and can fail CI/CD builds if governance policies aren't met. This is the recommended approach for automated governance enforcement. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_project_evaluator/latest/
E_3: Incorrect. Pre-hooks run SQL before model materialization and can't access metadata about documentation or tests. They're not designed for governance validation. You need to check metadata from schema.yml files, which requires a different approach.
E_4: Incorrect. Manual reviews are error-prone, time-consuming, and don't scale. While code review is important, governance policies should be automated so they're consistently enforced without relying on human vigilance.
E_5: Incorrect. While the dbt Cloud API can access metadata, building custom validation logic this way is unnecessarily complex. Governance packages like dbt_project_evaluator provide this functionality out of the box and integrate directly with dbt's testing framework.
Explanation: OVERALL: The dbt_project_evaluator package is specifically designed for governance and best practices enforcement. It provides tests that check for: documentation coverage, test coverage, model naming conventions, source freshness configuration, proper use of staging/intermediate/marts layers, and more. Install it via packages.yml, configure rules in dbt_project.yml, and run it like any other dbt test. Failed governance checks will fail your CI/CD pipeline, preventing non-compliant code from reaching production. You can also build custom governance checks by querying the graph object in macros or using dbt metadata queries. Other useful governance tools include dbt-checkpoint for pre-commit hooks and dbt-coverage for test/documentation coverage reporting. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/blog/align-with-dbt-project-evaluator and https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_project_evaluator/latest/

Question: Your dbt project has grown significantly and pipeline runs are taking too long. What is the FIRST optimization you should consider?
Q_1: Analyze the run logs to identify the slowest models and optimize them first (Pareto principle)
Q_2: Immediately increase the thread count to maximum to parallelize all models
Q_3: Convert all views to tables for faster query performance
Q_4: Change all models to ephemeral materialization to avoid creating database objects
Q_5: Implement incremental models for all tables regardless of size
E_1: Correct. The 80/20 rule applies to dbt performance: a small number of models typically consume most of the runtime. Start by reviewing run logs (or dbt Cloud's model timing) to identify bottlenecks. Focus optimization efforts on the slowest models first for maximum impact. Common optimizations include: converting slow views to tables, implementing incremental models for large datasets, optimizing SQL (removing unnecessary complexity, improving join logic), adding warehouse-specific optimizations (clustering, partitioning), and breaking monolithic models into smaller pieces. Measure, prioritize, optimize. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects
E_2: Incorrect. Blindly increasing threads without understanding bottlenecks can worsen performance by overwhelming the warehouse. Threads help when you have many independent models that can run in parallel, but if your bottleneck is a few slow models, more threads won't help. Optimize slow queries first, then tune concurrency.
E_3: Incorrect. Converting views to tables improves query performance but increases build time. This might actually slow down your pipeline if you're optimizing for build time. Make materialization decisions based on: how often the data is queried, freshness requirements, and build time constraints. Don't blanket-convert everything.
E_4: Incorrect. Ephemeral models can actually decrease performance since their logic is compiled into each dependent model. For complex transformations referenced by multiple downstream models, ephemeral creates redundancy. Use ephemeral sparingly for simple, lightweight logic.
E_5: Incorrect. Incremental models add complexity and aren't beneficial for small tables. The overhead of incremental logic (merge operations, maintaining unique keys) isn't worth it for datasets that rebuild quickly as full refreshes. Use incremental for large, growing datasets where full refreshes are slow.
Explanation: OVERALL: Performance optimization should be data-driven: (1) Measure: Review run logs, identify slow models, understand DAG structure, (2) Prioritize: Focus on the slowest models first (biggest impact), (3) Optimize SQL: Improve join logic, remove unnecessary complexity, filter early, (4) Choose appropriate materializations: incremental for large fact tables, tables for frequently queried dimensions, views for simple transformations, (5) Leverage warehouse features: clustering, partitioning, sort keys (via post-hooks or config), (6) Tune concurrency: adjust threads based on warehouse capacity and DAG structure, (7) Reduce scope: use Slim CI (state:modified+) for development, (8) Consider refactoring: break monolithic models into modular pieces. Monitor ongoing performance using dbt Cloud metrics or custom logging. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models#understanding-the-is_incremental-macro and https://discourse.getdbt.com/t/optimizing-dbt-performance/1247

Question: [SELECT 2] Which of the following statements about using the ref() function in dbt models are TRUE? (Select TWO)
Q_1: ref() automatically creates dependencies in the DAG, ensuring models run in the correct order
Q_2: ref() can only be used to reference models in the same directory as the current model
Q_3: ref() resolves to the appropriate schema and database at runtime based on your target configuration
Q_4: ref() requires the .sql extension when referencing models (e.g., ref('model.sql'))
Q_5: ref() can be used in schema.yml files to define test dependencies
Q_6: ref() provides better performance than hardcoded table names because it caches metadata
E_1: Correct. When you use {{ ref('model_name') }} in a model, dbt automatically adds a dependency edge in the directed acyclic graph (DAG). This ensures that model_name runs before the current model. dbt uses these dependencies to determine execution order and parallelization. This automatic dependency management is one of ref()'s most important features. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_2: Incorrect. ref() can reference models anywhere in your project, regardless of directory structure. The model name (filename without .sql) must be unique across the entire project, but location doesn't matter. You can ref('staging_customers') from any model, whether it's in the same folder or not.
E_3: Correct. ref() compiles to the fully-qualified table reference (database.schema.table) based on your environment configuration. In dev, it might resolve to 'dev_schema.model_name', while in prod it resolves to 'prod_schema.model_name'. This environment-aware resolution allows the same code to work across different targets without hardcoding table names. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_4: Incorrect. ref() takes only the model name without the file extension. Use {{ ref('model_name') }}, not {{ ref('model_name.sql') }}. The .sql extension is part of the filename but not part of the dbt resource name.
E_5: Incorrect. ref() is a Jinja function that only works in SQL and Jinja contexts (models, macros, tests). It cannot be used in YAML files like schema.yml. In YAML, you reference models by name directly in the 'models:' section.
E_6: Incorrect. ref() doesn't provide performance benefits through caching. Its benefits are environment portability, automatic dependency tracking, and compile-time validation. At query execution time, it resolves to a regular table reference with no special performance characteristics.
Explanation: OVERALL: The ref() function is fundamental to dbt's architecture, providing two critical capabilities: dependency management and environment-aware table resolution. When dbt compiles your project, it parses all ref() calls to build the DAG, determines execution order, and identifies which models can run in parallel. At compile time, ref() resolves to the actual table name based on your target configuration (development, staging, production), custom schemas, and database settings. This makes your code portable across environments. Additional benefits: ref() enables dbt to validate that referenced models exist, supports cross-database references (in dbt Cloud), and integrates with state-based selection (state:modified+). Always use ref() for dbt models and source() for raw tables. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects

Question: [SELECT 2] When implementing a multi-project dbt architecture where different teams maintain separate dbt projects, which approaches help maintain governance and prevent issues? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Use model access controls (public, protected, private) to define clear interfaces between projects and prevent unintended dependencies
Q_2: Implement cross-project dependencies using dbt packages or project dependencies to share models between teams
Q_3: Copy models from other projects into your project to avoid cross-project dependencies
Q_4: Deploy all projects to the same database schema to simplify references
Q_5: Require all projects to use identical dbt versions and configurations
Q_6: Merge all projects into a single monolithic dbt project for easier governance
E_1: Correct. Model access controls prevent teams from accidentally depending on another team's internal models. Mark only stable, documented models as 'public' for cross-project use, keep implementation details 'private', and use 'protected' for within-project sharing. This creates clear API boundaries between teams and prevents breaking changes from rippling across projects. Combined with contracts on public models, this enables safe multi-team development. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_2: Correct. In multi-project setups, teams can share models via: (1) dbt packages (install another team's project as a package), or (2) Cross-project ref (dbt v1.6+, reference models from another project). This allows Team A to build on Team B's public models while maintaining separate codebases. Use dependencies config in dbt_project.yml to declare cross-project dependencies. This approach supports: independent deployment schedules, team ownership boundaries, and selective sharing of public models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/project-dependencies
E_3: Incorrect. Copying models defeats the purpose of multi-project architecture and creates maintenance nightmares. You lose the benefits of the source team's updates, create code duplication, and break lineage. Always reference models from other projects rather than copying them.
