E_4: Incorrect. Deploying to the same schema eliminates team boundaries and creates namespace conflicts. Each team/project should have its own schema(s). Use ref() to reference across projects - it resolves schema names automatically. Shared schemas undermine multi-project governance.
E_5: Incorrect. One benefit of multi-project architecture is that teams can upgrade dbt independently. Requiring identical versions creates deployment bottlenecks. As long as public model contracts are maintained, projects can use different dbt versions.
E_6: Incorrect. This defeats the purpose of multi-project architecture. Separate projects enable: team autonomy, independent deployment, clear ownership boundaries, and parallel development. Governance comes from access controls and contracts, not from consolidation.
Explanation: OVERALL: Multi-project dbt architecture (dbt mesh) enables large organizations to scale. Key components: (1) Model access - public models are the API, private models are internal, (2) Model contracts - enforce public model structure to prevent breaking changes, (3) Project dependencies - declare which projects depend on others, (4) Cross-project ref - reference public models from other projects, (5) Groups - organize models within projects by team/domain. Benefits: team autonomy, independent deployment, clear ownership, reduced blast radius of changes. Governance: each project maintains its own code/tests/docs, public models have contracts, changes to public models require coordination, private models can change freely. Best practices: minimize cross-project dependencies (favor within-project), version public models carefully, document public model contracts, use semantic versioning for project releases. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/project-dependencies and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-mesh/mesh-1-intro

Question: [SELECT 3] When implementing a production dbt pipeline, which of the following are recommended best practices? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Use dbt build instead of separate dbt run and dbt test commands to execute models and tests in DAG order
Q_2: Implement source freshness checks to alert when upstream data hasn't been updated as expected
Q_3: Run all models with --full-refresh flag in production to ensure complete data accuracy
Q_4: Disable all tests in production runs to minimize runtime and focus only on data transformation
Q_5: Configure appropriate run timeouts and retry logic to handle transient failures gracefully
Q_6: Use the largest possible thread count to maximize parallelization and minimize total runtime
E_1: Correct. dbt build runs models and tests in dependency order, executing tests on a model immediately after it's built and before downstream models run. This provides earlier failure detection and prevents bad data from propagating. It's more efficient than running all models first, then all tests. dbt build respects the DAG and test dependencies, making it ideal for production pipelines. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_2: Correct. Source freshness monitoring (dbt source freshness) validates that source data is being updated according to expected SLAs. Configure loaded_at_field and freshness thresholds in sources.yml, then run checks before your pipeline. This prevents wasted compute on pipelines when source data is stale and provides early warning of upstream issues. Essential for production data quality. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources#snapshotting-source-data-freshness
E_3: Incorrect. Full refresh should be used sparingly in production, only when necessary (schema changes, data corruption, historical recalculation). Running full refresh routinely is wasteful, increases runtime and costs, and provides no benefit for properly implemented incremental models. Incremental updates are the production best practice.
E_4: Incorrect. Tests are critical in production to catch data quality issues before they affect downstream systems or reports. Disabling tests defeats the purpose of quality gates. Instead, use severity levels (warn/error) to control which tests block the pipeline and which only alert.
E_5: Correct. Production pipelines should have resilience built in: model-level timeouts prevent hanging queries, job-level timeouts prevent runaway processes, retry logic (dbt retry or orchestrator retries) handles transient failures, and alerting notifies on-call when issues occur. This creates robust pipelines that can recover from temporary issues automatically. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/timeout
E_6: Incorrect. Thread count should be tuned based on warehouse capacity, not maximized blindly. Excessive threads can overwhelm the warehouse, cause queuing, increase costs, or trigger rate limits. Start conservatively (4-8 threads) and increase based on monitoring. Optimal thread count depends on warehouse size, query complexity, and resource availability.
Explanation: OVERALL: Production dbt pipelines require careful design for reliability, efficiency, and data quality. Additional best practices include: (1) Use environments (dev/staging/prod) with separate schemas, (2) Implement CI/CD with state:modified+ for PR validation, (3) Schedule jobs at appropriate intervals, (4) Monitor run times and optimize slow models, (5) Use incremental models for large tables, (6) Configure alerting for failures and test warnings, (7) Document SLAs and dependencies, (8) Implement proper error handling and logging, (9) Use dbt Cloud job features or orchestration tools (Airflow, Prefect), (10) Regular review of run logs and performance metrics. Production pipelines should be idempotent, observable, and recoverable. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deployments and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices

Question: Your incremental model is processing significantly more rows than expected on each run, causing performance issues. After checking the logs, you notice that dbt is performing a full refresh every time. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: The incremental_strategy configuration is set to 'delete+insert' instead of 'merge'
Q_2: The model's unique_key is not properly indexed in the target table
Q_3: The threads configuration in dbt_project.yml is set too high for your warehouse
Q_4: The is_incremental() macro conditional is missing or incorrectly implemented in your model's WHERE clause
Q_5: The target schema specified in dbt_project.yml doesn't exist in your warehouse
E_1: Incorrect. The incremental_strategy affects how records are updated but doesn't cause full refreshes. Both 'delete+insert' and 'merge' strategies work incrementally. If the strategy were causing issues, you'd see problems with the update logic, not a full refresh.
E_2: Incorrect. While missing indexes can cause performance issues during the merge/update process, they don't cause dbt to perform a full refresh. Indexing affects query performance, not dbt's decision about whether to run incrementally or do a full refresh.
E_3: Incorrect. The threads setting controls parallelization but has no impact on whether a model runs incrementally or as a full refresh. Thread configuration affects how many models run simultaneously, not the incremental logic.
E_4: Correct. If your incremental model doesn't properly use {% if is_incremental() %} to add filtering logic, dbt will process all historical data on every run, even though it's writing to an existing table. The is_incremental() macro returns true when the target table exists and --full-refresh is not specified. Without this conditional, your model lacks the logic to process only new/changed records. This is the most common cause of incremental models behaving like full refreshes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models#understanding-the-is_incremental-macro
E_5: Incorrect. If the target schema didn't exist, you'd get a schema creation or permission error, not a full refresh. dbt typically creates schemas automatically if they don't exist (depending on permissions). This wouldn't cause the specific issue of processing all rows every run.
Explanation: OVERALL: The is_incremental() macro is essential for incremental models to function properly. It should wrap your filtering logic (typically in the WHERE clause) to only apply when the model is running incrementally. For example: {% if is_incremental() %} WHERE event_time > (select max(event_time) from {{ this }}) {% endif %}. Without this macro, your model will select all source data every time, even though it's writing to an incremental table. The macro returns true when: (1) the target table already exists, and (2) the --full-refresh flag is not used. Always test incremental models by running them twice and checking the logs to confirm they're processing fewer rows on the second run. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/is_incremental

Question: You want to add custom descriptions to your dbt models that include dynamic information like the date the documentation was last generated. What is the BEST way to achieve this?
Q_1: Edit the catalog.json file directly in the target directory to add custom descriptions
Q_2: Add a description field in dbt_project.yml with Jinja variables for dynamic content
Q_3: Create a docs block in a .md file and use the doc() function in your schema.yml, utilizing Jinja variables within the docs block
Q_4: Use a post-hook to write custom descriptions directly to the database table's COMMENT field
Q_5: Install a third-party package that extends dbt's documentation capabilities
E_1: Incorrect. The catalog.json file is generated by dbt and should never be manually edited. Any changes would be overwritten the next time you run dbt docs generate. Documentation should be defined in schema.yml files or doc blocks.
E_2: Incorrect. While you can add model-specific configurations in dbt_project.yml, descriptions for models should be defined in schema.yml files. Additionally, Jinja in descriptions is limited - you can't use variables like run dates in regular description fields.
E_3: Correct. Docs blocks allow you to write longer, formatted descriptions in markdown files, which you can then reference in schema.yml using the doc() function. Within docs blocks, you can use Jinja to include dynamic content, environment variables, or other dbt context variables. For example: {% docs my_model_description %} This model was generated on {{ run_started_at }}. {% enddocs %}, then reference it in schema.yml with description: '{{ doc("my_model_description") }}'. This is the recommended approach for rich, dynamic documentation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation#using-docs-blocks
E_4: Incorrect. While you can use post-hooks to add table comments in the database, this doesn't affect dbt's documentation site. dbt docs are generated from schema.yml files and docs blocks, not from database metadata. Also, mixing documentation strategies would create inconsistency.
E_5: Incorrect. While packages like dbt-codegen can help generate documentation templates, they don't provide functionality for dynamic content that isn't already available through docs blocks with Jinja. Using docs blocks is the native, supported way to create rich documentation.
Explanation: OVERALL: Docs blocks are powerful features for creating rich, reusable documentation in dbt. You create them in markdown files (typically in a docs/ or models/ directory) and reference them using the doc() function in schema.yml. Docs blocks support markdown formatting, making them ideal for longer descriptions, and they can include Jinja templating for dynamic content. This approach keeps your documentation DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) when you need to reference the same content in multiple places, and provides better organization for complex documentation. You can also include code snippets, tables, and links in docs blocks. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/description

Question: [SELECT 2] When should you use the error_if and warn_if configurations for tests? (Select TWO)
Q_1: When you want all test failures to immediately stop the dbt run
Q_2: When you want to fail a test only if the number of failures exceeds a threshold (e.g., error if more than 100 rows fail)
Q_3: When you want to completely disable a test temporarily
Q_4: When you want to create warning states for test results that fall between acceptable and error thresholds
Q_5: When you want tests to run faster by sampling fewer rows
Q_6: When you want to automatically fix data quality issues detected by tests
E_1: Incorrect. This is the default behavior (severity: error) and doesn't require error_if/warn_if. These configurations are for conditional logic based on the number or percentage of failures, not for stopping runs on any failure.
E_2: Correct. error_if and warn_if create threshold-based test logic. For example: error_if: '>100' means the test only errors if more than 100 rows fail the test; fewer failures result in a pass. This is useful when some failures are acceptable but many indicate a real problem. Examples: allowing a small number of late-arriving records, tolerating minimal data quality issues, accepting edge cases. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/error_if
E_3: Incorrect. To disable a test, remove it from schema.yml or use enabled: false configuration. error_if and warn_if are for conditional pass/fail logic based on failure counts, not for disabling tests.
E_4: Correct. You can combine error_if and warn_if to create three-tier test results: pass (few/no failures), warn (some failures), error (many failures). Example: warn_if: '>10', error_if: '>100' creates: 0-10 failures = pass, 11-100 = warning, 100+ = error. This provides graduated responses to data quality issues. Warnings log the issue without blocking pipelines. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/warn_if
E_5: Incorrect. error_if/warn_if don't affect test execution or sampling. For sampling, use the limit configuration. error_if/warn_if only change how test results are interpreted (pass/warn/error based on failure count).
E_6: Incorrect. Tests detect issues but don't fix them. error_if/warn_if control test severity based on failure counts. To fix issues, you need to modify source data or transformation logic in your models.
Explanation: OVERALL: error_if and warn_if enable sophisticated test logic based on failure thresholds. Syntax: error_if: '' where condition can be: (1) Absolute count: '>100' (more than 100 failures), (2) Percentage: '>5%' (more than 5% of rows fail), (3) Any failure: '!=0'. Combined usage: tests: - unique: {column_name: id, warn_if: '>10', error_if: '>100'}. Use cases: (1) Uniqueness with known duplicates - allow some, error on many, (2) Referential integrity with acceptable orphaned records, (3) Freshness checks with grace periods, (4) Data quality during migrations (higher tolerance initially). These configurations provide flexibility between binary pass/fail and the real-world messiness of data. Different from severity (which is fixed warn/error): error_if/warn_if are dynamic based on failure count. Monitoring warn-level failures over time helps identify degrading data quality before it becomes critical. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/error_if and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/warn_if

Question: Your team wants to enforce that all models in the 'marts' folder have at least one test defined. How can you implement this requirement?
Q_1: Add a check in your CI/CD pipeline that counts tests per model and fails if marts models lack tests
Q_2: Configure a required_tests setting in dbt_project.yml under the marts directory
Q_3: Manually review all pull requests to ensure mart models have tests
Q_4: Use the dbt_project_evaluator package to validate test coverage and fail CI if marts models don't meet the requirement
Q_5: Use a pre-hook on all marts models that validates test presence before execution
E_1: Incorrect. While this could work, it requires custom scripting and maintenance. dbt packages provide this functionality out-of-the-box, making custom CI scripts unnecessary. Use existing tools rather than building custom validation.
E_2: Incorrect. There's no built-in required_tests configuration in dbt_project.yml. Test coverage enforcement requires external packages or custom validation logic. dbt's configuration focuses on materialization, schema, and other model properties, not governance requirements.
E_3: Incorrect. Manual review doesn't scale and is error-prone. Governance policies should be automated to ensure consistent enforcement without relying on human vigilance. Manual review is a backup, not the primary enforcement mechanism.
E_4: Correct. The dbt_project_evaluator package includes tests that check for test coverage, including the ability to specify minimum test counts per model. Configure it to enforce that all marts models have at least one test, and run it as part of your CI pipeline. The package's tests will fail if governance requirements aren't met, preventing non-compliant code from being merged. This is the standard approach for automated governance enforcement. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_project_evaluator/latest/
E_5: Incorrect. Pre-hooks run SQL before model execution and can't access metadata about tests. They're not designed for governance validation. Test coverage validation requires examining the manifest.json or using governance packages.
Explanation: OVERALL: The dbt_project_evaluator package provides comprehensive governance checks including: test coverage per model, documentation coverage, naming convention validation, source configuration completeness, proper use of staging/intermediate/marts layers, and more. To implement: (1) Add dbt_project_evaluator to packages.yml, (2) Run dbt deps, (3) Configure rules in dbt_project.yml (e.g., minimum test count, which models require tests), (4) Run dbt_project_evaluator tests in CI (dbt test --select package:dbt_project_evaluator), (5) Fail the build if governance tests fail. The package is configurable, allowing you to set different requirements for different parts of your project. Alternative approaches: dbt-checkpoint for pre-commit hooks, dbt-coverage for coverage reporting, custom macros that query graph.nodes to validate coverage. Governance automation ensures standards are maintained as projects grow and team members change. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_project_evaluator/latest/ and https://docs.getdbt.com/blog/align-with-dbt-project-evaluator

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are important considerations when implementing model access controls and governance in dbt? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Use model contracts to define and enforce the structure of public models that other teams depend on
Q_2: Grant SELECT permissions manually in the database after dbt runs complete
Q_3: Use model access (public, protected, private) to control which models can be referenced across dbt groups or projects
Q_4: Store all sensitive data transformations in ephemeral models to prevent database materialization
Q_5: Use dbt snapshots to track all data access for audit purposes
Q_6: Implement clear ownership using meta fields and tags to document which team owns each model
E_1: Correct. Model contracts (dbt v1.5+) allow you to enforce column names, data types, and constraints on models. When a model has a contract, dbt validates the model output matches the contract specification. This is crucial for public models consumed by other teams - contracts prevent breaking changes and establish clear interfaces. Configure with contract: {enforced: true} and define columns in schema.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-contracts
E_2: Incorrect. Manual permission management doesn't scale and can't keep up with model changes. Use automated approaches: grants config in dbt models, post-hooks with GRANT statements, or on-run-end hooks. Automate permissions so they're consistently applied when models are created or updated.
E_3: Correct. Model access controls (dbt v1.6+) define visibility boundaries. Models can be: private (only referenceable within same directory/group), protected (referenceable within same project), or public (referenceable by any project). This enables multi-team governance by preventing unintended dependencies on internal models. Configure with access: public/protected/private in model config. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-access
E_4: Incorrect. Ephemeral models don't provide security - they're compiled into downstream models as CTEs, so the logic is still executed. Security should be handled through: proper database permissions, encryption, data masking, column-level security, or separate schemas for sensitive data. Materialization strategy doesn't provide access control.
E_5: Incorrect. Snapshots track changes to dimensional data (SCD Type 2), not data access. For audit logging of data access, use warehouse query logs, audit tables via post-hooks, or warehouse-specific audit features. Snapshots serve a different purpose.
E_6: Correct. Governance requires clear ownership. Use meta fields (meta: {owner: 'data-team', slack: '#data-help'}) and tags (tags: ['team:analytics']) to document ownership. This enables: accountability for data quality, escalation paths for issues, change approval workflows, and team-based access patterns. Query manifest.json to generate ownership reports or integrate with external governance systems. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta
Explanation: OVERALL: Modern dbt governance uses several mechanisms: (1) Model contracts - enforce structure of public interfaces, prevent breaking changes, (2) Model access - control visibility and dependencies across teams/projects, (3) Ownership metadata - document accountability through meta and tags, (4) Automated permissions - use grants config or hooks for database permissions, (5) Groups - organize models into logical units with access boundaries, (6) Tests - enforce data quality standards, (7) dbt_project_evaluator - validate governance policies. Governance workflow: (1) Define ownership boundaries (groups), (2) Set access levels (public/protected/private), (3) Establish contracts for public models, (4) Document ownership (meta/tags), (5) Automate permissions, (6) Validate with governance tests, (7) Enforce through CI/CD. This creates a scalable governance framework for multi-team dbt projects. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/about-model-governance and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/govern/model-contracts

Question: A model that previously ran successfully is now failing with a 'relation does not exist' error referencing an upstream model. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: The database connection in profiles.yml has changed
Q_2: The upstream model's materialization was changed from table to view
Q_3: The upstream model was renamed, deleted, or moved to a different folder, breaking the ref() dependency
Q_4: The schema configuration in dbt_project.yml was updated
Q_5: The target environment changed from development to production
E_1: Incorrect. If the database connection changed, you'd likely see authentication errors or connection failures, not 'relation does not exist' errors. This error specifically indicates that dbt is successfully connecting but can't find a particular table/view.
E_2: Incorrect. Changing materialization from table to view doesn't make the relation disappear - it just changes how it's stored. The object still exists in the database with the same name, so downstream references would continue to work. This wouldn't cause a 'relation does not exist' error.
E_3: Correct. The 'relation does not exist' error typically occurs when: (1) An upstream model was renamed (the ref() still uses the old name), (2) The upstream model was deleted entirely, (3) The model was moved and the build order changed, causing downstream models to run before their dependencies, or (4) The upstream model failed to build in this run. Check git history for renames/deletions, verify the ref() references are correct, and ensure the upstream model is successfully building. The ref() function validates at compile time but the error occurs at execution if the referenced model hasn't been created yet. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref
E_4: Incorrect. While schema changes could theoretically cause issues if not properly deployed, dbt's ref() function resolves to the correct schema based on configuration. If schema configuration changed, the downstream model's ref() would resolve to the new schema location. This wouldn't cause a 'relation does not exist' error unless the upstream model wasn't successfully built in the new schema.
E_5: Incorrect. The ref() function is environment-aware and automatically resolves to the correct database/schema for the target environment. Changing environments shouldn't cause 'relation does not exist' errors if both environments are properly configured and models have been run in both.
Explanation: OVERALL: The 'relation does not exist' error indicates dbt is trying to select from a table/view that doesn't exist in the database. Common causes: (1) Upstream model renamed without updating ref() calls, (2) Upstream model deleted, (3) Upstream model hasn't run yet (build order issue), (4) Upstream model failed to build, (5) Wrong target environment (model exists in dev but not prod). Debugging steps: (1) Check if the referenced model exists: dbt ls --select , (2) Verify ref() syntax is correct, (3) Check run logs to see if upstream model built successfully, (4) Review recent git changes for renames/deletions, (5) Examine the DAG to verify build order, (6) Try dbt compile to check for compilation errors. Prevention: (1) Use ref() not hardcoded table names, (2) Test changes in development first, (3) Review DAG before major refactoring, (4) Use CI to catch broken refs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref and https://discourse.getdbt.com/t/relation-does-not-exist/1521

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are best practices for maintaining high-quality dbt documentation? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Document the 'why' behind transformation logic, not just the 'what', especially for complex business rules
Q_2: Only document mart-layer models since staging and intermediate models are internal implementation details
Q_3: Avoid using docs blocks since inline descriptions in schema.yml are always sufficient
Q_4: Use consistent naming conventions and document them so users understand the model naming patterns (e.g., stg_, int_, fct_, dim_)
Q_5: Leverage dbt's built-in column-level lineage to show data flow, making it easier for users to understand upstream dependencies
Q_6: Make documentation a required part of the development workflow by enforcing documentation coverage in CI/CD using tools like dbt_project_evaluator
E_1: Correct. The SQL shows what transformations do, but documentation should explain why decisions were made: business context, reasoning behind specific logic, edge cases being handled, assumptions about data. For example, don't just say 'filters orders by status' - explain 'excludes cancelled and pending orders because revenue is only recognized on completed transactions per accounting policy'. Context helps maintainers understand intent and make correct changes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation
E_2: Incorrect. All models benefit from documentation. Staging and intermediate models need documentation for: the data team to maintain them, understanding transformation flow, debugging issues, onboarding new team members. Comprehensive documentation at every layer creates a complete picture of your data pipeline.
E_3: Incorrect. Docs blocks are essential for: complex explanations that need formatting, reusable documentation across multiple models, including code examples or tables, detailed business logic documentation. Short descriptions work in schema.yml, but docs blocks enable rich, maintainable documentation for complex cases.
E_4: Correct. Naming conventions make projects navigable and predictable. Document your conventions (in a docs block referenced from project README) so users understand: stg_ = staging (source standardization), int_ = intermediate (business logic), fct_ = facts (metrics, events), dim_ = dimensions (attributes). Consistent naming plus documentation creates self-documenting code where users can find what they need. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/1-guide-overview
E_5: Correct. Column-level lineage (in dbt Cloud) automatically traces how columns flow from sources through transformations to final models. Enable it by documenting columns in schema.yml. Users can click a column and see: which source columns it derives from, what transformations were applied, which downstream columns depend on it. This visual documentation is more powerful than text descriptions for understanding data flow. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/column-level-lineage
E_6: Correct. Documentation coverage should be enforced, not optional. Use dbt_project_evaluator or custom checks in CI to ensure: all public models have descriptions, all columns in marts are documented, all sources are described. Failing CI when documentation is missing ensures quality stays high as projects grow. Documentation debt compounds - enforce it from the start. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_project_evaluator/latest/
Explanation: OVERALL: High-quality documentation is essential for data democratization and project maintainability. Best practices: (1) Document business context and reasoning, not just technical details, (2) Maintain documentation at all layers (sources, staging, marts), (3) Use docs blocks for complex explanations with markdown formatting, (4) Document and follow consistent naming conventions, (5) Document all columns in user-facing models, (6) Include metric definitions and calculation logic, (7) Link to external resources (business glossary, wiki, dashboards), (8) Use meta fields for ownership and operational metadata, (9) Leverage column-level lineage for visual documentation, (10) Enforce documentation coverage in CI/CD. Documentation should answer: What is this data?, Where does it come from?, How is it calculated?, When is it updated?, Who owns it?, How should it be used? Treat documentation as code - version control it, review it in PRs, update it with changes. Good documentation reduces support burden, enables self-service analytics, and preserves institutional knowledge. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices

Question: Your organization requires that all models containing PII (personally identifiable information) be tagged appropriately for compliance tracking. How should you implement this requirement?
Q_1: Create a separate schema for all PII models and configure it in dbt_project.yml
Q_2: Add comments in the SQL files indicating which models contain PII
Q_3: Create a custom macro that scans column names for common PII patterns
Q_4: Use tags and meta fields in schema.yml to mark models containing PII, then query the manifest.json for compliance reporting
Q_5: Use a pre-hook to log PII model executions to a separate audit table
E_1: Incorrect. While separate schemas can help organize data, they don't provide metadata for compliance tracking. You'd still need a way to identify which models contain PII. Tags and meta fields are better for metadata that supports tracking and governance.
E_2: Incorrect. SQL comments aren't machine-readable metadata that can be used for automated compliance tracking, reporting, or access control. Comments are useful for humans but don't integrate with dbt's metadata system or governance tools.
E_3: Incorrect. Automated detection based on column names is unreliable and won't catch all PII. It also doesn't create trackable metadata. Explicit tagging by developers who understand the data is more accurate and creates proper metadata.
E_4: Correct. Tags (e.g., tags: ['pii', 'sensitive']) provide categorical labels, while meta fields (meta: {contains_pii: true, pii_types: ['email', 'ssn']}) provide structured metadata. Both appear in manifest.json and can be queried programmatically for compliance reporting, access control decisions, and governance workflows. This approach is version-controlled, documented, and integrates with dbt's metadata system. You can also use these tags for selective operations (e.g., dbt run --select tag:pii). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta
E_5: Incorrect. Pre-hooks can log execution but don't mark which models contain PII. You'd need another mechanism to identify PII models first. Tags and meta fields provide the identification layer that pre-hooks or other tools can then use.
Explanation: OVERALL: Tags and meta fields are dbt's primary mechanisms for governance metadata. Tags are simple labels (strings in a list) useful for categorization and selection. Meta fields are key-value pairs for structured metadata. Use cases: (1) Compliance: tag:pii, tag:gdpr, meta: {data_classification: 'restricted'}, (2) Ownership: meta: {owner: 'data-team', slack_channel: '#data-alerts'}, (3) SLAs: meta: {sla_hours: 2}, (4) Business context: meta: {business_unit: 'sales'}. Both can be set at model, column, source, or project level. Query manifest.json programmatically to: generate compliance reports, enforce access policies, validate governance rules, or integrate with external systems. Combine with dbt_project_evaluator or custom tests to enforce governance policies (e.g., all PII models must have specific tests). This creates a robust governance framework. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta, https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags, and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json

Question: You want to use a macro from a dbt package in your project. After installing the package with dbt deps, what is the correct way to use the macro?
Q_1: Import the package in dbt_project.yml before using the macro
Q_2: Copy the macro code into your macros/ directory
Q_3: Add the package name to the packages list in profiles.yml
Q_4: Use an import statement at the top of your model file
Q_5: Call the macro directly using package_name.macro_name() syntax
E_1: Incorrect. There's no import mechanism in dbt_project.yml for packages. Once you run dbt deps, package macros are automatically available in your project without additional import steps.
E_2: Incorrect. Copying package macros defeats the purpose of using packages. You lose the ability to update the package and have to maintain duplicate code. Always reference package macros directly.
E_3: Incorrect. profiles.yml contains connection credentials and target configurations, not package definitions. Packages are defined in packages.yml, not profiles.yml.
E_4: Incorrect. While Jinja has import/include statements, they're not required for package macros. Package macros are in the global namespace after dbt deps. You can use them directly: {{ dbt_utils.surrogate_key(['col1', 'col2']) }}.
E_5: Correct. After running dbt deps, package macros are available in your project's namespace. Use them with the syntax: {{ package_name.macro_name(arguments) }}. For example: {{ dbt_utils.surrogate_key(['id', 'created_at']) }} or {{ dbt_utils.date_spine(...) }}. The package name prefix prevents naming conflicts if multiple packages have macros with the same name. Some macros may also be available without the prefix if they're in the global namespace, but using the prefix is clearer and safer. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages#using-a-package
Explanation: OVERALL: Using package macros is straightforward: (1) Define the package in packages.yml, (2) Run dbt deps to install, (3) Use macros with package_name.macro_name() syntax. Common packages and their macros: dbt_utils (surrogate_key, pivot, union_relations, date_spine, safe_cast), dbt_expectations (statistical tests), codegen (generate_model_yaml, generate_source), audit_helper (compare_relations, compare_queries). Package macros work exactly like your local macros but are namespaced by package name. You can also create your own packages for internal code sharing. For advanced usage, you can override package macros by creating a macro with the same name in your project (dispatching). Check package documentation for available macros, parameters, and usage examples. Package macros are compiled into your models just like local macros - no runtime overhead. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages and https://hub.getdbt.com/

Question: You want to run only tests that are tagged as 'critical' in your CI pipeline. What command should you use?
Q_1: dbt run --select tag:critical
Q_2: dbt test --select tag:critical
Q_3: dbt compile --tests tag:critical
Q_4: dbt build --select test_type:critical
Q_5: dbt test --models tag:critical
E_1: Incorrect. dbt run executes models, not tests. While you can select models by tag, this command won't run tests. You need dbt test or dbt build to execute tests.
E_2: Correct. The dbt test command with --select tag:critical runs only tests that have the 'critical' tag. Tags can be applied to tests in schema.yml (at the test level or column level) or in dbt_project.yml. This enables selective test execution for different scenarios: quick tests in CI, comprehensive tests nightly, critical tests before deployment. You can also use exclusion: dbt test --exclude tag:slow. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/test-selection-examples
E_3: Incorrect. dbt compile only compiles models and tests to SQL without executing them. There's also no --tests flag with this syntax. To run tests, use dbt test with selection syntax.
E_4: Incorrect. test_type is not a valid selector. The valid selectors for tests include: tag:, test_name:, test_type:generic or test_type:singular, etc. To select by tag, use tag:critical not test_type:critical.
E_5: Incorrect. --models tag:critical would select models tagged as critical, then run tests on those models. This is different from running tests that are tagged as critical. The question asks to run critical tests, not tests of critical models. Use --select tag:critical to select tests by their tags.
Explanation: OVERALL: Test selection in dbt uses the same syntax as model selection but targets tests instead. Common test selection patterns: (1) By tag: dbt test --select tag:critical, (2) By test type: dbt test --select test_type:generic or test_type:singular, (3) By model: dbt test --select orders (tests on the orders model), (4) By package: dbt test --select package:dbt_utils, (5) By source: dbt test --select source:*, (6) By state: dbt test --select state:modified+. You can combine selectors: dbt test --select tag:nightly,test_type:generic. Test tagging strategy: 'critical' for must-pass tests, 'slow' for expensive tests, 'nightly' for comprehensive tests, 'data_quality' vs 'schema' for different concerns. In CI, you might run: dbt test --select state:modified+ tag:critical. This runs critical tests on modified models and their dependents. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/test-selection-examples and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/syntax

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are benefits of using the dbt_expectations package for testing? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Provides statistical tests like checking if column values fall within expected standard deviations from the mean
Q_2: Offers regex pattern matching tests to validate data formats like email addresses, phone numbers, or IDs
Q_3: Includes row-level tests like checking if row counts match between tables or fall within acceptable ranges
Q_4: Automatically generates staging models from source tables
Q_5: Supports set-based tests to validate that column values are within specific sets or match values from reference tables
Q_6: Replaces the need for all built-in dbt tests by providing superior alternatives
E_1: Correct. dbt_expectations includes statistical tests inspired by Great Expectations: expect_column_mean_to_be_between, expect_column_stdev_to_be_between, expect_column_quantile_values_to_be_between, and more. These tests validate data distributions and catch anomalies that simple range checks miss. Perfect for detecting data drift or quality degradation over time. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/calogica/dbt_expectations/latest/
E_2: Correct. The expect_column_values_to_match_regex and expect_column_values_to_match_like_pattern tests validate string formats using regular expressions or SQL LIKE patterns. Essential for validating: email formats, phone number patterns, ID formats, SKU patterns, and other formatted strings. Much more powerful than simple accepted_values tests. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/calogica/dbt_expectations/latest/
E_3: Correct. Tests like expect_table_row_count_to_equal, expect_table_row_count_to_be_between, expect_row_values_to_have_recent_data validate table-level metrics. Useful for: comparing source to target row counts (data loss detection), ensuring minimum data volume, detecting stale data, validating that incremental models are adding rows. These catch high-level data quality issues. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/calogica/dbt_expectations/latest/
E_4: Incorrect. dbt_expectations is a testing package, not a code generation tool. For code generation (creating model scaffolding), use dbt-codegen package. dbt_expectations focuses solely on expanding dbt's testing capabilities with advanced data quality checks.
E_5: Correct. Tests like expect_column_values_to_be_in_set verify that all values match an expected list, expect_column_values_to_be_in_type_list validates data types, and expect_column_distinct_values_to_equal_set ensures exactly the expected distinct values exist. These are more sophisticated than accepted_values - they can check for unexpected additions, missing expected values, or exact set matching. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/calogica/dbt_expectations/latest/
E_6: Incorrect. dbt_expectations complements built-in tests, not replaces them. Use built-in tests (unique, not_null, accepted_values, relationships) for standard validations - they're simple and fast. Use dbt_expectations for advanced scenarios that built-in tests don't cover: statistical validation, pattern matching, complex table-level checks, multi-column relationships.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt_expectations significantly expands dbt's testing capabilities with 50+ additional tests inspired by Great Expectations. Categories: (1) Column-level: regex patterns, value ranges, set membership, null rates, (2) Statistical: mean, median, standard deviation, quantiles, (3) Table-level: row counts, column counts, data freshness, (4) Multi-column: composite keys, column combinations, (5) Temporal: recent data checks, date range validations. Installation: add to packages.yml, run dbt deps. Usage: same as built-in tests in schema.yml. Example: - dbt_expectations.expect_column_mean_to_be_between: {column_name: revenue, min_value: 100, max_value: 500}. Best for: complex data quality requirements, statistical monitoring, production data validation, regulated industries requiring comprehensive testing. Combine with built-in tests for comprehensive coverage. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/calogica/dbt_expectations/latest/ and https://github.com/calogica/dbt-expectations

Question: A dbt model fails with an error: 'Compilation Error: dbt found two resources with the name "customer_orders"'. What is the MOST likely cause of this error and how should you fix it?
Q_1: Two models in different directories have the same filename; rename one model or use an alias in its configuration
Q_2: The model is defined in both dbt_project.yml and in a schema.yml file; remove one definition
Q_3: The same model is being imported from two different dbt packages; uninstall one package
Q_4: The model references itself in a circular dependency; remove the self-reference
Q_5: The database has two tables with the same name in different schemas; specify the schema in each model's configuration
E_1: Correct. dbt uses the filename (without .sql extension) as the unique identifier for models, regardless of directory structure. If you have customer_orders.sql in both models/staging/ and models/marts/, dbt will throw a compilation error. You can fix this by either renaming one file (e.g., stg_customer_orders.sql and fct_customer_orders.sql) or by adding an alias configuration to one model to change its database object name while keeping the filename. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/alias
E_2: Incorrect. dbt_project.yml contains project-level configurations, while schema.yml contains resource properties like tests and descriptions. Neither file defines a model's existence - that's determined by the .sql file itself. You cannot create duplicate model definitions this way.
E_3: Incorrect. While package naming conflicts can occur, dbt handles package models by namespacing them (e.g., package_name.model_name). If two packages had the same model name, you'd reference them as package1.customer_orders and package2.customer_orders. This error specifically indicates resources in your project, not packages.
E_4: Incorrect. Circular dependencies produce a different error message about cycle detection in the DAG, not a 'found two resources' error. This error is specifically about duplicate naming, not dependency issues.
E_5: Incorrect. This error occurs during dbt compilation before any database interaction happens. It's about dbt's internal resource naming, not database table names. Even if models target different schemas, they can't have the same filename in dbt.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt requires unique names for all resources within a project. The resource name comes from the filename (without extension) for models. Even if files are in different directories, they must have unique names. To fix duplicate names, you have two options: (1) Rename the file itself (recommended, especially if you use consistent naming conventions like stg_ or fct_ prefixes), or (2) Use the alias configuration to change what the model is called in the database while keeping the filename. The alias approach is useful when you need to maintain specific database naming conventions but want different logical names in dbt. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/alias and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/custom-schemas

Question: Your organization wants to implement a code review process for dbt changes. What is the BEST practice for reviewing dbt pull requests?
Q_1: Only review the final SQL output in the target/compiled/ directory
Q_2: Focus review on performance metrics and query execution times
Q_3: Only check that all tests pass before approving the PR
Q_4: Use Slim CI to run only modified models and their dependents, review the code changes, compiled SQL, and data quality test results
Q_5: Require that all models have full-refresh run successfully before merging
E_1: Incorrect. While compiled SQL is important to verify, it's generated from source files. Code review should focus on the source .sql files, schema.yml, and macros that are version controlled. Compiled SQL helps understand what's being executed but isn't the primary review artifact.
E_2: Incorrect. While performance is important, it shouldn't be the only focus of code review. Reviews should cover: correctness, code quality, documentation, tests, naming conventions, and adherence to project structure. Performance can be validated through separate performance testing.
E_3: Incorrect. Passing tests are necessary but not sufficient. Code review should evaluate: business logic correctness, code maintainability, documentation quality, appropriate test coverage (are there enough tests?), adherence to team conventions, and potential impacts on downstream models. Tests validate data quality but don't catch all issues.
E_4: Correct. Best practice for dbt PR review includes: (1) Automated Slim CI that runs state:modified+ to validate changes efficiently, (2) Code review of source changes (.sql, .yml, macros), (3) Verification of compiled SQL for correctness, (4) Review of test results and coverage, (5) Documentation completeness, (6) Adherence to naming and structure conventions. Slim CI (using dbt Cloud or custom CI) catches issues early while reviewers focus on logic, maintainability, and business requirements. This combines automated validation with human judgment. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/cloud-ci-job
E_5: Incorrect. Running all models with full-refresh in CI is wasteful and slow. Slim CI that runs only modified models and their downstream dependencies is more efficient. Full-refresh should only be required when schema changes necessitate it, not for every PR.
Explanation: OVERALL: Effective dbt PR review combines automated CI and human code review. Slim CI setup: (1) Configure dbt Cloud CI job or GitHub Actions, (2) Use state:modified+ selector to run only changed models and downstream dependents, (3) Use --defer to reference production data for upstream models, (4) Run tests on modified models, (5) Generate and check documentation. Human review checklist: (1) Does the logic correctly implement requirements?, (2) Are naming conventions followed?, (3) Is the code maintainable and well-structured?, (4) Is documentation complete and accurate?, (5) Are there appropriate tests?, (6) Will this impact downstream models appropriately?, (7) Are there any performance concerns?, (8) Does it follow the project's layering strategy? Additional practices: require approval from code owners, use PR templates, link to related tickets, test in staging environment before production deployment. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/cloud-ci-job and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-build-our-metrics/semantic-layer-1-intro

Question: You need to grant SELECT permissions on all models in your dbt project to a specific role in your data warehouse. What is the BEST approach?
Q_1: Manually run GRANT statements in your warehouse after each dbt run
Q_2: Use database-level default privileges or schema-level permission inheritance
Q_3: Configure a post-hook at the project level in dbt_project.yml that executes GRANT statements after each model runs
Q_4: Create a Python script that queries dbt metadata and grants permissions separately
Q_5: Use the dbt run-operation command to execute a custom macro after dbt run completes
E_1: Incorrect. Manual permission management doesn't scale, is error-prone, and doesn't work with dynamic model creation. Permissions need to be automated and consistently applied whenever models are created or refreshed.
E_2: Incorrect. While database-level defaults can work, they're not dbt-specific and may grant broader permissions than intended. They also don't integrate with dbt's lifecycle, making them less flexible than dbt-native approaches. For dbt-managed permissions, use hooks or grants config.
E_3: Correct. Post-hooks are ideal for permission management. Configure once in dbt_project.yml under models: with +post-hook: "GRANT SELECT ON {{ this }} TO ROLE analyst_role". This executes after every model materializes, ensuring permissions are consistently applied. You can also use the grants config (newer feature) for cleaner syntax: +grants: {select: ['analyst_role']}. This approach is automated, version-controlled, and scales across all models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/grants
E_4: Incorrect. External scripts add complexity and aren't integrated with dbt's execution lifecycle. They require separate scheduling and can get out of sync with model changes. Use dbt's built-in mechanisms (hooks, grants) instead.
E_5: Incorrect. run-operation with a custom macro could work but is less elegant than post-hooks or grants config. It requires a separate command execution and doesn't automatically run with dbt build. Post-hooks integrate directly with model execution.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt provides two main approaches for permission management: (1) Post-hooks: +post-hook: "GRANT SELECT ON {{ this }} TO ROLE analyst_role" - flexible, can use complex SQL, (2) grants config (dbt 1.2+): +grants: {select: ['role1', 'role2']} - cleaner syntax, cross-database compatible. Both can be configured at project, folder, or model level. The {{ this }} Jinja variable references the current model's database object. For complex scenarios, create a macro that handles grants logic and call it from a post-hook. Best practices: (1) Configure at project level for consistency, (2) Use variables for role names to support different environments, (3) Consider separate roles for different model layers (staging, marts), (4) Test permission grants in development, (5) Document permission strategy. On-run-end hooks can also grant permissions on all models after a run completes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/grants and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/pre-hook-post-hook

Question: You need to add audit columns (created_at, updated_at, loaded_at) to all models in your marts directory. What is the MOST maintainable approach?
Q_1: Manually add the audit columns to each model's SELECT statement
Q_2: Create a macro that adds audit columns and use a post-hook to call it after each model builds
Q_3: Create a macro for audit columns and include it in each model's SELECT: SELECT *, {{ audit_columns() }} FROM ...
Q_4: Use a Python post-hook to add metadata columns after each model runs
Q_5: Configure audit columns in dbt_project.yml under the marts section
E_1: Incorrect. Manual addition to every model is error-prone and not maintainable. When you add new models or need to change the audit logic, you'd have to update multiple files. This violates DRY principles.
E_2: Incorrect. Post-hooks run SQL after materialization but can't add columns to the model's output - the table is already created. You'd need to ALTER the table, which is inefficient and doesn't work well with all materialization types. Add columns during the SELECT, not after.
E_3: Correct. This is the maintainable approach: (1) Create a macro: {% macro audit_columns() %} current_timestamp() as loaded_at, current_timestamp() as dbt_updated_at {% endmacro %}, (2) Include in models: SELECT *, {{ audit_columns() }} FROM ..., (3) Updates to audit logic only need macro changes. This keeps audit logic DRY, centralized, and easy to maintain. All models using the macro automatically get updates. For system-wide application, you could even use a pre-hook or model compilation, but including in SELECT is most transparent. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_4: Incorrect. Python in dbt is for Python models, not for post-processing SQL models. Post-hooks are SQL, not Python. Even if this were possible, modifying tables after creation is less efficient than including columns in the original SELECT.
E_5: Incorrect. dbt_project.yml handles model configurations (materialization, schema, tags) but can't inject SQL columns into SELECT statements. Column-level logic requires macros or SQL in the model files themselves.
Explanation: OVERALL: Audit columns are a common pattern in analytics. Best practices: (1) Create a reusable macro for standard audit logic, (2) Include macro in model SELECT statements, (3) For truly universal application, consider using dbt meta-programming (modifying queries during compilation), but this is advanced. Common audit columns: loaded_at (when dbt loaded the data), dbt_updated_at (when the record was last updated), row_created_at (from source), row_updated_at (from source). Example macro: {% macro audit_columns() %} {{ dbt_utils.current_timestamp() }} as loaded_at, '{{ invocation_id }}' as dbt_invocation_id, '{{ run_started_at }}' as dbt_run_started_at {% endmacro %}. This provides metadata for debugging, data quality analysis, and understanding data freshness. Some teams use macros that add both standard and custom audit columns based on parameters. For more complex needs, consider dbt packages like dbt-meta-testing. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/invocation_id

Question: You need to create a staging model that standardizes column names and data types from a raw source table. Which approach follows dbt best practices?
Q_1: Use a Python dbt model to perform the transformations using pandas
Q_2: Create a view materialization with complex CASE statements for each column transformation
Q_3: Use macros to generate boilerplate transformation code, but don't create a staging layer if transformations are simple
Q_4: Create a single large model that combines staging, business logic, and final transformations
Q_5: Create a staging model (typically a view) that selects from the source, renames columns using aliases, and casts to appropriate data types
E_1: Incorrect. Python models are best for complex statistical operations, ML workloads, or transformations difficult to express in SQL. Simple column renaming and type casting should be done in SQL for better performance and simplicity. Python adds overhead and is unnecessary for basic staging transformations.
E_2: Incorrect. While view materialization is often appropriate for staging, complex CASE statements for basic transformations is unnecessarily complicated. Simple CAST and column aliasing (AS) is cleaner. CASE statements should be reserved for conditional logic, not routine type conversions.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt best practices strongly recommend a staging layer even for simple transformations. Staging models establish the foundation: standardized naming, typing, and basic cleaning. They separate source system peculiarities from business logic, making downstream models easier to maintain.
E_4: Incorrect. This violates the layered modeling approach. Best practice is to separate concerns: staging (standardization), intermediate (business logic), and marts (final presentation). Large monolithic models are hard to test, debug, and maintain. The staging layer should be focused solely on standardization.
E_5: Correct. This follows the dbt staging layer pattern: SELECT original_col AS standardized_col, CAST(another_col AS INTEGER) AS standardized_col2 FROM {{ source() }}. Staging models establish consistent naming conventions (snake_case), fix data types, and perform basic cleaning. They're typically views (efficient, always fresh) and follow the stg_<source>_<table> naming convention. This creates a clean, standardized foundation for downstream models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging
Explanation: OVERALL: The staging layer is the first transformation layer in dbt projects, sitting directly on top of sources. Best practices for staging models: (1) Use view materialization for freshness and efficiency, (2) One staging model per source table, (3) Name with stg_ _ convention, (4) Perform only light transformations: renaming, type casting, basic cleaning, (5) Don't include business logic, joins, or aggregations, (6) Reference using {{ source() }}, not raw table names, (7) Add column-level documentation. Staging models establish data consistency, making downstream models simpler and more maintainable. They isolate source system peculiarities (weird column names, inconsistent types) from your analytics logic. For type casting, many teams use custom macros or dbt_utils.safe_cast() to handle conversion errors gracefully. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging and https://docs.getdbt.com/guides/best-practices

Question: [SELECT 2] Which of the following are benefits of maintaining comprehensive dbt documentation? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Documentation automatically fixes data quality issues in source systems
Q_2: Documentation replaces the need for data tests by providing context about expected data
Q_3: Documentation enables faster query performance by creating indexes on documented columns
Q_4: Documentation enables data discovery and self-service analytics by helping users understand available data and its meaning
Q_5: Documentation facilitates onboarding new team members and provides institutional knowledge about data transformations
Q_6: Documentation automatically generates unit tests for all models and columns
E_1: Incorrect. Documentation describes data and transformations but doesn't fix data quality issues. Data quality problems must be addressed through validation (tests), data cleaning (transformations), or fixes at the source. Documentation helps identify and communicate about issues but doesn't resolve them.
E_2: Incorrect. Documentation and tests serve complementary but different purposes. Documentation explains what data means and how it's transformed. Tests validate data quality and business rules. Both are essential - documentation alone can't enforce data quality.
E_3: Incorrect. Documentation doesn't affect database performance or create indexes. Performance optimization requires database-specific configurations (clustering, partitioning, indexing) that are separate from documentation. Documentation improves understanding, not execution speed.
E_4: Correct. Well-documented dbt projects allow analysts and stakeholders to explore the data catalog, understand what data is available, learn what columns mean, see how metrics are calculated, and find the right tables for their needs. This reduces the burden on data engineers to answer basic questions and empowers self-service analytics. The dbt docs site provides searchable, navigable documentation that serves as a data catalog. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation
E_5: Correct. Comprehensive documentation serves as a knowledge base for the team. New members can understand the project structure, learn business logic, see why transformations were implemented certain ways, and get up to speed faster. It prevents knowledge silos and preserves context when team members leave. Documentation in schema.yml, docs blocks, and the dbt docs site creates a central reference for the team. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation
E_6: Incorrect. Documentation and tests are separate concerns. While some packages (like dbt-codegen) can help generate test scaffolding, documentation itself doesn't create tests. You must explicitly define tests in schema.yml or as test files.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt documentation serves multiple critical purposes: (1) Data discovery - users can search and explore available datasets, (2) Self-service - reduced dependency on data team for basic questions, (3) Knowledge transfer - onboarding and institutional knowledge, (4) Impact analysis - lineage helps understand downstream effects of changes, (5) Data governance - clear ownership and definitions, (6) Debugging - compiled SQL and model descriptions help troubleshoot issues. Best practices: document all models and sources, describe columns (especially calculated ones), use docs blocks for complex logic, include metric definitions and business context, maintain freshness as project evolves, leverage auto-generated lineage. The dbt docs site becomes your data catalog, replacing expensive third-party tools. Invest in documentation early - it pays dividends as projects grow. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/documentation and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-build-our-metrics/semantic-layer-5-refactor-existing-metrics

Question: You need to reference a model from an installed dbt package in your project. How should you reference it?
Q_1: Use source() to reference the package model
Q_2: Use ref() with the two-argument syntax: ref('package_name', 'model_name')
Q_3: Import the package model using a Jinja import statement
Q_4: Hardcode the schema and table name from the package's target database
Q_5: Copy the package model into your project and reference it as a local model
E_1: Incorrect. source() is for raw data tables not managed by dbt, not for package models. Package models are dbt models from an external package and should be referenced with ref(), not source().
E_2: Correct. To reference a model from a package, use the two-argument ref() syntax: {{ ref('dbt_utils', 'date_spine') }}. The first argument is the package name, the second is the model name. This explicitly identifies which package the model comes from, avoiding ambiguity if multiple packages have models with the same name. If the model name is unique across your project and all packages, you can use single-argument ref('model_name'), but two-argument is more explicit and safer. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref#ref-project-specific-models
E_3: Incorrect. Jinja import is for macros, not models. You import macros from packages to use their functions, but you reference models using ref(). Models are data transformations, macros are code functions - different mechanisms.
E_4: Incorrect. Never hardcode table names - that defeats the purpose of ref(). The ref() function provides environment-aware resolution, lineage tracking, and dependency management. Hardcoding breaks these benefits and makes your code fragile.
E_5: Incorrect. Copying package models into your project creates maintenance nightmares - you lose the ability to update the package, and you're maintaining duplicate code. Always reference package models with ref() rather than copying them.
Explanation: OVERALL: Package models are referenced using ref() with package-specific syntax. Two options: (1) Two-argument ref('package_name', 'model_name') - explicit, recommended when there might be name conflicts, (2) Single-argument ref('model_name') - works if the name is unique across your project and all packages. Best practice: use two-argument syntax for clarity. Package models appear in your DAG and lineage just like local models. You can build on top of package models, test them, and use them in your transformations. Common example: ref('dbt_utils', 'date_spine') for generating date dimensions. Note: You can also disable or override package models using configurations. Packages can depend on other packages, and dbt deps resolves the full dependency tree. When using packages: (1) Install with dbt deps, (2) Reference models with ref(), (3) Import macros with {% from 'package_name' import macro_name %} or use directly, (4) Review package documentation for available models/macros. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/ref

Question: You want to create a test that validates a complex business rule that can't be expressed with built-in tests. The validation logic is specific to one model. What type of test should you create?
Q_1: A generic test that can be reused across multiple models
Q_2: A singular test - a standalone SQL file in the tests/ directory
Q_3: A macro that runs SQL validation and returns a boolean
Q_4: A Python test using pytest framework
Q_5: A post-hook that validates the business rule after model execution
E_1: Incorrect. Generic tests are for reusable validation logic that applies to multiple models or columns. If the business rule is specific to one model and won't be reused, creating a generic test is over-engineering. Save generic tests for patterns you'll use multiple times.
E_2: Correct. Singular tests (formerly called data tests) are SQL files in the tests/ directory that contain model-specific validation logic. They're perfect for one-off business rules that don't fit generic tests. The SQL should return rows that fail the test (0 rows = test passes). For example, tests/assert_total_payment_amount_is_positive.sql: select * from {{ ref('fct_payments') }} where total_amount < 0. Singular tests are simple, flexible, and ideal for unique validation requirements. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests
E_3: Incorrect. While macros can contain validation logic, they're not tests themselves. Tests are queries that return failing rows. You could use a macro within a test, but for a model-specific check, a singular test SQL file is more straightforward.
E_4: Incorrect. dbt tests are SQL queries, not Python pytest tests. While dbt supports Python models, testing is done through SQL queries (both singular and generic tests). Python-based testing frameworks are separate from dbt's testing system.
E_5: Incorrect. Post-hooks execute SQL after model materialization but aren't part of the testing framework. They don't integrate with dbt test, won't show up in test results, and don't follow test severity configurations. Use dbt's native testing framework instead.
Explanation: OVERALL: Singular tests are standalone SQL files in the tests/ directory for one-off validation logic. Structure: write a SELECT query that returns rows violating the business rule. Examples: 'Revenue should never exceed $1M for a single transaction' - select * from {{ ref('sales') }} where revenue > 1000000; 'Order date must be before ship date' - select * from {{ ref('orders') }} where order_date > ship_date; 'Customer lifetime value should equal sum of order values' - compare aggregated data. Singular tests can use CTEs, joins, aggregations, and window functions - any SQL is valid. They support the same configurations as generic tests: severity, tags, store_failures. Name tests descriptively: assert_.sql. When to use singular vs generic: singular for unique business rules, generic for reusable patterns. Many teams start with singular tests and convert to generic tests when they find reusable patterns. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/writing-custom-generic-tests

Question: Your team wants to establish a naming convention policy that requires all staging models to start with 'stg_' and all mart models to start with 'fct_' or 'dim_'. How can you BEST enforce this in your dbt project?
Q_1: Add validation logic in a pre-hook that checks model names before execution
Q_2: Manually review all pull requests to ensure naming conventions are followed
Q_3: Configure the dbt_project.yml file with naming_convention settings
Q_4: Use dbt-checkpoint pre-commit hooks or dbt_project_evaluator package to validate naming conventions automatically
Q_5: Use model aliases to rename models in the database while allowing any filename
E_1: Incorrect. Pre-hooks run at execution time and can't validate naming conventions across the project. They also can't prevent models from being created with incorrect names - they'd just fail at runtime. You need compile-time or CI-time validation.
E_2: Incorrect. Manual review doesn't scale and is error-prone. Automated enforcement through governance tools or pre-commit hooks is more reliable. While code review is important, naming conventions should be automatically validated.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt_project.yml doesn't have built-in naming_convention settings that enforce naming rules. You need external tools like dbt-checkpoint, dbt_project_evaluator, or custom validation scripts to enforce naming conventions.
E_4: Correct. dbt-checkpoint provides pre-commit hooks that validate naming conventions before code is committed. The dbt_project_evaluator package includes tests that check for proper model naming patterns. Both can be configured with your specific conventions (e.g., staging models must match 'stg_*'). These tools fail the build if conventions are violated, providing automated enforcement. This is the recommended approach for governance policies. Reference: https://github.com/dbt-checkpoint/dbt-checkpoint and https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_project_evaluator/latest/
E_5: Incorrect. Aliases change the database object name but don't enforce filename conventions. This approach would make your codebase inconsistent - files could have any name while database objects follow conventions. The goal is to have consistent, predictable naming in both code and database.
Explanation: OVERALL: Enforcing naming conventions requires automated validation tools. dbt-checkpoint integrates with git pre-commit hooks to validate rules before code is committed. It includes checks for: model naming patterns, proper use of source(), required model properties, and more. The dbt_project_evaluator package runs as dbt tests and validates: naming conventions, folder structure, model type prefixes, and other governance rules. Both tools are configurable to match your team's standards. Additional governance approaches: custom Python scripts in CI/CD, dbt Cloud API validations, or SQLFluff with naming rules. Document your conventions clearly and configure automated enforcement to maintain consistency as projects grow. Common conventions: stg_* for staging, int_* for intermediate, fct_* for facts, dim_* for dimensions, rpt_* for reports. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/1-guide-overview and https://discourse.getdbt.com/t/enforcing-naming-conventions/1392

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following are valid use cases for dbt macros? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Creating reusable SQL snippets that can be called from multiple models with different parameters
Q_2: Storing large volumes of reference data that changes infrequently
Q_3: Implementing custom tests that can be reused across multiple models and columns
Q_4: Generating dynamic SQL based on database-specific syntax or capabilities
Q_5: Replacing the need for incremental models by manually managing data updates
Q_6: Creating hooks (on-run-start, on-run-end, pre-hook, post-hook) that execute SQL at specific points in the dbt execution lifecycle
E_1: Correct. This is one of the primary uses of macros. You can create a macro that accepts parameters and generates SQL, then call it from multiple models. For example, a macro that generates a standardized date spine or creates consistent surrogate keys. Macros with parameters promote DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principles. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_2: Incorrect. Storing data is not what macros do. Macros generate SQL code. For reference data, you should use seeds (CSV files) or source tables. Macros are code generation tools, not data storage mechanisms.
E_3: Correct. Generic (custom) tests are implemented as macros. You create a macro that accepts parameters like model and column_name, write test logic that returns failing rows, and apply it in schema.yml. This is how dbt's built-in tests (unique, not_null) work, and you can create your own following the same pattern. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/custom-generic-tests
E_4: Correct. Macros can use the target.type variable to generate different SQL for different warehouses. For example, dbt_utils.date_trunc() generates warehouse-specific date functions. This adapter-specific logic makes your dbt project portable across different data platforms. Macros can also check warehouse capabilities and adjust SQL accordingly. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/target
E_5: Incorrect. Macros don't replace incremental models. While macros can help with custom incremental logic, incremental materialization is a built-in feature with specific behaviors (table persistence, is_incremental() macro, etc.). Don't try to reimplement incremental model functionality with custom macros.
E_6: Correct. Hooks are often implemented as macros, especially when they need to generate dynamic SQL or accept parameters. For example, you might create a macro that grants permissions and call it as a post-hook. Macros make hooks reusable and more maintainable than inline SQL. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/pre-hook-post-hook
Explanation: OVERALL: Macros are dbt's mechanism for code generation and reusability. They're written in Jinja and can generate SQL, create custom tests, implement hooks, and encapsulate complex logic. Common macro patterns include: surrogate key generation, date operations, pivot table creation, union operations across similar tables, custom data type casting, and audit column additions. Macros can access the dbt context (ref, source, config, var, etc.) and the database (run_query for metadata queries). They're fundamental to dbt's extensibility. The dbt_utils package provides many useful macros you can use as examples. When writing macros, focus on: clear parameter names, good documentation, error handling, and warehouse compatibility. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros and https://docs.getdbt.com/guides/best-practices

Question: You want to test that the values in a status column are always one of: 'pending', 'approved', or 'rejected'. Which dbt test should you use?
Q_1: unique test on the status column
Q_2: not_null test on the status column
Q_3: relationships test comparing status to a reference table
Q_4: A singular test with custom SQL checking for invalid status values
Q_5: accepted_values test with the list of valid status values
E_1: Incorrect. The unique test verifies that all values in a column are different from each other. It doesn't check that values match a specific list. A status column will have many duplicate values (multiple rows with 'pending', etc.), which is expected and correct.
E_2: Incorrect. The not_null test only verifies that the column contains no NULL values. It doesn't validate that the values are from an approved list. You'd want both not_null AND accepted_values for complete validation.
E_3: Incorrect. The relationships test verifies foreign key constraints (that values exist in another table). While you could create a reference table of valid statuses, this is overkill for a simple list. The accepted_values test is designed specifically for this use case.
E_4: Incorrect. While a singular test would work, it's unnecessary when the built-in accepted_values test handles this exact scenario. Singular tests are for one-off logic that doesn't fit generic tests. Use built-in tests when available.
E_5: Correct. The accepted_values test is designed to verify that column values are from a specified list. Configure it in schema.yml: - accepted_values: {column_name: status, values: ['pending', 'approved', 'rejected']}. The test will fail if any rows contain values outside this list, catching typos, case sensitivity issues, or unexpected values. This is the standard approach for validating enumerated values. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/data-tests#accepted_values
Explanation: OVERALL: The accepted_values test is one of dbt's four built-in generic tests (along with unique, not_null, and relationships). It's perfect for columns with enumerated values: status codes, categories, types, flags, etc. Configuration: tests: - accepted_values: {column_name: status, values: ['val1', 'val2'], quote: false}. The quote parameter controls whether values are quoted in SQL (default true for strings). Common use cases: status columns, category columns, boolean-like flags, country codes, product types.
