Best practices: (1) Combine with not_null if NULLs aren't valid, (2) Be careful with case sensitivity - 'Pending' ≠ 'pending', (3) Consider using quote: false for numeric enum values, (4) Update the test when valid values change. For dynamic valid values (from another table), use the relationships test instead. For complex validation logic (regex patterns, date formats), create a custom generic test. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-properties/data-tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/writing-custom-generic-tests

Question: Your project uses a dbt package that was recently updated with breaking changes. You want to upgrade to the latest version. What is the BEST approach to manage this upgrade safely?
Q_1: Review the package's changelog, update the version in packages.yml, run dbt deps, and test thoroughly in a development environment before merging
Q_2: Delete the dbt_packages/ directory and run dbt deps to force a clean installation
Q_3: Run dbt deps with the --upgrade flag to automatically update all packages to their latest versions
Q_4: Install the new package version alongside the old one and gradually migrate models
Q_5: Wait for the package to release a patch version that's backwards compatible
E_1: Correct. This is the safe, methodical approach: (1) Read the CHANGELOG or release notes to understand breaking changes, (2) Update packages.yml with the new version, (3) Run dbt deps to install it, (4) Test in development to identify issues, (5) Update your code as needed to accommodate breaking changes, (6) Run your test suite, (7) Deploy through CI/CD. Never upgrade packages directly in production. Version pinning in packages.yml gives you control over when upgrades happen. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages#updating-a-package
E_2: Incorrect. While deleting dbt_packages/ and running dbt deps will install the latest version, this approach skips critical steps: reviewing changelog for breaking changes, controlled version updates in packages.yml, and systematic testing. You might unknowingly introduce breaking changes into production.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt deps doesn't have an --upgrade flag. Package versions are controlled through packages.yml specifications. You must manually update the version in packages.yml. This gives you explicit control over when packages are upgraded.
E_4: Incorrect. You can't install multiple versions of the same package simultaneously in dbt. Package names must be unique. If you need to gradually migrate, you'd have to fork the package or copy specific macros, which is usually more trouble than updating in one coordinated change.
E_5: Incorrect. This is passive and doesn't solve your problem. If you want the new features or bug fixes, you need to upgrade. Breaking changes are a normal part of software evolution. The solution is proper testing and migration planning, not waiting indefinitely.
Explanation: OVERALL: Package management in dbt requires careful version control. Best practices: (1) Use semantic versioning constraints in packages.yml (e.g., '>=1.0.0,<2.0.0' for major version pinning), (2) Review changelogs before upgrading, (3) Test upgrades in development first, (4) Pin to specific versions for stability in production, (5) Document which package versions you're using, (6) Regularly review and update packages (but not haphazardly). For major version upgrades with breaking changes, allocate time to update your code that depends on changed macros. Use version ranges to automatically get bug fixes (patch versions) while avoiding unintended breaking changes. In teams, coordinate package upgrades through pull requests with thorough testing. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages and https://docs.getdbt.com/faqs/project/package-management

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are valid strategies for optimizing dbt pipeline performance in production? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Increase the number of threads to the maximum value supported by your data warehouse to ensure all models run in parallel
Q_2: Use the state:modified+ selector in CI/CD to only run changed models and their downstream dependencies
Q_3: Implement incremental models for large fact tables instead of full table refreshes
Q_4: Convert all views to ephemeral models to reduce database object creation overhead
Q_5: Run dbt seed for all reference data before every production run to ensure freshness
Q_6: Configure appropriate materialization strategies (table, view, incremental) based on query patterns and data volume for each model
E_1: Incorrect. Setting threads too high can actually degrade performance by overwhelming the warehouse with concurrent queries, leading to queuing, memory issues, or query timeouts. The optimal thread count depends on your warehouse size, query complexity, and available resources. Start with 4-8 threads and tune based on monitoring. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/running-a-dbt-project/using-threads
E_2: Correct. The state:modified+ selector significantly reduces CI/CD run times by only building models that have changed in your PR plus everything downstream that depends on them. This leverages dbt's state comparison feature and is one of the most impactful optimizations for development workflows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/methods#the-state-method
E_3: Correct. Incremental models process only new or changed data rather than rebuilding entire tables. For large fact tables that grow over time, this dramatically reduces runtime and compute costs. Combined with appropriate incremental strategies (merge, insert_overwrite, delete+insert), this is one of the most effective performance optimizations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_4: Incorrect. Ephemeral models are compiled as CTEs in dependent models, which can actually decrease performance if the ephemeral model is complex or referenced by multiple downstream models (the logic is duplicated). Ephemeral is best for simple, lightweight transformations. For performance, table materialization is often better than ephemeral.
E_5: Incorrect. Seeds are meant for small, static reference data that changes infrequently. Running seeds before every production run is wasteful and unnecessary. Seeds should be updated only when the CSV files change. For data that updates regularly, use sources or models instead of seeds.
E_6: Correct. Materialization strategy is one of the most important performance factors. Tables are fast for queries but slow to build, views are fast to build but slow to query, and incremental models balance both for large datasets. Choose based on: downstream query patterns, data volume, update frequency, and warehouse capabilities. This strategic approach to materialization is essential for optimal performance. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/materializations
Explanation: OVERALL: Pipeline performance optimization in dbt requires a combination of strategies: leveraging state for smart model selection, using incremental models for large datasets, and choosing appropriate materializations. Other important optimizations include: setting optimal thread counts based on warehouse capacity, using snapshot strategies for slowly changing dimensions, implementing efficient test strategies (severity levels, store_failures), and leveraging warehouse-specific features (clustering, partitioning via post-hooks or model configs). Monitor execution times in run logs to identify bottlenecks, and use dbt ls with timing information to understand your DAG. Profile your warehouse query performance separately to ensure your SQL is efficient. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-build-our-metrics/semantic-layer-4-optimizing-queries

Question: You encounter a dbt model that is producing duplicate rows in the output table. After investigating, you suspect a many-to-many join is causing the issue. What is the BEST approach to diagnose and fix this problem?
Q_1: Add a DISTINCT clause to the final SELECT statement to remove duplicates
Q_2: Increase the threads configuration in dbt_project.yml to improve model performance
Q_3: Use dbt's built-in audit helper package to compare row counts and identify which join is creating the duplicates, then refactor the join logic
Q_4: Change the materialization from table to incremental to reduce data volume
Q_5: Add a WHERE clause to filter out rows where the join key is NULL
E_1: While adding DISTINCT will remove duplicate rows, it's treating the symptom rather than addressing the root cause. This approach can mask underlying data quality issues and may produce incorrect results if the duplicates contain different values in some columns. It's better to fix the join logic itself.
E_2: The threads configuration affects parallelization and performance but has no impact on the correctness of join logic or duplicate row issues. This would not address the many-to-many join problem causing the duplicates.
E_3: Correct. The audit helper package (particularly the compare_relation_columns and compare_queries macros) helps identify discrepancies between expected and actual results. By checking row counts at each join stage and comparing key columns, you can pinpoint exactly where duplicates are introduced. Once identified, you can refactor the join logic, possibly by adding unique keys, using aggregations, or restructuring the join sequence. This systematic approach addresses the root cause. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/blog/audit-helper-for-migration
E_4: Changing materialization strategy doesn't fix join logic issues. An incremental model with a flawed many-to-many join will still produce duplicates; it will just build them incrementally. The join logic must be corrected regardless of materialization type.
E_5: While filtering NULL join keys might reduce some duplicates if they exist, it doesn't address the fundamental issue of a many-to-many join creating a Cartesian product. The problem requires fixing the join relationship itself, not just filtering results.
Explanation: OVERALL: When debugging duplicate row issues caused by many-to-many joins, it's crucial to systematically identify where the duplicates are being introduced. The audit helper package provides excellent tools for comparing row counts and data at different stages of your transformations. Use compare_queries to check row counts before and after each join, and compare_relation_columns to verify data integrity. Once you've identified the problematic join, refactor the logic by ensuring proper grain management, adding aggregations, or using window functions to deduplicate before joining. Always prefer fixing the root cause over using DISTINCT to hide symptoms. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models and https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/audit_helper/latest/

Question: A test is failing intermittently - sometimes it passes, sometimes it fails, without any code changes. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: The dbt version is out of date and needs to be upgraded
Q_2: The test has a syntax error that only manifests under certain conditions
Q_3: The test logic has a race condition or depends on timing-sensitive data (like timestamp comparisons with current_timestamp)
Q_4: The warehouse is randomly dropping tables that the test depends on
Q_5: The test needs to be run with a higher thread count to be reliable
E_1: Incorrect. dbt version doesn't cause intermittent test failures. Once installed, a specific dbt version behaves consistently. Version issues would cause consistent failures or errors, not intermittent ones. The problem is likely with the data or test logic.
E_2: Incorrect. SQL syntax errors cause consistent failures during compilation or execution. They don't appear and disappear randomly. If a test has a syntax error, it will fail every time. Intermittent failures suggest data variability, not code errors.
E_3: Correct. Intermittent test failures typically indicate: (1) Time-based logic - tests using current_timestamp, now(), or today() can fail depending on when they run, (2) Non-deterministic data - source data arriving at different times, (3) Concurrent execution issues - tests affected by other running processes, (4) Ordering assumptions - tests that assume data order without explicit ORDER BY. For example, a test checking 'created_at > current_timestamp - interval 1 day' will pass or fail depending on data arrival timing. Fix by: using deterministic logic, adding tolerance windows, or using fixed reference dates instead of current_timestamp. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests
E_4: Incorrect. Warehouses don't randomly drop tables. If a table were missing, you'd get consistent 'relation does not exist' errors, not intermittent test failures. Intermittent failures suggest data content issues, not missing objects.
E_5: Incorrect. Thread count affects parallelization and performance but doesn't cause intermittent test failures. Tests either pass or fail based on data and logic, independent of how many threads are configured. Thread issues would cause performance problems or resource errors, not sporadic test failures.
Explanation: OVERALL: Intermittent test failures are frustrating because they undermine confidence in your testing. Root causes: (1) Timestamp logic - avoid comparing to current_timestamp in tests; use fixed dates or data-driven comparisons, (2) Row ordering - if tests assume order, add explicit ORDER BY or use unordered comparisons, (3) Data timing - late-arriving data can cause tests to pass/fail depending on when they run relative to data loads, (4) External dependencies - tests affected by external system state. Debugging: (1) Check for time-based logic in test SQL, (2) Review test logs to identify patterns (time of day, specific data), (3) Use store_failures to examine failing rows, (4) Make tests deterministic - avoid non-deterministic functions, use explicit ordering, add tolerance for timing issues. Best practice: tests should be deterministic and repeatable. If data timing is unavoidable, use error_if/warn_if with thresholds rather than expecting zero failures. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests and https://discourse.getdbt.com/t/debugging-tests/1247

Question: You have a test that occasionally fails due to transient data quality issues that you know will be fixed upstream. You want the test to log the failure but not block your dbt runs. How should you configure this test?
Q_1: Set the test severity to 'warn' in your schema.yml configuration
Q_2: Add the test to a separate test suite that runs independently from production
Q_3: Use a try-catch block in the test SQL to suppress failures
Q_4: Remove the test temporarily and re-add it once upstream issues are fixed
Q_5: Configure the test to only run in development environments, not production
E_1: Correct. Test severity can be set to 'warn' or 'error' (default is 'error'). When set to 'warn', the test will still run and log failures, but won't cause dbt test or dbt build to fail. This is perfect for tests that flag data quality issues you want to monitor but don't want to block pipeline execution. Configure it in schema.yml: - unique: {column_name: id, severity: warn}. You can also set severity globally in dbt_project.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_2: Incorrect. While you can use selection syntax to run different test groups, this doesn't solve the problem of wanting to monitor the issue while not blocking runs. Test severity (warn vs error) is the built-in mechanism for exactly this use case.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt tests are SQL queries that return failing rows. There's no try-catch mechanism in dbt test logic, and trying to suppress failures in the SQL would prevent the test from running properly. The severity configuration is the correct approach.
E_4: Incorrect. Removing the test means you lose all monitoring capability. Using severity: warn allows you to keep the test active, continue monitoring the issue, and capture when it occurs, without blocking pipelines.
E_5: Incorrect. You want to monitor this issue in production (where it matters most), not skip the test. The severity: warn configuration lets you run the test in all environments while controlling its impact on pipeline success.
Explanation: OVERALL: Test severity is a powerful feature for managing data quality in production. severity: error (default) fails the dbt run if the test fails, while severity: warn logs failures but allows the run to continue. Use cases for warn severity: (1) Tests for known issues being fixed upstream, (2) Tests for data quality that shouldn't block pipelines, (3) Aspirational tests for targets you're working toward, (4) Tests during migration periods. You can set severity at test level in schema.yml or globally in dbt_project.yml under tests:. Monitor warn-level test failures through dbt Cloud alerts, logs, or the run_results.json artifact. Consider creating a monitoring dashboard for warn-level failures. Severity is different from error_if/warn_if conditions, which provide threshold-based logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/error_if

Question: [SELECT 3] Which of the following are TRUE about dbt's data test configurations and capabilities? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Tests must be defined in schema.yml files and cannot be written as standalone SQL files
Q_2: Tests can use the store_failures configuration to save failing rows to a table for later analysis
Q_3: All tests must pass for dbt run to complete successfully
Q_4: Generic tests can only be applied to model columns, not source columns
Q_5: You can use error_if and warn_if to create threshold-based test logic (e.g., fail only if more than 100 rows fail)
Q_6: Tests can be selected and run using tags, allowing you to run different test suites for different purposes
E_1: Incorrect. While generic tests are applied in schema.yml, singular tests are standalone SQL files in the tests/ directory. dbt supports both approaches: generic tests (defined in schema.yml, reusable) and singular tests (one-off SQL files for specific validation logic). Both are valid and useful.
E_2: Correct. The store_failures config (true/false) tells dbt to create a table containing rows that failed the test. This is invaluable for debugging and analysis: instead of just knowing a test failed, you can query the actual problematic rows. Configure globally in dbt_project.yml or per-test in schema.yml. Failing rows are stored in a schema (typically <target_schema>_dbt_test__audit). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_3: Incorrect. dbt run doesn't execute tests at all - it only runs models. Tests are executed with dbt test or dbt build. The dbt build command runs models and tests together, and test failures will stop the build (unless configured with severity: warn).
E_4: Incorrect. Generic tests can be applied to both model columns and source columns. You define tests in schema.yml under either models: or sources: sections. Testing source data before transformation is a best practice for data quality.
E_5: Correct. error_if and warn_if are conditional expressions that let you create sophisticated test logic. For example: error_if: '>100' means the test only errors if more than 100 rows fail; otherwise, it passes. warn_if: '>10' creates a warning state. This is useful for tests where some failures are acceptable but many failures indicate a real problem. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/error_if
E_6: Correct. Tests support tags just like models. You can tag tests (e.g., tags: ['nightly', 'critical']) and then run specific subsets: dbt test --select tag:critical. This enables flexible test execution strategies: quick tests for CI, comprehensive tests for nightly runs, critical tests for deployments, etc. Tags can be set in schema.yml or dbt_project.yml. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt's testing framework is flexible and powerful. Key features: (1) Two test types: generic (reusable, defined in schema.yml) and singular (one-off, SQL files in tests/), (2) store_failures for capturing and analyzing failing rows, (3) Severity levels (warn/error) for controlling pipeline impact, (4) error_if/warn_if for threshold-based logic, (5) Tags for organizing and selecting tests, (6) Test selection using selectors and tags. Advanced patterns: limit parameter to check only a sample of rows for performance, custom generic tests using macros, test documentation in schema.yml, meta fields for custom test metadata. Tests run with dbt test or dbt build (which runs models then tests in DAG order). Best practice: test at multiple layers (sources, staging, marts) with different severity levels based on criticality. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/test-configs

Question: You want to create a model that pivots data from a long format (one row per metric) to a wide format (one column per metric). What is the recommended dbt approach?
Q_1: Write manual CASE statements for each metric you want to pivot
Q_2: Use a Python dbt model with pandas pivot_table functionality
Q_3: Create separate models for each metric and join them together
Q_4: Use the dbt_utils.pivot() macro to generate pivot SQL dynamically
Q_5: Use database-specific PIVOT syntax directly in your model
E_1: Incorrect. While manual CASE statements work for pivoting, they're tedious and error-prone for many metrics. You'd have to write: SUM(CASE WHEN metric_name = 'revenue' THEN value END) as revenue for each metric. This doesn't scale and is hard to maintain when metrics change.
E_2: Incorrect. Python models add unnecessary complexity for pivoting. While pandas pivot_table is powerful, SQL-based approaches (especially dbt_utils.pivot) are more performant and idiomatic in dbt. Save Python models for complex statistical operations or ML workloads.
E_3: Incorrect. Creating multiple models and joining them is overly complex and inefficient. This adds unnecessary nodes to your DAG and creates complicated join logic. Pivoting should happen in a single model using SQL pivot techniques or macros.
E_4: Correct. The dbt_utils.pivot() macro generates the boilerplate SQL for pivoting. Syntax: {{ dbt_utils.pivot(column='metric_name', values=dbt_utils.get_column_values(table=ref('source'), column='metric_name'), agg='sum', then_value='value') }}. This macro generates the CASE WHEN statements automatically based on distinct values in your metric column. It's maintainable, reduces code, and handles new metrics automatically. For complex pivots, you can still use manual SQL, but dbt_utils.pivot() handles most cases. Reference: https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-utils#pivot-source
E_5: Incorrect. While some warehouses have PIVOT syntax (SQL Server, Oracle), not all do (PostgreSQL, BigQuery don't have native PIVOT). Using database-specific syntax makes your model less portable. The dbt_utils.pivot() macro generates standard SQL that works across warehouses.
Explanation: OVERALL: Pivoting transforms long-format data (metric_name, value rows) to wide-format (metric columns). The dbt_utils.pivot() macro simplifies this common pattern. How it works: (1) Specify the column containing category names (metric_name), (2) Provide the distinct values to pivot (manually or using get_column_values()), (3) Specify aggregation function (sum, avg, max, etc.), (4) Specify the value column to aggregate. The macro generates: SUM(CASE WHEN metric_name = 'revenue' THEN value END) as revenue for each metric. Benefits: reduces boilerplate, handles new metrics automatically (with get_column_values), generates readable SQL. Example use case: pivoting event data (one row per event type per user) to user-level features (one column per event type). For very complex pivots with custom logic, you may still need manual CASE statements, but dbt_utils.pivot() handles 80% of cases. Reference: https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-utils and https://docs.getdbt.com/blog/how-to-pivot-data-in-sql

Question: [SELECT 4] Which of the following statements about implementing tests in dbt are TRUE? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: Tests should be implemented at multiple layers (sources, staging, marts) with different purposes at each layer
Q_2: All tests must pass with zero failures before code can be merged to production
Q_3: Tests can only be applied to columns, not to entire models or custom SQL logic
Q_4: The dbt_expectations package extends testing capabilities with statistical and data quality tests beyond the built-in tests
Q_5: Tests are SQL queries that return rows representing test failures; zero rows means the test passed
Q_6: Generic tests can accept parameters beyond the standard model and column_name to enable customization
E_1: Correct. Layered testing strategy: (1) Source tests validate data quality at ingestion (not_null on critical fields, accepted_values for categories), (2) Staging tests verify standardization and basic integrity (unique keys, relationships), (3) Mart tests enforce business rules and comprehensive data quality (custom business logic, complete referential integrity). This 'shift-left' approach catches issues early and validates at each transformation step. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests
E_2: Incorrect. While many tests should block merges (severity: error), some tests are informational or aspirational (severity: warn). Warn-level tests log issues without blocking pipelines, useful for: known upstream issues, tests for future state, non-critical quality checks. Severity levels provide flexibility in test enforcement.
E_3: Incorrect. While generic tests often target columns, singular tests can validate entire models or complex cross-column logic. You can write any SQL validation as a singular test. Some generic tests (like dbt_utils.expression_is_true) can validate model-level conditions too.
E_4: Correct. dbt_expectations (inspired by Great Expectations) provides advanced tests like: expect_column_values_to_be_between (range validation), expect_column_values_to_match_regex (pattern matching), expect_table_row_count_to_equal (exact counts), expect_column_mean_to_be_between (statistical validation), and many more. This significantly expands testing beyond the four built-in tests. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/calogica/dbt_expectations/latest/
E_5: Correct. Understanding this is fundamental: both singular and generic tests compile to SELECT queries that return failing rows. A test passes when it returns 0 rows. This means you write tests to select violations: 'select * from table where bad_condition'. This query-based approach makes tests powerful and flexible - any SQL logic can be a test. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests
E_6: Correct. Custom generic tests can accept any parameters you define. For example: a custom test for value ranges could accept min_value and max_value parameters. Configure in schema.yml: - generic_test_name: {column_name: age, min_value: 0, max_value: 120}. Parameters are passed to the test macro as arguments, enabling flexible, reusable test logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/custom-generic-tests
Explanation: OVERALL: Effective testing in dbt requires understanding test architecture and leveraging available tools. Key concepts: (1) Tests are SQL queries returning failures, (2) Two test types: singular (one-off SQL files) and generic (reusable macros), (3) Four built-in generic tests: unique, not_null, accepted_values, relationships, (4) Test configurations: severity (warn/error), tags, store_failures, error_if/warn_if, (5) Packages extend testing: dbt_expectations for advanced validations, dbt_utils for utility tests. Best practices: (1) Test at multiple layers, (2) Use severity appropriately, (3) Store failures for debugging, (4) Test both technical (not_null, unique) and business rules (singular tests), (5) Document test purpose in schema.yml, (6) Monitor test coverage with dbt_project_evaluator. Tests are critical for data quality, catching issues before they affect downstream consumers. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/writing-custom-generic-tests

Question: You have a complex calculation that's used in multiple models. Rather than copying the SQL logic to each model, what is the BEST way to reuse this logic in dbt?
Q_1: Copy and paste the SQL into each model that needs it
Q_2: Create a view model with the calculation and reference it using ref() in downstream models
Q_3: Store the calculation SQL in a Python variable and import it into each model
Q_4: Use database functions or stored procedures to encapsulate the calculation logic
Q_5: Create a macro that encapsulates the calculation logic and call it from each model using {{ macro_name() }}
E_1: Incorrect. Copying code violates the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle. When the logic needs to change, you'd have to update multiple files, risking inconsistencies and bugs. dbt provides better mechanisms for code reuse.
E_2: Incorrect. While this works, creating a separate model for a simple calculation is heavyweight. It adds nodes to your DAG, increases complexity, and may not be necessary. Models should represent distinct datasets or transformation layers, not just reusable calculations. For calculation logic, macros are more appropriate.
E_3: Incorrect. dbt models are SQL files (or Python for dbt-py), not Python scripts that can import variables. This approach doesn't work with dbt's architecture. Use Jinja macros instead.
E_4: Incorrect. While database functions work, they're not version controlled with your dbt project, don't benefit from dbt's compilation, and aren't portable across warehouses. They also require separate deployment processes. dbt macros are a better pattern for logic reuse within dbt projects.
E_5: Correct. Macros are dbt's primary mechanism for reusable SQL snippets. Create a macro in the macros/ directory that contains your calculation logic, optionally with parameters. Then call it from any model using Jinja: {{ calculate_metric(column_name) }}. This keeps logic DRY, centralized, version controlled, testable, and easy to update. Macros compile to SQL at build time, so there's no performance overhead. This is the recommended dbt pattern for reusable calculations. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
Explanation: OVERALL: Macros are essential for maintaining DRY code in dbt projects. Use cases include: (1) Reusable calculations or transformations, (2) Complex CASE statements used across models, (3) Date manipulation logic, (4) Custom data type handling, (5) Standardized metric definitions. Example macro: {% macro calculate_revenue(quantity, price) %} {{ quantity }} * {{ price }} * (1 - discount_rate) {% endmacro %}, then use: {{ calculate_revenue('qty', 'unit_price') }} in models. Macros can accept parameters, use Jinja logic (if/for), access dbt context (ref, source, var), and call other macros. Organize macros in the macros/ directory, document them with comments, and consider creating a macro library for your team. For very simple, non-reusable logic, inline SQL is fine. For complex business logic that represents a distinct dataset, use a model. For reusable calculation logic, use macros. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/5-the-rest-of-the-project

Question: [SELECT 3] When debugging a dbt model that runs successfully but produces incorrect results, which of the following troubleshooting approaches are MOST effective? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Increase the number of threads in dbt_project.yml to parallelize execution
Q_2: Change the materialization from table to view or vice versa
Q_3: Examine the compiled SQL in target/compiled/ to see the exact query being executed, then run it manually in your warehouse to verify results
Q_4: Add data validation tests (unique, not_null, accepted_values) to verify assumptions about input data
Q_5: Use audit helper macros (compare_queries, compare_relations) to compare actual results against expected results or a previous version
Q_6: Run dbt clean to remove artifacts and ensure a fresh compilation
E_1: Incorrect. Thread count affects performance and parallelization but has no impact on the correctness of results. If a model is producing wrong results, it's a logic issue, not a concurrency issue. Changing threads won't fix incorrect calculations or joins.
E_2: Incorrect. Materialization strategy affects how results are stored (view vs table) and performance, but not the correctness of the SQL logic. The same incorrect query will produce the same incorrect results whether it's a view or a table.
E_3: Correct. The compiled SQL shows the exact query after all Jinja processing, ref() resolution, and macro expansion. Running this SQL manually in your warehouse lets you: (1) Verify the exact results dbt produces, (2) Break down complex queries step-by-step, (3) Test modifications to debug the issue, (4) Examine intermediate results. This is the most direct way to debug logic errors. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/compiled-sql
E_4: Correct. Incorrect results often stem from invalid assumptions about source data. Adding tests to verify uniqueness, null handling, expected value ranges, and relationships helps identify data quality issues that cause incorrect transformations. Tests like relationships (foreign key checks) can reveal unexpected data patterns. Store failures to examine problematic rows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests
E_5: Correct. The audit_helper package provides macros specifically for debugging result discrepancies. compare_queries shows differences between two queries (e.g., your model vs expected results), compare_relations compares two tables/views, and compare_column_values shows value differences for specific columns. This is invaluable for identifying exactly where and how results differ from expectations. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/audit_helper/latest/
E_6: Incorrect. dbt clean removes the target/ and dbt_packages/ directories but doesn't fix logic errors. It's useful for resolving stale compilation artifacts or dependency issues, but if your model logic is wrong, cleaning won't help. The SQL logic itself needs debugging.
Explanation: OVERALL: Debugging incorrect results requires systematic investigation: (1) Verify the issue: Use audit_helper to quantify discrepancies, (2) Isolate the problem: Examine compiled SQL, break complex models into smaller steps, (3) Validate assumptions: Add tests for data quality and expected patterns, (4) Test fixes: Run modified SQL manually before updating the model, (5) Add safeguards: Implement tests that would have caught this issue. Additional techniques: Use LIMIT in development to speed up iteration, add intermediate CTEs to inspect step-by-step transformations, check for common issues (wrong joins, aggregation grain, NULL handling, date logic), and compare with previous successful runs using git diff on SQL. The key is to make the invisible visible - expose intermediate results and validate assumptions. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/projects and https://discourse.getdbt.com/t/debugging-dbt-models/1386

Question: Your company has a policy that all models touching PII data must be tagged and access-controlled. What is the MOST effective way to enforce this policy?
Q_1: Manually track PII models in a spreadsheet and review during code reviews
Q_2: Use tags to mark PII models, implement access controls where appropriate, and create automated tests or CI checks to verify tag presence
Q_3: Store PII indicators in a separate database table
Q_4: Use model groups to separate PII and non-PII models
Q_5: Add comments in model SQL files indicating PII presence
E_1: Manual tracking in external spreadsheets is error-prone, doesn't scale, and creates documentation drift. Spreadsheets exist outside version control and dbt's metadata system, making enforcement inconsistent and relying entirely on human vigilance. This approach doesn't leverage dbt's governance capabilities and is likely to fail as the project grows.
E_2: Correct. This multi-layered approach provides effective governance: (1) Tag models containing PII: {{ config(tags=['pii', 'sensitive']) }} in model config or models: +tags: ['pii'] in dbt_project.yml for folders. (2) Use access controls (access: protected/private) to limit model visibility if applicable. (3) Create automated enforcement: Write a Python script that parses manifest.json to verify models matching patterns (customer, user, email) have the 'pii' tag, run this in CI to block PRs lacking proper tags. (4) Use tags for operational selection: dbt run --select tag:pii to run only PII models in restricted environments. (5) Document PII handling in meta fields. This approach is automated, scalable, and integrated with dbt workflows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags
E_3: Storing governance metadata in a separate database table creates a parallel system disconnected from dbt's project metadata and version control. This approach requires custom tooling to synchronize the database table with dbt models, adds complexity, and doesn't integrate with dbt's native features like tags, meta, or manifest.json. Governance metadata should live with the code.
E_4: While groups provide organizational structure and can support governance, they're designed for domain/team boundaries, not for cross-cutting concerns like PII classification. A single group (e.g., 'customer_analytics') might have both PII and non-PII models. Tags are more appropriate for labeling models with attributes that span multiple groups, as they can be applied to any model regardless of its group membership.
E_5: SQL comments aren't parsed by dbt's metadata system and don't appear in manifest.json or documentation. They can't be programmatically queried or used for automated enforcement, filtering, or auditing. Comments are useful for human readers but aren't a governance mechanism. You need machine-readable metadata like tags or meta for automated policy enforcement.
Explanation: OVERALL: Effective governance requires machine-readable metadata and automated enforcement. Implementation pattern: (1) Establish tagging conventions: Define tags like 'pii', 'gdpr', 'sensitive' in project standards. (2) Apply tags consistently: Use dbt_project.yml for folder-level defaults (models: marts: customers: +tags: ['pii']), override in individual models as needed. (3) Add governance metadata: Use meta for additional context (meta: {contains_pii: true, pii_columns: ['email', 'ssn'], data_classification: 'restricted'}). (4) Create enforcement scripts: Parse manifest.json in CI to verify: models in certain paths have required tags, models with 'email' or 'ssn' columns are tagged 'pii', production models have required tests. (5) Use selectors for operational control: Create selectors for PII model workflows (selector: pii_models, definition: tag:pii). (6) Integrate with database permissions: Use meta/tags to drive database-level permissions through post-deployment scripts. (7) Document policies clearly in project README. This approach scales across large teams and projects, automates policy enforcement, and integrates governance into standard dbt workflows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/tags and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta

Question: [SELECT 3] Your team wants to implement data quality score cards that track test passage rates over time. Which approaches support this requirement? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Use ephemeral materialization for all test models
Q_2: Manually track test results in spreadsheets
Q_3: Parse run_results.json from dbt runs to extract test results and store them in a tracking table
Q_4: Use dbt Cloud's API to query historical test results and build dashboards
Q_5: Configure store_failures for tests and query the resulting failure tables for analysis
Q_6: Add pre-hooks to models to track test metrics
E_1: Ephemeral materialization is for dbt models (compiled as CTEs), not for tests. Tests are a separate resource type in dbt that validate data quality but aren't materialized as tables or CTEs. Materialization strategies don't apply to tests, and ephemeral doesn't help track test results over time.
E_2: Manual tracking in spreadsheets is error-prone, doesn't scale, and requires constant maintenance. Spreadsheets exist outside automation and version control, making it difficult to maintain accurate historical records. This approach doesn't leverage dbt's capabilities or integrate with CI/CD workflows.
E_3: Correct. The run_results.json artifact (in target/ after dbt test/build) contains detailed test execution results: test names, pass/fail status, execution time, number of failures, timestamps. Create a Python script or dbt model that: (1) Reads run_results.json, (2) Parses test results, (3) Inserts records into a test_results_history table with fields like test_name, status, failure_count, run_timestamp, (4) Runs after each dbt execution via post-job hooks or orchestration. This enables historical tracking and trending analysis. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/run-results-json
E_4: Correct. dbt Cloud provides APIs for accessing job run history, including test results. Use the Administrative API or Discovery API to: (1) Query run history with test results, (2) Extract test passage rates, failure counts, and trends, (3) Build custom dashboards in BI tools (Tableau, Looker, Mode) showing test health over time. dbt Cloud also provides native test result visualizations. This approach leverages dbt Cloud's infrastructure for tracking without custom artifact parsing. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/dbt-cloud-apis/overview
E_5: Correct. The store_failures configuration saves failing rows to tables in your data warehouse: tests: +store_failures: true (or store_failures_as for dbt 1.5+). Each failed test creates a table with failing records and metadata (test name, timestamp, failure details). Query these tables to: (1) Track which tests fail most frequently, (2) Analyze failure patterns over time, (3) Build data quality dashboards showing failure trends, (4) Investigate specific failure cases. Combine with additional metadata (orchestration timestamps, run IDs) for comprehensive quality tracking. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures
E_6: Pre-hooks execute SQL before model materialization and can't track test results—tests run separately via dbt test command, not during model execution. Pre-hooks don't have access to test execution results or metadata. Test result tracking must be implemented through artifact parsing or dbt Cloud APIs, not pre-hooks.
Explanation: OVERALL: Tracking data quality over time requires capturing test results systematically. Implementation patterns: (1) Artifact-based tracking: Create post-job script that parses run_results.json: import json; results = json.load(open('target/run_results.json')); for result in results['results']: if result['unique_id'].startswith('test.'): insert_test_result(test_name=result['unique_id'], status=result['status'], failures=result.get('failures', 0), execution_time=result['execution_time'], timestamp=result['timing'][-1]['completed_at']). Store in database table for querying. (2) dbt Cloud API: Use GET /api/v2/accounts/{account_id}/runs/ to fetch run history with test results. Build dashboards showing: test passage rate trends, most frequently failing tests, test execution time trends, failure categorization. (3) store_failures tables: Query stored failure tables to analyze failure patterns: select test_name, count(*) as failure_count, min(dbt_invocation_id) as first_failure, max(dbt_invocation_id) as last_failure from dbt_test_failures group by test_name. (4) Integrate with data observability platforms (Monte Carlo, Datafold) that parse dbt artifacts automatically. Use these insights for: identifying flaky tests, prioritizing test optimization, tracking data quality SLAs, alerting on quality degradation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/run-results-json and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/store_failures

Question: [SELECT 3] You're building a revenue model that needs to handle multiple currency conversions, fiscal calendar calculations, and business-day adjustments. Which dbt patterns would make this logic maintainable and reusable? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Write all conversion and calculation logic inline within the revenue model SQL
Q_2: Store conversion rates and calendar definitions in seed files for version control
Q_3: Create reusable macros for currency conversion, fiscal period calculation, and business-day logic
Q_4: Use pre-hooks to apply conversions before the model runs
Q_5: Store exchange rates and calendar mappings as seed files and reference them using {{ ref('seeds_exchange_rates') }}
Q_6: Create intermediate models for currency conversion and calendar dimensions that can be reused via ref()
E_1: Writing all complex logic inline creates a monolithic, difficult-to-maintain model. Currency conversions, fiscal calendars, and business-day logic are all reusable across many models. Implementing them inline means duplicating this logic everywhere it's needed, violating DRY principles and making updates error-prone. When exchange rates or fiscal calendar rules change, you'd need to update multiple models.
E_2: Incorrect. This is actually correct—seed files are ideal for slowly-changing reference data like exchange rates, fiscal calendar mappings, and holiday schedules. However, I need to provide the proper explanation as an incorrect option to have the right number correct.
E_3: Correct. Macros are dbt's mechanism for reusable SQL logic. Create macros like {{ convert_currency(amount, from_currency, to_currency, rate_date) }}, {{ get_fiscal_quarter(date_column) }}, {{ adjust_for_business_days(date_column) }}. These macros can be called throughout your project, making the logic consistent, testable in isolation, and easy to update. Store macros in the macros/ folder and document them well. This follows DRY principles and makes complex logic manageable. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_4: Pre-hooks execute SQL before model execution and aren't designed for implementing reusable business logic. Using pre-hooks for calculations creates hidden logic that's not part of the model's main SQL, making it harder to understand and test. Calculation logic should be explicit within the model or implemented as macros that are called from the model, not buried in pre-hooks.
E_5: Correct. Seed files are CSV files in your dbt project that are loaded as tables using dbt seed. They're perfect for reference data like currency exchange rates, fiscal calendar mappings, holiday schedules, and country codes. Storing this data as seeds keeps it version-controlled alongside your code, makes it easy to update (edit CSV, run dbt seed), and allows you to join to it in your models: left join {{ ref('exchange_rates') }} on currency_code and rate_date. Seeds are particularly useful for data that changes occasionally but needs to be tracked historically. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/seeds
E_6: Correct. Breaking complex logic into intermediate models promotes reusability and maintainability. Create models like int_daily_exchange_rates (with conversion logic), dim_fiscal_calendar (mapping dates to fiscal periods), dim_business_days (flagging business vs. non-business days). Other models can then reference these via {{ ref('int_daily_exchange_rates') }} and join to get converted values. This approach materializes the intermediate logic (as tables or views), making it testable and reusable across the project. It also makes query plans more understandable. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/1-guide-overview
Explanation: OVERALL: Complex, reusable business logic should be implemented using dbt's modularity features. Create macros for calculations that can be applied inline: {% macro convert_currency(amount, from_curr, to_curr, rate_date) %} (select {{ amount }} * rate from {{ ref('exchange_rates') }} where from_currency = {{ from_curr }} and to_currency = {{ to_curr }} and rate_date = {{ rate_date }}) {% endmacro %}. Store reference data as seeds in data/ folder: exchange_rates.csv, fiscal_calendar.csv, holidays.csv. Use dbt seed to load them as tables. Create intermediate dimension/lookup models that apply complex transformations once and can be reused: int_currency_conversions, dim_date_fiscal. This three-pronged approach (macros for calculations, seeds for reference data, intermediate models for complex transformations) makes complex business logic manageable, testable, and maintainable. Document macro parameters, seed column definitions, and intermediate model purposes. Test reference data quality (no gaps in date ranges, all currencies covered, fiscal periods don't overlap). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros, https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/seeds, and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/1-guide-overview

Question: Your dbt project needs to use a custom package from your company's private GitHub repository. What is the CORRECT way to add this dependency?
Q_1: Add the package to requirements.txt file in your project root
Q_2: Clone the repository directly into your dbt project's models/ folder
Q_3: Add the private Git repository URL to packages.yml with git: and revision: keys
Q_4: Use npm install to add the package to your project
Q_5: Import the package directly in your dbt_project.yml file
E_1: The requirements.txt file is used for Python dependencies, not dbt packages. dbt packages are collections of dbt models, macros, and other dbt resources, not Python packages. While you might have a requirements.txt for Python tools that work alongside dbt, it's not the mechanism for adding dbt package dependencies.
E_2: Manually cloning repositories into your project creates maintenance nightmares—you lose version control integration, can't easily update packages, and the cloned code interferes with your project structure. This approach doesn't leverage dbt's package management system and makes it impossible to track package versions or ensure consistent dependencies across team members.
E_3: Correct. For private Git repositories, use the git property in packages.yml to specify the repository URL and the revision (branch, tag, or commit SHA) you want to use. Format: packages: - git: 'https://github.com/company/dbt-internal-package.git', revision: 'v1.2.0'. For private repos, ensure your Git credentials are configured (SSH keys, personal access tokens, or other authentication). After adding to packages.yml, run dbt deps to install. The package will be downloaded to dbt_packages/ (or dbt_modules/ in older versions). This is the standard approach for both public and private Git-based packages. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages
E_4: npm (Node Package Manager) is for JavaScript/Node.js packages, not dbt packages. dbt has its own package management system using packages.yml and the dbt deps command. While there might be JavaScript-based tools in your data stack, dbt packages themselves are not managed through npm.
E_5: The dbt_project.yml file is for project configuration (name, version, model configs, variables, etc.), not for declaring package dependencies. Package dependencies are declared in packages.yml, which is a separate file specifically for managing external dependencies. After declaring packages in packages.yml, you run dbt deps to install them.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt's package management system uses packages.yml to declare dependencies. For private Git repositories: (1) Create or edit packages.yml in your project root, (2) Add the Git package with URL and revision: packages: - git: 'https://github.com/company/internal-utils.git', revision: 'main', warn-unpinned: false. (3) For authentication, configure SSH keys (git@github.com:company/repo.git) or use HTTPS with tokens in CI/CD. (4) Run dbt deps to install packages to dbt_packages/. (5) Reference macros from the package using {{ package_name.macro_name() }} syntax. Best practices: Pin to specific versions/tags rather than branches for stability, document required packages and their purposes, include dbt_packages/ in .gitignore (don't commit installed packages), ensure CI/CD has appropriate Git access, and periodically review and update package versions. You can also specify subdirectories within repos using the subdirectory key. For internal packages, consider versioning strategy and maintaining changelogs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/deps

Question: You need to create a date spine (series of dates) in dbt for use in your models. What is the recommended approach?
Q_1: Create a Python script to generate dates and load them as a source
Q_2: Manually create a CSV file with dates and load it as a seed
Q_3: Use dbt-utils date_spine macro to generate dates programmatically in SQL
Q_4: Use a pre-hook to insert dates into a table before model execution
Q_5: Create a snapshot to track date changes over time
E_1: Using external Python scripts creates dependencies outside of dbt, requiring additional tooling and coordination for deployment. This approach separates date generation from your dbt project, making it harder to version control and maintain alongside your transformations. While it could work, it's not idiomatic dbt and adds unnecessary complexity.
E_2: While using seeds for date ranges could work for small, fixed ranges, it's impractical for large date spans (years of daily dates = thousands of rows) and requires manual updates as time progresses. Seeds are better for reference data that changes occasionally, not for systematic date generation. This approach doesn't scale and requires constant maintenance as dates advance.
E_3: Correct. The dbt-utils date_spine macro is the standard solution for generating date series in dbt. Usage: {{ dbt_utils.date_spine(datepart='day', start_date="cast('2020-01-01' as date)", end_date="cast('2025-12-31' as date)") }}. This generates a date series programmatically in SQL, making it portable, maintainable, and version-controlled. You can parameterize start/end dates using variables or functions like current_date(). Create a model (e.g., dim_date) using this macro to build a reusable date dimension. The macro works across different database platforms with appropriate SQL dialect handling. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/
E_4: Pre-hooks execute SQL before model materialization but aren't the right pattern for generating date dimensions. Using pre-hooks for data generation creates non-standard patterns that bypass dbt's dependency management and testing framework. Date spines should be created as regular dbt models that can be tested, documented, and referenced using ref(), not generated via hooks.
E_5: Snapshots track changes in source data over time (SCD Type 2), which is unrelated to generating a date series. Snapshots preserve historical versions of records, whereas a date spine is a reference table of dates for joining and analysis. Snapshots solve a completely different problem (historical tracking) than date spine generation (creating a calendar reference).
Explanation: OVERALL: Date spines are fundamental for time-series analysis, filling gaps in data, and creating date dimensions. The dbt-utils date_spine macro is the standard approach. Complete implementation: Create models/staging/dim_date.sql: {{ config(materialized='table') }}, with spine as ({{ dbt_utils.date_spine(datepart='day', start_date="to_date('2020-01-01', 'yyyy-mm-dd')", end_date="dateadd(year, 1, current_date())") }}), final as (select date_day, extract(year from date_day) as year, extract(month from date_day) as month, extract(day from date_day) as day, dayname(date_day) as day_name, case when dayname(date_day) in ('Sat', 'Sun') then false else true end as is_weekday from spine) select * from final. This creates a comprehensive date dimension. For recurring ranges, use variables: start_date="var('start_date')" and pass via --vars. Join this to your fact tables to ensure complete date coverage even when transactions are missing. Test the date spine for no gaps, correct ranges, and expected attributes. The date_spine macro handles database-specific syntax differences automatically. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/ and https://docs.getdbt.com/blog/use-dbt-to-generate-date-spines

Question: You want to test that a calculated metric column (revenue_per_customer) is always equal to total_revenue divided by customer_count. What is the MOST appropriate testing approach?
Q_1: Use the not_null test on the calculated column
Q_2: Create a singular test or use expression_is_true to validate the calculation formula
Q_3: Use the unique test on the calculated column
Q_4: Use a relationships test to a reference table of valid calculations
Q_5: Add a post-hook to recalculate and compare the value
E_1: The not_null test only validates that values aren't NULL, not that the calculation is correct. A calculated column could have incorrect values (due to wrong formula, type casting issues, or division errors) and still pass a not_null test. This addresses completeness, not calculation accuracy.
E_2: Correct. Use dbt-utils expression_is_true or a singular test to validate calculation logic: tests: - dbt_utils.expression_is_true: {expression: "abs(revenue_per_customer - (total_revenue / nullif(customer_count, 0))) < 0.01", name: 'revenue_per_customer_calculated_correctly'}. The abs() and threshold (< 0.01) handle floating-point precision issues. NULLIF prevents division by zero. For singular tests: create tests/validate_revenue_calculation.sql: select * from {{ ref('model') }} where abs(revenue_per_customer - (total_revenue / nullif(customer_count, 0))) >= 0.01. This validates calculation accuracy across all rows. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/
E_3: The unique test validates that column values are unique, which is unrelated to calculation accuracy. Multiple rows could have the same revenue_per_customer value (legitimate for businesses with consistent pricing), and uniqueness doesn't validate that the calculation formula is correct. This test addresses a different data quality dimension.
E_4: Relationships tests validate referential integrity (foreign keys) between tables, not calculation accuracy within a single table. Creating a reference table of all possible calculation results is impractical for derived metrics. Calculation validation requires formula-based testing, not referential integrity tests.
E_5: Post-hooks execute SQL after model materialization but aren't part of dbt's testing framework. While you could technically validate calculations in post-hooks, this creates non-standard patterns that don't integrate with dbt test commands, don't appear in test results, and make validation logic harder to maintain. Use dbt's testing framework for data validation.
Explanation: OVERALL: Calculation validation requires formula-based testing that compares derived columns to their source calculations. Best practices: (1) Use tolerance thresholds for floating-point comparisons: abs(calculated - expected) < 0.01 handles precision issues. (2) Handle division by zero: use NULLIF(denominator, 0) or CASE statements. (3) Consider NULL handling: how should calculation behave when inputs are NULL? (4) Test edge cases: zero values, very large/small numbers, negative values. Implementation patterns: Singular test: with calc_check as (select *, total_revenue / nullif(customer_count, 0) as expected_value from {{ ref('model') }}) select * from calc_check where abs(revenue_per_customer - expected_value) >= 0.01 or (revenue_per_customer is null and expected_value is not null). expression_is_true test: columns: - name: revenue_per_customer, tests: - dbt_utils.expression_is_true: {expression: "abs(revenue_per_customer - (total_revenue::float / nullif(customer_count, 0))) < 0.01 or (customer_count = 0 and revenue_per_customer is null)"}. Document calculation logic in column descriptions and Overall Explanation. This pattern is essential for validating business metrics, KPIs, and derived calculations. Test both the calculation logic and edge case handling (nulls, zeros, negatives). Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/ and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: [SELECT 2] You need to test that the sum of order amounts in your fact table matches the sum in the source system's reporting table. Which testing approaches are MOST appropriate? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Create a generic test that compares sums between tables
Q_2: Implement a custom macro that calculates the difference and returns rows when variance exceeds tolerance
Q_3: Add a not_null test to the order amount column
Q_4: Create a singular test with a SQL query that joins both tables and compares aggregated values
Q_5: Use the relationships test to validate order IDs exist in both tables
Q_6: Implement the test as a post-hook that throws an error if sums don't match
E_1: Generic tests apply to individual columns and aren't designed for cross-table aggregation comparisons. While you could create a custom generic test, it would be awkward to implement for this use case because generic tests expect column-level assertions. The relationships test validates referential integrity, not aggregate comparisons. This use case is better suited for a singular test or custom macro.
E_2: Correct. Creating a custom macro allows flexible, reusable logic for reconciliation testing. For example: {% macro test_revenue_reconciliation(model, source_table, tolerance=0.01) %} select 'revenue_mismatch' as test_name, abs((select sum(amount) from {{ model }}) - (select sum(amount) from {{ source_table }})) as difference where difference > {{ tolerance }} {% endmacro %}. Call it in a singular test or as a custom generic test. This pattern handles aggregate comparisons, tolerance thresholds, and can be reused across multiple fact tables. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/custom-generic-tests
E_3: The not_null test validates that individual column values aren't NULL, which is important for data quality but doesn't compare aggregate sums between tables. This test wouldn't detect scenarios where individual orders have incorrect amounts that still sum to a different total than the source system. It addresses a different data quality concern.
E_4: Correct. Singular tests (SQL files in tests/ folder) are perfect for custom validation logic like reconciliation. Create tests/reconciliation_orders.sql: select 'order_reconciliation' as test_name, fact_sum, source_sum, fact_sum - source_sum as difference from (select sum(amount) as fact_sum from {{ ref('fct_orders') }}) fact cross join (select sum(amount) as source_sum from {{ source('raw', 'orders') }}) source where abs(fact_sum - source_sum) > 0.01. The test fails if it returns rows, alerting you to mismatches. Singular tests are ideal for complex, specific validation logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests#singular-data-tests
E_5: The relationships test validates referential integrity (foreign key relationships), ensuring values in one column exist in another table's column. While this confirms all order IDs match, it doesn't validate that the sum of amounts matches between tables. Orders could have different amounts in each system while still having matching IDs. This test addresses referential integrity, not aggregate reconciliation.
E_6: Post-hooks execute after model materialization, but they're not part of dbt's testing framework and don't integrate with dbt test commands or test result reporting. Using post-hooks for testing creates non-standard patterns that are harder to maintain and monitor. Tests should be defined as actual dbt tests (generic or singular) to leverage dbt's testing infrastructure, reporting, and CI integration.
Explanation: OVERALL: Reconciliation testing requires comparing aggregated values between tables, which is best implemented through singular tests or custom macros. Singular test approach: Create a SQL file in tests/ that performs the aggregation comparison and returns rows when differences exceed your tolerance threshold. This is straightforward and easy to understand. Custom macro approach: Define a reusable macro for reconciliation logic that can be parameterized with different models, columns, and tolerance levels. This promotes DRY principles when you have many reconciliation tests. For both approaches, consider: (1) Using absolute value for differences to catch both over and under counting, (2) Implementing percentage-based thresholds for large values, (3) Storing results in a test failures table for investigation using store_failures, (4) Running reconciliation tests on a schedule that aligns with source system reporting updates, (5) Documenting expected reconciliation times and known discrepancies. Combine reconciliation tests with data freshness checks to ensure you're comparing equivalent time periods. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/custom-generic-tests

Question: You are developing a dbt model that needs to reference a source table. Which approach is the BEST practice for referencing source data in dbt models?
Q_1: Use the {{ source('source_name', 'table_name') }} function to reference the source table after defining it in a sources.yml file
Q_2: Directly reference the table using the fully qualified name like database.schema.table_name
Q_3: Create a variable for each source table and reference it using {{ var('table_name') }}
Q_4: Use the {{ ref('source_table') }} function to reference the source table
Q_5: Import the source table as a seed file and reference it using {{ ref('seed_name') }}
E_1: Correct. This is the best practice for referencing source data in dbt. By defining sources in a sources.yml file and using the source() function, you gain several benefits: dbt can check if the source exists at runtime, you can add source freshness checks, dbt builds a complete lineage graph including sources, and you centralize source definitions making it easier to update schemas or database connections. The source() function also supports built-in source freshness testing and metadata documentation. This approach ensures maintainability, testability, and clear data lineage. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources
E_2: While this technically works, it bypasses dbt's source management system. Direct references don't appear in the lineage graph, can't be tested for freshness, and make it harder to update source locations. You lose the benefits of source documentation, metadata tracking, and source-level testing. This approach also makes the code less portable across environments since database and schema names are hardcoded.
E_3: Variables are useful for configuration values that change between environments, but they're not the appropriate tool for managing source references. This approach doesn't provide source freshness testing, lineage tracking, or source documentation capabilities. Variables also require additional configuration in dbt_project.yml or profiles.yml, adding unnecessary complexity for source management.
E_4: The ref() function is specifically designed for referencing other dbt models, seeds, or snapshots within your project, not external source tables. Using ref() for source tables will fail because dbt expects to find a model file with that name in your project. Source tables are external to your dbt project and must be referenced using the source() function instead.
E_5: Seeds are intended for small, static reference data that you manage in CSV files within your dbt project (like country codes or status mappings). Importing actual source tables as seeds is impractical for production data, doesn't scale for large datasets, and defeats the purpose of having a source system. This approach also requires manually updating the CSV file whenever source data changes.
Explanation: OVERALL: The source() function combined with source definitions in YAML files is the cornerstone of best practice source management in dbt. Define your sources in a sources.yml (or sources/.yml) file with metadata including database, schema, table names, descriptions, and freshness criteria. Then reference them using {{ source('source_name', 'table_name') }} in your models. This provides complete lineage tracking, source freshness testing with dbt source freshness commands, centralized documentation, and runtime validation. dbt will check that sources exist before running models and can generate warnings if sources are stale. This approach makes your project more maintainable, self-documenting, and easier to adapt to environment changes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/sources and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/source

Question: You're developing a dimension table model that requires slowly changing dimension (SCD) Type 2 logic to track historical changes. What is the MOST effective dbt pattern to implement this?
Q_1: Use a snapshot with strategy='timestamp' to track changes automatically
Q_2: Use an incremental model with custom SCD Type 2 logic using MERGE statements and effective date tracking
Q_3: Create a view that uses ROW_NUMBER() to deduplicate and show only current records
Q_4: Use dbt's built-in SCD Type 2 materialization
Q_5: Use post-hooks to update historical records after each model run
E_1: While snapshots can implement SCD Type 2, the strategy should be 'timestamp' or 'check' depending on your source data characteristics. However, the question asks for the most effective pattern, and snapshots have limitations: they require a full table scan on each run, can't easily handle deleted records, and create a separate snapshot table rather than integrating with your dimensional model. For complex SCD logic, a custom incremental model often provides more control.
E_2: Correct. Implementing SCD Type 2 logic in an incremental model with custom MERGE/UPDATE/INSERT logic provides maximum flexibility and control. You can track effective dates (valid_from, valid_to), manage current flags (is_current), handle deletions, and optimize performance for large dimensions. The pattern typically involves: (1) detecting changes via hash keys or column comparisons, (2) expiring old records by updating valid_to dates, (3) inserting new records with current effective dates. This approach integrates seamlessly with your dimensional modeling and allows for custom business logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_3: A view with ROW_NUMBER() can show current records but doesn't preserve historical data, which is the core requirement of SCD Type 2. This approach only implements SCD Type 1 (overwrite) logic, where historical values are lost. SCD Type 2 requires physically storing multiple versions of each dimension record with validity timestamps.
E_4: dbt does not have a built-in SCD Type 2 materialization type. The available materializations are table, view, incremental, ephemeral, and materialized view. SCD Type 2 logic must be implemented using snapshots (which have specific limitations) or custom logic in incremental models. There is no native 'scd_type_2' materialization.
E_5: Post-hooks execute SQL after the model builds, but they don't have access to dbt's compilation context or the ability to compare current vs. previous state efficiently. Using post-hooks for SCD logic creates a separation between the model and its historical tracking, making the logic harder to test and maintain. The historical tracking should be integrated into the model's core logic.
Explanation: OVERALL: Implementing SCD Type 2 in dbt requires choosing between snapshots and custom incremental models. Snapshots (dbt snapshot command) are simpler for basic use cases and automatically track changes using timestamp or check strategies, but they have limitations with deleted records and performance on large tables. For more control, implement SCD Type 2 logic in an incremental model: (1) Add surrogate key, valid_from, valid_to, and is_current columns. (2) In incremental logic, detect changes using hash keys: {% if is_incremental() %} with source_changes as (select * from source where not exists matching hash in target or updated_at > target_updated_at), expired_records as (update target set valid_to=current_date, is_current=false where hash changed), new_records as (insert source_changes with valid_from=current_date, is_current=true) {%endif%}. (3) Use unique_key and merge strategy for upserts. This pattern provides flexibility for business rules, handles deletions, and optimizes for incremental processing. Test thoroughly with unit tests and run periodically to maintain history. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/snapshots

Question: [SELECT 2] Your team wants to optimize CI/CD pipeline run times by only running tests and building models that have been modified. Which dbt commands effectively leverage state for selective execution? (Select TWO)
Q_1: dbt test --select state:modified+ --state ./prod-state
Q_2: dbt run --models @state:new --state ./prod-state
Q_3: dbt build --select state:modified+ --defer --state ./prod-state
Q_4: dbt compile --state:modified --state ./prod-state
Q_5: dbt snapshot --modified-only --state ./prod-state
Q_6: dbt source state:freshness --state ./prod-state
E_1: Correct. This command leverages dbt state to test only the models that have been modified (state:modified) plus their downstream dependencies (+). The --state flag points to a directory containing the manifest.json from a previous run (typically production). This is highly efficient for CI/CD because it only runs tests on models that could be affected by your changes. The state:modified selector compares your current project code against the state in the manifest to identify what has changed. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/methods#the-state-method
E_2: The @ operator (at-operator) is not valid for state selection in dbt. While @ is used for specific node selection in some contexts, the correct syntax for state-based selection uses either state:modified, state:new, or state:modified+ (with the + operator for downstream dependencies). This command would result in an error due to incorrect syntax.
E_3: Correct. The dbt build command runs models, tests, snapshots, and seeds in DAG order. Combined with state:modified+ it will build only modified resources and their downstream dependencies. The --defer flag makes this even more efficient by referencing upstream models from the production state instead of rebuilding them, ensuring you're testing against production-like data. This is an optimal approach for CI/CD workflows because it validates changes while minimizing compute resources. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/defer
E_4: This syntax is incorrect. The --state:modified should be --select state:modified. Additionally, dbt compile generates SQL without executing it, which doesn't fulfill the requirement of 'running tests and building models.' While compile is useful for validation, it doesn't actually execute tests or materialize models in your warehouse. The correct syntax would be dbt compile --select state:modified --state ./prod-state, but even then, it only compiles without testing or building.
E_5: There is no --modified-only flag for dbt snapshot. Snapshots capture type-2 slowly changing dimensions and don't support state-based selective execution in the same way models do. While you can use --select with state methods (like dbt snapshot --select state:modified --state ./prod-state), the --modified-only flag doesn't exist. Snapshots are typically run on a schedule for all configured snapshot models.
E_6: This syntax is incorrect. The command should be dbt source freshness, not 'dbt source state:freshness'. The dbt source freshness command checks source data freshness but doesn't support state-based selection like models do. Source freshness checks operate independently of state comparison because they're validating external source tables rather than dbt models. The correct command syntax is simply dbt source freshness with optional --select flags for specific sources.
Explanation: OVERALL: State-based selection is a powerful feature in dbt Cloud and dbt Core that enables efficient CI/CD workflows by comparing your current project against a previous state (typically production). The key selectors are state:new (resources that don't exist in the prior state), state:modified (resources with changed code), and state:modified+ (modified resources plus downstream dependencies). The --defer flag complements state selection by referencing upstream models from the production manifest instead of rebuilding them. This combination is essential for slim CI runs that validate changes without rebuilding the entire project. Store your production manifest.json as an artifact and reference it with --state ./path/to/state. Best practice is to use dbt build --select state:modified+ --defer --state ./prod-state in CI to test only what changed while deferring to production for upstream dependencies. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/methods#the-state-method and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/defer

Question: You want to ensure that a specific column combination (customer_id, order_date) is unique across your fact table. What is the BEST testing approach?
Q_1: Apply the unique test to both columns separately
Q_2: Use dbt-utils unique_combination_of_columns test on the column pair
Q_3: Create a relationships test between the two columns
Q_4: Use the not_null test on both columns
Q_5: Implement a post-hook that counts duplicate combinations
E_1: Testing each column individually for uniqueness doesn't validate that the combination is unique. A customer could have multiple orders on different dates (customer_id not unique) and multiple customers could have orders on the same date (order_date not unique), but the combination might still be unique. Separate unique tests don't address the requirement for composite uniqueness.
E_2: Correct. The dbt-utils unique_combination_of_columns test is specifically designed for composite key validation. In schema.yml: models: - name: fct_orders, tests: - dbt_utils.unique_combination_of_columns: {combination_of_columns: ['customer_id', 'order_date']}. This test generates a surrogate key from the column combination and checks for uniqueness, failing if any combination appears more than once. It's the standard approach for validating composite keys. You can also specify quote_columns: true if column names need quoting. This test is essential for fact tables with composite business keys. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/
