Q_1: dbt uses the macro from the package that was installed first, based on the order in packages.yml and alphabetically by package name
Q_2: dbt throws an error and requires you to explicitly namespace all macro calls
Q_3: dbt merges the macros from all packages into a single combined version
Q_4: dbt uses the macro from whichever package has the highest version number
Q_5: dbt randomly selects one of the overlapping macros
E_1: Correct. dbt's macro resolution follows a specific search order: (1) Macros in your project's macros/ folder (highest priority), (2) Macros from installed packages in alphabetical order by package name, (3) dbt's built-in global macros. If multiple packages define the same macro name, dbt uses the one from the alphabetically first package. For example, if both 'aaa_package' and 'zzz_package' define get_data(), dbt uses aaa_package.get_data(). To explicitly call a specific package's macro, use the package namespace: {{ dbt_utils.surrogate_key() }} or {{ dbt_expectations.expect_column_values_to_be_unique() }}. This namespacing bypasses the search order and calls the specific package macro. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/dispatch
E_2: dbt doesn't throw errors for overlapping macro names; it silently resolves them based on search order (project macros > packages alphabetically > global macros). While explicit namespacing is a best practice for clarity, it's not required. dbt will execute successfully using its resolution logic, though the macro used might not be the one you intended if you're not aware of the search order.
E_3: dbt doesn't merge or combine macros with the same name from multiple packages. Each macro is a distinct code unit, and dbt selects one based on its resolution order. Merging macros would be impossible in most cases as they likely have different implementations and purposes. dbt uses one macro and ignores others with the same name.
E_4: Package version numbers don't affect macro resolution order. dbt resolves macros based on namespace (project vs package vs global) and alphabetical package name order, not version numbers. Two packages at v1.0.0 and v2.5.0 would be resolved alphabetically by package name, not by version.
E_5: dbt's macro resolution is deterministic and follows a specific, predictable search order, not random selection. The resolution is consistent across runs: project macros first, then packages alphabetically, then global macros. Random selection would make dbt behavior unpredictable and unreliable.
Explanation: OVERALL: Understanding macro resolution prevents unexpected behavior in projects using multiple packages. Resolution order: (1) Project macros in macros/ folder: Highest priority. If you define a macro with the same name as a package macro, yours takes precedence. (2) Package macros alphabetically: If multiple packages define the same macro, dbt uses the one from the alphabetically first package (not first in packages.yml, but alphabetically by name). (3) dbt global macros: Built-in macros like ref(), source(), config(). Best practices: (1) Use explicit namespacing for package macros to avoid ambiguity: {{ dbt_utils.surrogate_key() }}, not {{ surrogate_key() }}. (2) Check for macro conflicts when adding new packages: Use dbt ls --resource-type macro to list all available macros. (3) Override package macros intentionally: Create project macro with same name to customize behavior. (4) Document macro sources: Comment which package a macro comes from if using non-namespaced calls. (5) Use adapter.dispatch() for cross-database macro variants. For conflicts between packages you don't control, you can create a wrapper macro in your project that calls the specific package version you want: {% macro surrogate_key(fields) %} {{ dbt_utils.surrogate_key(fields) }} {% endmacro %}. This makes your preference explicit. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros#macro-namespacing and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/dispatch

Question: You need to implement a test that validates whether the revenue values in your orders table are always positive. Which approach follows dbt best practices for implementing this test?
Q_1: Use the dbt-utils package's accepted_range test with min_value set to 0
Q_2: Add a WHERE clause in the model SQL to filter out negative values
Q_3: Create a post-hook that throws an error if negative values are found
Q_4: Use a not_null test on the revenue column
Q_5: Define a relationships test comparing revenue to a reference table of valid values
E_1: Correct. The dbt-utils package provides the accepted_range test specifically for validating numeric values fall within a specified range. Setting min_value: 0 (or min_value: 0.01 if you want strictly positive) is the standard, maintainable way to test for positive values. This approach is reusable, well-documented, and follows community best practices. You can apply it in your schema.yml file like: tests: - dbt_utils.accepted_range: min_value: 0. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests
E_2: Filtering negative values in the model SQL would remove the bad data from your output, which hides data quality issues rather than alerting you to them. Tests should validate data quality and fail when issues are detected, not silently remove problematic records. This approach prevents proper monitoring and debugging of upstream data issues.
E_3: Post-hooks run after the model has been materialized, which means the table with potentially invalid data has already been created. While you could use this approach, it's not following dbt conventions for testing. Tests should be defined in schema files and run using dbt test, not implemented as hooks. This also makes the test harder to discover and maintain.
E_4: The not_null test only validates that values are not NULL; it doesn't check whether numeric values are positive, negative, or zero. A revenue column could be -100 and still pass a not_null test. This test doesn't address the requirement of validating positive values.
E_5: The relationships test validates referential integrity between tables (foreign key relationships), not numeric ranges or value constraints. This test checks if values in one column exist in another table's column, which is not appropriate for validating whether revenue values are positive.
Explanation: OVERALL: When testing numeric constraints like positive values, the accepted_range test from dbt-utils is the standard solution. It's specifically designed for this use case and provides clear, maintainable configuration. To implement, first ensure dbt-utils is installed in your packages.yml, then add the test to your schema.yml: columns: - name: revenue, tests: - dbt_utils.accepted_range: {min_value: 0}. For strictly positive values (excluding zero), use min_value: 0.01 or an appropriate small positive number. The test will fail if any revenue values fall outside the specified range, alerting you to data quality issues. You can also specify max_value if needed for upper bounds. This approach integrates seamlessly with dbt test commands and provides clear failure messages. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/tests and https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/

Question: [SELECT 3] You need to create a reusable piece of SQL logic that can be called from multiple models in your dbt project. Which of the following are valid approaches for code reusability in dbt? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Create a stored procedure in your database and call it using CALL statements in your models
Q_2: Create a macro in the macros/ directory and call it using {{ macro_name(args) }} in your models
Q_3: Create a base model that other models can reference using {{ ref('base_model') }}
Q_4: Use Python functions defined in external .py files and import them into your model SQL
Q_5: Copy and paste the SQL code into each model that needs it
Q_6: Reference reusable SQL snippets using {{ ref('model') }} combined with CTEs in your models
E_1: While you can technically create and call stored procedures from dbt models, this approach has significant drawbacks. Stored procedures are database-specific code that lives outside your dbt project, aren't version-controlled with your transformations, won't appear in dbt's lineage graph, and make your project less portable across database platforms. Stored procedures also require additional database permissions and don't benefit from dbt's compilation and testing features. dbt provides better, native solutions for code reusability.
E_2: Correct. Macros are dbt's primary mechanism for reusable SQL logic. Create macro files in the macros/ directory (or subdirectories) using {% macro macro_name(arguments) %}...{% endmacro %} syntax. Macros can contain SQL snippets, complex logic, or entire query patterns. Call them using Jinja syntax {{ macro_name(args) }} anywhere in your models. Macros are compiled during dbt's compilation phase, support arguments and default values, can include control flow logic (if statements, loops), and are portable across database platforms when using dbt's cross-database macros. Macros are version-controlled with your project and can be tested independently. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros
E_3: Correct. Using base models (or staging models) that downstream models reference is a fundamental pattern for code reusability in dbt. Create a model with shared transformation logic, then reference it in multiple downstream models using {{ ref('base_model') }}. This approach promotes the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle by ensuring core business logic is defined once. Base models appear in the lineage graph showing dependencies clearly, are materialized in the database (unlike macros which are compiled inline), and can be tested independently. This is particularly effective for staging models that standardize source data for use across multiple marts. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/models
E_4: Standard SQL models in dbt cannot directly import or call Python functions. While dbt does support Python models (models written in Python rather than SQL), these are separate from SQL models and follow different patterns. You cannot import Python functions into .sql model files. For reusable logic in SQL models, use macros (Jinja) or references to other models. Python models are a specific feature for different use cases, not a mechanism for sharing code between SQL models.
E_5: Copy-pasting code violates the DRY principle and creates significant maintenance problems. When logic needs to change, you must update it in multiple places, increasing the risk of inconsistencies and errors. This approach makes code harder to test, debug, and maintain. It's the opposite of best practice for code reusability. Always use macros, base models, or other dbt-native reusability patterns instead of duplicating code.
E_6: Correct. Using CTEs (Common Table Expressions) to reference other models is an effective pattern for building layered transformations. Start your model with CTEs that reference base models or staging models: WITH base_data as (SELECT * FROM {{ ref('base_model') }}), then build additional transformation layers in subsequent CTEs. This creates readable, maintainable code where each CTE represents a logical transformation step. The pattern of stacking CTEs that reference other models promotes reusability because the referenced models can be used by multiple downstream models, and each model's logic is clearly separated into steps. This is a core pattern in dbt best practices. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt provides multiple complementary approaches for code reusability. Macros (defined in the macros/ directory using Jinja syntax) are ideal for reusable SQL snippets, dynamic SQL generation, and cross-database abstraction. Use macros for logic that generates SQL code, needs to be configurable with arguments, or should be applied consistently across many models (e.g., date formatting, standard calculations). Base models are perfect for reusable transformations of actual data. Create staging models that clean and standardize source data, then reference them in multiple downstream models using {{ ref() }}. This creates clear lineage and allows you to materialize intermediate results. The CTE pattern combined with {{ ref() }} provides readable, layered transformations where each model builds on previous models. Structure your project hierarchically: sources → staging models (basic cleaning) → intermediate models (reusable business logic) → marts (final analytics tables). Each layer can be referenced and reused by multiple downstream models. For cross-project reusability, consider creating dbt packages. Combine these patterns effectively: use macros for SQL generation logic, base models for reusable data transformations, and CTEs for structuring individual model logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros, https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging, and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/packages

Question: [SELECT 3] You're building a customer segmentation model that requires complex window functions, CTEs, and multiple aggregation levels. What are the BEST practices for structuring this model? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Write all logic in a single SQL file to keep everything together
Q_2: Use ephemeral materialization for all intermediate steps
Q_3: Use a combination of source -> staging -> intermediate -> marts layers for clear data flow
Q_4: Break down the model into staging, intermediate, and final mart layers using ref() between them
Q_5: Use CTEs within the model to organize logic into readable, sequential steps
Q_6: Add extensive inline comments explaining every calculation and window function
E_1: Writing all complex logic in a single file creates a monolithic model that's difficult to understand, test, and maintain. When a model has multiple aggregation levels and complex transformations, it becomes hard to debug issues, add tests at intermediate stages, and reuse logic. This violates the modularity principle that makes dbt projects maintainable. Large single files also make code review and collaboration more difficult.
E_2: While ephemeral models can simplify DAG visualization by not creating database objects, using them for all intermediate steps in a complex model can cause performance issues. Each ephemeral model is compiled as a CTE that's repeated in every downstream model referencing it. For complex window functions and aggregations, this can result in redundant computation. Ephemeral is best for simple transformations, not complex multi-step calculations.
E_3: Incorrect. This choice is actually a correct answer choice, but let me explain why. Using ephemeral models for all intermediate steps would cause the complex window functions and aggregations to be re-computed multiple times if multiple downstream models reference them. This is inefficient for resource-intensive calculations.
E_4: Correct. Breaking complex models into layers (staging for light transformations, intermediate for business logic and aggregations, marts for final presentation) makes each piece understandable and testable. For a customer segmentation model: (1) stg_customers: Clean and standardize source data, (2) int_customer_orders: Calculate customer order aggregations, (3) int_customer_behavior: Apply window functions for behavior metrics, (4) fct_customer_segments: Final segmentation logic. Each layer is independently testable, can be materialized appropriately (view for staging, table for expensive intermediate calculations, table for marts), and can be reused by other models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/1-guide-overview
E_5: Correct. CTEs (Common Table Expressions) are essential for organizing complex SQL within a single model file. They make the query readable by breaking it into logical steps with descriptive names. For example: with customer_base as (...), order_aggregates as (...), behavior_metrics as (...), segment_assignment as (...) select * from segment_assignment. CTEs should be used for each logical transformation step, with clear naming that describes what each CTE produces. This approach keeps SQL understandable while maintaining all logic within the model for cases where creating separate models would be over-engineering. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging
E_6: Correct. Complex window functions, aggregations, and business logic should be thoroughly documented with inline comments. Explain the 'why' behind calculations, not just the 'what'. For example: '-- Calculate 90-day rolling average to smooth seasonal variations' or '-- Using ROW_NUMBER() PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC to find most recent order'. Comments make it easier for future maintainers (including yourself) to understand complex logic. Combine inline comments with model-level documentation in schema.yml files. However, if comments become excessive, it might indicate the logic should be broken into more granular models. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-style/0-how-we-style-our-dbt-projects
Explanation: OVERALL: Complex models benefit from both architectural patterns (layering) and code organization (CTEs, comments). The layered approach: Break your segmentation model into intermediate steps: stg_customers (standardized customer data), int_customer_metrics (aggregated order stats), int_customer_behavior (window function calculations for RFM or cohorts), fct_customer_segments (final segmentation logic). Each layer can be tested independently and materialized appropriately. Within each model, use CTEs to organize the transformation steps: with base as (select...), calculate_rfm as (select..., row_number() over (partition by...) as...), assign_segments as (case when...). Name CTEs descriptively. Add inline comments for complex logic, especially window functions and business rules. Document the overall model purpose and methodology in schema.yml. This combination of modularity, readability, and documentation makes complex models maintainable and understandable. Avoid premature optimization—start with clear structure and optimize materialization only where performance requires it. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/1-guide-overview and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-style/0-how-we-style-our-dbt-projects

Question: [SELECT 2] An incremental model is running slowly and consuming excessive resources. The model processes 1 million new rows daily but scans the entire 500 million row table on each run. What optimizations would improve performance? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Change the materialization to ephemeral to avoid creating tables
Q_2: Convert the model to a view materialization
Q_3: Implement table partitioning on a date column and use partition filters in the incremental logic
Q_4: Add cluster keys to organize data by frequently filtered columns
Q_5: Disable all tests on the model to reduce execution time
Q_6: Set materialized: 'table' with a full refresh on every run
E_1: Ephemeral models don't materialize as database objects; they're compiled as CTEs in downstream models. For a model with 500 million rows, making it ephemeral would cause catastrophic performance issues—the entire 500 million row transformation would be re-executed in every downstream query. Ephemeral is for small, simple transformations, not large fact tables. This would make performance worse, not better.
E_2: Views don't store data physically; they execute the full query each time they're accessed. Converting a 500 million row table to a view would make performance dramatically worse—every query would need to scan and transform the entire dataset. Views are appropriate for lightweight transformations or providing different perspectives on data, not for large, complex transformations that should be materialized.
E_3: Correct. Table partitioning (available in Snowflake, BigQuery, and other modern warehouses) physically organizes data by partition key (typically date). When your incremental logic filters on the partitioned column (WHERE event_date >= '2024-01-01'), the database only scans relevant partitions, dramatically reducing the data scanned. Implement by: (1) Configure partition in model config: {{ config(partition_by={'field': 'event_date', 'data_type': 'date'}) }}, (2) Ensure incremental logic filters on partition column: {% if is_incremental() %} where event_date > (select max(event_date) from {{ this }}) {% endif %}. This can reduce scans from 500M to 1M rows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/bigquery-configs#partition-clause
E_4: Correct. Clustering (available in Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks) organizes data within partitions based on specified columns, enabling efficient pruning when those columns are used in filters. If your incremental logic filters by customer_id or region, clustering on those columns allows the database to skip irrelevant micro-partitions. Implement: {{ config(cluster_by=['customer_id', 'event_date']) }}. Clustering works best when queries filter or join on cluster keys. Combined with partitioning, this provides powerful performance optimization. Monitor cluster efficiency using database-specific metrics (Snowflake: SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/snowflake-configs#clustering-keys
E_5: Disabling tests eliminates data quality validation and doesn't address the core performance issue—the slow model execution itself. Tests run after the model builds, so disabling them would only save test execution time, not the model build time where the real performance problem exists. This sacrifices data quality without solving the underlying issue.
E_6: Full table refreshes require scanning and rebuilding the entire 500 million row table, which is the opposite of what's needed. The problem description indicates the model should be incremental (processing 1 million new rows daily), not doing full rebuilds. Full refreshes would make performance worse and consume even more resources. Incremental models exist specifically to avoid full refreshes for large tables.
Explanation: OVERALL: Optimizing incremental models for large tables requires leveraging database-specific features. Partitioning divides tables into segments based on a column (usually date), allowing queries to scan only relevant partitions. Clustering organizes data within partitions by frequently-filtered columns, enabling micro-partition pruning. Best practices: (1) Partition on the column used in incremental logic (typically date/timestamp), (2) Cluster on columns used in WHERE/JOIN clauses, (3) Ensure incremental logic includes partition filter: {% if is_incremental() %} where event_date > (select max(event_date) from {{ this }}) {% endif %}, (4) Monitor query performance with EXPLAIN plans and warehouse-specific profiling tools, (5) Consider incremental_strategy: 'delete+insert' for simpler logic when updates are minimal, (6) Use materialized views where supported for automatically maintained aggregations, (7) Regularly analyze and optimize clustering with OPTIMIZE TABLE (Databricks) or automatic clustering (Snowflake/BigQuery). Test performance improvements with production-scale data in development environments. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/bigquery-configs#partitioning

Question: You are building a dimensional model in dbt and need to implement a slowly changing dimension (SCD) Type 2 for a customer dimension that tracks historical changes. Which dbt feature is BEST suited for this use case?
Q_1: Create an incremental model with merge strategy and track changes manually using CASE statements
Q_2: Use a view materialization with window functions to track historical versions
Q_3: Implement a snapshot using dbt's snapshot functionality with timestamp or check strategy
Q_4: Use an ephemeral model with a post-hook to capture changes
Q_5: Create a seed file with all historical records and update it manually whenever data changes
E_1: While you could technically implement SCD Type 2 logic manually in an incremental model, this requires complex SQL to detect changes, expire old records, and insert new versions with appropriate valid_from and valid_to timestamps. This approach is error-prone, difficult to maintain, and requires you to handle all the SCD Type 2 logic yourself including generating surrogate keys and managing effective dates. dbt provides a better, purpose-built solution.
E_2: Views don't store historical data; they're just saved queries that execute each time they're queried. Views query the current state of source data and cannot maintain historical records once the source data changes. SCD Type 2 requires persisting historical versions of records, which views cannot do. You need a materialization strategy that actually stores data over time.
E_3: Correct. dbt snapshots are specifically designed for implementing SCD Type 2 dimensions. Snapshots capture and preserve historical records by adding dbt_valid_from, dbt_valid_to, and dbt_updated_at columns automatically. You define a snapshot with either a timestamp strategy (tracks changes based on an updated_at field) or check strategy (detects changes by comparing all or specific columns). When you run dbt snapshot, it identifies new and changed records, expires old versions by setting dbt_valid_to, and inserts new versions. This provides a robust, tested implementation of SCD Type 2 without requiring custom logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/snapshots
E_4: Ephemeral models don't create tables or views in the database; they're compiled as CTEs (common table expressions) within models that reference them. Since ephemeral models don't persist any data, they cannot store historical records. Additionally, post-hooks run SQL after a model builds but don't provide SCD Type 2 functionality. This approach fundamentally cannot work for maintaining historical dimensions.
E_5: Seeds are for small, static reference data stored in CSV files within your repository. Manually updating a CSV file every time dimension data changes is completely impractical for any real-world scenario. This approach doesn't scale, requires manual intervention, defeats the purpose of automated data pipelines, and would result in an enormous CSV file. Seeds are not designed for or appropriate for managing SCD Type 2 dimensions.
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt snapshots provide a turnkey solution for SCD Type 2 dimensions by automatically tracking changes in mutable source tables. Create a snapshot in the snapshots/ directory and configure it with either timestamp strategy (uses an updated_at-style column to detect changes) or check strategy (compares specified columns or all columns to detect changes). When you run dbt snapshot, dbt identifies records that are new or changed, sets dbt_valid_to on old versions to mark them as historical, and inserts new versions with current dbt_valid_from values. Configure snapshots with unique_key to identify records and target_schema to specify where snapshots are stored. Snapshots build full historical tables showing all versions of records over time, enabling temporal queries and historical analysis. For the check strategy, specify check_cols to track specific columns or use 'all' to track all columns. Snapshots work best when run on a regular schedule (daily or more frequently) and are ideal for tracking customer attributes, product details, or any other slowly changing reference data. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/snapshots and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/snapshot-configs

Question: You need to create a model that pivots monthly revenue data from rows into columns (one column per month). What SQL pattern is MOST appropriate?
Q_1: Use UNION ALL to combine monthly data
Q_2: Use multiple JOINs to combine monthly tables
Q_3: Use GROUP BY to aggregate monthly data
Q_4: Create ephemeral models for each month and combine them
Q_5: Use CASE statements within SUM/MAX aggregations: SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Jan' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) as jan_revenue
E_1: UNION ALL stacks rows vertically, combining similar datasets into a longer table. This is the opposite of pivoting, which transforms rows into columns to create a wider table. UNION operations add more rows, while pivoting redistributes existing rows into a columnar format. This pattern doesn't address the pivoting requirement.
E_2: While you could technically create monthly aggregations as separate CTEs and JOIN them (each month's data as a separate table joined by a common key), this is inefficient and doesn't scale. You'd need one JOIN per month, creating unwieldy SQL for many time periods. This approach doesn't leverage SQL's aggregation capabilities for pivoting.
E_3: GROUP BY aggregates data but doesn't pivot it. GROUP BY month would create rows for each month with aggregated values, not columns for each month. While GROUP BY is part of pivoting logic, alone it doesn't transform row-based monthly data into column-based format. Pivoting requires conditional aggregation or PIVOT syntax.
E_4: Creating separate ephemeral models for each month creates unnecessary complexity and doesn't provide a pivoting solution. You'd still need to combine them horizontally (as columns), which brings you back to the JOIN problem. Ephemeral materialization doesn't help with pivoting—it's a materialization strategy, not a data transformation pattern.
E_5: Correct. Conditional aggregation is the standard cross-database pattern for pivoting. Pattern: SELECT customer_id, SUM(CASE WHEN month_name = 'January' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) as jan_revenue, SUM(CASE WHEN month_name = 'February' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) as feb_revenue, SUM(CASE WHEN month_name = 'March' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) as mar_revenue FROM monthly_revenue GROUP BY customer_id. This transforms row-based monthly data into columns. Some databases (SQL Server, Oracle) have native PIVOT syntax, but CASE-based aggregation works across all platforms and is more flexible. For dynamic month lists, use macros to generate CASE statements. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/3-intermediate
Explanation: OVERALL: Pivoting in SQL requires conditional aggregation with CASE statements. Complete pattern: with base_data as (select customer_id, date_trunc('month', order_date) as month, sum(revenue) as monthly_revenue from orders group by 1, 2) select customer_id, sum(case when month = '2024-01-01' then monthly_revenue else 0 end) as jan_2024, sum(case when month = '2024-02-01' then monthly_revenue else 0 end) as feb_2024, sum(case when month = '2024-03-01' then monthly_revenue else 0 end) as mar_2024 from base_data group by customer_id. For dynamic pivoting (unknown number of months), create a dbt macro: {% macro pivot_months(column_name, values, aggregation='sum') %} {% for value in values %} {{ aggregation }}(case when month = '{{ value }}' then {{ column_name }} else 0 end) as {{ value | replace('-', '_') }}{% if not loop.last %},{% endif %} {% endfor %} {% endmacro %}. Use it: select customer_id, {{ pivot_months('revenue', ['2024-01', '2024-02', '2024-03']) }} from base_data group by customer_id. Alternative: Some databases support PIVOT syntax (SQL Server, Oracle). For cross-database compatibility, conditional aggregation is preferred. Test for NULL handling (use COALESCE), verify aggregation logic (sum vs max vs min), and validate that GROUP BY includes all non-pivoted dimensions. Pivoting is common for reporting, time-series analysis, and dashboard preparation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/jinja-macros and https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/3-intermediate

Question: [SELECT 3] Your organization wants to maintain a comprehensive data catalog that includes business definitions, data lineage, and usage statistics. Which dbt features support this? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Using ephemeral materialization for all models
Q_2: Leveraging manifest.json and catalog.json artifacts for metadata extraction
Q_3: Storing catalog information in external spreadsheets
Q_4: Using pre-hooks to generate catalog metadata
Q_5: Using exposures to document downstream usage of models (dashboards, reports, ML models)
Q_6: Defining comprehensive descriptions, tags, and meta properties in schema.yml files
E_1: Ephemeral materialization compiles models as CTEs rather than creating database objects, which is a performance pattern unrelated to data cataloging. Ephemeral models are still documented in dbt docs but don't provide any special cataloging capabilities. Materialization type doesn't affect documentation quality or catalog completeness.
E_2: Correct. dbt generates manifest.json (project structure, models, tests, dependencies) and catalog.json (database metadata, table/column statistics) after running dbt docs generate. These artifacts contain comprehensive metadata that can be ingested by data catalog tools (Alation, Collibra, DataHub) or custom solutions. manifest.json includes model configurations, descriptions, tags, lineage; catalog.json includes row counts, column types, and database-level metadata. These artifacts enable automated catalog updates synchronized with code changes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json
E_3: External spreadsheets create documentation drift, require manual maintenance, and don't integrate with dbt's metadata system. Spreadsheets can't capture lineage automatically, don't update with code changes, and exist outside version control. This approach doesn't leverage dbt's capabilities and creates a parallel, error-prone documentation system.
E_4: Pre-hooks execute SQL before model materialization and can't generate documentation artifacts or catalog metadata. Pre-hooks operate in the SQL execution phase and don't have access to dbt's project-wide metadata or documentation system. Catalog metadata is generated by dbt docs generate, not through SQL hooks.
E_5: Correct. Exposures (defined in schema.yml) document how dbt models are consumed by downstream tools and processes: exposures: - name: weekly_sales_dashboard, type: dashboard, maturity: high, url: https://tableau.com/sales, owner: {name: 'BI Team', email: 'bi@company.com'}, depends_on: [ref('fct_sales'), ref('dim_customers')], description: 'Executive sales dashboard'. Exposures appear in dbt documentation and lineage graphs, showing where models are used. This closes the loop on data lineage—from sources through models to consumption points. Essential for impact analysis and data catalogs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/exposures
E_6: Correct. Rich documentation in schema.yml provides the foundation for a comprehensive data catalog: models: - name: fct_orders, description: 'Order fact table containing one row per order with customer and product dimensions', meta: {domain: 'sales', owner: 'analytics_team', update_frequency: 'hourly', contains_pii: false}, tags: ['daily', 'critical'], columns: - {name: order_id, description: 'Unique identifier for each order', tests: [unique, not_null]}, - {name: customer_id, description: 'Foreign key to dim_customers'}. These definitions appear in dbt docs, manifest.json, and can be integrated into enterprise data catalogs. Comprehensive documentation at the source ensures catalog accuracy. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation
Explanation: OVERALL: Building a comprehensive data catalog with dbt: (1) Document thoroughly: Write descriptions for all models, columns, and sources in schema.yml. Use doc blocks for extensive documentation. Add meta fields for ownership, domains, SLAs, and classifications. Apply tags for categorization. (2) Generate artifacts: Run dbt docs generate to create manifest.json and catalog.json. These contain all project metadata, lineage, and database statistics. (3) Define exposures: Document downstream usage (dashboards, reports, ML models) to show complete data lineage from sources to consumption. (4) Integrate with catalog tools: Many enterprise data catalog platforms (Alation, Collibra, Monte Carlo, DataHub) have native dbt integrations that ingest manifest.json and catalog.json. For custom integrations, parse these JSON artifacts to populate your catalog. (5) Use dbt Cloud: dbt Cloud provides a discovery UI with search, lineage, and documentation. (6) Maintain freshness: Keep documentation current through CI checks, required descriptions in governance policies, and regular documentation reviews. The combination of dbt docs (for technical users) and enterprise catalogs (for broader organization) provides comprehensive data discovery. Automate catalog updates via CI/CD to ensure documentation stays synchronized with code changes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json

Question: [SELECT 2] A dbt model is producing unexpected NULL values in calculated columns that should never be NULL. What debugging approaches would help identify the root cause? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Increase the threads setting in profiles.yml to improve query performance
Q_2: Change the model's materialization from table to view
Q_3: Use COALESCE or NULL handling functions to trace which input columns contain NULL values
Q_4: Add not_null tests to the model to prevent deployment
Q_5: Examine the compiled SQL in target/compiled/ and run it directly in your database with added diagnostic columns
Q_6: Disable the model using the enabled: false configuration
E_1: The threads configuration affects parallelization and performance but has no impact on query logic or NULL value generation. NULL values in calculated columns are caused by data issues (NULL inputs, division by zero, failed CAST operations, etc.) or SQL logic problems, not parallelization settings. Changing threads won't help diagnose or fix NULL generation.
E_2: Materialization strategy determines how the query results are stored in the database (table vs. view vs. incremental), but it doesn't affect the SQL logic or the values produced. A model that produces NULLs as a table will produce the same NULLs as a view. Changing materialization is not a debugging technique for data quality issues.
E_3: Correct. Adding COALESCE, IS NULL checks, or NULLIF functions helps identify where NULLs originate. For example, if a calculation is amount * discount_rate and producing NULLs, add intermediate columns: coalesce(amount, 0) as amount_check, coalesce(discount_rate, 0) as rate_check to see which input is NULL. You can also add: case when amount is null then 'amount_null' when discount_rate is null then 'rate_null' else 'both_present' end as null_source. This systematic approach identifies the NULL propagation path. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/guides/debugging-errors
E_4: Adding not_null tests is good practice and will catch the issue in testing, but tests alone don't help you understand why NULLs are being generated—they just alert you to the problem. Tests are valuable for detection and prevention, but you still need to debug the root cause. In this case, tests would fail without explaining whether NULLs come from source data, join mismatches, or calculation logic.
E_5: Correct. Looking at the compiled SQL (after Jinja rendering) shows the exact SQL being executed, which is essential for debugging. Copy this SQL and modify it to add diagnostic columns that help trace NULL generation: SELECT *, CASE WHEN col1 IS NULL THEN 'col1_null' WHEN col2 IS NULL THEN 'col2_null' END as null_reason, col1, col2, calculated_col FROM .... Run this directly in your database to understand the data flow and identify which inputs or transformation steps introduce NULLs. This hands-on debugging is often the fastest way to understand complex issues. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/project-configs/target-path
E_6: Disabling the model stops it from building but doesn't help debug why it's producing NULLs. This is avoidance, not debugging. You need to identify and fix the root cause, not disable the model. Disabling might be appropriate temporarily while fixing, but it's not a debugging technique.
Explanation: OVERALL: Debugging unexpected NULLs requires systematic investigation of data flow. Start by examining the compiled SQL in target/compiled/ to see the exact query being executed. Add diagnostic columns to track NULL propagation: coalesce(col1, -999) as col1_flag to distinguish NULLs from other values, case when col1 is null or col2 is null then 'null_input' else 'valid' end as input_status. Common NULL causes: (1) LEFT/RIGHT JOIN mismatches where join conditions don't match, (2) NULL values in source data propagating through calculations (any operation on NULL returns NULL), (3) Data type conversion failures (CAST operations failing silently), (4) Division by zero in some databases, (5) Aggregate functions on empty groups. Use database-specific functions: NULLIF, COALESCE, IFNULL (MySQL), NVL (Oracle), ISNULL (SQL Server). Build incrementally—comment out complex calculations and add them back one at a time to isolate the problematic logic. Add not_null tests after fixing to prevent regression. Consider adding intermediate models to materialize checkpoints in your transformation logic. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/guides/debugging-errors and https://docs.getdbt.com/faqs/Models/sql-dialect

Question: In dbt Cloud, you want to run only models that have changed since the last successful production run, along with their downstream dependencies. What command accomplishes this?
Q_1: dbt run --select state:modified
Q_2: dbt run --select state:new
Q_3: dbt run --models modified
Q_4: dbt run --full-refresh
Q_5: dbt run --select state:modified+ --state path/to/previous/manifest
E_1: The state:modified selector identifies models that have changed, but it doesn't include downstream dependencies. This would run only the changed models themselves, not the downstream models that depend on them. Downstream models might produce stale results if their upstream dependencies have changed but the downstream models themselves haven't been refreshed. You need to include the + operator for downstream selection.
E_2: The state:new selector identifies only newly created models that didn't exist in the previous state, not models that have been modified. This would miss changed existing models entirely and wouldn't include downstream dependencies. This selector is useful for identifying net-new models but doesn't address the requirement of running modified models and their dependents.
E_3: The 'modified' keyword without the state: prefix is not a valid dbt selector. State-based selection requires the state: prefix and the --state flag pointing to previous run artifacts. This command would fail with an error indicating 'modified' is not a recognized selector method. The correct syntax requires state:modified with appropriate state configuration.
E_4: The --full-refresh flag forces incremental models to rebuild completely from scratch, but it runs all models (or the specified selection), not just changed ones. This is the opposite of efficient state-based selection—it's a full rebuild. --full-refresh is used when you need to reset incremental models, not for selective execution based on changes.
E_5: Correct. This command uses state-based selection to run modified models and all downstream dependencies. The state:modified selector identifies models that have changed (code, config, or upstream dependencies), and the + operator includes all downstream models. The --state flag points to artifacts (manifest.json, run_results.json) from the previous production run. In dbt Cloud, the --state flag is automatically configured for CI jobs using the defer feature, making this pattern seamless. This approach ensures: (1) Only necessary models run, saving time and compute, (2) Downstream models are refreshed to reflect upstream changes, (3) Test coverage includes all affected models. This is the foundation of Slim CI in dbt Cloud. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/syntax#the-plus-operator
Explanation: OVERALL: State-based selection enables efficient CI/CD by running only what's changed and what's affected. Key concepts: (1) State comparison: dbt compares current project state (manifest.json) with previous state (from --state path) to identify differences. (2) state:modified: Selects models that changed (SQL, config, tests, dependencies). (3) + operator: Includes downstream dependencies (state:modified+ runs changed models and everything downstream). (4) --state flag: Points to previous run artifacts (manifest.json, run_results.json). In dbt Cloud CI jobs, use: 'dbt build --select state:modified+' with defer enabled. dbt Cloud automatically handles --state configuration. Additional selectors: state:new (new resources), state:modified.configs (config-only changes), 1+state:modified+2 (1 level upstream, changed models, 2 levels downstream). Slim CI workflow: (1) Production job saves artifacts, (2) CI job compares against production state, (3) Only changed/affected models run, (4) Tests run on affected models. This dramatically reduces CI run time (minutes vs. hours for large projects) while maintaining comprehensive validation. Ensure production artifacts are accessible (dbt Cloud manages this automatically, self-hosted requires artifact storage). Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/continuous-integration and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/syntax

Question: You want to test that dates in your orders table fall within a reasonable range (not in the future, not before business inception). What is the BEST testing approach?
Q_1: Use the not_null test on the date column
Q_2: Create a relationships test to a calendar dimension table
Q_3: Use dbt-utils expression_is_true test to validate date ranges
Q_4: Add a WHERE clause in the model to filter invalid dates
Q_5: Use the unique test to ensure no duplicate dates
E_1: The not_null test only validates that values aren't NULL, not that they fall within a specific range. A date could be '2099-12-31' or '1800-01-01' and still pass a not_null test. This test addresses a different data quality concern (completeness) rather than validity of date ranges.
E_2: The relationships test validates referential integrity (foreign key relationships), ensuring date values exist in a reference table. While you could create a calendar table with only valid dates and test relationships, this is overly complex for simple date range validation. It also requires maintaining a calendar table. Range validation is more directly addressed with expression-based tests.
E_3: Correct. The expression_is_true test from dbt-utils is perfect for custom assertions like date range validation. In your schema.yml: columns: - name: order_date, tests: - dbt_utils.expression_is_true: {expression: '>= date("2020-01-01") and <= current_date()', name: 'order_date_in_valid_range'}. This creates a test that fails if any rows violate the expression. You can also use expression: '>= (select min_date from {{ ref("company_metadata") }})' for dynamic bounds. This approach is flexible, readable, and handles any SQL expression. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/
E_4: Filtering invalid dates in the model SQL removes bad data rather than alerting you to quality issues. This hides problems instead of detecting them. Data quality issues should be surfaced through tests so they can be investigated and fixed at the source. Silently filtering bad data can mask upstream problems and lead to incomplete datasets.
E_5: The unique test validates that column values are unique, which is unrelated to date range validation. An orders table would typically have many orders per date (duplicates are expected), and uniqueness doesn't validate whether dates are reasonable. This test addresses a completely different data quality dimension.
Explanation: OVERALL: Range validation for dates requires expression-based testing. The dbt-utils expression_is_true test provides the most flexible solution. Example implementations: (1) Basic range: dbt_utils.expression_is_true: {expression: '>= "2020-01-01" and <= current_date'}. (2) Dynamic range from reference table: expression: '>= (select company_founded_date from {{ ref("company_info") }})'.(3) Relative range: expression: 'between current_date - interval 10 years and current_date'. (4) Multiple conditions: expression: '> "2020-01-01" and < current_date + interval 1 day and date != "2021-02-30"'. Give tests descriptive names (name: 'order_date_not_in_future') for clear failure messages. For simpler range validation on numeric columns, use dbt-utils.accepted_range. Consider implementing both low-bound and high-bound as separate tests for clearer failure messages. Use severity: warn for soft validation during development, error for production enforcement. Document your date range logic and rationale in test configuration. Combine with not_null to ensure completeness. These tests catch data quality issues early, preventing bad data from propagating through transformations. Reference: https://hub.getdbt.com/dbt-labs/dbt_utils/latest/ and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests

Question: A dbt run command completes successfully, but users report that a specific model's table is empty in the database. What is the MOST likely cause?
Q_1: The model is configured with enabled: false
Q_2: The threads configuration is set too low in profiles.yml
Q_3: The model's SQL query has a syntax error
Q_4: The model's WHERE clause filters out all rows, or source tables are empty
Q_5: The model uses ephemeral materialization
E_1: If enabled: false, the model wouldn't run at all and dbt would skip it with a message. The command completing successfully suggests the model did execute. An enabled: false configuration would prevent the model from being included in the DAG, not cause it to run and produce an empty table.
E_2: Thread configuration affects how many models run in parallel but doesn't impact whether individual models succeed or how much data they produce. Low thread count would make execution slower but wouldn't cause a model to create an empty table. This is a performance setting, not a data logic issue.
E_3: If the SQL had a syntax error, the model execution would fail and dbt run would report an error, not complete successfully. Syntax errors prevent model compilation and execution, causing the dbt command to fail with clear error messages. The scenario states the command completed successfully, ruling out syntax errors.
E_4: Correct. This is the most common cause of empty tables when dbt runs successfully. The model executes without errors but produces zero rows because: (1) WHERE clause conditions filter out all data (where event_date > current_date might exclude everything), (2) Source tables are empty or don't contain data matching join conditions, (3) Incremental logic filters to a date range with no data, (4) JOIN conditions don't match any rows (especially INNER JOINs). The SQL is valid and executes successfully, but the result set is empty. Debug by running the compiled SQL in target/compiled/ and removing filters incrementally to see where data disappears. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/guides/debugging-errors
E_5: Ephemeral models aren't materialized in the database at all—they exist only as CTEs in downstream models. If a model were ephemeral, users wouldn't find any table (empty or otherwise) to query. The scenario describes finding an empty table, which indicates the model is materialized as a table or view, not ephemeral. This materialization creates no database objects for users to query.
Explanation: OVERALL: Empty tables from successful dbt runs indicate a data logic issue, not a configuration or syntax problem. Common causes: (1) Overly restrictive WHERE clauses filtering all rows, (2) Empty source tables, (3) JOIN conditions with no matches (INNER/LEFT JOIN on mismatched keys), (4) Incremental logic querying non-existent date ranges, (5) CASE/IF statements returning NULL for all rows then filtered by WHERE col IS NOT NULL. To debug: Check target/compiled/[model].sql for the exact SQL executed. Run this SQL directly in your database. Remove filters incrementally (comment out WHERE clauses, change INNER to LEFT JOIN with LEFT.key IS NULL to see unmatched rows). Check upstream source/model row counts. Add row count logging: {{ log('Row count: ' ~ row_count, info=True) }}. Verify incremental logic isn't skipping all data. Check date/time filters for timezone issues or off-by-one errors. Implement data freshness checks and row count tests (dbt-utils.expression_is_true: expression: 'count(*) > 0') to catch empty tables early. Always test with representative data in development. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/guides/debugging-errors and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/log

Question: [SELECT 2] You want to generate entity relationship diagrams (ERD) automatically from your dbt project documentation. Which approaches enable this? (Select TWO)
Q_1: Manually create ERDs using diagramming tools and store them in project folder
Q_2: Use pre-hooks to generate diagrams before model execution
Q_3: Use dbt docs generate to create the documentation site, which includes lineage graphs showing model relationships
Q_4: Store table relationships in a separate database for querying
Q_5: Use tags to define entity relationships
Q_6: Use dbt artifacts (manifest.json) with external tools like dbterd or SchemaS py to generate ERDs
E_1: Manual ERDs exist outside dbt's automated documentation system, require separate maintenance, and become outdated as your project evolves. While you can embed manually-created diagrams in documentation via image links, this doesn't leverage dbt's metadata and lineage information. Manual approaches don't scale and create documentation drift.
E_2: Pre-hooks execute SQL before model materialization and can't generate documentation artifacts or ERDs. Pre-hooks operate in the SQL execution phase and don't have access to dbt's metadata graph or documentation generation capabilities. ERD generation requires parsing dbt's project metadata, not executing SQL queries.
E_3: Correct. The dbt docs generate command creates a documentation site that includes interactive lineage graphs (DAG visualization). While not traditional ERDs, these graphs show model dependencies, sources, and relationships. Click any model to see its lineage (upstream and downstream). The lineage view is automatically generated from ref() and source() functions in your models. For more traditional ERD-style views, the lineage graphs can be exported or you can use the manifest.json to build custom ERDs. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation
E_4: Storing relationships in a separate database creates a parallel metadata system disconnected from dbt's project metadata. This approach requires custom tooling, doesn't integrate with dbt docs, and adds maintenance complexity. dbt already captures relationships through ref(), source(), and relationship tests—duplicating this in a database is redundant.
E_5: Tags provide labels for organization and selection but don't define relationships between models. While you might use tags to categorize models, tags don't capture the dependency structure or foreign key relationships needed for ERDs. Relationships in dbt are expressed through ref() functions and relationship tests, not tags.
E_6: Correct. dbt's manifest.json contains comprehensive metadata about models, columns, relationships, and dependencies. External tools like dbterd parse manifest.json to generate traditional ERDs showing tables, columns, and relationships (from dbt relationship tests). Workflow: (1) Run dbt docs generate to create manifest.json, (2) Use dbterd tool: dbterd run -t /path/to/target, (3) Generate ERD in various formats (Mermaid, GraphViz, PlantUML). This leverages dbt's metadata for automated, up-to-date ERD generation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt provides multiple approaches for visualizing project structure and relationships. Native dbt docs: Run dbt docs generate && dbt docs serve to create the documentation site with interactive lineage graphs. The lineage view shows the full DAG: models, sources, tests, and dependencies. Click models to see immediate upstream/downstream dependencies. Use the graph selectors (+model_name+ syntax) to explore specific lineage branches. External ERD tools: Tools like dbterd, SchemaS py, or custom scripts parse manifest.json (in target/ after dbt docs generate) to create traditional ERDs. Example with dbterd: pip install dbterd, dbterd run -t ./target -o ./output --select tag:finance, generates ERD diagrams in multiple formats. These tools extract relationships from dbt's relationship tests and foreign key definitions. Custom solutions: Parse manifest.json in Python to extract nodes and edges, use graph libraries (NetworkX, Graphviz) to render custom visualizations, integrate with BI tools or wikis. For governance, relationships should be defined through relationship tests in schema.yml, which makes them queryable in manifest.json. Document relationships clearly in model descriptions and column definitions. Automated ERD generation ensures documentation stays current with code changes. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/documentation and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json

Question: Your organization runs dbt in production on a scheduled basis and needs to ensure that data quality issues are caught early. What is the BEST approach for integrating data quality checks into your dbt pipeline?
Q_1: Run dbt run followed by a separate script that queries the data warehouse to validate data quality
Q_2: Use dbt build which runs models, tests, snapshots, and seeds in dependency order, failing fast if tests don't pass
Q_3: Schedule dbt test to run daily and dbt run to run separately on a different schedule
Q_4: Only use schema tests in development and disable them in production to improve performance
Q_5: Configure on-run-start hooks to run all tests before any models are built
E_1: While this approach can work, it introduces unnecessary complexity and separation between transformation and testing. External validation scripts don't integrate with dbt's lineage, aren't version-controlled with your transformations, and don't benefit from dbt's built-in test framework. This approach also makes it harder to track which models have quality issues and doesn't leverage dbt's test execution capabilities or error handling.
E_2: Correct. The dbt build command is the best approach for production pipelines because it executes resources in DAG order while respecting dependencies and running tests at the appropriate points. When you run dbt build, it will build a model, run its tests, and only proceed to downstream models if tests pass. This 'fail fast' behavior prevents bad data from propagating through your pipeline. It's more efficient than running dbt run and dbt test separately because it interleaves execution and testing. dbt build is the recommended command for production deployments as it ensures data quality at each step of the transformation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build
E_3: Running tests and models on separate schedules defeats the purpose of data quality checks because you might deploy bad data before tests run or catch issues. This approach doesn't prevent bad data from flowing downstream and creates a time gap between when data is transformed and when quality is validated. Integrated testing with dbt build or running dbt test immediately after dbt run is far more effective.
E_4: This is the opposite of best practice. Tests are most critical in production where data quality issues can impact business decisions and downstream systems. While you might run more comprehensive tests in CI/CD, production should absolutely include essential data quality tests. Disabling tests in production removes your safety net and makes it impossible to detect data anomalies, source data changes, or transformation errors in real-time.
E_5: on-run-start hooks execute before any dbt operations, but running all tests at the start doesn't make logical sense because tests validate the output of models. You can't test models that haven't been built yet. Tests should run after their corresponding models are built, which is exactly what dbt build does. on-run-start hooks are better suited for pre-run setup tasks like creating schemas or setting session parameters, not for testing model outputs.
Explanation: OVERALL: The dbt build command represents the modern best practice for production dbt pipelines because it intelligently orchestrates models, tests, snapshots, and seeds in dependency order. Unlike dbt run which builds all models and then requires a separate dbt test command, dbt build interleaves testing with building. It constructs each model, immediately runs its tests, and only proceeds to downstream dependencies if all tests pass. This fail-fast behavior prevents cascading failures and stops bad data from propagating. For production deployments, use dbt build with appropriate error handling and alerting. You can configure store_failures to save failing test records for investigation and set severity levels (warn vs. error) to control pipeline behavior. Combine dbt build with dbt source freshness checks at the start of your pipeline to validate input data before transformation. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/build and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity

Question: You're building a funnel analysis model tracking user progression through stages (signup, activation, purchase). What SQL pattern is MOST appropriate for this transformation?
Q_1: Use multiple INNER JOINs to connect each funnel stage
Q_2: Create separate aggregations for each stage and manually compare them
Q_3: Use UNION to combine all stages into one table
Q_4: Use database cursors to loop through each user's journey
Q_5: Use LEFT JOINs from the first stage to subsequent stages, calculating conversion rates between steps
E_1: INNER JOINs would only return users who completed ALL funnel stages, eliminating users who dropped off at any step. For funnel analysis, you need to see drop-off points and progression rates, which requires keeping all users regardless of how far they progressed. INNER JOINs filter out partial progressions, making them inappropriate for funnel analysis.
E_2: While separate aggregations could work, they make it difficult to track individual user progression through stages and calculate conversion rates between steps. This approach also makes the analysis cumbersome and doesn't efficiently show the funnel flow. You'd need complex post-processing to associate users across stages.
E_3: UNION stacks rows vertically, combining similar datasets. For funnel analysis, you need to show progression across stages for individual users, not stack stage events into a long table. While you might use UNION to combine event tables initially, it's not the pattern for calculating funnel metrics and conversion rates between stages.
E_4: Cursors and procedural looping are procedural programming patterns that are inefficient in SQL and not supported in modern cloud data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift). SQL is optimized for set-based operations, not row-by-row processing. Using cursors would create extremely slow, unmaintainable code. dbt promotes declarative SQL patterns, not procedural code.
E_5: Correct. Funnel analysis requires LEFT JOINs to preserve all users from the starting stage and track how many progress to each subsequent stage. Pattern: with signup as (select user_id, signup_date from events where event = 'signup'), activation as (select user_id, activation_date from events where event = 'activation'), purchase as (select user_id, purchase_date from events where event = 'purchase') select s.user_id, s.signup_date, a.activation_date, p.purchase_date, case when a.user_id is not null then 1 else 0 end as activated, case when p.user_id is not null then 1 else 0 end as purchased from signup s left join activation a on s.user_id = a.user_id left join purchase p on s.user_id = p.user_id. This pattern shows progression and drop-off at each stage. Aggregate to get conversion rates: count(activated)/count(*) as activation_rate. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/3-intermediate
Explanation: OVERALL: Funnel analysis with SQL requires LEFT JOINs to track user progression through sequential stages while preserving drop-offs. Complete pattern: (1) Define each stage as a CTE with standardized columns (user_id, timestamp, stage-specific attributes). (2) Start with the widest funnel stage (typically signup/first touch). (3) LEFT JOIN each subsequent stage. (4) Add boolean flags for stage completion: case when stage_table.user_id is not null then 1 else 0 end. (5) Calculate time-to-conversion: datediff(day, earlier_stage_date, later_stage_date). (6) Apply time windows if needed: and activation_date <= signup_date + interval '7 days'. Final aggregation: select count(*) as signup_count, sum(activated) as activation_count, sum(purchased) as purchase_count, sum(activated)::float / count(*) as signup_to_activation_rate, sum(purchased)::float / nullif(sum(activated), 0) as activation_to_purchase_rate. For time-series funnels, partition by cohort (signup month). For complex funnels with multiple paths, use CASE statements or window functions (row_number() over (partition by user_id order by event_date)) to sequence events. Test for data quality: users with later events but no earlier events (orphaned conversions), duplicate events per user, timestamps out of order. This pattern is fundamental for product analytics, marketing attribution, and user behavior analysis. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/3-intermediate

Question: [SELECT 4] Your team is building incremental models in dbt and wants to ensure efficient processing. Which of the following are valid strategies for the incremental_strategy configuration? (Select FOUR)
Q_1: truncate
Q_2: upsert
Q_3: append
Q_4: merge
Q_5: delete+insert
Q_6: insert_overwrite
E_1: This is not a valid incremental strategy in dbt. While 'truncate and reload' is a common data warehouse pattern, dbt doesn't offer 'truncate' as an incremental_strategy option. If you need to rebuild a table from scratch, you would use a full refresh (dbt run --full-refresh) rather than configuring a truncate strategy. The available strategies are designed for incremental processing, not full table replacement.
E_2: While 'upsert' describes the behavior of some incremental strategies, it's not a valid incremental_strategy value in dbt. The actual strategy that performs upsert-like behavior is called 'merge' (for most databases) or 'delete+insert'. The term 'upsert' is used conceptually in documentation but isn't a configuration option you can set for incremental_strategy.
E_3: Correct. The 'append' strategy is one of the core incremental strategies available in dbt. It simply adds new rows to the existing table without checking for duplicates or updating existing records. This is the most performant strategy when you have immutable event data and can guarantee that new runs only add new records. It works by running an INSERT statement with the new rows. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_4: Correct. The 'merge' strategy uses a MERGE statement (or equivalent database-specific syntax) to upsert records based on a unique key. It updates existing rows when the unique_key matches and inserts new rows when no match is found. This is the default strategy for most databases and is appropriate when you need to update existing records or handle late-arriving data. The merge strategy requires defining a unique_key in the model configuration. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_5: Correct. The 'delete+insert' strategy is a two-step process that first deletes rows matching the unique_key from the new data, then inserts all new rows. This strategy is useful for databases that don't support MERGE statements efficiently or when you want more predictable performance. It's particularly common with Redshift and other databases where MERGE operations can be slow. This strategy ensures data consistency while being more performant than merge in certain scenarios. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
E_6: Correct. The 'insert_overwrite' strategy is available for BigQuery and some other databases. It uses partitioning to overwrite only the partitions that have new data, rather than the entire table. This strategy is highly efficient for partitioned tables because it only rewrites the affected partitions. In BigQuery, it uses the INSERT OVERWRITE statement with partition filters, making it optimal for time-partitioned event data where recent partitions are frequently updated. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models
Explanation: OVERALL: dbt supports multiple incremental strategies, each optimized for different use cases and databases. The 'append' strategy is simplest and fastest for immutable event data. The 'merge' strategy handles updates and inserts using MERGE statements, ideal for slowly changing dimensions. The 'delete+insert' strategy provides similar functionality to merge but with more predictable performance on certain databases. The 'insert_overwrite' strategy is optimal for partitioned tables in BigQuery, allowing efficient updates of specific partitions. The choice of strategy depends on your data characteristics (immutable vs. mutable), database platform, and performance requirements. You configure the strategy using the incremental_strategy config in your model's config block or dbt_project.yml. Always define a unique_key for strategies other than append to ensure correct record matching. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/incremental-models and https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/incremental_strategy

Question: [SELECT 3] Your organization is setting up dbt Cloud for production data pipeline orchestration. Which of the following are recommended best practices for managing production dbt runs? (Select THREE)
Q_1: Schedule jobs using dbt Cloud's built-in scheduler with appropriate run intervals based on data freshness requirements
Q_2: Disable all tests in production to maximize pipeline performance and reduce runtime
Q_3: Always run dbt with --full-refresh in production to ensure complete data accuracy
Q_4: Configure separate environments for development, staging, and production with appropriate credentials and schemas
Q_5: Implement alerting and monitoring for job failures, test failures, and data freshness issues
Q_6: Run all dbt models in a single monolithic job to ensure proper dependency order
E_1: Correct. dbt Cloud provides a robust job scheduler that allows you to define when and how often your dbt jobs run. You should schedule jobs based on your data freshness requirements and source data availability. For example, if source data arrives hourly, schedule your staging models to run hourly, and schedule downstream marts to run after staging completes. The dbt Cloud scheduler supports cron-style scheduling, dependency-based triggering, and integration with dbt Cloud's environment management. This provides reliable, managed orchestration without requiring external tools. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deployments
E_2: This is the opposite of best practice. Tests are critically important in production to catch data quality issues before they impact downstream consumers. While you might selectively configure some tests to warn rather than error, or optimize expensive tests, completely disabling tests removes your primary mechanism for data quality assurance. Production is where data quality matters most, and tests should be an integral part of your production pipeline execution.
E_3: Running --full-refresh on every production run is inefficient and unnecessary for most models. Full refreshes rebuild entire tables from scratch, which consumes excessive compute resources and time. Incremental models are specifically designed to avoid this by processing only new or changed data. Full refreshes should only be run when necessary (schema changes, logic changes requiring historical reprocessing, or incremental model issues), not as a default production pattern.
E_4: Correct. Environment separation is a fundamental best practice for dbt deployments. Each environment should have its own credentials, target schemas, and potentially different configurations. Development environments use developer credentials and personal schemas for safe experimentation. Staging environments mirror production configuration for pre-deployment validation. Production environments use service account credentials with appropriate permissions and write to production schemas. This separation prevents developers from accidentally modifying production data, enables safe testing, and follows security best practices. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/environments/environments-in-dbt
E_5: Correct. Production data pipelines require robust monitoring and alerting to ensure data reliability. dbt Cloud provides native notifications for job failures, test failures, and source freshness violations. You can configure notifications to Slack, email, or webhooks to alert data teams when issues occur. Additionally, monitor job duration trends to identify performance degradation, track test failure rates for data quality insights, and set up alerts for source freshness checks to catch upstream data delays. Proactive monitoring enables quick response to issues before they impact business users. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/job-notifications
E_6: While a single job can handle dependencies correctly, this approach doesn't scale well and reduces flexibility. Best practice is to organize jobs logically (e.g., separate jobs for source freshness checks, staging layer, marts layer, and snapshots) and use job dependencies or appropriate scheduling to ensure proper execution order. Breaking jobs into logical components allows for: partial reruns when specific layers fail, different schedules for different layers based on data needs, more granular monitoring and alerting, and better resource allocation. Modern orchestration in dbt Cloud supports job chaining and dependencies for this purpose.
Explanation: OVERALL: Effective production dbt pipeline management requires thoughtful orchestration, environment hygiene, and operational monitoring. Use dbt Cloud's scheduler to run jobs at appropriate intervals matching your data freshness SLAs. Structure jobs logically (e.g., separate jobs for hourly staging updates vs. daily mart builds) and leverage job dependencies to chain them appropriately. Maintain strict environment separation with development, staging, and production environments using different credentials, schemas, and configurations. Configure comprehensive monitoring including job failure alerts, test failure notifications, and source freshness warnings via Slack, email, or PagerDuty integrations. Run dbt build in production to ensure tests gate downstream execution. Use slim CI with state comparison for efficient CI/CD validation. Implement retry logic for transient failures, document runbook procedures for common issues, and regularly review job performance metrics. Consider using dbt Cloud's API for custom monitoring dashboards or integrating with broader observability platforms. Always test changes in staging before production deployment. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/deployments, https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/deploy/job-notifications, and https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/collaborate/environments/environments-in-dbt

Question: You have implemented a `unique` test on the `order_id` column in your `stg_orders` model, but the test is failing because some orders have multiple rows due to a data quality issue in the source system. Your stakeholders need the data available immediately while the source team fixes the issue. What is the BEST temporary approach to handle this situation?
Q_1: Remove the `unique` test from the model until the source data issue is resolved
Q_2: Add a WHERE clause filter in the staging model to exclude duplicate order_id values
Q_3: Configure the test with `error_if: '>10'` to allow the pipeline to continue while surfacing the data quality issue
Q_4: Change the test to `warn_if: '>0'` so it never fails but still reports the issue
Q_5: Create a pre-hook that deletes duplicate rows before the model materializes
E_1: Removing the test entirely eliminates visibility into the data quality issue and provides no alerting or monitoring. While this allows the pipeline to run, it removes the safeguard that detects duplicates. If the source team takes time to fix the issue or the issue worsens, you won't know about it. Tests should remain in place to provide ongoing monitoring, even if their failure thresholds are temporarily adjusted. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/data-tests
E_2: Filtering out duplicates in the staging model is not a testing approach—it's data transformation that hides the underlying data quality issue. Staging models should maintain the same grain as the source and not arbitrarily exclude data. If you filter duplicates without a clear business rule (which duplicate to keep?), you're making data decisions that could impact downstream analysis. The issue should be visible and tracked, not hidden. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/best-practices/how-we-structure/2-staging-intermediate-marts
E_3: This is the BEST approach. Using test severity thresholds like `error_if: '>10'` allows you to set an acceptable threshold for test failures while still monitoring the issue. The test will pass if there are 10 or fewer duplicate order_ids but fail if the problem exceeds that threshold. This provides a balance between allowing the pipeline to run (meeting immediate business needs) and maintaining data quality monitoring (surfacing when the issue worsens). You can also combine with `warn_if: '>0'` to still get warnings for any duplicates. This configuration should be documented and temporary. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_4: Using `warn_if: '>0'` instead of proper error thresholds means the test will always warn but never fail, regardless of how bad the data quality issue becomes. If thousands of duplicates appear, the pipeline will still run without error. While warnings provide visibility, they're often ignored in automated pipelines. A better approach is to use `error_if` with a reasonable threshold that allows some tolerance while still failing if the issue significantly worsens. Reference: https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/severity
E_5: Pre-hooks that delete data are risky and violate dbt best practices. Automatically deleting duplicates without a clear business rule or stakeholder approval can lead to data loss and incorrect analysis. Which duplicate should be kept?
